CERTIFICATE  OF  NATIONAL  LABOR  RELATIONS
BOARD.

The  National  Labor  Relations  Board,  by  its  Secre-
tary  duly  authorised  by  Rule  and  Regulation  of  the
National  Labor  Relations  Board  dated  the  16th  day  of
October,  1935,  a  copy  of  which  Rule  and  Regulation  is
attached  hereto  and  made  e  part  hereof,  doeg hereby
certify  that,  except  as  noted  below,  the  documents  an-
nexed  hereto  constitute  a  full  and  accurate  transcript  of
the  entire  record  in  a  proceeding  had  before  said  Board
entitled  “In  the  Matter  of  Jones  & Laughlin  Steel  Corpo-
ration,  and  Amalgamated  Association  of  Iron,  Steel  and
Tin  Workers  of  North  America,  Beaver  Valley  Lodge
No.  200,”  the  same  being  Case  No.  C-57  before  said
Board,  said  transcript  including  the  pleadings,  testimony  -

a

and  evidence  upon  which  the  order  of  the  Board  in
said  proceeding  was  entered,  and  including  also  the
findings  and  order  of  the  Board.
>

are  as  follows:

ciation  of  Iron,  Steel  and  Tin  Workers  of

on  January  23,  1936.

NATIONAL  LABOR  RELATIONS  BOARD
Washington,  D.  C.  —

Rule  and  Regulation  authorizing  the
Secretary  of  the  Board  to  certify
copies  of  papers  and  documents

By  virtue  of  the  authority  vested  in  it  by the  Na-
tional  Labor  Relations  Act,  approved  July  5, 1935,  the
National  Labor  Relations  Board  hereby  authorizes  the
Secretary  to  certify  copies  of  all  papers  and  documents
which  are  a  part  of  any  of  the  files  or  records  of  the
Board  as  may  be  necessary  or  desirable  from  time  to
time.  This  Rule  and  Regulation  shall  be  effective  upon
its  publication  in  the  following  manner,  namely  by  the

6

‘by the  members  of the  Board  and
the filing  of said  original  with  the Secretary
of the
Board,  at whose  office  the  original
shall  be available
for  public  inspection  and where copies of it  shall be
available  to  the  public.

i

Signed  at  Washington,  D.  C.  this  16th  day  of  Octo-

ber,  1935.

(Signed)  J.  Warren  Madden,  Chairman
(Signed)  Edwin  S.-  Smith,  Member
(Signed)  Jno.  M.  Carmody,  Member

CHARGE

Pursuant  to  Section  10  (b) of  the  National  Labor
Relations  Act,  the  undersigned  hereby  charges  that
Jones  &  Laughlin  Steel  Corporation,  Aliquippa  Works, :
Aliquippa,  Pa., has engaged  in and is engaging  in unfair

7

labor  practices  within  the  meaning  of Section  8, sub-
sections  (1)  and  (3)  of said  Act,  in that

The  Jones  & Laughlin  Steel  Corporation,  Aliquippa
Works,  of  Aliquippa,  Pennsylvania,  have  unjustifiably
discharged  and  demote  certain  of  their  employees,  all
of  whom  have  been  and  are  members  of  the  complain-
ant,  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200,  of  Aliquippa,  Penn-
sylvania,  which  is  affiliated  with  ah:  Amalgamated  As-
sociation  of  Iron,  Steel  and  Tin  Workers  of  North  Am-
erica,  and  with  the  American  Federation  of Labor.  That
all  of  the  members  of  the  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200
were  and  are  employees  of  the  respondent.

—~

That  these  discharges  and  demotion  of  the  em-
ployees  of  the  respondent,  who  are  members  of  the
Union,  were  unjustified,  were  discriminatory,  and .with-
out  cause  or  reason.
That  the  respondent  discharged
and  demoted  these  employees  because  of  their  member-
ship  and  activity  in  the  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200.
*
That  the  respondent  has  discharged  and  demoted
these  employees  so  as  to  intimidate,  coerce,  and  other-
wise  influence  or  force  other  employees  of  the  company
from  joining  the  Union,  or,  if  already  members,  from
taking  an  active  part  in  the  Union,  and  with  the  intent
and  purpose  of,  first  interfering  with  the  self-organiza-
tion  of  the  employees  of  Jones  & Laughlia,  and,  second,
with  the  intent  of  frustrating  or  otherwise  Lreaking  up
the  activities  of  the  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200,  and
with  a  view  of  eventually  dissolving  the  Union.

That  a  summary  of these discharges  and demotion
of said  employees  is more  fully  set forth  in  Exhibit  “A”,
attached  hereto  and  made  part  hereof.

The  undersigned  further  charges  that  said  unfair
labor  practices  are  unfair  labor  practices  affecting  com-
merce  within  the  meaning  of  said  Act.

Name  and  address  of  person  or  labor  organization
making  the  charge.
(If made  by  a  labor  organization,
give also  the name  and  official  position  of  the  person

Albert  Atattalo  President
Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200
130  Grandview  Avenue,  Aliquippa,  Pa.  '

Subscribed  and  sworn  to  before  me  this  23rd  day

of  January,  1936

;

°
Robert  H.  Kleeb

Regional  Attorngy

SUMMARY  OF DISCHARGES  AND  DEMOTION
1.  DOMENIC  BRANDY.  Check  No.  10110,  jigman
on  coal  washer,  coke  department,  employed  by the  com-
pany  since  1910,  and  employed  for  eight  (8)  years  on
the  coal  washer.
He  was  suspended  from  ‘work  for  two  (2)  weeks,
beginning  August  5th,  1935.  He  had  never  been  sus-
pended  from  work  before.  He is a trustee  of the Union
and  prominently  active.  On  November  28th,  1935,  he  .
wae discharged,  for the reasons set forth in the body of
the  “CHARGE.”.
‘2  ANGELO VOLPE.  Check  No.  11757,  craneman,
tabe department.  He  has  been  in  the  employ
of  the  company  since  1914,  and  employed  as  a  crane-

_  Welded

7

man  for  the  past  five  (5)  years.  He  was  discharged  on
July  3ist,  1935,  for the  reasons  set  forth  in  the  body  of
the  “CHARGE.”

3.  HARRY  PHILLIPS.  Was  formerly  president
of ‘the  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200,  and  has been  an
active  member  of  the  Union.  He  was  discharged  on.
July  20th,  1935,  for  the  reasons  set  forth  in  the  body  of
the  “CHARGE.”

4.  MARTIN  DUNN.

Check  No.  135,  craneman,
Sinter  Plant,  employed  by  the  company  since  1920,  and
employed  as  a  craneman  for  over  a  year.  He was dis-
charged  on  July  9th,  1935,  for  the  reasons  set  forth  in
‘the  body  of  the “CHARGE.”

5.  WILLIAM  J.  COLLINS.  Check  No.  18268,  em-
ployed  as  a  carpenter  in  the  service  department  since
1926.  He  had  never  been  discharged,  quit,  layed-off,  or
transferred.  He  was  a  very  active  member  of  the com-
plainant  lodge  ever  sinee  the  date  of  its  organization  in
October  1934.
He  was  discharged  on  December  3rd,
1935,  for  the  reasons  set  forth  in  the  body  of  the
“CHARGE.”

.

6.  GEORGE  MARROLL.  .He  was  a fitter  at  the
Aliquippa  pliant,  and  ha:  been  very  active  as  a  member
of  the  complainant  Union.  He  was  discharged  on  De-
cember  5th,  1935,  for  the  reasons  set  forth  in  the  body
of  the  “CHARGE.”

;

7.  ROYAL  BOYER.

Check  No.  9619,  nail  mill,
wire  department.  He has  been  working  since  1924.  He
was  suspended  on  October  29th,  1935,  for  a period  of
two  (2)  weeks.
Prior  thereto,  and  afterwards,  when
he  returned  to  work,  up  to  and  until  December  9th,  he

was subjected to distrimination  and unfair tactics by
the inspector  and foreman,  because  of his Union activi-
ties.  On December  9th, 1995, he was discharged  for the
reasons  set forth in the body  of the “CHARGE.”

8  GULIO  YACOBUCCI.  He has been working for
respo. dent since 1923.  He was  discharged  on  Decem-
ber 4th, 1905, fas the reasons cat forth in the body of
the “CHARGE.

9  MARTIN  GERSTNER.  He  was  employed  by
for the past six (6) years.  He was @
the respondent
motor inspector.  He is Financial  Secretary  of the Union,
and very  active  in its behalf.  He was discharged on
December  16th,  1935,  for the reasons  set forth  in the
body  of the “CHARGE.”

10.  ANGELO  RAZZANO.  Check  No.  22611,  tractor
operator  in the seamless  tube department.  He has been
in the employ  of the company  off and on since 1924 and
has been  employed  steadily  for the past  seven  years.
He is a  very active  member  of the Union.  He  was dis-
charged  on January  13th, 1936, for the reasons  set forth
in the body of the “CHARGE.”

11.  ELY  BOZICH  Check  No.  17622

He  has
worked  for the company  off and on for the past fifteen
years and has been  employed  as a  laborer  in the north
mill boiler  house  for the past eight  years.  He is  an ac-

in the Union.  He was discharged

12.  RONALD B. COX.  Check

No. 21718.  He has
worked  for the company  for seven years as an electric
hoisting  craneman  in  the  seamless  tube  department. .
He  was discharged  on January  6th, 1956, for the reasons

13.  MARCO  LUKICH.  CHECK  No.  15078,  mill-
wright.  He  has  been  im the  employ  of the company
since  1910,  and  employed  as  a  millwright  for  the  past
ven  (10)  years.

On June  18th, 1935,  he  was demoted  from  a  mill-
wright  to a  crane hooker.  This  demotion  resulted  in
a  decrease  from  SEVENTY-ONE  AND  ONE-HALF
(71%c)  CENTS  an  hour,  to  FIFTY-TWO  (S2cy  CENTS
an hour.

On August  5th, 1935,  he was demoted  from  a  crane
to a laborer.  This demotion
resulted  in a de-
hooker
crease  from  FIFTY-TWO  (S2c)  CENTS  AN hoar,  to
FORTY-SEVEN  (47c)  CENTS  an  hoir  in  pay.

On August 6th, 1995, he  was sent home, and told not
12th, 1936,
to return  to work  until called  for.  On August
he was put back to work as a  laborer.

He  has been active in the Union, and he  was des
moted,  as aforesaid,  for the reasons  set forth  in the
body  of the “CHARGE.”

BEFORE  THE  NATIONAL  LABOR  RELATIONS
BOARD

Sixth  Region

In  the  Matter  of

|

JONES  &  LAUGHLIN  STEEL

CORPORATION

and

‘CASE  NO.  VI  C-46

AMALGAMATED  ASSOCIATION
OF  IRON,  STEEL  AND  TIN
WORKERS  OF  NORTH  AM-
ERICA,  BEAVER  VALLEY
LODGE  NO.  200

J

It  having  been  charged  by  the  Amalgamated  Asso-
ciatian  of  Iron,  Steel  and  Tin  Workers  of  North  Ameri-
ca,  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200,  that  the  Jones  &
Laughlin  Steel  Corporation,  hereinafter  called  the  re-
spondent,  has  engaged  in  and  is now  engaging  in  certain
unfair  labor  practices  affecting  commerce  as  set  forth
and  defined  in  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  ap-
proved  July  5,  1935,
the  National  Labor  Relations
Board,  by  its  Regional  Director  for  the  Sixth  Region,
as  agent  of  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board  desig-
nated  by  National  Labor  Relations  Board  Rules  and
Regulations,  Series  1,  Article  IV,  Section  1,  hereby  al-
leges  the  following:

1.  The  respondent  is  and  has  been  since  Decem-
ber,  1922,  a  corporation  organized  and  existing  by  vir-
tue  of  the  laws  of  the  Commonwealth  of  Pennsylvania,
having  its  principal  office  and  place  of  business  in  the
City  of  Pittsburgh,  County  of  Allegheny,  and  Common-
wealth  of  Pennsylvania,  and  is  now  and  has  continu-

13

ously  been  engaged  at  a  place  of  business  in  the  Bor-
ough  of  Aliquippa,  County  of  Beaver,  Commonwealth
the  Aliquippa
(hereinafter  called
of  Pennsylvania,
Works)  in  the  production,  sale  and  distribution  of  iron
and  steel  products.

2  The  respondent  in  the  course  and  conduct  of its
business  causes  and  has  continuously  caused  a  ‘prepon-—
derant  portion  of  the  raw  materials  used  in the  produc-
tion  of  its  iron  and  steel  products  to  be  purchased  and
transported  in  interstate  commerce  from  and  through
states  of the  United  States  other  than  the  Commonwealth
of  Pennsylvania  to  the  plant  of  the  respondent  in  Ali-
quippa  in  the  Commonwealth  of  Pennsylvania,  and
causes  and  has  continuously  caused  a  preponderant
portion  of  the  iron  and  steel  products  produced  by  it
to  be  sold  and  transported  in  interstate  commerce  from
the  Aliquippa  Works  in  the  Commonwealth  of  Penn-
sylvania  to,  into  and  through  states  of  the  United  States
other  than  the  Commonwealth  of  Pennsylvania,  all  of
the  aforesaid  constituting  a  continuous  flow  of  com-
merce  among  the  several  states.

3.  The  respondent  by its: officers  and  agents,  while
engaged  at  the  Aliquippa  Works  as  described  above,  did
discharge

Domenic  Brandy  on  the  28th  day  of  November,  1935.
Angelo  Volpe  on  the  31st  day  of  July, 1935..
Harry  Phillips  on  the  20th  day  of  July,  1935.
Martin  Dunn  on  the  9th  day of July,  1935.
Wm.  J.  Collins  on  the  3rd  day  of  December,  1935.
George  Marroll  on  the  5th  day  of December,  1935.
Royal  Boyer  on  the  9th  day  of  December,  1935.
Gulio  Yacobucci-on  the  4th  day  of December,  1935.
Martin  Gerstner  on  the  16th day  of  December,  1935.
Angelo  Razzano  on  the  13th day  of  January,  1936.
Eli  Bozich  on  the  13th  day  of January,  1936.
Ronald B. Cox  on  the  6th  day  of  January,  1936.

14

hereinafter  referred  to  collectively  as  the  above-named
individuals,  employed  by  the  respondent  at  the  Aliquip-
pa  Works,  and  has  at  all  times  since  said  dates  refused
to  reinstate  said  above-named  individuals  and  each  of
them.

4  The  respondent  by its  officers  and  agents,  while
engaged  at  the  Aliquippa  Works  as  described  above  did
demote

Marco  Lukich  on  the  5th  day  of  August,  1935
hereinafter  referred  to collectively  with  the  above-named
individuals,  employed  by  the  respondent  at  the  Ali-
quippa  Works,  and  has  at  all  times  since  said  date  re-
fused  to  reinstate  to  his  former  position  the  above-
named  individual.

5.  The  respondent  discharged  and  refuses  to  rein-
state  the  discharged  individuals  named  in  paragraph  3
and  to  reinstate  to  his  former  position  the.  demoted  in-
dividual  named  in  paragraph  4 for  the  reason  that  said
above-named  individuals  joined  and  assisted  a  labor
organization  known  as  the  Amalgamated  Association  of
Iron,  Steel  and  Tin  Workers  of  North  America,  Beaver
Valley  Lodge  No.  200,  and  engaged  in  concerted  activi-
‘ties  with  other  employees  at  the  Aliquippa  Works  for
the  purpose  of  collective  bargaining  and  other  mutual
aid  and  protection.

—

6.  By  its  discharge  and  demotion  of  the  said
above-named  individuals  and  each  of  them,  and  by  its
‘refusal  to  reinstate  said  above-named  individuals  and
each  of them,  as  above  set  forth,  the  respondent  did
‘interfere  with,  restrain,  and  coerce  and  is  interfering
with;  restraining,  and  coercing  its  employees  in  the  ex-
ercise  of the  rights  guaranteed  in  Section  7 of  said  Act,

15

and  did  thereby  engage  in  and  is  thereby  engaging  in
an  unfair  labor  practice  within  the  meaning  of  Section
8,  subdivision  (1)  of  said  Act.

7.  By  its  discharge  and  demotion  of  said  above-
named  individuals and each  of  them,  and  by  its  refusal
to  reinstate  said  above-named  individuals  and  each  of
them,  as  above  set  forth,  the  respondent  did  discrimi-
nate  and  is  discriminating  in  regard  to  the  hire  and
tenure  of  employment  of  said  above-named  individuals
and  each  of  them  and  did  discourage,  and  is  discour-
aging  membership  in  the  Amalgamated  Association  of
Iron,  Steel  and  Tin  Workers  of  North  America,  Beaver
Valley  Lodge  No.  200,  and  did- thereby  engage  in  and  is
thereby  engaging  in  unfair  labor  practices  within  the
meaning  of  Section  8,  subdivision  (3)  of  said  Act.

8.  The  aforesaid  unfair  labor  practices  of  the  re-
spondent  occur  in  commerce  among  the  several  states,
and  on  the  basis  of  experience  in  the  aforesaid  plant
and  others  in  the  same  and  other  industries,  burden  and
obstruct  such  commerce  and  the  free  flow  thereof,  and
have  led  and  tend  to  lead  to  labor  disputes  burdening
and  obstructing  commerce  and  the  free-flow  thereof.

9.  The  aforesaid  acts  of  the  respondent,  as.  set
forth  in  paragraphs  3  to  7  inclusive,  constitute  unfair
labor  practices  affecting  commerce  within  the  meaning
of  Section  8,  subdivisions  (1)  and  (3),  and  Section  2,
subdivisions  (6)  and  (7)  of  said  Act.

WHEREFGRE,  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board
on  the  23rd  of Jandary,  1936,  issues  its  complaint  against
the  Jones  &  Laughlin  Steel  Corporation,  respondent
herein.

|

DECISION

STATEMENT  OF  THE  CASE

On  January  23,  1936,  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200,
of the  Amalgamated  Association  of  Iron,  Steel  and  Tin
Workers  of  North  America,  hereinafter  referred  to  as
the Union,  filed  a  aaa with  the — Director  for  —

the  Sixth  Region  against  the Jones
& Laughlin  Steel  Cor-
poration,  Pittsburgh,  Pennsylvania,  hereinafter  referred
to  as  the  respondent,  charging  the  respondent  with  vio-
lations  of Section  8, subdivisions  (1)  and  (3)  of  the  Na-
tional  Labor  Relations  Act,  approved  July  5; 1935,  here-
inafter  referred  to  as  the  Act.  Thereupon,  a  complaint
and  notice  of  hearing  signed  by Clinton  S$.  Golden,  Re-
gional  Director  for  the  Sixth  Region,  were  issued  and
duly  served-  upon  the  respondent.
The  complaint
charged  the  respondent  with  violations  of  Section  8,
subdivisions  (1)  and  (3)  because  of  the  discharge  of
and  refusal  to  reinstate  12*  employees  and  the  demo-
tion  of  one  employee,  all  at  its  Aliquippa  Works,  for
the  reason  that  they  joined  and  assisted  the  Union  and
engaged  in  concerted  activities  with  other  employees
at  the  Aliquippa  Works  for  the  purpose  of  collective
bargaining  and  other  mutual  aid  and  protection.

The  respondent  filed  a  special  appearance  and_an-
swer.
For  answer  it  admitted  the  discharges  and  de-
motion,  but  averred  that  in  all  cases  they  were  made
because  of inefficiency  and  violation of the  respondent's
rules.  The  respondent  further  claimed-that  the  Act  vio-
lates  the Fifth  and  Seventh  Amendments  to  the  Consti-
tution  of the  United  States,  that  it confers  judicial  power
on  the  Board  in  contravention  of that  Constitution,  and
that  the  activities  of  respondent  are  intrastate  and  not
subject  to  regulation  by  Congress  or  the  Board.

By order  of  the  Board  dated-February  27,  1936,  the
proceeding  was transferred  to and  continued  before  the

~  2
tional Labor Relations  Board Rules and Regulations  «

'  Series 1.

A hearing  was beld on March  2 and 3, 1936 at Pitts’
burgh,  Pennsylvania,  before  the Board,  at  which  hear-
ing full opportunity  to be beard,  to  examine  and crose-
examine  witnesses  and  to  introduce  evidence  bearing  |
on the issue,  was afforded  to  all parties.  The  Board
and the respondent  both  presented  evidence  as to the
nature  of  the  respondent’s  business  operations.
The
re pondent  then  moved  te dismiss  on  the ground  that
on  the  evidence  it  conclusively  appeared  that  neither
the  respondent’s  operations  nor  its  labor  relations  were
in or affected  interstate  commerve.  The motion to dis
miss
was denied.  Thereupon,
the respondent's  counsel
took  no further  part in the hearimg  and withdrew.  A
further  hearing.was  held  in Washington,  D. C., April 2
to April  8, 1936.

“s

Upon the evidence

adduced  of the hearing
and from
the entire record  now before it, including  the tranacript
of the hearing  and exhibits  introduced,  the Board  makes
the following:

FINDINGS  OF  FACT

L

The  Respondent

_.
The  Jones  & Laughlin  Steel  Corporation  is  a  Penn-
sylvania  corporation  which,  together  with  its subsidiaries
—19  in naumber—manufactures  and distributes  a  widely
diversified  lime  of stee]  aad pigiron.  The  respondent

24

is  the  fourth  largest.  producer  of  steel  in  the  United
States.
It has an  annual  capacity  of 3,189, 700  N.T.  coke;
3,000,000  G.T.  pig-iron;  3,660,000  G.T.  steel  ingots;  and
2,837,600  G.T.  of  finished  steel  hot  rolled  products.
In
the  year.  1934  the  respondent  shipped  880,031  N.T.  of
steel  products,  valued  (together  with  certain  other  gross.
receipts)  at  $47,957,338.36.
It  has  two  plants,  one  in
Pittsburgh  and  one  nearby  in  Aliquippa.  The  assets  of  it
and  its  consolidated  companies  were  valued,  as  of  De-
cember  31,  1934,  at  $181,532,641.36.
It  employs  over  22,-
000  persons,  about  10,000  of  them  at its  Aliquippa  plant.

In  a  registration  statement  filed  with  the  Securities
and  Exchange  Commission  by  the  respondent,  ‘it  char-
acterized  itself  as  “completely  integrated,  owning  and
operating  ore, coal,  and  limestone  properties,  lake  and
river  transportation  facilities  and  terminal  railroads
located  at  its  manufacturing  plants,  at  Pittsburgh  and
Aliquippa,  Pennsylvania”.
The  respondent  owns  or
controls  mines  in  Michigan  and  Minnesota  estimated  to
contain  approximately  60,000,000  tons  of  ore.
It  oper-
ates  four  ore  steamships  on  the  Great  Lakes,  used  in
transportation  of ore  to  its  factories.
It owns  coal  mines
in  Pennsylvania  estimated  to  contain  600,000,000  tons  of
recoverable  coal.  Much  of  this  coal  is  located  adjacent
to  the  Monongahela  River.
The  respondent  owns  and
operates  ten  tow  boats  and  165  steel  barges  used  in
The  re-
carrying  coal  from  the  mines  to  its  factories.
spondent  owns  limestone  properties,  in  various  pl&ces
Iron  ore,  coal,  and
limestone  are  the  three  important  materials  used  in
producing  pig-iron  and  steel.

.  in  Pennsylvania  and  West  Virginia.

The  respondent  owns  and  operates  the  Mononga-
hela  Connecting  Railroad—40.99  miles  of  standard  guage
track—which  connects  the  plants  of the  Pittsburgh  works

>

p+)

and  forms  an  interconnection  with  the  Pennsylvania,
New  York  Central  and  Baltimore  and  Ohio  railroad  sys-
tems  at  Pittsburgh,  Pennsylvania.  This  line  has  30  loco-
motives  and  713  freight.  cars.
It  owns  the  Aliquippa
and  Southern  Railroad  Company—43.20  miles  of  stand-
ard  guage  track—which  connects  the  Aliquippa  Works
with  the  Pittsburgh  and  Lake  Erie  (part  of  the  New
York  Central  System).  The  Aliquippa  line  has  19  loco-
motives  and  614  freight  cars.
It  reports  to  the  Interstate
Commerce  Commission  as  a  common  carrier.
Thus,  by
means  of its  own  railroads,  the  respondent  ships  its  raw
materials  into  its  works  and  ships  out  the  finished  and
semi-finished  products.’  The.  cars  of  the  Aliquippa  line,
for  example,  are  carried  to  the  point  of  loading  or  un-
loading  and  there  are  handled  by  the  respondent’s  em-
ployees  with  the  help  of.  the  respondent's  cranes  and
loading  equipment.  A  system  of  tracks  connects  all
parts  of  the  works,  buildings,  and  yards  with  each  other
and  with  the  rails  of  the  Aliquippa  line  and  from  that
to  the New  York  Central  line.  The  respondent  is  the
largest  shipper  over  the  Pittsburgh  and  Lake  Erie.  More
than  97%  of  the  shipments  originating  at  Aliquippa  are
made  by the  respondent.
If operations  at  the  Aliquippa
Works  were  interrupted  by  labor  difficulties  or  other-
wise  the  business  of  the  Pittsburgh  &  Lake  Erie—an
interstate  carrier—would  be. seriously  crippled.  —

The  respondent  has  far-flung  properties  and  many
employees  engaged  in the  distribution  of  its  products.
.  Much  of  the  product  is  shipped  to  its  warehouses  in
Chicago,  Detroit,  Cincinnati,  and  Memphis—to  the  last
two  places  by means  of  its  own  barges  and  transporta-

-'

In  Long  Island  City,  New  York  and
tion  equipment.
in  New  Orleans  it  operates  structural  steel  fabricating
shops  in  connection  with  the.  warehousing  of  semi-fin-
ished  materials  sent  from  its  Works.  Through  one  of
its  wholly-owned  subsidiaries  it  owns,  leases  and  oper-
ates  stores,  warehouses,  and  yards  for  the  distribution
of equipment  and  supplies  for  drilling  and  operating  oil
and  gas  mills  and  for  pipe  lines,  refineries,  and  pump-
It has  sales  offices  in 20  cities  in  the  United
ing stations.
States  and  a  wholly-owned  subsidiary  devoted  ex-
It  ad-
clusively  to  distributing  its  product  in  Canada.
Approximately
75  per  cent  of  its  product  is  shipped  out  of  Pennsy}
vania.*

-  vertises  in  newspapers  and  magazines.

27

detached,

The  ramilcations  of  the  Jones  &  Laughlin  Steel
Corporation  are  thus  as  broadly  extended  as  the  nation
itself.
It  is  impossible  to  isolate  the  operations  of  the
Works  in  Pittsburgh  and  Aliquippa  or  to  consider  them
as
separate—“local”—  phenomena.  These
Works  might  be  likened  to  a  heart  of  a  self-contained,
highly  integrated  body.  They  draw  in  the  raw  materials
from  Michigan,  Minnesota,  West  Virginia,  Pennsylvania
in  part  through  arteries  and  by  means  controlled  by
the  respondent;  they  transform  the  materials  and  then
pump  them  out  to  all  parts  of  the  nation.  through  the
vast  mechanism  which  the  respondent  has  elaborated.

In  these  and  many  other  respects  the  respondent
shows  the  characteristics  of  the  steel  industry  as a
whole.  This  industry  had,  as  of  1934,  an  investment  of
$4,705,976,350.  To  support  its  activity  33,000  men  mine
ore,  44,000  men  mine  coal,  4,000  men  quarry  limestone,
16,000  men  manufacture  coke,  343,000  men  manufacture
steel,  and  83,000  men  transport  its  product.
The  con-
ception  of  the  industry  as  a  conduit  through  which  ma-
terials  pass  in  a  continuous  stream  from  mine  to  con-
sumer,  undergoing  transformations  en  route,
at  the
mine,  at  the  mill,  at  the  fabricating  plant,  until  they
are  delivered  to  the  consumer  becomes,  for technical
and  economic  reasons,  more  and  more  exact.

The  steel  industry  involves  enormous  movements  of
materials  back  and  forth  across  the  length  and  breadth
of  the  nation.
Of  approximately  25,000,000  tons  of  ore
mined  in  the  United  States  in  1934,  15,000,000  were

entire  process  through  the  medium  of  a  single  company
is  typical  of  the  few  giant  corporations  which  control
the  bulk  of  the  steel  production  in  this  country.  Two
corporations,  the  United  States  Steel  Corporation  and
the  Bethlehem  Steel  Corporation, have  over  50%  of  the
steel  capacity  of  the  country.  Add  to  this  eight  more
companies,  among  them  the  respondent,  and  91%  of  the
nation’s  capacity  is accounted  for.  This  great  size  is  in
part  an  outgrowth  of  technical  forces.

the  steel  industry  as  a  whole  and  in  its  most  im-

The  device
of  the  “transit  rate”  for  steel  products—“transit  rate”
on  numerous  products  are  permitted  by  the  Inter-
state  Commerce  Commission—which  is  used  by  the  re-
spondent  illustrates  in  particular  that  processing  and
fabrication  are  simply  transformations  of  materials  en
route  in  the  stream  of  commerce.  Under  the  transit  rate
a  steel  fabricator  who  buys  steel  forms  from  a  steel
producer  at  point  A,  performs  on  them  certain  limited
work  at point  B  and  then  ships  the  product  to  point  C,
receives  from  the  railroads  the  privilege  of  the  through
rate  from  A  to  C,  (point  B being  considered  only  as  a
temporary  stopping  place where  service  is performed  in
transit).  The  respondent  has  availed  itself  of the  transit
rate  to  fabricate  steel  thus  in  transit  ‘for  delivery  in
states  other  than  Pennsylvania.
The  great  importance
of  transportation  to  the  steel  industry  and  of  the  steel
industry  to  the  railroads  is shown  by the  fact  that  12%
of  the  railroads’  freight  is  attributable  to  the  activity
of  the  steel  industry.  |

The  price  ‘system  of  the  steel  industry  emphasizes
the  close  relation  between  shipment  and  manufacture
of  the  product.  We  refer  to  the  famous  basing  point
system  of quoting  prices.  “The  essence  of  the  multiple
basing  point  system  in  the  steel  industry,”  says  the
ission*  of  the  price  system  now
Federal  Trade
found,in  its use of a device  of  calcula-
in use, “is to  be
tion  whereby  buyers  pf a  commodity  located  at  any
given  point  are  charged  by  the  industry  a  definite
uniform  price  for  delivery  at  that  point,  regardless  of
the  point  of  shipment  ....”  This  system  enlarges  the
market  in  which  many  companies  can  compete,  since
it  neutralizes  transportation  differentials  based  on  dif-
fering  distances  between  the  plants  of  competifors  and
*Report  of  Federal  Trade  Commission  to the  President  with

Respect  to the Basing  Point  System,  p. 2,, November  1904.

31

@  given  customer.
It  no  doubt  assists  many  of  the  com-
panies  in  maintaining  their  great  size,  their  broad  struc-
tures of distribution,  and  their  high  degree  of  integra-
tion.
It  reveals  fully  the  fact  that  transportation  of
the  product  is  not  only  the  final  stage  of  that  integra-
tion,  but  one  which  conditions  the  extent  and  nature
of  the  earlier  stages.  :  The  mining  of  materials,  their
transportation  to  and  collection  at  a  point,  their  trans-
formation  there  into  pig-iron,  into  steel  shapes in  all‘
stages  of  finish,  their  reshipment  for  further  fabrica-
tion,  for  use  in  railways,  automobiles,  buildings: . all  -
this  is  one  giant,  indivisible  economic  process:  it  is
commerce  among  the  states.

We  conclude  that  the  operations  of  the  respondent
constitute  a  continuous  flow  of  trade,  traffic,  and  com-
merce  among  the  several  states.

J
Organization  of  the  Union  in  Aliquippa

“

Aliquippa  is  a  city  of  about  30,000  inhabitants,
10,000  of whom  are  employed  by the  respondent,  Thus,
one  in  every  three  is  dependent  upon  the  respondent
for  employment.  Assuming  that an’ average  proportion
of  persons  have  families  this  means  that  pearly  every
one  looks  to  the  respondent  for  his  livelihood.
The
respondent  owns  im  Aliquippa a street  railway  system,
a  motor  coach  system, and  the  water  supply  system.  It
owns  128  acres  of  improved  and  unimproved  property,
approximately  1,174  acres  of  farm  land,  and  674  dwell-
ings  occupied  by  employees  all  located  in  and  about
‘Aliquippa.  The  respondent  has  its  own  Police  force—
the  “J  & L Police.”

On August  4, 1934,  the Amalgamated  Association
of Irom, Steel and Tin Workers  chartered  the Beaver
Valley Lodge,  a  labor organization,  with the following

_  officers  and  charter  members:  Harry  V.  Phillips,  presi-
dent;  Angelo  Volpe,  vice  president;  B.  S.  McDonald,
recording  secretary;  Martin  Gerstner,  financial  secre-
tary;  James  A,  Dunn,  Treasurer;  Andrew  Smith,  guide;
Allen  James,  inside  guard;  E.  L.. Ponder,  outside  guard.
The  union  sought,  thereafter,  to  organize  the  men
_
in  the  Aliquippa  Works.  Their  efforts  were  countered
Officers  of  the  union  and  organ-
by systematic  terror.
izers  who.came  into  Aliquippa  were  ‘followed  about by
the  private  police  of  the  resyondent—the  “J  &  L  Po-.
lice.”  The  more  important  umon  officers  were  honored
by  the  respondent  with  permanent  shadows  and  were
followed  even  into  the  neighboring  town  of  Ambridge
where  they  carried  on  their  activities  because  of  the
difficult  situation  in Aliquippa.  The  house  of Gerstner,
the  financial  secretary,  at  which  an  organization  meet-
ing had  been  held,  was  surrounded  day  and  night  by
the  J & L Police,  and  the  employment  agent  of  the  re-
spondent  sat  near  Gerstner’s  doorway  noting  down  the
names  of those  who  entered  the  house.  Persons  coming
out of the  house  were  questioned.  Some  were  mysteri-
ously beaten  and hit on the head while  walking  in the
in August,  1933, a union  organ-
streets.  A  year earlier
izer,  John.  S.  Meyer,  had  come  to  Aliquippa  and  dis-
tributed  union  pamphlets.  As  he  went  along  the  street
who beat him  severely.
He  was then taken  before  a  Justice  of Police,  fined  $,
and  refused  a  transcript  of  record  for  purposes  of  ap-.
peal.  Until he left  town  he  was  trailed  by automobiles
owned  by the  respondent.

‘he  was set upon  by  two persons

|

The officers  and  organizers  found  it impossible,  in
August  and September  of  1934,  to  secure  a  public  place,
in  Aliquippa for a  union  meeting.  They  were  refused
the Italian  Hall, the  Slovak  Hall, the  Serbian  Hall;  they
were refused  the  use  of open  lots.  They  held  their.
meetings in an  open  lot  in  Ambridge,  a  town  across

33

In  October,  union  officers
the  river  from Aliquippa.
‘complained  of  the  Aliquippa  situation  to  the  Governor
of ‘the  State.
State  police  were  sent  to  Aliquippa.  On
October  14,  1934  the  first  open  meeting  of  the  union
It  was  addressed  by Mrs.  Pin-
was  held  in  Aliquippa.
chot,  the  wife  of  the  Governor  of  the  State.

In  June,  1933  the  respondent  installed  at  Aliquippa
The
a  so-called  “Plan  of  Employees’  Representation”.
plan  provides  for  the  number  and  qualification  of  rep-
resentatives  under  the  plan..  Under  the  original  plan
a  representative  must  have  been  in  the  employ  of-the  .
respondent  for  a period  of  at  least  one  year  immediately
prior  to  the  day  of  his  nomination.
As  amended  in
May  18,  1935  the  requirement  that  the  representative  be
an  employee  was  suspended  during  the  period  that  the
National  Industrial  Recovery  Act  (NIRA)  was  in  force.
The  plan  provides  for  the  election  of  officers,  for  meet-
ings,  and  for  settlement  of  grievances.

The  question  of  adopting  this  plan  was  not,  appar-
ently,  ever  put  to  a  vote  of  the  employees.  They  were
invited  simply  to  elect  employee  representatives.
In
the  elections in  June,  1935  (after  the  NIRA  had  been
declared  unconstitutional)  many  employees—practically
all  concerned  in  this  complaint—were  urged  two  and
three  times  by the  respondent’s  foremen  and  supervisors
to  vote  in  the  election,  which  was  being  held  on  the
respondent’s  premises.

-1.  Martin  Gerstner.—Gerstner  was  employed  by
the  respondent  in  1930  and  worked’  regularly  until  his
discharge  on  December  14,  1935.  He  had  worked  pre-
viously  for  15  years  with  the  American  Rolling  Mill

34

Company.  He  was  first  employed  by  the  respondent
as a  wire  man.  He  was  transferred to the  blooming

the time  of his  discharge.  Gerstner
was  one  of the
charter  members  of the  union;  was  financial  secretary;
The  first
and  was  extremely  active  in  organization.
meeting  of  the  union  in  Aliquippa  was  held  outside  of

was  followed  by them.  In the  summer  of  1935  he  was
given  less  work  than the  other  motor  inspectors  on  the
job,  though  some  were  junior  to  him  in  employment.
The  reason  given  for  his  discharge  was  that  a  nut  had

3.  Angelo  Volpe.—Volpe  was  first  employed  by

partment.  He  was  promoted  in  1916  to  the  job  of
-  greaser  at  the  rolls;  two  years  later  to  the  job  of
rougher.  He  quit  in  1918  and  returned  in  1919.  From

his  discharge  on  July  31,  1935.

cause.
At  this  time  he  was  laid  off  for  15  days,  because

4.  Angelo  Razzano.—Razzano  had  been  employed

had.

10.  Ronald  Cox.—Cox  had  been  employed  3 1/2

he  was  called  into  the  office  of  the  general  manager

the  house;  that  the  local  merchants  would  not  extend

down  and  throw  the  key  in  the  river  before  they  would
recognize  an  outside  union.
this,  the  general  manager  wrote  something  on  a  paper.

*

e

*

.-

e

It  is  clear  that  the  respondent  discharged  these  ten
men  because  of  their  union  activity  and  for  the  purpose’
of  discouraging  membership  in  the  union.
The  initial
attempts  of  the  union  to  organize  in  1934  were  met  by
violent  terroristic  action.  The  coming  of the  State  Po-
The  advent  of
lice  made  such  action  more  difficult.
NIRA  gave  a  fillip  to  organization.
But  immediately
after  the  invalidation  of  NIRA  the  respondent  began
anew  its  campaign,  this  time  in  a  more  subtle  form.
Great  pressure  was  brought  to  bear  on  union  members
to  make  them  vote  under  the  employee  representation
plan.
This  was  relatively  unsuccessful.  Foremen  and
supervisors  of  the  respondent  vilified  the  union,  made
the  union  leaders  continuously  conscious  of  the  re-
spondents’  active  hostility.

Then  in  July,  1935  scarcely  a  month  after  the  in-
validation  of  NIRA  and  just  shortly  after  the  employee
representation  elections,  the  president  of  the  union,  his

42

half-brother,  and  the  vice  president  were  discharged.
There  followed  a  series  of  discharges,  all  of  men  active
and  vigorous  in  pursuit  of  the  union’s  aims.  Three  of
them  were  officers,  others  had  special  qualities  as
leaders  of  particular  greups—Brandy,  the  Italian;
Boyer,  the  Negro.  In  nearly  every  case  the  pattern  is
The  victim  is  an  old  employee—the  least
the  same.
length  of  service  seems  to  have  been  six  years,  the
.  longest  26  years.  During  his  entire  employment  he  has
served  to  the  apparent  satisfaction  of  the  respondent.
Then  suddenly  with  seeming  caprice  he  is  discharged
for  a  routine  fault  or  omission,  to  which,  normally,
slight  pendlty  or  no  penalty  attaches.  Some  cases  are
Razzano,  for  eight  years  @  top-
clearer  than  others.
notch  tractor  driver,  is  discharged  for  leaving  open  @
Dunn,  for  15  years  a  crane  operator,  is  dis-
door.
But  the  cases
charged  for  leaving  a  key  on  4  bench.
taken  together  reinforce  each  other,  reveal  the  plan,  the
persistent  attack  from  which  each  springs.  We  find
that  the  respondent  has  discriminated  with  respect  to
hire  and  tenure  of  employment  against  the  persons
named  in  the complaint  as  amended,  except  Gulio  Yaco-
bucci  and  Marko  Lukich,  as  to  whom  there  is  no  evi-
dence,  for  the  purpose  of  discouraging  membership  in
the  union,  and  that  by  such  acts,  the  respondent  has
interfered  with,  restrained,  and  coerced  its  employees
in  the  exercise  of  the  rights  guaranteéd  in  Section  7 of
the  Act.

In  the  past,  disputes  relating  to  the  orgapizational  -
activities  of  labor  have  seriously  disrupted  operations
in the steel  industry.  There  was the. great  steel  strike  of
1919  in  which  it  is  said  more  than  360,000  employees
went  out  on  strike  because  the steel  companies  refused
to  meet  any  union  representatives  whatsoever  for  the
Judge  Gary  of  the
p
United  States  Steel  Corporation  said  at  that  time  that  ~
it  is  “the  policy  of  our  corporation  not  to  deal  with

of  collective  bargaining.

43

union  labor  leaders.”  Coming  to  more  recent  times,  in
1934,  9,844  men  suffered  a  loss  of  264,810  man-days  of
work  with  consequent  serious  injury  to  industry  and
commerce.  We  find  that  the  aforesaid  -acts  of  the  re-
spondent  tend  to  labor  disputes  burdening  and  obstruct-
ing commerce  and  the  free  flow  of  commerce.

-  CONCLUSIONS  OF  LAW

Upon  the  basis  of  the  foregoing  findings  of  fact  the

Board  makes  the  following  conclusions  of law:

1.  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200,  Amalgamated
Association  of Iron,  Steel  & Tin  Workers  of North  Amer-
ica,  is  a  labor  organization,  within  the  meaning  of  Sec-
tion  2, subdivision  (5)  of  the  Act.

2  By  its  discharge  of  Domenic  Brandy,  Angelo
Volpe,  Harry  V.  Phillips,  Martin  Dunn,  George  Maroll,
Roval  Boyer,  Martin  Gerstner,  Angelo  Razzano,  Eli
Bozich,  and  Ronald  B,  Cox,  and  each  of  them,  for  the
reason  that  they  and  each  of  them  joined  and  assisted
the  union,  the  respondent  did  interfere  with,  restrain,
and  coerce,  and  is  interfering  with,  restrainng,  and  co-
ercing  its  employees  in  the  exercise  of  the  rights  guar-
anteed  in  Section  7 of  the  Act,  and  by  all  of  said  acts
and  each  of  them  did  thereby  engage  in  and  is  thereby
engaging  in  unfair  labor  practices,  within  the  meaning
of  Section  8,  subdivision  (1)  of  the  Act.

3.  Hy  its  discharges  of  the  persons  aforesaid,  as
set  forth  in  paragraph  2 hereof,  and  each  of  them,  the
respondent  did  discriminate  and  is  discriminating  in
regard  to  the  hire  and  tenure  of  employment  of  said
persons  and  each  of  them,  and  did  thus  discourage  and
is  thus  discouraging  membership  in  the  union,  and  by
_  all  of  said  acts  and  each  of  them  did  thereby  engage  in
and  is  thereby  engaging  in  unfair  labor  practices,  with-
in  the  meaning  of  Section  8,  subdivision  (3)  of  said
Act.

|

)

44

4.  ‘The  unfair  labor  practices  in  which  the  re-
spondent  has  engaged  and  is  engaging  are  unfair  labor
practices  affecting  commerce,  within  the  meaning  of
Section  2, subdivisions  (6)  and  (7)  of  said  Act.

ORDER

On  the  basis  of  the  findings  and  conclusions  of  law,
and  pursuant  to  Section  10,  subdivision  (c)  of  the  Na-
tional  Labor  Relations  Act,  the  National  Labor  Rela-
tions  Board  hereby  ord  rs  that  the  respondent,  Jones
& Laughlin  Steel  Corpor:  tion:

1.  Cease  and  desist  from  in  any  manner  interfer-
ing  with,  restraining  or  coercing  its  employees  in  the
exercise  of  their  rights  to  self-organization,  to  form,
join  or  assist  labor  organizations,  to bargain  collectively
-through  representatives  of  their  own  choosing,  and  to
engage  in  concerted  activities  for  the  purpose  of  collec-  °
tive  bargaining  or  other  mutual  aid  or  protection,  as
guaranteed  in  Section  7 of the  National  Labor  Relations
Act;

;

2.  Cease  and  desist.  from  discouraging  member-
ship  in  the  union  or  any  other  labor  organization  of
its  employees,  by  discrimination  in  regard  to  hire  or
tenure  of  employment  or  any  term  or  condition  of  em-
ployment;

;

3.  Take  the  following  affirmative:  action  which

the  Board  finds  will  effectuate  the  policies  of  the  Act.

(a)  .  Offer  to  Domenic  Brandy,  Angelo  Volpe,  Harry
V.  Phillips,  Martin  Dunn,  George  Maroll,  Royal  Boyer,
Martin  Gerstner,  Angelo  Razzano,  Eli  Bozich,  -  and.
the  respective  positions
Ronald  B.  Cox  employment  jn
all  rights  and  privileges
formerly  held  by  them  with
previously  enjoyed;  and

®

(b)

Make  whole  said  Dominic  Brandy,  Angelo
Volpe,  Harry  V.  Phillips,  Martin  Dunn,  George  Maroll,
Royal  Boyer,  Martin  Gerstner,  Angelo  Razzano,  Eli

45

Bozich,  and  Ronald  B..Cox  for  any  losses  of  pay they
‘have  suffered  by reason  of  their  discharge,  by payment
to  each  of  them,  respectivély,  of  a  sum  equal  to  that
which  each  would  normally  have  earned  as  wages,  at
the  rate  set  out  in  the  appendix,  during  the  period
from  the  date  of  his  discharge  to  the  date  of  offer  of
employment  as  ordered  hereunder,  less  amounts  earned
by  each  during  such  period;

(c)

Post  immediately  notices  to  its  employees  in
conspicuous  places  in  each  shop  and  yard  of  the  Ali-
quippa  Works  stating  (1)  that  the  respondent  will  not
discharge  or  in  any  manner  discriminate  against  mem-
bers  of,  or  those  desiring  to  become  members  of,  Beaver
Valley  Lodge  No.  200,  Amalgamated  Association  of  Iron,  ~
Steel  & Tin  Workers  of  North  America,  or  persons  as-
sisting  said  organization  or  otherwise  engaging  in  union
activity,  and  (2)  that  such  notices  will  be  posted  for  a
period  of  at  least  thirty  (30)  consecutive  days  from  the
date  of  posting.

And  it  is  further  ordered  that the  complaint  be,
and  is  hereby  dismissed,  without  prejudice,  with  re-
spect  to  Gulio  Yacobucci  and  Marko  Lukich.

Signed  at  Washington,  D.  C.  this  9th  day  of  April,

1936.

TO  THE  HONORABLE  THE  JUDGES  OF  THE  UNITED»
STATES  CIRCUIT  COURT  OF  APPEALS  FOR  THE
FIFTH  CIRCUIT:

The  National  Labor  Relations  Board  herewith
amends  its  “Petition  for  the  Enforcement  of  an  Order
of  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board”,  which  said
Petition  was  filed  in  the  United  States  Circuit  Court  of
Appeals  for  the  Fifth  Circuit  on  April  11,  1936,  is  num-
bered  No.  8088  on  the  docket  of  said  Court,  and  is
awaiting  the  deterMfination  of  said  Court,  by  adding
thereto  the  following  paragraph:

7.  Said  order  was  duly  served  upon  respondent
on  April  10,  1936.  At  all  times  from  the  service  of  said
order  upon  respondent  down  to  and  including  the  8th
day  of  May,  1936,  the  respondent  has  wholly  failed  and
refused  to  comply  with  each  and  every  part  of  said
order,  is  in  —  under  the  provisions  of  said  order,

45b

and  upon  ‘information  and  belief,  will  not  in  the  future
comply  with  said  order  unless  ordered  so  to  do  by  this
Honorable  Court.

cerning  the  system,  which  I  should  like  to  state  now

MR.  REED:  The  usual  practice  on  a  preliminary
injunction  is to have  a  preliminary  hearing  on  the  ques-
tion  of  jurisdiction,  because  the  respondent,  if  that  is
decided  against  him,  may  decide  either  not  to  cross
examine  or  not  to  produce  witnesses.  He  has  a  right  to
have  the  ‘issue  raised  and  decided  at  least  for  the
The  Board,  of  course,  may  change  his  mind
later,  but  he  has  a  right  to  have  the  ruling  against
him  on  the  matter  of  whether  or  not  the  Board  has  juris-
diction.
Otherwise  he  is  put  to  an  election  that  is  un-
fair,  and  that  is  the  usual  practice  on  a  special  appear-
ance.

'

DIRECT  EXAMINATION
Q  (By  Mr.  Reed)  What  is  your  full  name?
"A  F.  E.  Jamieson.

Q  What  is  your  occupation,  Mr.  Jamieson?
A  Iam  manager  of  orders,  Jones  & Laughlin  Steel

Corporation.

Q  How  long  have  you  been  employed  by the  Jones

& Laughlin  Steel  Corporation?

A  It will  be  24  years  this  year,  in  August.
Q  Are  you  located  at  Alequippa?  Where  is  your

office?

A  I  am  at  the  general  office.

82

Q  The  general  office?

Are  you  familiar  with  the

Alequippa  plant?

A  Yes,  sir.
Q  What  are  the  principal  products  of  the  Ale-

.

quippa  plant?  What  do  you  make  there?

A  Well,  we  produce  tubular  products,  that  is,  pipe,
tin  plate,  wire,  wire  products,  some  structural  sizes  of
shapes,  bars,  skelp,  sheet  bars,  billets,  and  slabs.

Q  Those  are  all  manufactured  products,  are  they?
MR.  WATTS:  [object  to  that  as  calling  for  a  con-
,

clusion.

CHAIRMAN  MADDEN:  Objection  overruled.
MR.  WATTS:  Exception.
Q  (By  Mr.  Reed)

The  question  was,  are  they  all

manufactured  products?

|

|

A  All  manufactured,  yes,  sir.
Q  Now,  what  materials  do  you  use  in  manufactur-

ing  those  products?

:

A  Well,  we  start  in  with  the  iron  ore  and  coke
and  limestone  which  go  into  the  blast  furnace  in  suc-
cessive  layers  and  are  melted  down  to  produce  what
is  known  as  pig  iron.  That  is  mostly  in  the  case  of
Alequippa  used  in  the  molten  state;  very  little  of  it.
is  actually  pigged,  molded  into  small  pigs,  as  they  call
them.  It  is  used  in  the  molten  shape  to  produce  either
Bessemer  or  what  we  call  open  hearth  steel.
In  the
Bessemer  converters  and  the  open  hearth  furnaces  the
stell  is  poured  into  ingot  molds,  as  we  call  them,  that
stand  upright,  and  the  stell  is  taken  out  of  the  open
hearth or the  Bessemer  to  the  ladles,  and  from  the  ladles
poured  into  the  molds.
It  is  taken  away  then  and
allowed  to  cool  off,  and  after  it  is  cold,  or  after it. is
cool  enough,  the  mold.  is  taken  off  and  the  resulting
ingot  is  then  used  for  the  further  production  of  our
products.

7

MR.  CARMODY:  Doesn’t  it go  into  a  soaking  pit

first?

83

in  the/ground.

WITNESS:  I  was  just  going  to  say  that  the
is  to  put  it  into  the  soaking  pit.  The  soak-
just  another  way  of  heating  the  furnace,  down  -
The  steel  is  brought  up  to  the  right
ture  in  the  soaking  pit  and  taken  to  the  bloom-
At  that  point  it  is  rolled  down  from  this  ingot
into  slabs  which  are  rectangular,  flat  pieces  of
eel,  or  blooms  which  are  square;  and  from  there  in
the  case  of. Alequippa  the  slab  goes  to  the  shear  and  is
shipped  outside  of  the  plant  to  the  South  Side  works.
The  bloom  goes  to  the  village  mill  or  the  bar  mill  where
it is  reduced  to  billets  or  sheet  bars  or  skelp.
Skeip
is used  for  making  pipe,  some  sizes  of  pipe.  The  billets  —
'  are  used  for  rolling  down  into  angles  or  flat  bars  of
skelps  or  wire,  made  into  rod  billets,  which  are  used
for  making  wire.

The  blooms  are,  some  of  them,  rolled  into  rounds
in  our  seamless  tube  department  for  making  seamless
tubing.

Sheet  bars,  so-called,  are  taken  to  the  tin  plate  de-

partment  for  making  tin  plate.

We  ship  some  sheet  bars  outside  the  plant  te  cus-
tomers.  Some  of  the billets are  shipped  outside,  but
very  little  oi  them.

I do  not  know  that  there  is  anything  further  that
I  can  say  about  our  use  of  the  steel  except  if  you
would  ask  -me  questions.

Q  .(By  Mr.  Reed)  Then  I gather  from  your  testi-
mony  that  what  comes  into  your  plant  is  coal  and  ore
and  minerals,  scrap  iron,  and  things  of  that  nature,
and  that  what  goes  out  after  manufacture  are  tubes,
pipes,  sheets,  and finished  products.  Js  that  right?

A  All  we  get  is, we  start  with  the  iron  ore  and  the

coke.

A  Two  stages.

ing,  Mr.  Reed,  to  hear  you  argue  this Question,  but  I

DIRECT  EXAMINATION  (Continued)

Q  (By  Mr.  Watts)  When  you  were  last  testify-
ing,  Mr.  Payne,  you had reached  the  point  in your
testimony  where  you  had  described  the  holding  of  the

135

first  open  air  meeting  at  Ambridge.  Now,  I  want  you
to  go  on  from  there,  and  I want  to  ask  you  if  at  that
time  you  went  to  Aliquippa  and  endeavored  there  to
secure  a  meeting  place,  a  meeting  hall,  so  that  you
would  not  have  to  have  outdoor  meetings  across  the
river  in  Ambridge?

A  Yes,  we  made  every  effort.
Q  What  success  did  you  have?

Tell  the  Board

what  effort  you  made  and  how  it  turned  out.

A  First  of  all,  when  I  got  into  Ambridge  and
located  and  got  acquainted  with  these  men,  I was  told
I would  get  my  head  knocked  off,  if  I was  sent  in  there
to  organize  but  I  have  not  got  my  head  knocked  off
yet.

,

We  held  a  series  of meetings  at  Ambridge  during
the  month  of  September  and  the  first  part  of  October,
and  in  the  meantime,  while  holding~this  series  of  meet-
ings,  on  different  periods,  different.  days  of  the  week,
we  across  to  Aliquippa,  a  delegation  and  myself,  usual-
ly  two  or  three  or  four  went  over.
For  what  purpose?
What  is  that?
For  what  purpose  did  you  go  there?
We  went  over  there  to  make  contact  with  the
men  and  also  to-try  to  secure  a  hall  whereby  we  could
hold  our  meetings,  hold  them  amongst  the  men  in  Ali-
quippa,  so  that  they  would  not  have  to  come  across  to
Ambridge.  all  the  time,  and  this  time  these  men  were
under  sort  of  a  shadow—like  a  shadow  of  a  club,  I
would  say,  and  they  were  justfull  of  fear.  To  me  they
were  terrorized.
I  never  experienced  anything  like  it
in  my  life.

Q  What  men  do  you  mean?
A  The  men  employed  by  Jones  &  Laughlin  Ali-
quippa  plant,  and  in  going  into  Aliquippa,  we  had  to
go  in  there  kind  of—it  was  kind  of  guerilla  warfare.
We  had  to  rush  in  and  rush  out,  owing  to  the  fact  that

136

as  soon  as  we  would  show  up  in  the  town  of  Aliquippa
we  would  be  more  or  less  followed  around  by  one  or
two  or  three  of  the  J &  L  police.

Q  (By  Chairman  Madden)  Afoot  or  otherwise?
A  Mostly  in ‘machines,  and  if  you  stood  around
awhile,  you  would  probably  see  one  or  two  or  more
slinking  spies,  or  they  probably  would  come  close  to
you  and  stand  around,  to  see  just  what  would  occur,
maybe  make  me  feel  as  if  }  was  amongst  a  gang  of
racketeers.

Q  (By  Mr. Watts)

Tell,us  what  success  you  had
when  you  went  to  the  owners  of  any  halls  at  Aliquippa
and  asked  them  to  rent  them?

;

A  We  went  to  West  Aliquippa  one  afternoon,  a
delegation  and  myself.  We  went  around  to  the  Italian
hall  theré  to  request  the  renting  of  this  hall  to  hold
a  meeting  and  to  have  Mrs.  Pinchot  speak.
Well,  the
janitor  met  us  and  he  was  fair  and  candid  with  us
and  told  us  point  blank  that  it’  was  useless  to  talk
to  him  about  holding  a  meeting  there  in  their  hall  for
the  purpose  of  a  Union.  We  tried  to—we  asked  him  on
whose  authority  he  was  doing  this—the  head  of  the  or-
ganization,  they  had  been  instructed  not  to  let  out
their  hall  at  any  time  to  the  Union  organization.

Q  Did  you  go  to  other  — to nt  get  a  hall

for  meeting  purposes?

A  From  there  we  went  across  to  the  Slovak  Hall,
with  the  same  success.  We  also  tried  to  get  the  Ser-
bian  Hall,  with  no  success,  because,  in  fact,  one  of  the
head  men  of  the  Serbian  Hall  was  one  of  the  men  of
what  I  called  the  type  of  stool  pigeon  for  the  com-
pany.

Q  Did  you  try  to  get any  open  lots?
A  We  did  one  afternoon—at  least  one  morning,

we  went  across  to  Aliquippa  and  we  tried  to  get  two  |
lots,  with  no  avail.  We  failed  to  get  those.

137

Q  So  that  you  could  not  rent  either  a  hall  or  a
lot  to  hold  a  public  meeting  in  Aliquippa;  is  that  —

A  Absolutely  not.
Q  Now,  on  the  2th  of  September,  1934,  did  you
have  a  certain  experience  with  the  police  force  of  Ali-
quippa?

_  A  Yes;  we  did.

Q  Will  you  state  that  for  the  Board,  please,  in

your  own  way?

A  It  was  our  purpose  this  day  to  go  across  to
Aliquippa  and  meet  some  of  the  boys,  that  is,  Harry
Phillips  and  Angelo  Volp  and  Dominic  Brandy  and
several  of  the  other  boys  and  Mr.  Cox,  and  we  met  at
Harry  Phillips’  home.  We  had  been  around  different
places  prior  to  that,  though,  but  there  we  ended  our
visit,  at  Harry  Phillips’  home,  and  it  came  down  from
there.

it  was  Hollywood,  up  on  the  left  side,  and  came  into
Aliquippa.  We  were  coming  down  the  street,  and  Mr.
Toogood  and  Mr.  Clinton  Stevens,  another  organizer
'  of  the  bridge  works,  structural  iron  works—we  were
coming  down,  passing  the  school  house  about  noon,  I
take  it—yes—we  were  passing  the  school  house,  and  -
coming  on  down  to  Franklin  Avenue.  There  is  a  police
officer  on  duty  there,  Police  Officer  Shea.

A  Well,  traffic  duty.

constable.  Where  I  come  from,  that  is  what  they  call
them.

I  might  state  that  prior  to  this  the
men  that  were  instrumental,  to  my  way  of  thinking,
of  organizing  Aliquippa,  were  such  as  Harry  Phillips,
Angelo  Volte,  Domonic  Brandy,  a  man  by  the  name
of  Gum,  Martin  Gerstner,  Lang,  a  man  by  the  name
of Dunn,  Fred  Dunn.  These  men  were  the  only  ones
—well,  there  were  several  others,  but  I  couldn’t  men-
tion  them  right  now,  but  these  men  were  the  only  ones
that  had  no  fear  for  the  J &  L  force  at  any  time,  and

141

they  were  really  instrumental  in  organizing  Aliquippa.
They  were  very,  very  active,  and  they  came  over  to
Ambridge  at  all  times,  using  a  machine  to  bring  men
at  the  time  they  came  over  to  Ambridge.
The  men
were  in  fear  at  all  times,  so  much  so  that,  in  fact,  when
these  men  were  at  our  headquarters,  at  the  Ambridge
Hotel,  they  were  in  such  a state  of  fear  that  they  were
shaking,  and  they  didn’t  want  anyone  to  see  them  en-
ter  or  leave  the  hotel.
That  is  some  of  the  conditions
that  we  found.”

Q  What  had  happened  in  Ambridge  Saturday?
~
A  Harris,  Donnelly  and  all  the  Jones &  Laughlin
police  force  chaved  us  all  over  Ambridge,  me  and  Mar-
tin  Gerstener,  to  see  where  we  were  going.

to  send  for  our  charter.

Q  You  were  offered  a  job  on  the  police  force  the
Did  you  —  the

job  or  not?

Q  During  this  same  period  of  time  and  after
had  that  job  offered  to you,  which  you  refused,  did
suffer  any  personal  violence  in  Alequippa?
A  I did»  It  was the latter  part  of August,  I just
not—I  have  not  got  the  date  down—I  was  ordered
o  work  at  9:30  in the evening  and  I  was  delivering
“some  union  papers  around  the  town,  and  the  chief  of
police,  Ambrose  Donnelly  of  the  Jones  &  Laughlin
police,  and  another  man  from  the-Jones  &  Laughlin
police  was  in a  car  up  in  front  of  my  house.  They
asked  me what  I  was  doing  and  I said  I was  delivering
these  Amalgamated  Journals.  And  I said,  “You  fellows
better  take  one.  You  are  liable  to  get  educated.”

,

|

They  said,  “Just  a  minute;  we  want  to  talk  to  you.”
I said, “I can not stand  here  and  talk to you  because
I have  to  be  at  work  at  9:30.  I have  to  change  clothes
and  get  down  there.”

On  my  way  going  down  to  work  I would  see this
car,  and the first  time  it  came  up  and circled  around
me  and  the  chief  of\police  and  Donnelly  and  this  other
man,  Slater,  was  in it.  The  next  time  I  got  a  little
further  down  the  street  and  the  chief  of  police  was
not  there  but  Donnelly  and  Slater  was.

When  I got  about  a  block  away  from  the  “Y”,  right
in  front  of  the  Strand  Theatre  that  was  the  last  time
that  they  passed  me  and  they  went  back  down  towards
the  “Y”.

I starfed  to go in the mill  and  I saw  a  couple  of
men  I wanted  to  catch  up  with  and  I started  hurrying
up to get up with  them,  and  just  going  in the tunnel
I  noticed  that  there  was  a  light  out.  I did not  notice
that at first but I noticed it later,  a street  light  was out.
It  was kind  of dark  there  and there  was a  man standing
alongside  of  some  parked  automobiles  and  a  man
standing  about  ten feet  further  up the sidewalk.

As I  was going  by he said,  “Harry  Phillips,  just  a

SESS ae  Ne

minute;  I  want  to  see  you.”  And  as  I  turned  to  see
who  it  was  I  got  a  righthander  in  the  jaw,  and  the
minute  I, got  that  another  fellow  came  up  and  hit  me
over  the  head  with  a  blackjack.
I  was  fortunate  to
get  the  blow  on  the  jaw  because  it forced  my  head  back.
because  I  had  a  soft  hat  on  and  it  stopped  the  other
blow  which  was going  for  the  back  of  my  ear.

Then  they  started  pushing  me  in  between  some
parked  automobiles.  I  do  not  know  how I did  it,  but
I got  hold  of  both  of  them.mround  the  neck  and  I got
them  both  down  on  the  ground.  I hollered  for  help,  I
was  pretty  dizzy,  I  could  not  get  my  bearings.  There
was  two  fellows  coming  along,  going  to  work,  and
they  pulled  one  fellow  away  from  me  and  the  other
fellow  scrambied  around  and  got  out  and  went  up  over
The  man  they  pdlled  away  from
the  railroad  tracks.
me  had  a  screw  driver  and  a  blackjack,  and  as  I  was

and  missed.

road  station.  As I looked  up  I saw  four  men.  I could

ing for  an  officer.  At  all  times  there  is  an  officer  in  the

lin  stool  pigeons  meeting  trains,  automobiles,  and  every-
thing.
They  were  right  in  that  vicinity  but  I  could

I went  to  the  police  station  and  I went  in  and  told
the sergeant  on  the desk that  I  was waylaid  down  by the
“Y".

Q  (By  Mr.  Watts)

Tell  the  Board  the  circum-
stances  of  your  discharge,  what  was  said  to  you  and
what  was  the  supposed  cause  of  your  discharge.
A  I was  working  from  12:00  to  8:00,  it  was  my
first  turn  at  12:00  to  8:00  of  that  week.
It  started  on
a  Saturday  morning  and  I  came  to  work.  First,  I
had  a  breakdown  on  No.  3 crane.  I fixed  that.  Then
on  No.  1  the  dope  box  on  the  drive  shaft  fell  off,  or
busted  off,  and  I  had  that  fixed  temporarily  to  allow
him  to  go  out  and  charge  the  heat.
I had  some  trouble
with  No.  3’s  merry-go-round,  and I fixed  that.
All  during  the  night  I  was  pretty  busy  up  until
about,  I  would  say,  3:30,  and  I  got  caught  up  and  I
went  over  to  the  restaurant  to  eat.
It  was  a  very  hot  night,  and  after  I left  the  restau-
rant  I went  over  to  the  wash  house,  I was  pretty  well
chafed  and  I  decided  I  would  wash  myself  and  put
some  vaseline  I had  on  where  I was  chafed  from  the
heat.  At  that  time  I heard  the  whistle  blow  and  I saw
my  millwright  going  to  answer  the  whistle  so  I finished

174

up  what  I was-doing  and  I went  over  and  answered  the
whistle.  When  I got  there  the  man  on  the  pump  told
So,  I  talked  with  the
me  everything  was  all  right.
man  on  No.  2 for  a  while,  we  lit  a  cigarette,  and  then
I said,  “I  have  to  checks  bolts,  we  are  all  out  of  bolts.”  —

I no  sooner  got  in  the  shanty  to  check  bolts  when
I went  out  and  the  foreman  on
the  whistle  blew  again.
the  day  turn  was  out  at  4:00  o’clock  in  the  morning.
He  said,  “Why  don’t  you  answer  that  whistle?”

I said  first,  “I  didn’t  hear  the  first  whistle,  but  the
second  time  he  blew  I heard  it  and  I  was  over  in  the
wash  house  and  could  not  get  over  right  away.”

He  said,  “You  fellows  coming  out  at  night  always
want  the  millwrights  to  do  all  the  work  around  here.”
He  said,  “You  can  get  to  hell  out  of  here.”

I said,  “What  does  this  mean?  Am I fired?”
He  says,  “Yes,  it  means  you  are  to  get  to  hell  out

of  here.”

I  says,  “O.K.”
He  says—then  he  started  talking  to  me  for a  little

while  and  then  I  said,  “Wil!  I  take  my  tools?’

He  said,  “Get  the  hell  out  of  here.

You  are  going

to  have  plenty  of  time  to  sell  papers  from  now  on.”

Q  At  the  time  of  your  discharge  what  was  your

rate  of  pay?

A  50-1/4  cents  and  tonnage.
Q  What  Were  you  averaging  each  week?
A  We  never  got  paid  weekly.  We  got  paid  semi-

monthly  and  it  was  between  $50  and  $60.

Q  Have  you  earned  any  money  since  the  time  of

‘your  discharge?

A  I worked  for  the  State  of  Pennsylvania  on  the
highways  for  $16  a  week  for  four  weeks,  and  I  got
about  $300  from  W.P.A.  work.

Q  Now,  since  the  time  of  your  discharge  have  you

been‘  dispossessed  from  your  home?

A_  I have  been.

three  months  and  every  time  I  worked  for  Bill  Sam-
pule,  I tell  you,  I would  do  twice  as  much  work  as  |

wass  supposed  to.

Q.  That  is  the  difference  between  the  limit  stops

and  the  inspection?

A  No;  the  time  that  this  subject  came  up  about

testing  the  limit  stops  on  the  hooks.

Q  Just  a  minute,  so  that  the  Board  will  under-
stand  that.  Will  you  explain  briefly  to  them  what  you
-  mean  by what  the  difference  is  between  trying  a  crane

and  inspecting  a  crane?

A  Well,  the  limit  stop  is  a  mechanical  switch  that
is  operated  by a  weight,  and  when  your  hooks  come  up
and  hit-this  weight  and  release  the  tension,  the  switch
automatically  shuts  off,  so  thai  the  hoist  cannot  go  any
higher  and  break  the  cables.  The  result  would  be  that
the  hooks  would  drop  down  on  the  floor  and  probably
This  limit  stop
injure  somebody  or  do  some  damage.
is  actuated. by hand,  so  that  you  could  look  at  it  and
see  that  it  was  making  the  contact,  as  I  always  had.
done;  but  previous  to  this  the  foreman  had  suggested
that  we  hit  them  with  hooks  to  see  if  they  worked.  I
said  at  the  time  that  I thought  that  was  an  unsafe  way
of  doing  it,  because  if  the  controller  would  stick  while
you  were  trying  it  in  that  fashion,  you  would  get  into

205

trouble  and  have  an  accident.

said,  and  I never  had,  while  I  worked  there,  tried  the
limit  stops  in  that  fashion,

.

mill.  Then  I only  worked  about  three  months  in  the

;

Vesta  Coal  Company,  in  its  labor  relations?  .

“A  Yes;  United  Mine  Workers,  District  No.  5, sub-
district  No.  4 have  a  contract  with  the  Vesta  Coal  Com-
pany  at  their  mines  4,  5  and  6,  and  employee  in  the
neighborhood  of 2250  miners.  —
Q  When  was  this  contractual  relationship  most

recently  entered  into?

ae

A  Well,  for  years  we  had  a  contract  with  the

237

Vesta  Coal  Company,  the  officers  and  members  of  the
United  Mine  Workers,  District  No.  5,  until  1927,  when
we  had  a.strike  in  here  that  lasted  from  April  1, 1927,
until  about  October  1,  1928.  From  that  time  on  until
July,  1933,  we  had  no  contractual  relations  with  them.
In  the  months  of June,-July  and  August,  we  reorganized
those  mines;  in  fact,  we  rehabilitated  the  United  Mine
Workers,  and  those  men  joined  our  Union  of  their  own
free  will,  and  after  the  commercial  agreement  was
made,  we  then  immediately  negotiated  contracts  with
all  the  captive  mine  operators  in  western  Pennsylvania,
that is, all  the  steel  company  mimes  and  public  utilities.
Q  Prior  to  1927,  at  which  time  there  was  a  con-
-tract  between  the  employees,  through  their  Union,  and
the  Vesta  Coal  Company,  how  many  years  was  that  con-
tract  in  existence,  if you  know?  That  is,  prior  to  1927?  °

A  Well,  Vesta  No.1  mine  probably was  in  opera-
tion  back  in,  say,  1901,  it  opened  up  and  as  soon  as
that  mine  was  opened  up  and  there  were  ten  or  more
men  there,  the  United  Mine  Workers  organized  it and
‘had  a  contract  with  them.

Q  After  this  in  1927,  I  think  you  said,  it  ended

in  1928?

|

-  A  October,  1928.

Q  Between  the  time  of  the  resumption  of  opera-
tions  in  these  coal  mines  in  1928  and  entering  into  this
contract  in  1933,—I  think  you  said  it  was  1933.

-

A  1933.
Q  What,  if  anything,  was  the  attitude  of  these
men;  what  was  the  relationship  of  the  miners  and  the
Vesta  Coal  Company  during  that  period  of  time?  Was

.  it a  productive,  harmonious  period?

A  Well,  the  men  were  unorganized;;  they  were
working  as  individuals,  instead  of  collectively,  with  the
company,  and  if  the  management  didn’t  like  the  color
of  the  men’s  hair  or  their  eyes  or  their  nationality  or

238

even  the  color  of  their  necktie,  they  could  fire  them  and
evict  them  out  of  their  homes...  There  was  more  or  less
confusion  and  misunde  \tanding  and  contention  all  dur-
ing that  period.

Q  Would  you  say  that  thi  hindered  production?
A  Absolutely.
Q  To  the  point  where  the  men  were  not  working
full  capacity,  that  is  personal  capacity,  because  of  that
contention ?

A  Oh,  yes,  due  to  the  fact  that  those  men  had
been  United  Mine  Workers  and  enjoyed  the  benefit  of
collective  bargaining  and  the  relationship  and  under-
standing  that  had  existed  there  for  years.

Q  Then,  as  a  result  of  the  rehabilitation  of  the

organization,  the  ultimate  end  was  a  contract?

A  Yes.
Q  That  contract is between  the  Vesta  Coal  Com-

pany  and  the  United  Mine  Workers?
oe

A  Yes.
Q  And  it  provides  the  regular  systematic  set-up  of

:

bargaining?

A  It  provides  for  collective  bargaining  and  for
regulating  hours,  wages  and  working  conditions  for  the
workers  in  the  yard  and  all  those  that  enter  into  mining.
There  is  machinery  set  up  under  the  contract,  where,  if
a  man  has  a  grievance,  whether  it  is  a  real  grievance
-  or  an  imaginary  grievance,  if  he  can’t  have  proper  ad-
justment  made  with  the  mine  management,  he  calls  in
the  mine  committee.
That  is  part  of  the  local  union.
The-local  union  is  composed  of  all  men  employed  in
and  around  the  mine.
They  have  a  president,  vice-
president  and  secretary  and  treasurer,  and  it  is  this
mine  committee  that  takes  up  grievances.
If  the  mine
committee  is  unable  to  reach  a  satisfactory  settlement
in  the  grievance  or  dispute,  then  the  district  drganizer .
is  called  in,  or  one  of  the  representatives,  to  take  the

239

grievance  up  with  the  management.  If  they  are  unable
to  reach  a  satisfactory  settlement  in  line  With  the  agree-
ment,  then  there  is  a  board  of  two  operatof$  and  two
miners  that  sit  to  listen  to  the  grievance  and  render  a
decision.

Q  Has  the  Vesta  Coal  Company,  through  the  of-
ficers,  expressed  to  you  satisfaction  with  this  set-up  and
the  result  it  has  brought  about?

A  Well,  we  get  along  very,  very  harmoniously
and  have  fine  relations  and  a  good  understanding  as  a
result  of  collective  bargaining  and  collective  reasoning
It  is  more-  satisfactory  for  every-
with  the  company.
body  concerned.’

Q  There  exists  now  and  has  existed  since  1933  a
present  cooperation  between  the  men  and  their  bosses?

A  Absolutely.

of production  and  morale  jn  the  ranks  of  the  employees
oduction  has  bettered,
of  the  Vesta  Coal  Compan
‘and  more  harmoni-
and  the  men  are  working  better
ously?

;

A  Yes.

I  would  say  from  the

-

duction  and  cooperation  and  safety  afd  everything  else
that  enters  inte  the  miming  of  coal  and  production,  —
there  is  a  much  better  feeling  prevailing  between  both

what  the  cost  of  production  is;  they  know  what  their

a  ton  of  coal  or  doing  an  hour’s  work  or  doing  seme
dead  work  in  the  yard—anything  that  enters  into  min-
,
ing.

Q  So  you  would  say,  from  your  experience  with

240

‘the  Vesta  Coal  Company  during  the  agreement  period
of  the  strike  period,  where  the  strike  was  in  effect,  that
the  harmonious  period  during  the  time  of  this  agree-
ment  was  brought  about  by  the  company’s  recognition
of  the  Union,  recognition  of  the  right  of  those  men  to
organize  as  they  pleased  and  the  recognition  of  those
men  to  bargain  collectively  with  their  employers  for
rates  of  pay  and  hours  and  conditons  of  employment?

.

:

A  That  is  absolutely  true.
Q  When  does  the  present  contract  between  the
United  Mine  Workers  and  the  Vesta  Coal  Company  ter-
minate?

A  It  runs  from  October  1, 1935,  until  April  1, 1937.
Was  that  contract,
Q  (By  Chairman  Madden)
when  it  was  made  in  October  to  be  retroactive  to  last
April?

A  No;  it  expired  last  April,  and  as  a  result  of
waiting  on  the  passage  of  the  Guffey-Snyder  legislation
and  one  thing  andanother,  we  had  extensions,  and  the
officers  of  the  United  Mine  Workers-of  America  would
either  send  a  telegram  or  a  message  to  the  local  union
and  tell  them  that  they  had  agreed  to  an  extension.

Q  So  you  are  working  under  that  contract  until

October?

A  Until  a  new  contract  was  to  be  put  into  effect.
Do  you  recall  whether,  at  the
Q  (By  Mr.  Kleeb)
time  of  the  strike,  those  men  produced  any  coal  for
Do  you  re-
the  Jones  &  Laughlin  Steel  Corporation?
call  that?

A  During  the  strike?
Q  During  the  strike  of  1927.
A  Well,  yes;  the  company  tried  to  work  their  men
on  a  non-union  basis,  and  had  coal  and  iron  police-
men—

,

Q  Strife  and  fighting?

2A! .

A.

Strife.

and  one  of  our  national  organizers,  during  the  zero
period,  asked  Mr.  Edwards  not  to  throw  the  miners  out
of  their  homes,  that  they  would  freeze  and  he  said  “To
Hell with  the  miners  and  their  families  and  everything
else”.

Q  Would  you  say  that  that  strike  affected  the
production  of  the  Jones  &  Laughlin  Steel  Corporation?
A  There  is  not  any  question  about  it,  and  their

MR.  KLEEB:  That  is  ail.  Are  there  any  further

'

Q  (By  Chairman  Madden)

What  is  the  situation
at  the  Vesta  mines  now  as
the  independence  and
self-respect  of  the  men?  Is  it  noticeably  different  from:
what  it was  before  they  got  the contract  or  knew  that
they  were  fully  organized?

A  There  is  a  greater  feeling  of  security  among
the  men  that  are  employed  at  the  Vesta  mines,  and,  at
the  sanre  time,  they  know  that  when  they  got  this  con-
tract  with  the  company  they  had  not  only  won  their
economic  and  industrial  freedom,  but  they  won  their
political  freedom.
Prior  to  the  coming  of this  contract,
when  election  day  came  around,  whether  it  was  for  a
constable  or  a  judge  in  Washington  County  or  a  County
Commissioner  or  the  Governor  of  the  Commonwealth
‘of  Pennsylvania  they  had  to  vote  the  way  the  bosses
told  them.
The  bosses  told  them  how  to  go  out  and
mark  their  ballot;  if  they  didn’t,  they  would  get  fired.
It  means  industrial,  economic  and  political  and  social  .
freedom  for  the  men  that  are  employed  at  those  mines
—the  restoration  of  civil  liberty  and  political  rights.

Q  (By  Mr.  Carmody)  Mr.  Fagan,  do  you  know

intendents  and  approximately  the  same  foremen,  havé

been  at the  Vesta  Coal  Company  over  these  years  when
you  were  dealing  with  them?

management  the  same  when  you  had  no  contract  as  it  is
when  you  have  a  contract?

A  In  some  respects.

Joe  Edwards  was  the  general
superintendent  during  the  early  part  of  the  strike  and,
of  course,  he-is  retired  now  because  of -his  age,  but  a
lot  of  the  mine  foremen  and  superintendents  at  the
The  ones  that  were  there  prior
mines  are  still  there.
to  1927,  during  the  strike  period,  are  there  today,  and
those  men  will  not  say  publicly,  but  they  will  admit
privately,  that  they  are  much  happier  today  than  they
were  when  working  on  a  non-uiion  basis.

Q  Has  it.been  your  experience,  both  with  the
Vesta  Coal  Company  and  with  other  coal  companies
in  the  5th  District,  with  whom  you  have  dealt  over  a
long  period  of  years,  that  the  foremen  and  superintend-
ents  always  carry  out  the  policy  of  the  management
in  labor  relations?

A  That  is  true,  absolutely..  They  are  governed
by  the  policy  laid  down  by  the  president  of  the  com-
pany  or  the  board  of  directors.

Q  If  the  company  makes  an  agreement  with  a
trade  union  that  calls  for  certain  rights  on  both  sides,
is  it  likely  that  a  foreman  or  superintendent  will  carry
on  anti-union  propaganda  while  that  strike  is  in  exist-
ence?

A  Now  and  then  you  will  find  a  boss  that  is  prob-
ably,  for  selfish  motives,  mainly,  trying  to  get  ahead
in  the  company,  or  something  like  that,  or  get  the  super-
intendent’s  position,  and  he  may  still  continue  to  carry
on  a  campaign  to  keep  what  is  known  as  the  brother-
hood  or  independent  union,  or  spread  propaganda  to
injure  the  United-Mine  Workers  of  America  or  any
You  will  find
organization  that  may  have  a  contract.
that  occasionally.

:

243

Q  Well,  if  that  happened,  would  your  organiza-
tion  be  likely  to  take  that  matter  up  with  the  higher
management  and  complain  about  it?

.  A  We  would  lay  that  on  the  desk  of  the  manage-
ment  of  the  company  and  would  make  a  complaint,  as
to  whether  that  was  the  way  they  were  going  to  live
up  to  this  contract,  to  be  back-biting  and  carrying  on  in
that  manner,  and  we  would  make  it  pretty  warm  for
them,  and,  as  a  rule,  we  do.

Q  Now,  generally  speaking,  do  you  find  that  no
matter  what  your  past  relationships  have  been  with  a
company,  whether  you  had  a  contract  that  had  gone
over  a  period  of  three  or  four  years,  and  when  a  com-
pany  has  operated  open  shop,  as  has  happened  in  the
mines  of  the  Pittsburgh  District  on  one  or  two  occa-
sions,  generally  speaking,  do  you  find,  in  spite  of  those
difficulties,  that  when  you  do  enter  into  a  contract,
the  relationships  of  the  men  of  the  organization  with
the  company  are  fair  and  reasonable  and  peaceable,
after  everything  has  been  sweep  away  and  a  contract
has  been  8igned?

_

A  It  has  been  our  policy,  when  we  made  a  con-
tract  with  the  coal  operator,  whether  it  is  in  a  commer-
cial  or  captive  mine,  we  say  “Now,  we  have  had  our
differences;  we  fought  one  another;  but  we  have  sat
down  around  this  table  to  agree  to  make  a  contract;
we  are  making  this  contract  in  a  spirit  of  cooperation,  —
and  whatever  is  in  there  for  the  protection  of  the
miners,  we  expect  them  to  get  it,  both  in  letter  and
spirit,  and  whatever  is  in  there  to  protect  the  company,
they  will  be  treated  likewise.”  As  a-rule,  we  have  with
practically  all  of  the  coal  operators  that  we  have  here
in  western  Pennsylvania  a  very  fine  relation.
spect  the  agreement;  we  respect  the  epinions  of  the  men
that  we  deal  with,  and,  as  a  rule,  we  have  a  cooperation
of  practically  100  per  cent,  and  we  have  in  western
Pennsylvania  40,000  organized  mine  workers.
