Q.  Now,  explain  briefly  what  your  job  is;  just

what  do  you  do  as  a  coal  washer  or  jig man?

A..  My job  was  to  separate  the  coal  from  the  slate.
The  machinery  that  is  working  in  there,  the  kind  they

part  that  is  attached  is  generally  not  given  to  the  man?

’.  A  This  came  this  way,  when  I came  to  work  it  was
half  past  eleven  in  the  night;  I  come  to  work;  I  work
always  at  night  from  12:00  to  8:30  in  the  morning;  I
come  to  work  and  lay my  dinner  bucket  on  the  bench—
Q  Without  going  into  a  discussion  now  of what
happened,  when  they  gave  you  this  they  forgot  to  tear
off  the  second  sheet?

A  Yes.  Then  he  told  me,  he  said,  “George,  you
done  with  the work.”  He  took’ me in  the  office  and  I
says,  “Jim,  are  you  going  to  fire  me?”

He  says,  “Yes,  sir.”

this  and  what  for  and  this  and  this,  and  he  says,  “Well,

and  when  he  wrote  them  slips  the  top  he  wrote  with  a
pencil  and  another  one  with  ink  comes  down,  both
together,  and  the  bottom  slip  is  supposed  to  go  to  the
main  office  to  the  chief  of  timekeepers  in  the  general
office,  what  they  call.

ALBERT  ATALLAH,  called  as  a  witness  for  the
National  Labor  Relations  Board,  being  first  duly  sworn,

CHAIRMAN  MADDEN:  Yes,  go  ahead.
THE  WITNESS:  That  will  save  time.
August  22,  1933,  I  was  sent  to  Aliquippa  by  the
Amalgamated  Union.  We  find  the  place  but  we  could
hardly  get  there  because  every  place  was  pretty  near
closed  to  any  organizer  or  to  any  stranger  in  the  town
of  Aliquippa.

On  the  23rd  I looked  around  the  town  and  talked.

with  some  ‘people,  with  some  working  men.

355

On  the  24th  of  August  we  took  a  bundle  of  pam-
phlets  and  news  from  the  Américan  Federation  of  La-
bor  and  went  to  the  railroad  station  corner  and  the
railroad  bridge  with  our  organizer.  We  was  approached
there  by  different  fellows  who  left,the  plant  at  that
time  and  gave  them  the  pamphlets.

‘.

The  Chief  of  Police  of  Aliquippa  came  to  me  and
told  me  to  move  up  the  sidewalk,  which  I did,  and  he
stated,  “Why  don’t  they  send  a  young  man  to  do  this
distributing  in  here?”

I  asked  him  why,  gad  he  said,  “Well,  you  are  not
fast  enough  to  run  because  you  are  too  old”,  and  he  left
:
:
laughing.
Several  men  from  Jones  &  Laughlin  Corporation
approached  me  and  asked  me  about  the  white  collar
men,  what  we  are  going  to  do,  did  we  intend  to  hold
‘any  meeting  in  Aliquippa.

I said,  “Yes.”

One  particularly  said,  “Well,  how  are  you  going  to
hold  any  meeting  here  in  this  town?
This  town  fs

owned  by  the  Jones  &  Laughlin  Corporation.
don’t  want  any  union.  They  have  their  own  company

I noticed  right  away  that  he  was  some  official  man

have  been  satisfied  with  the  company  union  and  they

On  fhe  same  day,  on  the  24th  of  August,  about  9:00
o'clock  I left  my  boarding  house  to  meet  some  working
men  down  town  to  talk  something  over  about  the  union.
After  a  couple  of  hours  I left  for  home  at  five  minutes
to  eleven.
Walking  on  the  sidewalks,  on  Franklin
Street,  if  I remember  right,  there  was  two  or  three  men
standing  in  a  bunch  and  when  I approached  them  they
was  talking  amongst  themselves.  As  I approached  them
they  asked  me,  “Hey,  give  me  a  cigarette.”

396

I said,  “I  don’t  smoke.”

I  started  to  go  faster  and
they  stepped  behind  me  and  followed  me  fast  too.
When  I  passed  my  lodging  house,  just  about  the  next
house,  then  I turned  back  and  as  soon  as  I turned  back
they  said,  “Here  is  the  guy  who  tried  to  organize  a
union  in  Aliquippa.”  They  hit  me  on  the  head  and  I
jumped  over  a  natural  fence,  they  followed  me  to  the

-  porch,  and  beat  me  upon  the  porch.

I  cried  for  help,  I  ran  back  of  a  screen  door,  and
the  other  door  was  locked.
I  tried  to  hide  myself,  and
they  grabbed  me  and  pulled  me  out  on  the  porch  back
again.

SXAMINATION  BY  MR.  CARMODY

Q  (By  Mr.  Carmody)  How  old  were  you  then,  at

the  time  of  that  attack?

A  52
Q  Were  they  younger  men.  than  you?
A  Oh,  yes,  about  23,  or  24  or  2).
Q  ‘How  many  of  them?
A  Two.
Q  All  right.
A  There  was  more  men  standing  on  the  sidewalk
and  one  came  with  the  policeman  and  they  say,  “Stop;
that  is  enough.”

They  took  me  in  a  car  and  they  took  me  to  the
police  station.
They  searched  me  there  of  all  papers
and  what  little  change  I  have  in  my  pocket  and  when
I asked  for  an  explanation  the  same  man  who  beat  me
up  and  some  other  fellows  there,  they  asked  me,  “How
do  you  like  Aliquippa  now?  How  do  you  like  to  or-
ganize  Aliquippa  men  in  a  union  here?”
And _ they
laughed.

They  put  me  in  the  jail  and  I  was  there  about
three-quarters  of  an  hour,  and  after  that  they  took  me
out  and  asked  $5  deposit,  the  policeman,  I do  not  know
who  he  was.  I  said,  “I-  don’t  have  any  money,”  and
they  gave  me  the  papers  and  the  Chief  said,  “Go  home.”

357

I refused  to  go  because  I said  if  I go  out  maybe  I
He  sent  one  officer  with  me  who  advised
be  killed.
me  to  leave  the  town,  when  he  took  me  down  fo  the
lodging  house.
_  That  was  Thursday,  or  something  like  that,  and  on
a  Monday  morning  we  had  a  hearing  before  Justice  of
I  had  an  attorney  but  there  was  no
the  Peace  Kane.

hearing,  we  did  not  have  no  hearing  over  there.

,

I said,  “I  don’t  have  any.”

I said,  “Do  as  you  please.”  Then  my  friend  Dob-
bins  asked  the  Justice  of  the  Peace  to  give  me  time  to
bring  the  money  and  we  being  the  money  the  next
morning  to  the  Squire’s  office.
There  was  about  ten  plain  men  in  an  alley  and  one
After  I
bumped  into  me  but  I  did  not  say  anything.
paid  the  $5  I asked  for  a  transcript.  He  said  he  could

He
let  me  go  free  he  would  lose  his  job  as  a  squire.
said,  “Go  to  court  in  Beaver  Falls,  see  the  district  at-

right,  to  get  the  transcript  from  the  Squire  of  Aliquip-

Then  we  went  back  to  Aliquippa  and  we  was  fol-
lowed  by  I don’t  know  how  many  men.  We  slept  there

358

overnight  because  we  could  not  go  that  day  home.
There  was  about  three  cars  parked  alongside  the  curb
and  I know  they  was  company  cars  because  I  sent  the
numbers  in  to  Harrisburg  and  we  got  the  names.

in  organizing,

everything  you  feared  for  your  life?

steel  for  the  purpose  of  fabricating  and  reshipping  to

tariff  is  a compilation  of  practically  all  of  the  shipments
that  are  entitled  to  the  privilege  in  transit?

A  That  is  covering  the  Pennsylvania  Lines.  There
may  be  other  privileges  not  mentioned  in  that  tariff,
published  by  other  carriers,  but  the  Pennsylvania  Rail-
road  tariff  covers  practically  them  all.

-bolting,  boring,  burning,  countersinking,  cutting,  drill-
punching,
ing,  flanging,
reaming,  riveting,  sawing,  shearing,  straightening,  tap-

painting,

gagging,

planing,

mills :

Angles,  bars,  beams,  bolts,  castings,  channels,  col-
umns,  ells  girders,  masts,  nuts,  plates,  rivets,  rods,  tees,

Now,  the  castings  are  restricted,  and  the  privilege.

rial  consisting  of  the  numerous  articles

other  articles.

lege.

are  not  rolled.  They  are  cast.

Q  Yes;  1 understand  they  are  cast.  Do  you  under-

stand  why  that  rule  happened  to  be  made  that  way?

A  In  the  erection  of  bridges,  they  use  a  base  plate
for  the  girders  and  piers  that  are  cast  and  rough  ma-
chined.  That  is  about  the  only  casting  that  is  used  under
the  fabrication.
It  is  what  is  known  as  a  bearing  plate
for  bridges.

plication  for  this  privilege  and  agree  to  abide by  the

’

Q  And  what  is  that  privilege?
A  The  fabricator  is  entitled  to  a  refund  in  the
charges,  or  in  other  words,  the  material  as  from  the  mill
shipped  to  a  transit  point  is  charged  at  the  local  rate,
and  after  fabrication  and  being  reshipped,  it  is  also
charged  at  the  local  rate,  and  the  fabricator  is  entitled
to  claim  the  difference  betwen  the  sum  of  the  two  locals  -
and  the  through  rate  from  the  original  point  of  ship-
ment  to  final  destination.

ns

A  Of  the  fabricated  product.

in  the  regulations  of  the  tariff,  and  ships  to  a  customer,

ference  between  what  it  would  have  cost  to  ship  from

origin  to  that  ultimate  consignee  ?

A  Yes,  sir.

of strike issues,  letters to  either  or both

.

A  second  general  type  of  executive  intervention
may  be  described  under  the  head  of  publicity.
The
president  has  frequently  ordered  the  publication  of  the
results  of  investigations  made  under  his  direction,  in
order  to  influence  public  opinion  and  thus  hasten
settlement.

the  party  plaintiff  or  defendant  in  the  ccnking  of  an

A  Yes.
Q  Can  you  tell  us  how  many  such  instances  you
know  of  in  which  the  federal  government  itself  was  e
party  plaintiff  seeking  injunctive  relief?

A  In  connection  with  these  particular  dispntes
which  I studied,  it  would  be  easier  just  to  mention, par-
ticular  ones.
That  was  the case  in  the Pullmen  strike
of 1894.  It  was the case in the coal strike  of 1919.  It

ee

406

A  All filed  in  federal  courts.
Q  And  these  were  suits,  as  I understand  it,  to  en-
join  workers  from  certain  activities,  on  the  ground  that
they  were  violations  of  the Sherman  anti-trust  act?

A  These  particular  ten  suits  were  of  that  nature.
Among  the  ten,  however,  there  were  one  or  two  cases
where  injunction  was  sought  both  against  the  workers
and  against  the  employers,  on  the  ground  that  a  par-
ticular  trade  agreement  constituted  a  violation  of  the
Sherman  act.

Q  Yes.
A  Most  of  them,  however,  were’  directed  against

workers  and  not  against  employers  as.  well.

Q  And  do!  understand  your testimony  that  of  the
applications  for  injunction  which  were  filed,  the  major
proportion  of  them  were  granted  by  the  courts?

A  Of  the  ten  government  suits  for  injunction  filed
uttder  the  Sherman  act,  the  first  one  in  1893,  eight  were
successful.

Q  Now,  what  instances,  and  what  are  their  num-
bers,  have  there  been  of  injunctions  other  than  under
the  Sherman  anti-trust  act  in  which  the  government
was  party  plaintiff  and  in  which  the  defendants  were
workeré?

A  As  you  probably  know,  it  isn’t  easy  to  say
exactly  how  many  injunctions  were  issued  in  the  fed-
éral  courts,  and  it  is  much  more  difficult  to  say  how
many  have  beén  issued  in  state  courts.

injunction.  cases  can  find  their  way  into  the  official
reports.

A’  There  were  two  other  types  of  executive  ‘inter-

Another  was  the  institution  of  court

purpose  was  to break  a  strike.  There
have  been  a
number  of  casés  under  other-acts  of  less  importance.

Q  Now,
ing  about  an  investigation  by  presidential commission
of  the causes  and the -history  of the Pullman  strike.

eome

two  thousand  pages.

Q  Will  you  state  briefly what  the findings of the
commission  were ‘with respect to the causes  of the
Pullman  strike?

A  The  commission  pointed  out that though there
were  a  number  of  immediate  causes,  the  principal  ones
had  to  do  with  the fact that  wages  had been reduced;
that the rental of company  houses, in which most of the
employees  lived,  had  been  maintained  at  their  previous
level.  The  company  pointed  out—
Q  You  mean  the  commission?,
A  The  commission—I  beg  your  sendan.  The
commission  pointed  out  that  a  protest  grievance  com-
mittee  of  the  men,’  organized  after''a  meetitig  of  the
men  to  represent  them,  somewhat  informally, had ap-
peared  to  protest  against  this  thing,  and  the  company
had  assured  the  members  of  that  Committee ‘that:  ere
would  be  no  action  against  any  of  the  mentber
who  appeared  to  make  the  ‘protest.

Within  three  or  four  days, however,  the  outétend-
ing leaders  of  that.  committee’  were  dinchakged;  and
cent immedietely:  led. to..e  strike,

Despite  .  thai

that  immediate  situation, | however,  and
the occasion for  the widespread  boycott  by the American
Railway  Union,  which,  was the most important, phase  of
the  so-called  Pullman  strike,  the commission,  pointed

;

a6

out ‘that .at.the  bottom  of  the  whole  situation  was  the
company’s  ettitude  with  respect  to  dealing  with  its
'
employees.

The  commission  said,  for  example,  that—

“The Pullman  Company  is: hostile,  to  the  idea
of  conferring  with  organized  labor  in  the  settle-
ment  ,of  differences  arising  between  it  and  its

employees.”

It said,  farther:

“The  Company  does  not  recognize  that  labor
in
organizations  have  any  place  or  necessity.
Pullman,  when  the  company  fixes  wages  and
rents,  and  refuses  to  treat  with  labor  organiza-
tions.  The  laborer  can  work  or  quit  in  the  terms
offered,  that  is  the  limit  of  his  rights.
This  po-
sition  secures  all  the  advantages  of  the  concen-
tration  of  capital,  ability,  power  and  control
for  the  conipany  in  its  labor  relations  and  de-
prives  the  employees  of  any  such  advantage
or  protection  as  a  labor  union  /might  afford.
In  this  respect  the  Pullman  Conpany  is  behind

the  age.”

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Now,  Professor  Berman,  let  us  move  on  to  Pres-
Did  he  have  occasion  to

ident  Theodore  Roosevelt.
intervene  in  labor  disputes?

A  President  Roosevelt,  probably  next  to  Presi-
dent  Wilson,  intervened  more  frequently  in  labor  dis-
putes  than  anyone  else,  The  first  and  perhaps  the  most
important  of  ‘his  interventions  was  in  connection  with
the  anthracite  coal  strike  of  1902.

Q  What  did  President  Roosevelt  do  with  respect

to  that strike ?—briefly,

A  The»  strike’  started  and  proceeded  for  some
months.  A great  mahy  complaints  reached  ‘him,  especi-
ally  from  public  officials  of  New  Engtand  and  New
York,  pointing  out  ‘that  with  the  approach’  of  winter
those  states,  depending  as  they  were  for  domestic  heat-
ing purposes  upon.  anthracite  fuel,  were  likely  to  suffer
very  severe  hardships;  and  asking  him ‘if he  wouldn’t
do  what  he could, iv order  to  bring  about  a  settlement.
Phe  President:  euceseded,  after  considerable  diffi-
culty, in  bringing abaut’a ‘meeting  Between representa-
tives  of  the  United.  Mine  Workers  and  of  ‘the  coal
operatois  Asithe  resalt‘of  that meeting  it  was agreed

427

that  he  should  appoint  a  commission  .which  would  ia-
vestigate  the  dispute,  make  FOCeeneNn
and  de-
cide  the  important  questions  involved.

©

Q  State  whether  or  not  one  of. the canees of  the
strike  was  the  refusal  in  the  first  instance  by the operat-
ors  to  meet  with  representatives  of  the  United  Mine
Workers  and  discuss  the  matter  of  a  new  wage  scale.
A  The  operators  had,  as  a  matter  of  fact,  never
made  prior  to  that  time  an  agreement  with  the  United
Mine  Workers  covering  anthracite  coal,  and  they  re-
fused  to  meet  with  the  representatives  of  the  miners.

Q  Can yon  sell me heeae 1G Gb  ee aaa

to  its  termination?

A  It  lasted  for  five  months,
Q  And  how  many  workers,  approximately?
A  About  150,000  workers.
Q  Did  this  strike  commission  to  which  you  have

referred  make  an  official  report?

A  It  did.
Q  And  can  you  briefly  summarize  the highlights
of  the  report  with  respect  to  the  findings  of  the  com-
mission  concerning  the  causes  of  the  strike,  and  any
recommendations  that  the  commission  made  for  the
averting  of  the  strike?

:

A  I think  that  this  brief  quotation  from  the  report

is  pertinent:

“The  occasion  of  the  strike  of  1902 was the

demand  of  the  United  Mine  Workers  of America
for  an  increase  in  wages,  and  decrease-in  time.
The  cause  lies  deeper  than  the occasion,  and :it is
to  be  found  in  the  desire  for  recognition  by  the
operators  of  the  miners’  union”.

Q  Did  the  commission  make any  estimate  as  to  the

losses  from  the strike?

A  It  estimated  that logses  in  wages  amounted  to
some  twenty-five  million  ddilars;  that  there  were
twenty-eight  miltion  dollars  lost  in  freight  receipts;
and  that  the  United  Mine  Workers  in  the  payment  of

E

428

‘relief  went  to  the  expense  of  one  million  eight  hundred
thousand  dollars.

Q  What  were  the  recommendations  of  the  com-
missions  with  respect  to  the  averting  of  future  strikes?

—briefly.

’

.

A  The  commission  suggested,  after  going  into  a
great  deal  of  detail  about  the  inequalities  in  bargkin-
ing  power,  that  a  plan  be  set  up  under  which  all  ques-
tions  of  difference  between  the employer  and  his  em-
ployees  should  be  considered  in  conference;  that  em-
ployees  shall  have  the  right  to  choose  from  their  own
ranks  workers  to  represent  them—and  the  commission
emphasizes  the  fact  that  that  right  shall  be  the  right
to  choose  representatives  or  agents  as  the  men  may
choose;  and  to  have  those  agents  officially  recognized
as  the  representatives  of  the  men  by  the  operators.

Q  I  show  you  what  purports  to  be a  summary
of  the  report  of  that-commission,  and  quotations  from
the  findings  of  the  commission,  and  ask  you  whether
or  not  the  summary  is  a  fair  one  and  the  quotations
correct,  to the  best  of  your  information.

A  I believe  so,  yes.

Q  What  action  did  President  Roosevelt  take  with

A  There,  again,  troops  were  sent.

:

Q  Let  us  move  on  to  President  Wilson.

Did  Pres-

ident.  Wilson  intervene  in  labor  disputes?

A  I think  his  record  of  intervention  is  more  x.

tensive  than  that  of  any  other  President.

Q.,  With respect  to a threatened  strike  of conductors
and  fraigmen  on  the  eastern  railways,  what  action
did  the  President  or  any  other  branch  of  the  federal
government  take?

.

A: In  that  instance  the  employers  and  the  employ-
ees  on  the. railways,  the  enstern  railways,  came  to  a
breakdown .of negotiations  which  commenced  in  1912
By. 1913 it looked  as  though  a  strike  would  take  place.
In June or July  an  actual  strike  vote  was  taken,  and  the
conductors  and  trainmen  voted  overwhelmingly  to  go
on  strike.
teat  l

AEA

23)

ae

The  President  asked, the two  parties  to  meet  with.
him, in conference.  On  examination of thi issues, it was
made  clear  to him  that  the  strike  could  be  avoided  by
arbitration,  but  not  by  arbitration  according  to  the
Erdman  Act  of  1898.  The  Erdman’  Act provided  ma-
chinery
for the mediation  af labor  disputes  on  the  rail-
ways,  and it  also provided  for  the setting  up of  a board

ae

of  three  parties,  one  representing  each  of  the contest-
ants,  and  a  third  party  impartial.

ilways  had  hed grins they  regarded  as  an
“a unfortunate  experience  in  having  an  impartial’ person
render  a  decision.  The  impartial  person,  being  neither
a  representative  of  the  workers  or  of  the employers,
was  likely,  in  their  opinion,  to  be ignorant  of  the  issues.
As  a  consequence,  they  refused  to  give  an  important
issue  for  settlement  over  to  one  person.

On  examination  of  the  situation  it  was  made
clear  that  if  the  law  would  provide  for  arbitration  by
a  board  of  three,  or  a board  of  six,  a  settlement  could
be  made  of  the  issues  and  the  matter  settled  by  arbi-
tration.

As  the  result  of  that  situation,  the  President  called
into  conference  not  only  the  most  important  officers
of  the  railways  and  of  the  railway  union,  but  alsa
the  majority  and  minority  representatives  and  leaders  on
the  flood  of  Congress.  There  was  at  that  time  an  act
before  Congress  to  modify  the  Erdman  law...  An  agree-
ment  was  made  that  the  law  should  take  a  certain  form,
and  the  President  thereupon  used  his  influence  to  have
the  law  passed  in  the  form  which  hed  been  agreed  to
in  cOwference  among  the  parties.

>  And  was  such  a  law  passed  by  Congreant
A.  Such  a  law  actually  passed  and  became  known

as  the  Newlands  Act.

.

Q  And  is  that  an  example  of  federal  interven-
tion  in  the  form  of  joint.  action  by both  the  President
and.  Congress  with  respect  to  a  particular  digpute?
A.  The  President  suggested,  the  way  of settling  the
_
thing  and the Congress  passed  the measure  which  would
provide  means  of  arbitration  to  avoid the  atrike.

Whereupon

HUGH  L.  KERWIN
was  produced  as  a  witness  for  and  on  behalf  of  the  Na-
tional  Labor  Relations  Board  and,  having  been  first
duly  sworn  by  the  Chairman,  was  examined  and  testi-
fied  as  follows:

DIRECT  EXAMINATION

BY  MR.  WIRIN:

Your  name  is  Hugh  L.  Kerwin,  Senior?
No  “senior”  about  it—just  Hugh  L.  Kerwin.
You  live  in  wengien?
Yes,  sir.
And  you  are  the  Director  of  Conciliation?
T am.
And  is that  a  department  or  a  service  of  the

United  States  Department  of  Labor?

A  It  is  a  service  of  the  United  States  Department

of  Labor.

Q  How  long  has  the  De

ent  of  Conciliation

been  in  existence?

A  ‘ft came  into existence  with the creation  of the

have  you  been  connected  with

A

:

been Director  of Conciliation
since
part with the ‘Secretary
of con-
the matters

|

tion.

of  Conciliation  through
1 take it?
administrations,

433

Q  And  do  you  have  any  persons  under  your  charge

who  seek  to  conciliate  and  adjust  labor  disputes?

A  Yes;  we  have  commissioners  of  conciliation,

field  representatives.

Q  How  many  commissioners  of ennai  ¢ do you

have  now?

A  We  have  thirty-eight,  in  the  field.
Q  And  what  has  been  the  number  of  commis-
sioners  of  conciliation  during  the  last  few  years?  How
has  the  number  ranged?

A  On  an  average  of  thirty  to  thirty-five  commis-
sioners,  except  during  the  World  War  period,  when
we  had  as  high  as  fifty-five  or  sixty.

;

Q  Do  they  all  work  under  ese supervision?
A  Yes.
Q  Are  you  familiar  in  a  supervisory  way  with

the  work  that  they  do?

A  I am.
Q  What  was  the  occasion  for  the  creation  of  the

;

Department  of  Conciliation?

A  Well,  the  organic  act  of  the  Department  of
Labor  states  that  the  Department  of. Labor  was  cre-
ated  to  foster  and  promote  the  interests  of  the  wage
earners  of  the  United  States  and  to  advance  their  op-
Then,  follow-
portunities  for.  profitable  employment.
ing  that,  it  was  stated  in  the  act  that  the  Secretary  of
Labor  was  authorized  to  act  as  a  mediator  or  to  ap-
point  commissioners  of  conciliation  in  industriel  dis-
putes,  whenever  in  the  judgment  of  the  Secretary  of
Labor  the  interests  of  industrial  peace  may  require  it
to  be  done.

Ever  since  along  before  the  war,  ieamediately  fol-
lowing  the  Civil  War,  there  has  been  a  demand  for
labor  for  a  separate  department  in  the  federal  gov-
ernment,  presided  over  by  a  cabinet  officer  or  official.
And  from  time  to  time,  in  an  attempt  to  meet  the
wishes  of  labor,  they  had  created  the  Bureau  of  Labor
and  the  Bureau  of  Labor  Statistics,  and  finally  the  De-

484

partment  of  Commerce  and  Labor  was  created  in
1908.

Labor  continued  to  persist  in  its  efforts  to  have  a
department  created  in  the  interests  of  the  wage  earn-
ers  of  this  country,  so  that  finally,  in  1913—those  were
the  days  of the  62d Congress—they  put  into  existence
the  Department  of  Labor  by  separating  four  bureaus
from  the  Department  of  Commerce,  and  providing  for
mediation  service  or  conciliation  service.

Q  Now,  since  its  inception,  how  many  labor  dis-
putes  has  the  Department  of  Conciliation  concerned
itself  with?
Do  you  have  those  figures,  or  approxi-
mate  figures?

A  Approximately  fifteen  thousand  nine  hundred

specific  disputes.

Q  And  do  you  have  an  approximation  of  the
number  of  workers  that  were  involved  in  those  dis-
putes?

A  Nearly  sixteen  million  workers  or  wage  earn-

oan.

Q  Now,  will  you  describe  briefly  the  functioning
-  Of  your  service  and  the  manner  in  which  your  depart-
ment  seeks  to  adjust  or  conciliate  disputes—just  giv-
ing us a  brief sketch of it?

;

A  Yes.  The  Secretary,  first-of  all,  was  author-
ized  to  intervene  whenever  in  the  judgment  of  the
Secretary  the  interests  of  industrial  peace  may  require
it  to  be  done;  but  we  have  ‘pursued  a  policy in the  De-
partment  of  Labor  of  the  general  proposition  of  await-
ing the  request  of someone,  some  one  of  three  parties—
the  employers,  the  employees,  or  the  public  directly  af-
we  have  not  relinquished  the  right  to
intervene  when  a  major  proposition  confronted  the  gov-
ernment.
Frequently  the  Secretary  has  instructed  us
to proceed  to take up  the case without  awaiting  a  call
from ‘one of the three parties mentioned.
When  the  commissioner  of  conciliation  is  assigned
to  a case, ‘his functions  are  purely  those  of  a  negotiator,

ugh

435

mediator,  or  it  might  be  somewhat  analogous  to  @  con-
sular  officer  in  the  State  Department.  He  seeks  to
bring  the  contending  interests  together,  without  being
working  under  drastic  instructions  to  follow  out  any
set  principles,  bécause,  in  the  handling  of problems  of
conciliation, a  great  degree  of  elasticity  is  mecessary,
because  no  two  strikes  or  threatened strikes are’ exactly
a4
alike.  They  may  be in  the  same  industry and  in
same  city at  the same  time,  with  the same  demands,  or
with  the  same  grievance;  but  they  differ..  So  that  the
 conciliator  must  of  necessity  be  somewhat  free  in  his
movements  and  in  the  manner  in  which  he  handles  the
affair.

-

Now,  he  tries  to  bring  about  a  situation  whereby
the  directly  interested  parties,  the  employers  and  the
employees,  will  solve  their  own  problems  in  their  own
way,  guided  and  advised  and  counseled  by  a  man  who
has  had  experience  in  somewhat  similar  situations  and
who  has  seen  other  arrangements  tried  out  in  various
strikes  and  threatened  strikes  and  who  can  bring  to
bear  at  the  proper  time  this  information  and  suggestion
and  recommendations,  so  that  while  his  work  is  entire-
ly voluntary  and  he  has  no  right  to  make  any  decisions
and  orders,  or  to  seek  to  enforce  any  terms.  He  does
submit  the  recommendations,  suggestions,  and  hé  works
with  the  respective  parties  in  joint  conference  and. with
separate  units  to  try  and bring  them  on  to  some  com-
mon  ground.

Q  Have  you  completed  substantially  your  state-

ment?

A  ‘I  might  say  here  that  this,  I think,  might  cover
it.  Through  this  conciliation  service  the  Department
of  Labor  can  be  helpful  in.  industrial  disputes,  first
through  conciliation  and  mediation,  by  aiding  the  dis-
putants  to  settle  their  difficulties  by  negotiation;  sec-
ondly,  by  the  commissioner  of  conciliation  drawing  on
his  knowledge  of  trade  agreements  in  the  same  indus-
try,  developing  a  plan  and  using  the  same  as  a  basis

be dt eh

Fame \
i Ele Veber

436

for  discussion  at  meetings  arranged  by  the  employers
and  employees;  and,  third,  through  the  commissioner
of  conciliation,  upon  the  request  of  both  parties,  draft-
ing  a  plan  or  a  suggested  plan  of  settlement  and  sub-
mitting  it  as  a  recommendation.

Fourth,  by  devising  methods  of  arbitration,  when
necessary,  through  disinterested  parties  or  through  par-
ties  selected  by  interests  concerned,  and  a  referee  se-
lected,  generally  from  the  outside,  or  named  by  some
responsible  government  official.

Fifth,  the  commissioner  sometimes  is called  upon  to
make  independent  investigations  covering  the  matters
in  dispute,  so  that  the  commissioners  may  be  in  a  posi-
tion  to  advise  employers  and  employees  engaged  in  in-
dustrial  controversy.
Frequently  these  investigations
the  commissioners  make  are  the  basis  of  a  settlement,
providing  the  basis  for  a  settlement,  because  you  find
very  often  that  both  parties  are  very  unfamiliar  with
the  actual  conditions  that  exist  in  the  plant  affected  by
the  dispute.

I think  that  covers  it.
Q  Now,  let  me  ask  you  whether  or  not  your  De-
partment  has  conducted  or  supervised  the  conduct  of
elections  of  employees  by  secret  ballot?

A  Yes,  they  have.
Q  Can  you  tell  the  Board  how  many  such  elec-
tions  were  conducted  and  how  many  workers  were  in-
volved?

A  Just  off  the  record,  wouldn't  it  be  well  to  put  in

this  affidavit?

Q  Just  let  us  have  the  figures  now,  and  I will  ask

you  questions  later  which  will  cover  that.

A  All  right.
In  the  course  of  its  experience  with  elections  in  re-
cent  years,  the  Department  has conducted  or  supervised
the  holding  of nine  elections  upon  direct  request  of  one
of  the  parties  to  a  labor  controversy—in  some  instances
upon  the request  of: the  employer,  and  in  all  such  in-
stances  upon  the  agreement  of  all  parties  concerned.

437

In  twenty-nine  cases  the  Department  was  requested
to  co-operate  in  the  supervision  of  the  elections  by other
governmental  agencies,  namely,  the  National  Labor
Board,  the  Petroleum  Labor  Board.
Q  What  was  the  experience  of  your  Department
in  the  holding  of  elections  as  to  whether  or  not  the
holding  of  secret  elections  was  conducive  towards  in-
dustrial  peace,  or  industrial  unrest?
A  Well,  we  found  that  it was  a  very  useful  method
in  the  bringing  about  of  a  better  understanding  between
the  contending  interests,  and  we  found  it was  very  help-
ful,  and  when  the  results  were  announced  and  accepted
it  was  very  helpful  in  the  negotiations  that  followed
after  the  election  was  held  and  the  proper  bargaining
agency  had  been  determined.
Q  Now,  Mr.  Kerwin,  I assume  you  are  familiar  in
a  general  way  with  numerous  reports  by  governmental
agencies  appointed  to  investigate  the  causes  of  a  gen-
I  will  ask  you  whether  or  not  from  your
eral  strike.
experience  you  have  found  it  wiser  to  intervene  in  a
dispute  in  an  effort  to  avert  a  strike,  rather  than  to
investigate  the  cause  as  of  a  strike  if  a  strike  has  taken
place?

|

A  Yes.  We  direct  our  efforts  towards  the  pre-
vention  of  strikes  and  lockouts,  and  during  the  years
there  has  been  @  gradual  change  in  the  relativity  of
strikes  and  of  threatened  strikes.  During  the  past  nine
months,  for  instance—and  I  checked  up  on  this  this
morning—from  the  close  of  the  last  fiscal  year,  it
stands,  the  percentages  stand,  51  per  cent  strikes  and
lockouts  and  49  per  cent  averted  strikes  and  lockouts.
Q  Now,  let  me  ask  you  whether  or  not  your  serv-
ice,  as  representative  of  the  Labor  Department  co-
operates  in  any  way  or  coordinates  in  any  way  the
work  of  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board?

A  Yes,  our  service  has  cooperated  with  the  Na-
tional  Labor  Relations  Board  and  with  the  Regional
Boards  of  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board.

,

438

Q  Will  you  tell  the  Board  in  what  manner  you  ;

have  cooperated  with  it?.

A  Well,  from  the  information  received  at  the  De-
partment  for  assistance  or  the  assignment  of  a  commis-
sioner  of  conciliation  in  a  labor  dispute,  if  the.  telegram
or  the  letter  or  the  telephonic  conversation  would  seem
to  indicate  that  it  is  a  matter  that  has  to  do  with  col-
lective  bargaining  or  unfair  labor  practices,  we  immedi-
ately  advised  them  to  get in touch  with  the  nearest
regional  labor  board  and  notify  your  people  here  in
the  office  of  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board.

Or,  if  our  commissioners,  on  reaching  the  scene  of
a  strike  or  a  lockout,  or  the  scene  -of  a  threatened
strike,  find  that  it  comes  under  the  purview  of  this
Board,  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board,  we  immedi-
ately  turn  it  over  to  them.

Q  In  other  words,  you  don’t  attempt  to  conciliate
any  condition  which  involves  an  unfair  labor  practice,
as  defined  by  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act?

A  Only  under  these  conditions,  that  frequently
the  regional  labor  board  officials  ask  that  our  commis-
sioners  remain  on  the  situation  and  assist  them.

were  subniitted  to  us.

EDWARD  BERMAN

was  recalled  as  a  witness  for  and  on  behalf  of  the
National  Labor  Relations  Board  and,  having  been  pre-
viously  duly  sworn,  testified  as  follows:

DIRECT  EXAMINATION

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  I think  we  had  completed,  had  we  not,  Profes-
sor  Berman,  the  intervention  of  the  President  in  the
conductors  and  the  trainmen  threatened  strike?

A  That  is  right.
Q  What  about  the  Colorado  strike  of  1913  that

Mr.  Carmody  asked  about?

A  I  should  say  that  the  Colorado  strike  of  1913
and  1914  represents  the  most  extensive  single  instance
of  executive  intervention  in  a  labor  dispute.

In  that  instance,  President  Wilson  not  only  carried
on  or  attempted  to  get  the parties  to  meet  with  him;  he
used  all  the  influence  he  possessed  to  bring  together
the  Colorado  Fuel  and‘Iron  Company,  which  was  the

44)

—

most  important  company  in  connection  with  this  issue,
and  the  workers.

President  Wilson  wrote  numerous  letters  to  both
parties,  especially  to  the  employers,  and  he  carried  on
I don’t  know  the  number  of docu-
a  concerted  activity.
ments  for  which  he  is responsible,  but  I suspect  it prob-
ably  runs  into  the  twenties—constantly  attempting  to
get  some  sort  of  peaceful  settlement.

An  event  took  place  in  connection  with  that  strike
which  has  come  down  in  labor  history  under  the  name
of  the  Ludlow  Massacre.

:

Q  Just  describe  that  briefly,  won't  you?
A  In  that  particular  instance,  the  Colorado  Na-
tional  Guard  set  fire  to  a  tent  colony,  the  miners  hav-
ing been  evicted  from  their  houses  by the  company,  and
in  this  particular  tent  there  had  been  dug  what  might
appear  to  be  something  like  a  tornado  cellar,  in  the
hope  that  in  the  case  of  wild  shooting,  the  women  and
the  children  might  be  put  in  there—and,  as  a  matter
of  fact,  during  this  particular  event  there  were  women
and  children  and  one  or  two  old  people  hiding  in  that
cellar,  which  was  under  the  tent.

The  tent  was  set  fire  to,  and the  result  was  the  loss
That  gave
of  life  of  practically  all  of  those  people.
rise  to  certain  extensive  attention  on  the  part  of  the
public,  and  so  much  concern  on  the  part  of  the  Federal
Government  with  respect  to  this  strike,  that  President
Wilson  sent  in  Federal  troops.

|

I think  it  should  be  pointed  out  in  this  connection
that  those  Federal  troops  which  were  under  pretty  close
supervision,  under  the  direct  orders  of  the  President—
fairly  direct,  and  perhaps  more  direct  than  any  other
instance  of  the  use  of  Federal  troops—succeeded  in  not
only  bringing  peace,  as  far  as  prevent  violence  was  con-
cerned,  into  that  strike  area,  but  saw  to  it  that  none  of
the  troops  were  to  be  used  to  import  strike  breakers
from  outside  of  the  area.

I consider  the  activity  of  President  Wilson  in  con-

442

nection  with  the  Colorado  strike  as  a  very  civilized  and  |
statesmanlike  attempt  to  handle  an  issue  which  had  be-
Of  course,  he  made
come  extraordinarily  dangerous.
no  attempt—he  did  not  succeed,  that  is—in  preventing
the  strike  in  the  first  place;  but  he  make  every  effort
to  settle  it  quickly,  and  his  sending  of  troops,  I  think,
did  result  in  bringing  about  a  more  satisfactory  condi-
tion  in  the  area.

By  that  time  the  situation  had  given  rise  to  such
widespread  attention,  although  the  strike  was  practical-
ly dead.

The  Colorado  Fuel  and  Iron  Company  invited  Mc-
Kenzie  King,  who  has  since  been  Premier  of  Canada,
to  come  in  and  investigate  the  situation,  and,  as  a  re-
sult,  a  certain  plan  of  employer  representation—or
employee  representation—which  was  one  of  the  earliest
of  the  widespread  company  union  schemes,  was  put
into  effect.

.

;

Kenzie  King,  now  Premier  of  Canada,  was  invited,  John

inspection  of  the  area?  Is  that  not  true?

eted  by  women  and  children  from  the  area.

BY  MR.  WIRIN::.
Q  Now,  referring  to  Mr.  West's  arid  the  Commis-
sion’s  finding  concerning  the  causes  of  that  strike,  will

45

you  state  briefly  what  Mr.  West  listed  as  the  primary
causes  of  the  strike?

A  This  is  a  direct  quotation  from  Mr.  West:
“The  struggle  in  Colorado  was  primarily  a  struggle
against  arbitrary  power,  in  which  the  question  of  wages
was  secondary,  as  an  immediate  issue.”

Q  What  does  the  reference  to  “arbitrary  power”

mean?  “Arbitrary  power”  of  whom?

A  Of  the  employer,  of  the  company.
Q  I  will  ask  you  whether  or  not  summary  dis-
charges  by  the  company,  the  black  list,  armed  guards,
and  the  use  of  spies  were  part  of  the  grievances  of  the
employees?

A  They  were.
Q  And  part  of  the  causes  of  the  strike?
A  Yes.
Q  I think  this  has  been  covered,  but  to  make  sure
that  it  was,  is  it  true  that  the  major  demand  of  the
striking  miners  was  for  the  recognition  of  the  Union?

A  It  was.
Q  I call  to  your  attention  the  following  quotation

from  Mr.  West’s  report:

“The  situation  in  Colorado  approached  a  cpedition
actual

of  absolute  prostration  of  government  and  of
revolution” —

Q  And  I ask  you  whether  or  not  the  finding  of
Mr.  West  in  that  connection  is  supposed  by  your  own
investigation  of.  the  matter  from  reports  that  you  have
read?

A_  I  should  say  that  if  by  a  state  of  revolution
you:  mean  a  state  in  which  two  important  parties  of
armed  camps  are  fighting  each  other,  in  which  the
ordinary  processes  of  law  are.  not  in  operation,  in
which  violence  is  a.very  common  occurrence,  that  that
is  true.
That  would  not  be  my  formal  definition  of
“revolution,”  but  I think  that  is  one  aspect  of  most  rev-

-  olutions.

dustrial  Commission,  commonly  designated
Walsh  Commission.

as

the

MR.  CARMODY:  Are  you  leaving  the  Colorado  sit-

uation?

MR.  WIRIN:  I am  for  the  time  being.
MR.-  CARMODY:  While  we  are  on  the  subject  |
would  like  to  ask  in  what  work  these  ).1en  were  en-
gaged  who  were  on  strike?
‘

THE  WITNESS:  In  mining.
MR.  CARMODY:  Were  the  men  in  the  steel  mills

on  strike  at  that  time?

THE  WITNESS:  The  strike,  before  it  was  over,  in-
volved  some  steel  workers.
I  think  essentially,  how-
ever,  it  involved  the  miners  employed  by  the  Colorado
Fuel  and  Iron  Company.

MR.  CARMODY:  And  what  were  they  mining?
THE  WITNESS:  They  were  mining  coal.
BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Can  you  tell  us  how  long  the  ‘strike  lasted  and
-how  many  workers  were  involved,  approximately?
A  This  Colorado  strike  started  in  1913—I  do  not
recall  the  exact  length  of  time  for  which  it  was  carried
on—but  I should  say  it  was  about  a  year  or  somewhat
less;  and  it  involved  all  of  the  employees,  especially
the  miners,  that  is, of  the  Colorado  Fuel  and  Iron  Com-
pany.
Some  other  companies  became  involved  later  on.
I do  not  have  immediately  available  a  statement  of  the
number  who  were  involved.
I should  sayeprobably  no
more  than  ten  or  fifteen  thousand  workgrs  were  in-
volved.

of  the  United  Staes  Government,  of  the President,  has
been  questioned  with  respect  to  sending  Federal  troops
into  a  state  where  the operation  is a  mining  operation?
THE  WITNESS:  Perhaps  I could  answer  that  bet-
ter,  Mr.  Carmody,  if  I express  what  I believe  to  be  the
facts  about  the  conditions  under  which  Federal  troops
may  be  sent  in:

;

447

es

MR.  CARMODY:  I  don’t  know  that  we  are  pre-
pared  at  this  moment  to  go  into  that—and  we  will  just
pass  the  question.

|

THE  WITNESS:  In  this  particular  instance  there
is  no  general  case  of  which  I know,  in  which  the  fact
that  a  coal  strike  or  something  of  that  kind.  was  in-
volved,  and  which  was  offered  as  an  objection  to the
use  of  Federal  troops.

MR.  CARMODY:  There  was  no  injunction  against

the  entrance  of  these  troops  into  the  state?

THE  WITNESS:  No,  and  there  were  no  direct

court  processes  involved.
BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  To  your  knowledge,  was  the  authority  of  the
Federal  government  to  order  troops  questioned ?—And,
if  so,  what  became  of  those  questions?

-

A  It  was  not  questioned  and,  on  the  contrary,  the
Federal  troops  were  sent  in  to a certain  extent  because
of  the  request  of  officials  in  the  State  of  Colorado.

Q  Now,  leaving  the  Colorado  strike  and  returning
to  the  report  of  the  Industrial  Commission,  under  an
Act  of  Congress  of  1912,  will  you_tell  us  briefly  what
was  the  nature  of  the  Commission,  by  whom  it  was  ap-
pointed,  and  under  what  authority  of  law?

A  Congress  passed  an  Act  on  August  23rd,  1912
which  set  up  the  United  States  Commission  on  Indus-
trial  Relations.
It  was  given  the  task  of  inquiring  into
the  Federal  labor  situation  in  the  country,  including
agriculture,  and  especially  with  respect  to  the  situation
regarding  relations  between  labor  and  corporate  organ-
izations,  business  organizations  or  corporations;  the  ef-
fect  of  industrial  conditions  on  public  welfare;  the  ex-
‘tent  and  results  of  methods  of  collective  bargaining;
methods  for  avoiding  or  adjusting  labor  disputes;  and
to  discover  the  ‘underlying  causes  of  dissatisfaction  in
the  industrial  situation  generally.

The  Chairman  of  the  Board  was  Frank  P.  Walsh,
and  the  Board  was  made  up  of  representatives  who

448

were  in  general  rather  well  known  throughout  the  coun-
try, for  their  interest  in  industrial  problems.

Q  Was  Professor  John  R.  Commons  a  member  of

the  Commission?

A  Professor  Commons  was  a  member,  and  also
Mr.  Harris  Weinstock  of  California,  who  was  at  that
time  or  later  a  Commissioner  of  Labor  in  that  state;  Mr.
Austin  B.  Garretson,  who  I believe  at  that  time  was  a
member  of  one  of  the  railway  unions,  was  also  a  mem-
ber.

.

MR.  CARMODY:  The  Order  of  Railway  Conductors.
BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Who  was  the  Director  of  Research  and  Investi-

gation?

A  That  was  Basil  Manly.
Q  Did
the  Commission

through  the  country?

hold

public

hearings

A  It  did.

It  examined  over  seven  hundred  wit-
nesses,  and  its  hearings  actually  took  place  on  as  many
as  184  days.

Q  Lasting  for  a  period  of  about  six  months?
A  About  that.
Q  Now,  did  the  Commission  make  a  report  of  the

hearings  and  of  its  findings  and  recommendations?

A  The  Commission  published,  I  believe,  twelve
of  which,  I think,  was  de-

volumes—all  but  one  voi
voted  to  a  report  of  the  be  at  the  hearings.

There  was  no  unanimous  report.  A  number  of—I
should  say  two—reports  were  made.
One  report  was
made  with  Frank  Walsh,  the  Chairman,  writing  the  re-
port,  and  a  number  of  persons,  three  others,  agreeing
with  him  in  every  detail—although  some  of them  added
additional  minor  recommendations  of  their  own  in  sep-
arate  accompanying  re
'
The  other  major
rt  was  written  by  Professor
John  R.  Commons,  and.  a  number  of  the  members  of
the  Commission  agreed  with  him;  but  the  same  thing
was  true  there.

:

Even  though  there  was  not  wnanimous  report,  and.
even  though  there was considerable  difference  of opin
ion among  the various  members  of the Commission,  the
members  of the Commission  were apparently  all unani-
mous.  Rents  Oot Gem  a  Oe  find-

trade  unions,  and  the  arbitrary  power  exercised  in  in-
dustry  as  a  cause  of  industriel disturbance  and  indus-
trial  disputes.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Now,  Professor  Berman,  did  the  Commission
make  any  recommendations  as  the  result  of  its  elab-

a |

450

, )

orate  investigation,  for  Federal  action, in  order  to  avert
some  Of  the disastrous  strikes  which  it  had  considered?
A  Yes, the Commission  first  pointed  out  that  one
of the ttajor causes, perhaps  the  most  important  causes
of industrial  unrest,  was the denial  of  the right of or-
ganization.  it went  into  a  considerable  discussion  of
the  significance  of the  fight  for  collective  bargaining,
and of ‘the existence  of any quality  in bargaining  power
among  employers  and  employees.

The  Commission  came  to  the  conclusion  that,  as
the  result  of  its  investigation,  it  would  appear—and
this  is  a  quotation—“That  every  means  should  be  used
to extend  and strengthen  organization  throughout  the
entire  industrial  field.”

THE  WITNESS:  They  felt  thet  it  was  not  sue-
which  I suppose  meant  favorable:to  ther.
In this particular  instance  the Railway.  Uiiions  had
believed  themaaives to have  heen  the retipients of a
number  of  unjust arbitration
decisions,  and though  they

452

were  very. willing  to  engage  in negotiations  with  the
employers,  and  such  negotiations  were  conducted  over :
a  considerable  period  of  time,  they  refused  to  submit
the  issue  to  arbitration.

The  dispute  settled  down  to  a  dispute  ‘over  the
eight-hour  dey.  On  the  railroads  that,  as  a  matter  of
fact,  was  not  purely  an  eight-hour  issue.
The  basic
working  day  up  to  that  time  had  been  ten  hours  and
over-time  commenced  for  work  done  after  ten  hours  in
ome  day.

The  demand  for  an  eight-hour  day  was  thus  really
a demand  for  a  basic  eight-hour  day,  and  the  payment
of  over-time  for  work  done  in  excess  of  eight  hours.
The issue thus  was a  mix issue as to  the length  of  the
working  day,  together  with  a  rather  hidden  issue  over
increase  in wages  involved  in  the  reduction  of  the  basic
working  day.

Q  Now,  with  respect  to  the  coal  strike  of  1922,  is
that  the  coal  strike  that  Mr.  Carmody  had  reference  to?

A  Yes.
Q  And  what  did  the  President  do,  if  anything,  in

that  connection?

A  In  that  connection the  President  attempted  to
bring  about  a  conference  of  the  two  sides.
First  the
attempts  were  made  by  his  Secretary  of  Labor,  Davis,
and  then  he  called  a  conference  himself.  and  held  it  in
the  White  House.  He  threatened  to  call  out  troops  if  a
settlement  were  not  effected,  and  then  he  asked  Con-
gress  for  a  law  providing  for  the  appointment  of  a  Com-
mission  of Investigation.
Such  a  law  was  passed  and
John  Hays  Hammond  was  appointed  as  the  Chairman.
Q  Now,  with  respect  to  the  railway  shopmen’s

strike  af  1922  was  Federal  intervention  resorted  to?

A  That  is.one  of  the,  I think,  most  extensive  ex-

amples  of  intervention  by  President  Harding.

practices  which  are  defined  by  the  act  as  unfair  prac-
tices,  would  the  elimination  of  those  practices  tend  to
avert  the  strikes  which  are  caused  by  those  practices?
A  I think  that  it  would  be fair  to  say  that  there
are  usually  other  causes  of  strikes  than  those,  that  is,
even  though  the  discharge  of  a  worker,  or  what  is
regarded  here  as  an  unfair  practice  in  another  respect—
but  there  are  frequently  other  causes—but  I shoufd  say
that  this  is  one  of  the  most  important,  and  the  elim-
ination  of  that  is  a  cause  that  would  tend  to  reduce
the  number  of  strikes.

authors  persons  of  sufficiently  good  reputation,  that
the  Board  may  take  judicial  notice  of  it.

That  is  all  of  this  witness.
CHAIRMAN  MADDEN:  Thank  you  very  much,

Mr.  Slichter.

MR.  WIRIN:  I  would  like  to  call  Dr.  Leiserson,

as  the  next  witness.

A  That  is  right.

tion  Board?

A’  I am.

A  I am  the  chairman.

.

a  member  of  the  Board?

ber  of  any  governmental  agency  concerning  itself  with

dent,  shortly  after  the  enactment  of  the  National  Re-
covery  Act.

out  of  the  job,  of  handling  their  labor,  by  hiring  con-

Now,  there  are  just  thousands  and  thousands  of

the  purposes  for  which  they  are  hired?

a  rule,  for

A  I can’t  say  that.  Employers  think  that  they  are
competent—I  can’t  say  about  that.
I  know  some  very
competent  people  who,  are  in  that  business  of  repre-

In  the  literature  of  personnel  management,  or  in  the
epeeches  that  are  made  by  both  employers  and _ per-

him  by his  first  name,  and  therefore  the  relationships
were  good  and  friendly,  like  in the  family.

Now,  with  the  growth  of  large  scale  operations,  the
employer  has  lost  contact  with  his  employees,  and  he
doesn’t  know  their  first  names  any  more,  and  therefore
he  has  to  get somebody,  and  he is  busy  with  a  lot  of
other  things,  and  therefore  he  has  to  get  somebody,  and  .
specialize.  them  at  the  function  of  handling  what  he

used  to  do  in  a  friendly  way  himeelf,  before:

Well,  now,  personally,  I don’t  think  that  that  squares
with  the  facts,  but  anyway,  it  is  a  theory  that  they  go
~.

The  fact  is  that  they  have  these  personel  man-
agers,  or  labor  maneggers,  with  a  specialized  function
‘of  handling  the  employees.
7”  The  fact is also that  when some  of the most bitter

dustries,  where  the  employer  does  know  his  employees

as  a  matter  of  fact,  the  history  of  labor  organizations |
shows  that  the  most  bitter  conflicts,  and  the  origin  of

.

Q  Let  me  ask  you  whether  or  not,  just  as  the
employers  on  fhe  one  hand  hire  persons  whom  they
consider  experts  in  the  field  of  labor  relations  in deal-
ing  with  their  employees,  to  the  advantage  of  the  em-
ployer,  whether  or  not  employees  have  resorted  to
the  use  of  experts,  and  if  so,  what  are  they?

A  These  ‘questions  that  you  are  asking,  really  go
into  the  theory  of  collective  bargaining,  and  the  pur-
pose  of  employees  in  setting  up  unions.

All  that  is involved  there  is  this:  With  the  devebop-
ment  of  industry  and  corporate  enterprises,  just  as
one person  with  his own money  can’t  run mueh  of  a
business,  but  has  to  chip  in  with  a  lot  of  other  people,
and  form  a  corporate  enterprise  to  raise  the  capital  and
so  on,  so  on  the  employees’  side,  if  he  is  going  to  get,
if  the  employee  is  going  to  get  a  decent  wage  for  his
labor,  he  finds  that  he  can’t  do  it by his  own  knowledge
in  the  labor  market,  and  -he  is  ignorant  of  the  labor
market,  and  he  has  disputes  with  the  persons  who  hire
him.

He  doesn’t  know  how  to  handle  those  disputes,
and  every  employee,  besides  being  a  laborer,  has  to  be
a merchant,  and  he  has  to  sell  his  labor.

For  some  years  I ran  & public  employment  bureau

498

that  brought  laborers  and  jobs  together,  and  my  ex-
perience  in  that  was  this:
From  interviewing  and
handling  thousands  and  thousands  of  workers  that  came
to  the  office,
I  found  that  if  a  fellow  was  a  first-
class  workman,  careful  and  intent  on  his  work,  he  was
a  rotten  salesman.  When  he  went  to  an  employer,
he  didn’t  know  the  first  thing  about  how  to  sell  himself
to  the  employer  to  get  a  job.
If  he  could  only  get  on
it, he  would  soon  demonstrate  it.

But  he  mumbled,  and  he  was  afraid  to  talk,  and

all  of  that.

On  the  other  Hand,  the  fellow  that  could  come  in
and  talk  and  sell  himself  quickly,  they  were  pretty
rotten  workmen.

Salesmanship  and  careful  workmanship  don’t  us-
ually  go  together.  Now,  the  same  way  with  respect  to
people  who  are  engineering  managers  of  lurge  corpo-
rations,  or  good  financial  managers.
They  are  not
usually  good  salesmen,  so  they  hire  men  at  very  large
salaries,  to  seli  their  labor.

Those  are  the  fellows  that  we  have  to  look  out  for,

these  high-pressure  salesmen.

Now,  all  that  collective  bargaining  means:  is  that
just  as  the  employer  hires  men,  specialists  in  the  hiring
of  labor,  they  call  personnel  managers,  or  labor  man-
agers,  or  industrial  relations  directors,  they  are  out-
siders  in  the  industry.  They  don’t  know  anything  about
the  work,  particularly,  but  they  are  experts  in  hiring,
and  handling  labor.

Now,  the  employees  need  something  just  like  that.
How  are  they  going  to  get  it?  The fellow  who  gets
$25.00  a  week,  how  is  he  going  to  hire  a  high  pressure
salesman  to  sell  his  labor  for  him?

He  chips  in  through  an  organization,  just  like  thous-
ands  of  farmers  chip  in  and  form  a  cooperative  selling
organization,  and  then  they  get  a  high-powered  sales-
man  who  knows  how  to  sell  their  stuff,  and  a  high
salaried  one,  s9  the  employees  put  in  together  their

499

)

dues  in  an-organization,  and  they  hire  a  fellow  that
they call a  business manager  or  a  business  agent, and
employers  who  don’t  like  it often  call  him  walking  dele-
gate,  but  they  are  the  personnel  managers or industrial
relations  directors  for  the  employees.
Now,  the  purpose  of  collective  bargaining  is  to  put
the  two  groups  that  have  to  buy  and  sell  labor,  and  de-
termine  the  conditions  on  an  equality,  so  that  each
one  can  have  the  same  kind  of  competent  people  to
make  the  employment  contract  or  the  conditions  for
the  employment  contract.
Q  Now,  Dr.  Leiserson,  with  respect  to  the  kind
‘\
of  person  who  becomes  the  representative  of  labor,
does  the  person  change  as  the  relationship  is  changed?
What  I mean  by that  is, when  the  situation  is one  where
strikes  are  the  order  of  the  day,  is  the  situation  differ-
ent?

:

A  That  is  true  on  both  sides,  in  this  way:  When
the  employer  feels  in  handling  his  labor,  the  proper
way  to  do  it  is  to  prevent  them  from  having  an  organ-
ization,  to  see  that  each  one  is  willing  to  accept  the
employers  dictation  of  terms  or  else  go,  then  the  em-
ployer  doesn’t  hire  these  psychologists,  and  that  kind
of  personnel  manager.

}
He  is  more  likely  to  hire  an  ex-saloon  keeper,  and
a  detective,  and  people  of  that  kind,  who  noses  around
in  the  employees’  meeting,  because  they  are  on  a  fight-
ing  basis,  and  he  will  hire  organizations  that  exist  to
help  destroy  labor  organizations,  and  the  same  way  on
the  employees’  side.

When  the  employees  attempt  to  set  up  their  coop-
erative  organization,  and  they  go  to  the  employer  and
he  fires  them,  because  he  has  known  all  about  it  before
through  spies  or  other  methods,  obviously  a  nice,  gentle-
manlike  fellow  is  not  going  to  survive  as  the  business
agent  of  that  kind  of  an  organization.  He  has  to  be
a  really  high-pressure  salesman,  and  he  is  the  warrior
type,  and  he  has  to  krdw  how  to  threaten,  “If  you

500

are  going  to  fire  us,”  he  has  to  say  to  the  employer—
“we  will all walk  out and  tie  up  your  plant.”

That  is  the  kind  of  fellow  that  survives  when  the
employer  has  on  his  side  the  kind  of  people  that  want
to  destroy  their  organization.

When  they  both  get  on  the  basis  of  recognizing
each  other’s  rights,  and  wanting  to  deal  with  each  other,
then  often  difficulties
arise  in  the  first  years,  be-
cause  you  have  got  generals  and  warriors  trying  to  do
business  on  a  business  basis,  just  the  same  as  when
generals  and  warriers  try  to  make  treaties’  of  peace,
and  maintain  peace.

Then  wherever  you  have  collective  bargaining  over
a  long  period  of  time,  you  will  find  that  the  type  of
warring  business  agents  of  both  sides  tend  to  be  elim-
inated,  and  a  business  like  person  on  each  side  tends
to  survive  as  the  person  that  represents  the  two  contract-
ing  and  bargaining  parties.

Q  Now,  you  have  already  told  us,  I- believe,  about
the  advantages  which  the  worker  gets  from  being  a  mem-
ber  of  a  labor  organization  that  is  responsible—what

sponsible  unions,  as  in  the  case  of  the  men’s  clothing

A  Well,  there  is  a  lot  said  by  the  employees  to

has  his  organizations,  and  he  gets  advantages  from  or-
ganization,  and  he  knows  that  he  gets  advantages  from
his  organization.
The  employee  has  his  organization,
and  he  knows  he  gets  those  advantages.

Now,  it  is true if they  deal  with  each  other  in a
civilized  and businesslike  way,  instead  of a war-like  way,
that  there  are  benefits  for  both  sides.
For  example,
most  of  the first  collective  agreements  were  set  up  in
competitive  industries,’  covering  a  large  field,  so.  that
every  employer  knows;  and  every  other,  knows  that

every  other  employer  is paying  the  same  rate of wages
as  written  into  the  contract,  and  there is no.  chiseling

equalizes  labor  costs  and  that is one  of  the  main  ad-

The  other  advantage  is that the  innumereble  disputes

ee

a

fit  as  a  result  of  these  collective  agreements?  -

arily  doesn’t  know  anything  about  labor  disputes  until

see

But  when  the  public,  through  the  Government,  has

the  public  doesn’t  know  that  it  is  getting  the  benefits.

Q  Does  the  public  foot  the  bill  in  the  end?

Q  With  respect  to  the  men’s  clothing  industry,  all
of  the  men  folk  of  American  contribute  to  the  cost  of

A  As  far  as  they  buy  clothes.
Q  Now,  in  terms  of  your  experience  in  the  men’s
clothing  industry,  what  is  your  opinion  as  to  whether
or  not  this  proposition.  is  true,  namely,  that  certain
causes  of  disputes,  like  wage  disputes,  and  disputes  as
to  the  number  of  working  hours,  can  be  conciliated  or
mediated  or  compromised  or  adjusted,  but  that  certain
other  causes  of  disputes,  namely,  interferences  by  em-
ployers  with  the  right  to  collective  argaining,  can  not
be  conciliated  or  compromised,  but  should  be  protected
by  law?

A  That  doesn’t  apply  to  any  industry.

Of  course,
it  is  obvious  that  if  you  have  a  dispute  over  wages,
and  disputes  as  to  what  hours  should  be  worked,  and
‘things  like  that,  there  is  no  absolute  testimony  or  rights
that  you  are  entitled  to  eight  hours  or  seven  hours,  or
$25.00  a  week  or  $30.00  a  week.
Those  are  questions
that  people  are  willing  te  compromise  on,  and  can  com-
promise  on,  and  if  it  goes  to  an  impartial  third  party,
an  arbitration  board,  it  is  a  question  that  they  can
settle  in  the  light  of  comparable  circumstances.

So  ordinarily  it  is  said  that  those  questions  are

arbitratable.

But  when  the  question  comes  up,  “Shall  I  have  a
right  to  join  a  labor  organization,  without  interference
by the  employers?  or  “Shall  I have  the  right  to  bargain
collectively  with  the  employer?”  or  “Shall  the  employer
have  a  right  to  determine  conditions  without  interfer-
ence  by  his  employees?”  as  he  calls  it,  well,  that  is  a
question  that  is  ordinarily  called  not  arbitratable,  be-
cause  each  says,  “Why,  that  is a  matter  of principle,  and
if  I recognize  the  union,  I  would  be  letting  them  run
my  business’  and  the  union  says,  “If  we  let  the  em-
ployer  lay  down  the  terms,  then  we  have  nothing  to
say  as  to  what  we  will  get  for  our  labor.”

503

Now  those  are  questions  of  principle.
Now,  it  is  always  on  questions  of  principle  that
people  go  to  war  about,  and  kill  each  other,  and  of
On  these  other  matters,  you  can
strikes,  and  so  on.
arbitrate.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  What  are  your  duties  as  chairman  of  the  Na-

tional  Mediation  Board?

A  Well,  I have  general  charge  of  the  work  of  the
-  Board,  and  it  has  some  duties  very  similar  to  that  of
the:  National  Labor  Relations  Board.  We  settle  disputes
that  arise  among  employees,  as  to  who  is  their  repre-
sentative  for  the  purpose  of  collective  bargaining,  if our
services  are  invoked,  of  course,  and  we  conduct  elec-
tions  and  certify  who  is the  duly  designated  representa-
tive.  That  is  one  duty.

We  also  have  mediation  duties,  and  we  mediate
disputes  between  employers,  and  employees,  whenever
_  they  want  to’  change  their  agreements  or  make  modi-

504

fication  of  the  agreement,  and  if  strikes  should  occur
or  threats  of  strikes,  we  have  to  settle  those,  and  we
have  to  arrange  for  arbitrations,  and  we  have  some  du-
ties  in connection  with  the  Board  set  up  under  the  Rail-
way  Labor  Act  for  interpreting  and  applying  the  various
collective  bargaining  agreements.

That  is  similar  to  these  Clothing  Boards  that  were

mentioned.

|

_

The  Railway  Labor  Board  Act  sets  up  a  Board  in
‘Chicago,  named  the  National  Railroad  Adjustment
Board,  which  is  a  permanent  body  for  effecting  an
industrial  court  for  interpreting  and  applying  the  col-
lective  agreements.

Q  Let  me  ask  you,  in  the  course  of  your  duties  as
Chairman  of  the  National  Mediation  Board,  and  in  the
course  of  your  general  studies,  have  you  familiarized
yourself  with  the  history  of  labor  relations,  on  the
railways?

|

A  Yes,  very  much.

For  our  first  annual  report
we—or  I personally  made  the  survey,  and  prepared  the
‘review  of  all  of  the  railway  legislation  since  1888,
and  something  of  its  operation,  and  the  story  is  in-
cluded  in  our  report.

Q  Will  you  tell  us  when  was  the  first  legislation
with  respect  to  labor  relations  on  the  American  rail-
ways?

A  The  first  Act-  was  in  1888,  and  there  have  been
a  series  of  very  bitter  railroad  strikes,  and  first  in
1877,  and  1878,  and  then  in  1896,  and  a  lot  of  violence,
armed  forces  on  both  sides,  and  the  countgy  was  aroused
by  it,  and  many  bills  had  been  introduced,  but  finally
in  1888  a  bill  was  passed.

Now,  if  you  will  let  me  develop  this  story  for  you,
it  seems  to  me  that  the  experience  of  Congress  with
the  first  railway  labor  legislation  shows  in  my  mind  that
the  Railway  Labor  Act,  or  the  Wagner  Labor  Disputes
Bill,  under  which  your  Board  is  operating,  is  just  an
attempt  of  Congress  to  apply  to other  industries  in  in-

505

tersfate  commerce  the  principle  that  they have  found
here  to  be  necessary  for  handling  labor  disputes.

Let  me  show  you  how  it  developed.
The  first  Act  of  1888  was,  now we can see  it, was
unintelligent.  We  can  always  be  wise  in  hindsight,  you
see.

How  was  it  unintelligent?

Whenever,  there  are
strikes,  people  say,  “Oh,  they  ought  to  arbitrate;”  or
They
else,  “The  Government  ought  to  investigate.”
are  not  thinking  of  what  are  the  underlying  causes,
and  so  you  find  in  this  Act  of  1888,  two  principles  laid
down,  that  when  there  is  a  strike,  if  both  sides  agree
to  arbitrate,  an  Arbitration  Board  shall  be  set  up,  and  a  ©
lot  of  description  of  how  the  Arbitration  Board  should
.  be  set  up;  or  the  President  may  appoint  a  Board,  or  a
Commission  to  investigate  this  strike,  and  inform  the
public.

That  was  the  attempt  to  deal  with  the  question.  —
Now,  I say  “unintelligent”  because,  not  to  be  critical
but  merely  when  we  see  later  what  happened—the
people  didn’t  know  just  what  needed  to  be  done,  and
they  catch  on  these  words  like  “arbitration”  and  “in-
vestigation”  and  as  4  matter  of fact  this  Act  was  in
effect  for  ten  years,  and  not  a  single  Arbitration  Board
was  set  up  in  those  ten  years  and  only  one  investiga-
tion  was  made,  and  that  was  during‘the  bitter  American
Railway  Labor  Union  strike,  that  Pullman  strike.  That
was  after  it  was  all  over,  and  all  of  the  damage  was

-  done,  and  the  question  was  to  lay  the  blame.

But  the  Commission  that  was  appointed  made  cer-
tain  definite  recommendations,  and  we  learned  some-
thing  from  the  war,  and  in  1896  a  new  bill  was  enacted
into  law,  known  as  :the  Herndon  Act.

Q.  May I interrupt  yeu  and  ask  you  whether  or  not
the  Commission  that  you  have  reference  to  attempted
to  ascertain  fome  of  the  causes  and  underlying  facts?
A  They  went  into  it  elaborately,  and  had  some

praise  and  blame,  and  finally,  a  recommendation.

506

Q  Did  they  ascertain  whether  or  not  the  refusal
to  engage  in  collective  bargaining  by  the  employers  was
one  of the  causes  or  a  factor  in  the  strike?

A  Well,  the  term  “collective  bargaining”  was  not
as  popular  or  well-known  in  those  days  as  it  is  now,
but  the  Commission, for  instance,  in  its  report,  which
was  made  in  1894,  said  that  “the  Pullman  Company  is
Lostile  to  the  idea  of  conferring  with  organized  labor
in  the  settlement  of  differences  arising  between  it  and
its  employees.”

And  in  another  place,  on  page  26,  “The  Company,
the  Pullman  Company,  does  not  recognize  that  labor
organizations  have  any  place  or  necessity  in  Pullmans,
and  when  the  company  fixes  wages  and  rents  and
refuses  to  treat  with  labor  organizations,  the  laborer
can  work  or  quit  on  the  terms  offered,  and  that  is  the
limit  of  his  rights.”

This  Commission  goes  on  to  say:  “This  position
secures  all  of  the  advantages  of  the  concentration  of
capital,  ability,  power  and  control  for  the  company  in
its  labor  relations,  and  deprives  the  employees  of  any
such  advantage  or  protection  as  a  labor  union  might  of-
fer.  In  this  respect,  the  company  was  behind  the  age.”
Q  Let  me  interrupt  you  for  a  moment  and  ask  you
to  go  back  to  the  bitter  strike  prior  to  1888,  which  you
have  mentioned,  on  the  railways;  were  any  of  those
strikes  called  by  an  attiiude
the  part  of  the  oper-
ators  similar  to  that  d
d  by  the  Commission
investigating  the  Pullman  strike?

A  It  was  exactly  the  same  issue,  and  the  same  is-
sue  appears  in  every  industry,  and  in  every  time  when
employees  first  want  to  organize,  and  deal  collectively.
The  employers  don’t  like  it.  You  can’t  blame  them;
you  and  I might  not  like  it; either.  We  have  had  our
Own  way  up  to  a  certain
e,  and  then  the  employ-
ees  become  selfconscious,
Jjand  they  say,  “We  want
to  have  our  own  organization,  just  as  the  employers
have  and  we  want  to  deal  on  that  basis,”  and*the  em-

_

507

ployer  refuses,  and  it is  when  he  refusea  that way  that
you  have  the  bitter  fight  on  for  recognition.

.

It  is  comparable  to  the  fight  between  kings  and  the /

people  that  set  ep  parliament  for  the  first time.  The’
employers  know  that  once  you  set  up  the  right  of  the
employees  to  be  represented  in  negotiating  with  them,
you  are  doing  what—or  they  take  the  same  position  4s
King  John;  put  four  and  twenty  overlords  over  me,  and
he  says,  “You  put  this  union  over  me  to  run  the  bus-
iness.”

Now,  that  is  what  causes  strikes.
Now,  it  is  interesting  to  see  that  this  Commission
said,  back  in  1894,  that  anyone  who  denied  rights  to
employees  to  organize,  and  deal  with  the  employer,
was  behind  the  age  then,  and  we  know  those  rights
have  been  denied  right  along,  and  not  only  that,  it  was
perfectly  legal  to  deny  those  rights,  all  through.

For  example,  the  essence  of  the  present  railway
legislation,  and  the  Wagner  Labor  Disputes  Act,  is
-  merely  the  establishing  for  the  first  time,  really,  of  a
right  to  organize  the  employees.  True,  we  have  talked
and  courts  have  said,  “Employees  have  the  right  to  or-
ganize,”  and  they  have  said  that  for  a  hundred  years.
Well,  you  know  that  early  in  this  century,  and  in
the 19th  century,  when  employees  struck  for  any  reas-
_  on,  when  -they  struck  for  any  reason  it  was  a  con-
spiracy,  and  one  of  the  first  conspiracy  cases  in  Phila-
delphia,  in  1802,  the  judge  who  heard  the  evidence  said,
“These  men  are  charged  with  one  of  two  things,  either
they  have  combined  together  to  raise  their  own  wages,
or  else  they  have  combined  together  for  the  purpose
of  inducing  other  people  not  to  take  lower  wages  than
they  are  willing  to  accept,”  and  he  proceeded  to  instruct
the  jury  that  the  rule  of  law  condemns  both..
It  is  per-
fectly  illegal.

It  was  a  leading  case  in  1802,  when  Chief Justice
Shaw,  in  Commonwealth  against  Hunt,  of  the  Supreme
Court  of  Massachusetts,  analyzed  this  situation,  and  he

508

said,  “Just  as  people  are  allowed  to combine  for  instance,
and  say,  “We  won't  drink  liquer,  we  are  going  to  ip-
duce  other  people  not  to  drink  liquor.”  We  recognize
that  that  is  a  good  purpose,  in  organization,  and  for
similar  good  purposes  now,  he  says,  we  must  apply  -
that  to  the  situation  when  working  people  combine  for
the  purpose  of  improving  their  own  condition,  and  they
want  to  get  better  wages  and  better  terms  of  employ-
ment,  and  have  lower  hours,  and  that  is  a  good  pur-
pose  in  their  eyes,  and  so,  from  that  time  on,  the  law
has  been,  so  far  as  I  can  understand  it,  I  am  not  a
lawyer,  although  I  have  studied  ibe  labor  law  in  rela-
tion  to  labor,  has  been  the  rule,  that  workmen  have
the  right  to  organize  for  legal  purposes,  but  if  you
analyze  that,  it  wasn’t  the  right  jn  any  real  sense.
It
wasn’t  a  legal  right.

If  I have  a  right  to  own  property,  and  you  have  a
right  to  come  and  take  it  away  from  me,  and  the  only
way  that  I  can  keep  my  property  is  by  my  strength,
Maybe  you
‘that  isn’t  a  right,  as  I  know  the  law.
lawyers  know  it  a  little  better,  but  I don’t  think  so.

Now,  the  employer  had  the  equal  right  to  destroy
the  employees’  organization.
That  is  rather  interesting.
When  the  investors  combine  in  a  corporation,  and  they
set  up  this  artificial  entity,  we  call  a  company,  that  is
exactly  similar  to  employees  combining  in  an  artificial
entity,  which  is  an  unincorporated  association,  usually,
but  some  unions  are  incorporated.

that  would  be an illegal  strike  under  the  laws,  as  |

;

Now,  what  this  railway  labor  legislation  now  does,
and  what  your  Act  attempts  to  do,  is  to  say  to  employ-
ers,  “You  have  the  right  to  organize,  and  the  enployees_
have  no  right  to  strike  to  destroy  your  organization,
and  the  employees  have  the  right  to  organize.  We  have
got  to  take  away  this  one  thing  that  you  had.  left,
namely,  the  right  to  destroy  these  labor  organizations.
And  so  the  essential  of  this  new  legislation,  as  I see
it,  is  the  action  by  Congress  to  prevent  trespassing  on
the  employees’  right  to  organize.

_

Now,  I will  show  you  how  in  this  Railway  Labor

legislation  that  was  developed.

learned  this  investigation  business  didn’t  help  much.

other,  for  about  eight  yeers,  and  then  by  that  time,  ap-
