Q  Let  me  ask  you  this  question  at  this  point:
With  respect  to  the  disputes  that  were  settled,  were
the  employees  represented  by  an  organization  of  any
kind?

A  What  happened,  réally  was  this:

By  that  time
the  Train  Service  Brotherhoods,  which  made  the  four,
locomotive  engineers,  firemen,  trainmen  and  conductars,
had  established  their  existence  and  were  powerful
enough  so  that  most  of  the  railroads  had  to  deal  with
them.
So  when  they  threatened  to  strike,  when  there
were  disputes,  these  mediators  were  able  to  come  in  and
arrange  between  the  representatives  of  these  brother-
hoods,  and  the  representatives  of  the  carriers,  settle-
ments,  or  agreements  that  settled  this  dispute.

Now  that  worked  so  well  in  the  latter  part  of  this
Herdman  Act  that  in  1913,  the  Neulands  Act  was  adop-
ted,  which  strengthened  these  mediation  provisions,
and  made  the  Board  permanent,  to  mediate  in  these
disputes,  and  provided  that  they  shall  do  everything
possible  to  settle  the  disputes  on  the  request  of  either
party,  and  if  they  couldn’t  adjust  it  by  mediation,
getting  them  to  make  an  agreement,  then  they  should
try  to induce  the  parties  to  arbitrate  disputes,  and  under
those  circumstances  some,  or  a  considerable.  number  of
arbitrations  were  entered  into.

The  important  thing  to  remember  is  this:

That
these  people  who  were  now  settling  their  disputes  by
mediation  and  arbitration,  had  by  force  through  all
of  those  strikes  and  threats  of  strikes,  established  their
right  to  organize,  so  that  the  railroad  companies,  and

511

the  carriers,  were  afraid  to  destroy  those  a
and  they  couldn’t  do  it.

Well,  they  couldn’t  do  it,  they  had  to  deal  with
them,  and  during  these  years  they  have  developed
this  business  relationship  that  I  was  telling  you  about,
and  so  now  you  hear  over  and  over  again,  that  repre-
busin-
sentatives  of  the  railroad  brotherhoods  are
properly,
esslike  people,  who  manage  the  union  aff
and  so  on,  and  they  are  good  unions,  and  so  on;  but
the  only  difference  between  them  and  the  others  is  that
they  are  older,  having  been  in  the  business  for  many
years,  they  have  developed  a  type  of  responsible  bus-
inesslike  leadership  and  it  never  occurs  to  a  failroad
manager,  now,  to  say,  “I  won't  deal  with  you,”  because
he  recognizes  the  men,  who  come  and  deal  with  him  as
equals,  and  they  represent  labor  just  the  same  as  he
recognizes  the  legal  counsel  of  some  company  that  sells
him  materials  or  something  like  that.
On  that  basis,  you  can  get  along.
Q  Now,  let  me  ask  you  this,  at  this  point.
Did  both  the  Herdman  Act  and  the  Neulands
Act,  in  so  far  as  they  set  up  mediation  machinery,  as-
sume  the  existence  of  organizations  on  the  part  of
employees,  to  represent  them,  as  a  basis?

A  Most  of  it  dealt  with  disputes  where  there  were
organizations  of  employees,  and  employers  recognized
and  dealt  with  them.

--,

Q  Let  me  please  ask  you  then,  could  the  disputes
have  been  mediated  as  a  practical  matter  or  adjusted,
if  the  workers  weren’t  represented  by  some  organiza-
tion  in  collective  bargaining?

A  Well,  if  any  considerable  number  of  employees
‘go  out  on  strike  or  have  a  dispute  with  the  employer,
somebody  has  got  to  talk  for  them,  and  they  can’t  all
talk  at  once,  but  they  have  got  to  have  a  representa-
tive,  and  even  if  they  did  try  to  talk  at  once,  evegy  one
of  them  wouldn’t  know  what  to  say.  He  doesn’t  realize
All  he  knows,  he  has  grievances,  and

~-  the  problems.

512

he  is  rather  inarticulate  in  stating  those  grievances  and
likely  to  just  blame  the  management  for  everything.

But  when  they  get  together,  in  the  meeting,  and
talking  it  over,  and  then  they  work  out  their  problems,
and  they  appoint  a  committee,  or  a  person,  to  repre-
sent  them,  who  tells  them,  “Now,  there  is  no  use  of
your  just  blowing  off  steam  like  that;  I can’t  go  and  ne-
gotiate  anything  that  way.
You  have  got  to  give  me
What  shall  we  do  about  this, and  what
instructions.
shall  we  do  about  that,”  and  it  is  by  that  process  that
representation  developed,  and  intelligent  business  bar-
gaining  comes  around.

MR.  SMITH:  On  that  same  point,  was  it  the  lack
of  adequate  organization  on  the  part  of  the  railway
workers,  or  perhaps  a  failure  to  recognize  such  or-
ganization  as  existed  which  was  responsible  for  there
being  no  arbitrations  under  the  Herdman  Act?

THE  WITNESS:  More  than  that.  For  instance,  the
early  Acts,  when  they  provided  for  arbitration,  provided
that  an  organization  had  to  be  bound  to  live  up  to  it.
There  couldn't  be  any  arbitration  with  a  lot  of  indi-
viduals,  and  there  was  some  vague  provision  in  the  Act,
if  one  hundred  individuals,  for  example,  would  get  to-
gether  and  sign,  a  petition,  that  they  would  live  up  to
the  agreement,  then  they  also  might  come  under  the  ar-
bitration.

The  whole  thing  presumed  organization,  and  there
can’t  be any arbitration,
obviousty,  if there is to be any
responsibility  to live  up  to the award,  unless  there  is
some  kind  of  an  entity  and authority  to  enforce  the
award  so far as the  employees  are concerned.

Otherwise,  the  law  was  not  changed  in  essentials.
The  United  States  Board  of  Mediation  of  five  was
abolished,  and  now  our  National  Mediation  Board  of
three  was  substituted  for  it,  but  it  said,  “Whereas,  un-
der the 1928 Act, if a  dispute  arose,  one group  of  em-
ployees  said,  ‘We  represent  the  employees,  or  ore  or-
ganization,’ and another  came  to  the  employer,  and  the
employer  said,  ‘I  can’t  deal  with  any  of you,  I  don’t
know  who  represents  the  employees,’  or  ‘I  prefer  to
deal with  this  one, and I think that this  one  is the
proper  one’”—that  cause  a  lot  of  disputes,

The  old United  States  Mediation  Board  could  only
handle  them  by agreement  with  the  carrier,  and  if they
could  agree  by  mediation,  and  if  they  could  get  the
carrier  to  agree by mediation  to  say,  “Well,  let  us  take
a  vote,  you,  the  mediator,  take  a  vote,  and  whichever
organization  gets  the  majority,  that  will  be  the  repre-
sentative”  and  then  it would  work  all  right.

.

Well,  in  those  six  years  they  only  had  about  nine
elections  altogether  on that basis, and  there  was  a  great

out  into  a  strike,  or  strikes.

Q  Please  state Whether  or  not  in  your  experience
and  the experience  kite National  Mediation  Board

vepresent.  We  have  never  in all our experience  certi-

to do so; it is always

with  a  good  deal  of disturbance

When,  however,  we come  in and  conduct  the elec-

certification  hy the  Board  that  such  and  such  an’ organ-
ization  represents  the  majority  of  emp
of  this
cla:
the  employer  then  deals  with  them,  makes
greement  with  them,  handles  grievances  through  them,
and  thereafter  all  of  that  disputing  and  unrest  is  gone.
In  that  sense  there  is  no  question  that  an  enormous

operations  and  efficiency  of  the  property,  is  avoided
when  we get the thing  thus settled  once  and  for all.

We  often  have  management  say  to  us  that  they  are
not  interested  in  the  dispute,  because  the  law  requires
them  not  to  be;  but  they  would  like  to  get the thing  set-
tled,  and  we  run  off  the  election  and  settle  it for  them.
Q  Let  me  ask  you whether  or  not  in your  general

=  and  your  experience  is  the same?

_

A  My experience  has been the same,  whether  it is
;
that industry  or in other industries.  When  I was with
~~  the Petroleum  Labor  Board  or  with  the  original  Na-
®  tional  Labor  Board, we  found  the  same  situation,  with

of

Q I take  it,  then,  that  your  experience  on  the  Na-
tional  Labor  Board  and  on  the  Petroleum  Labor  Policy
Board,  differed  from  your  experience  on  the  Nationa!
Mediation  Board  in  that  in  respect  to  the  former  Boards
there  was  no  machinery  of  law  or  authority  of  law
for  the  enforcement—at  least  for  the  efficient  enforce-
ment—of  the  rulings  of  the  two  former  Boards?

“A  Yes;  in  this  sense  it  is  true.

For  example,
when  we  certified  a  certain  representative—and  per-
haps  I  told  you  this  yesterday—on  the  railway,  like  it
came  up  in  the  Virginian  Railway,  and  the  employer
attempted  to  deal  with  the  organization  that  represented
‘the  minority,  that  lost  the  election,  the  organization
that  was  certified  under  our  law  as  representing  the
majority  went  into  the  Federal  Court  and  the  Federal
Court  said  that  their  right  to  bargain  collectively  was
a  property  right  and  would  be  injured  by  the  com-
pany  dealing  with  the  other;  and  they  got  an  injunction
enjoining  the  carrier  from  dealing  with  this  other  as-
sociation  that  had  lost  in  the  election.  That  then  settled
the  thing,  and  I  am  glad  to  say  that  on  the  railrods
generally  we  have  very  little  difficulty  in  that.
The
carriers  tell  us,  “Come  in  and  certify  to  us  who  is  the
real  representative.  We  don’t  care  whether  you  are
holding  an  election  or  checking  authorizations.  When
we  have  your  certification  we  will  treat  with  those
representatives  and  know  that  we  are  following  the
law.”

Q  What  was  your  experience  while  on  the  Pe  -
troleurn  Labor  Policy  Board  with  respect  to  discharges
of employees  for  union  activities?  Were  there  any  com-
plaints  of such  discharges?
|
-  A  When  the  Petroleum  Labor  Policy  Board  began

531

to  function—in  fact,  the  reason  it was  formed  at  all—
it  was  became  the  administrator  of  the  code  received
an  enormous  number  of complaints,  running  up  into  the
thousands,  that  the  labor  provisions  of  the  code  were
not  being  carried  out.

‘Now,  some  of  those  complaints  had  to  do  with.  the
wages  and  hours  and  they  did  make  provisions  Origin-  .
ally  or  at  first  by having  the  Code  Authority  look  into
those,  and  those  did  not  bother  them  so much.  But
those  that  had  to  do  with  the  right  to  bargain  collectively
and  being  represented  by  representatives  of  their  own
choosing,.they  could  not  let  the  Code  Authority  enforce
those  provisions,  because  the  Code  Authority  was  made
up  entirely  of  employers.

In  a  few  cases  where  they  did  attempt  to  do  it,  the
experience,  as  the  administrator  found,  was  that  the
Code  Authority  did  not  pay  any  attention  to  the  com-
plaints.

_

So  the  Administrator  found  it  necessary  to  set  up  a
Board  of  his  own  similar  to  the  National  Labor  Board,
operated  for  the  general  codes  that  were  under  the
National  Recovery  Administrator.

The  Petroleum  Code  was  transferred  from  the  Na-
tional  Recovery  Administrator  to  the  Department  of  In-
terior,  so  that  the  Secretary  of  the  Interior  acted  in
place  of  General  Johnson,  let  us  say.
Now,  he  then
found  it  necessary  to  set  up  this  Board.

When  we  begar  to  operate.  January  1,  1934,  we
found  several  hundred,  at  least,  complaints,  of  two
varieties:  one,  that  the  employer  refused  to  deal  with
organizations  of  employees  when  they  represented  a
majority;  and,  second,  that  a  lot  of men  were  discharged
or  discriminated  against  for  one  reason  or another, when
they  would  try  to  set  up  an  organization  or  when  com-
_  Mittees  of  an  organization  went  to  see  the  employer,
and  they  would  be  discharged  for  union  activities.  We
had  many  of  those  instances.

The  Board  attempted  fo  investigafe  those  and  wher-

532

ever  possible  to  get  the  employer  to  reinstate  the  peo
ple  who  were  discharged  where  it  looked  as  if  it  was
done  for  their  union  activities.
In  quite  a  few  cases
we  succeeded  in  doing  that.
In  others  we  actually  had
to:  make  decisions,  ordering  the  reinstatement  of  the
people.
In  many  we  had  to  run  elections.

As  long  as  I was  connected  with  the  Board,  about
eight  months,  from  January  to  August,  we  ran  about
fifty  elections,  or  settled  fifty  representation  disputes  by
elections  or  by checking  authorization.

Q  Now,  Dr.  Leiserson,  was  the  personnel  of  the

Board  a  bi-partisan  or  impartial?

A  It  was  made  up  of  three  neutrals.
Q  Did  you  find  that  many  of  the  complaints  by
workers,  alleging  acts  by  employers  interfering  with
their  activities,  were  true  in  fact?

A  Oh,  yes.

It is a  difficult  thing  to  establish  proof
that  men  were  discharged  or  discriminated  against  for
union  activity  only.
In  almost  all  cases  there  were  a
lot  of  surrounding  circumstances.
We  found  some
cases  where  it  was. obvious,  as  I  recall  one  decision.
We  said  that,  considering  the  evidence,  there  could
not  have  been  any  other  reason  for  this  discharge,  con-
sidering  the  record  of  the  man  and  so  on,  except  his
union  activities.

But  usually  the  case  was  mixed  up  with  the  em-
ployer  having  sent  somebody  to  attend  the  union  meet-
ing  or  to  sit  outside  the  union  meeting  as  the  men
walked  in  and  check  off  who  was  there,  and  with  call-
ing  meetings  of  the  employees  and  making  talks  to
them  as  to  which  organization  was  better.

Usually  there  was  found  to be  a  set  of circumstances
pretty  much  like  that  that  I  read  yesterday  from  the
,  Decision  of Judge  Hopkins  in Kansas,  showing  the  things
that  the  employer  had done  to  interfere,  influence,  or
coerce;  and, incidental  to  that program  the  discharges
happened  to  be  there  also.  Wherever  you  had  that,
it was easy to establish  that the discharges  were  just
part  of  the  program  of  interference.

533

Q  Now,  in  one  of the cases  that  is pending  be-
fore the  Board  and in which  testimony  is being  taken
now,  namely,  Wheeling  Steel  Corporation  case,  there
are  allegations  that  the  Wheeling  Steel  Corporation
engaged  in  unfair  labor  practices  in  financing  and
sponsoring  a  company  union.

Did  you  find  in  the  course  of  your  experience  on
the  Petroleum  Labor  Policy  Board  that  employers
engaged  in  such  practices?

A  Yes.

That  is  a  universal  thing.
It  was  found
on  the  railroads,  and  that  is  why  Congress  prohibited
it by  express  statute,  these  amendments  of  1934.

This  Decision  of Judge  Hopkins  that  I read  yesterday
involved  a  case  where  the  employer  undertook  to  assist
the  company  union  by  deducting  from  their  wages
dues  and  turning  it  over  to  fhe  union,  and  then  the  list
of  the  members  be  given  to  the  master  mechanic  or
some  other  official,  so  that  they  would  know  just  who
belonged  to  the  company  union  and  who  did  not.

That  is  a  universal  practice  in  all  the  industries
that  I  have  come  in  contact  with  where  they  have  at-
tempts  at  interference  with  the  employees’  right  to
organize.  And  so  in  the  petroleum  industry,  one  of  the
first  cases  we  had—I  think  it  was  Case  No.  2—the  Mag-
nolia  Petroleum  Company,  the  employer  set  up  the  re-
presentation  plan.
It  was  devised  by attorneys  for  the
company  and  by  a  personnel  manager  they  had.  Then
they  called  for  election  of  representatives,  and  then
when  the  representatives  were  gotten  together,  they
met—in  Dallas,  Texas,  as  I recall  it—and  the  expenses
of  the  meeting  and  the  transportation  and  everything
were  paid  for  by  the  company.

The  officials  Sf  the  company  were  present  at  the
meeting.
The  plan  was  presumably  adopted  at  this
meeting,  which  was  attended  not  only  by  representa-
tives  of the  employees  but  by an  equal  number  of repre-
sentatives  of  the  management.

Then  certain  questions  came  up.  The  main  question

534

came  up  at  this  meeting  where  some  twelve  repre-
sentatives  of  the  employees  demanded  that  a  phrase
be  put  into  the  plan  that  would  reproduce  what  was
known  as  Section  7-A  of  the  National  Industrial  Recov-
ery  Act,  guaranteeing  the  rights  of  the  employees.

The  personnel  manager  for  the  company  said  that
was  not  necessary,  that  “This  is  better  than  collective
bargaining;  this  is  the  golden  rule.”
Those  were  the
words  that  he  used.

But  at  least  twelve  men  insisted,  and  there  was  a
good  deal  of  discussion  about  it  and  apparently  senti-
ment  for  including,  and  so  the  meeting  was  adjourned
for  lunch.
At  lunch  they  had  the  ‘president  of  the
company,  the  general  counsel  of  the  company,  and  the
vice  president  addressed  a  dinner  of  all  these  repre-
sentatives.

Q  Do  you  know  who  paid  for  the  dinner?
A  Oh,  yes.  The  evidence  is  that  the  company  en-
All  the  leading  officials  of  the  company  were  there.

tertained  all  these  representatives.

That  was  in  the  evidence.

quest  of  putting  in  Section  7-A  was  withdrawn.

Then  when  they  got  back  in  the  afternoon,  the  re-
Now,  the  Board  found  on  the  evidence:  it  raised  two
questions  as  to  whether  under  that  Act,  that  Section
7-A,  first,  the  employer  had  a  right  to  set  up  a  plan  like
that  for  the  employees,  to  ge’  it  established  not  by  the
employees  voting  for  it  but  by  the  employee  repre-
sentatives,  the  management  representatives,  to  adopt  it,
never  referring  it back  to  the  employees  for  their  opin-
ion,  and  so  on.  And  the  Board  held  that  that  was  in
violation  of  Section  7-A.
The  second  question  was  that  the  employer  claimed
that  this  plan  was  a  contract,  and  so  you  could  not  have
a  new  election  with  new  representatives.
The  Board
held  it  was  not  a  contract.
The  company  appealed  the  case  over  the  Board  to
The  Administrator  appointed  the

the  Administrator.

535

Solicitor  of  the  Department  to  hear  the  appeal,  and
he  went  over  the  legal  question  and  so  on  and  he
upheld  the  decision.

.

But  that  is  a  typical  example  of  what  has  hap-
pened  with  the  organization  of  these  company  unions.
No  personnel  manager  really  denies  th  .  that  is  what
they  are  doing.  They  claim  it is  better  for  the  employ-
ees  than  if  the  employees  had  their  own  o:  ganization.
But  no  company  that  sets  up  this  company  organiza-
tion  will  really  deny  that  the  facts  are  any  different,
and  the  same  things  happen  in  all  such  cases.

Q  Now,  did  the  National  Labor  Board,  the  Pe-
troleum  Labor  Policy  Board,  and  does  the  National
Mediation  Board,  draw  a  distinction  between  a  com-
pany  union  and  a  company.  financed  or  sponsored
union?

A  Well,  this  term  “company  union”  is  very  con-
fusing.  Many  people  define  it  as  a  union  confined  to
the  employees  of  one  company.  A  union  confined  to  the
employees  of  one  company  is  a  perfectly  legal  and
perfectly  sound  organization,  if the  employees  want  that.
If  the  employees  want  to  organize  that  way,  that  is
their  business;  and  there  are  a  good  many  bona  fide
organizations  of  that  kind.  The  only  disadvantage  of :it
is that  it  is  likely  to  be  weaker  than  if it
e  a  brand
of  a  national  organization.  And such  unions  soon  dis-
coved  that,  and  although  they  may  be  organized  first
within  a  company,  they  will  join  with  some  national
organization  after  they  are  in  existence  a  short  time.
But  that  is  an  organization  of  employees  that  happens
to  be  confined  fo  the  employees  of  one  company.

What  is  ordinarily  meant  by  a  company  union  is
&  union  sponsored,  encouraged,  financed,  and  dom-
inated  by  the  company.  Now,  that  might  not  include
the  employees  of  only  one  plant.
It  might  include  the
_  employees  of  a  dozen  or  more  plants.
'

___  The  issue  is,  if you  use  the  term  “company  union,”
it should  be  defined;  and  what  is  bad  about  it  is  that

536

it  is  dominated,  financed,  and  controlled  by  the  em-
ployer,  which  means  that  he  is  interfering  with  the
freedom  of the  employees  organizing  their  own  organ-
ization  or  choosing  their  own  representatives.

MR.  SMITH:  More  than  that,  is  it  not  true  that
the  structure  of  many  so-called  company  unions  or  em-
ployer  representation  plans  is  such  as  to  make  the
process of collective  bargaining,  as  we  have  tradi-
tionally  understood  it,  difficult?
THE  WITNESS:  Yes.  Many  things  are  referred
to  as  company  unions  that  are  not  unions’  or  organ-
izations  at  all.  For  instance,  in  many  plants  the  em-
ployer  announces  that  he  would  like  to  have  better
channels  of  communication  with  his  employees,  and  so
we  will  have  every  department  or  certain  departments
elect  representatives.  There  is  no  organization,  no  dues,
no  meeting  of  those  organizations.
Once  in  one,  two,
or  three  years,  there  is  this  election.
There  is  really
no  responsibility  of  the  representatives  toward  their
group.  And  so  those  ought  not  to  be  called  company
unions,  because  they  are  not  unions:  they  are  not  or-
@anizations  at  all.  There  are  many  instances  of  that
kind.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  When  you  use  the  phrase  “company  union,”
you  are  refering  to  those  groups  that  exist  for  the  pur-
pose  of  collective  bargaining?

,

A  Yes.
.Q  Now,  what  was  the  experience  of  the  three  gov-
ernmental  agencies  that  you  have  been  a  member  of,
namely,  the  National  Labor  Board,  Petroleum  Labor
Policy  Board,  and Nation  Mediation  Board,  as  to  wheth-
er  or  not  these  practices  of  employers  which  you  have
been  describing,  in  the  form  of  interference  with  em-
'  ployee  activities,  through  company  financed  unions—
what  has been  the  experience  in  so far as to  wheter  or
not  these  practices  lead  or  cause  unrest  amongst  em-
ployees?

537

A  There  is no  question  that if there is anything

that  the  employees  resent  more  than  anything  else,
is when  they are trying  to exercise  their  right  as Ameri-
can  citizens  to  associate  themselves  for  their  common
benefit.
It  is  supposed  to  be  a  liberty  of  individuals,
not  only  to  act  as  individuals  but  to  act  in
for  legitimate  purposes,  and  that  when  they  want  to
exercise  that,  the  employer  insisting  and complaining
against  the  government  interfering  with  his liberty,  the
employer  insists  that  his  liberties  include  the  inter-
ference  with  the  employees’  liberties  to  associate.  There

|

And  it  goes  on  the  theory,  the  theory  of  the  employer,
that  his  interests  and  the  interests  of  the  employees
are  not  the  same,  because  if  he  though  they~  were
the  same,  he  would  of  course  say,  “All  right,  you  go
and  elect  your  own  representatives;  I will  elect  mine,
and  we  wil]  work  together.”
But  he  does  not  act  on
that  at  all.
If  he  would  not  interfere  with  his  employ-
ees.  He  is  afraid  that  the  moment  they  organize  there
will  be  a  conflict  of  interest,  and  that  he  must  control  it.
Now,  the  experience  is  universal]  that  the  employees
resent  that  more  than  anything  else,  and  more  than
that.  When  the  representatives  of  the  employees,  the
active  members,  the  committeemen,  get  their  heads
chopped  off,  as  they  say,  that  is  they  get  fired  or  trans-
ferred  or  disciplined,  or  some  other  way,  then  every
employee  becomes  frightened—frightened  because  he
attended  the  union  meetings,  he  is sympathetic  with  the
movement,  and  his  head  is  coming  off  next.  And  that
is  what  causes  more  bad  relations  than  any  other
single  thing  that  I know  of.  And  that  is  quite  aside
from  the  right  of  the  employee  that  he  ought  to  have
for  himself,  regardless  of  whether  their  interests  are
im  common  or  not.

There  are  many  things  on  which  the  buyers  and
sellers  interests  are  not  the  same.  Regardless  of  wheth-

538

er  they.  are  or  not,  they  have  a  right  to  act  for  them-
selves  without  the  interference  of  the  other  party.

You  will  find  in  the  decision  of  Justice  Hughes  in
the  Texas  and  New  Orleans  case  that  he  said  that
Congress  recognized  that  they wanted  to  promote  friend-
ly relations  and  that  the  best  way  to  do  that  was  to  have
the  free  representatives  of  both  sides  get  together,  and
there  was  no  reason  why  Congress  may  not  do  that,
many  not  promote  cooperation  that  way,  through  the
representatives  of  both  parites.

Now,  you  will  find—to  show  you  just  how  uni-
versal  this  is—you  will  find  that  when  the  amendments,
the  1934  amendments,  to  the  Railway  Labor  Act  were
before  —  Commissioner  Eastman,  the  Federal
Coordinator  of Transportation,  appeared  before  the
Congressional  Commiftee.
He  had  récommended  this
legislation,  and  he  described  the  practices  of  com-
pany  unions—and  the  practices  on  the  railroads  are
no  different  from  what  they  are,  I  am  quite  sure,  in
your  case.

|

;

“Speaking  of  influence  inherent  in  certain

“You  must  realize  the  influence

which  a  company  is able  to exert  over  its

asking.  It is like  the power  of life  and death,
for  it  means  the  power  to  deprive  a man  of
the  very  means  of  subsistence.” The  in-
fluence  may  be  exerted  at  the time  when ‘a

ficers  or in other  ways  meeting  or helping  to_

CHAIRMAN  MADDEN:  Is  it  not  true,  Dr.  Leiser-
son,  that  for  those  matters  there  is  and  has been  for
hundreds  of years  plenty  of  law?

THE  WITNESS:  Yes,  there  is  plenty  of  local  regu-
lations.
Sometimes  the  charge  will  be  made  that  a
Jabor  organization  in  its  campaign  got  out  fraudulent
publicity  to  win: the  election.
Well,  we  have  seen  pub-
licity  that  President  Roosevelt  is  a  communist  and  that
he  is  following  a  communistic  policy.  Now,  that  sort
of  thing  happens  in  elections.
But  that  is  exercising
and  perhaps  abusing  the  freedom  of  people  to  try  to  get
votes  from  each  other,  and  insofar  as  it  may  be  libel
and  fraudulent  and  so  one,  there  are  plenty  of  laws  to
take  care  of  those  things.
That  is  not,  the  issue  in  labor
relations.

\

One  employee  can  not  have  any  power  over  an-
other  employee  to  make  him  vote_one  way  or  another,
except  if  he  has  management  authority  to  fire  him,  or
refuse  to  hire  him,  or  discriminate  against  him,  or  re-
fuse  to  promote  him.
And  therefore  it  is  perfectly
logical  for  Congress  or  for  a  legislature  when  it  acts
on  the  question  to  say,  “There  is  no  problem  here  as
between  the  employees,  because  that  is  taken  care  of  by
legislation  in  existence  and  by  police  regulations.”

The  problem  is  of  one  party  coercing  the  other
party  in  the  choice  of  its  representative,  and  that  is  all
that  Congress .deals  with.

Now,  we  have found  in  all  the  elections  that  I have
been  in,  no  problem,  as  among  the  employees,  that
could  not  be  handled  by  a  competent  mediator.
Every
once  in  a  while  outside  the  polling  places  there  would
be  some  pretty  hot  arguments,  and  the  mediator  will  go
out  and  say,  “You  had  better  quit  that,”  and  chase  them
away.
That  is about  all  the  problem  there  is  on  that,
and  there  isn’t  anything  for  Congress  to  do  or  to  deal
with  in  that  regard.
BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  In  general,  with  respect  to  the  activities  of  em-

543

ployees  in  their  relations  with  each  other,  in  your  ex-
perience,  is  it  necessary  that  there  be  Federal  legislation
to  prohibit  employees  from  exescising  so-called  im-
proper  influence  or  coercion  over  their  co-employees?
A  Not  at  all.  The  question  as  it sometimes  comes
up,  the  employers  sometimes  put  up  the  argument  in
this  way:  when  there  is  a  dispute  between  two  or  more
who  want  to  represent  the  employees,  then  the  employ-
ees  will  go  to  each  other’s  homes  to  induce  them  to  vote
for  this  organization  or  for  that  organization.
The  em-
ployer  feels  that  he  should  have  the  right  to  send  his
superintendent  or  his  agent  to  go  to  the  house  of  the
employees  in  the  same  way.

But  there  is  all  the  difference  in  the  world  when
equals,  who  have  a right  to  vote  in  the  election,  go  to
each  other’s  homes  to  argue  and  convert  each  other,
than  when  a  person  or  a  company,  that  has  no  right  to
choose  these  reprensentatives,  sends  one  of  its  agents  to
presumably  only  argue  with  them.
But  an  argument
with  your  boss  is  a  great  deal  more  than  an  argument,
and  that  is  what  the  law  needs  to  deal  with,  that  prob-
lem.

The  other  is  no  problem.

I  can  say  from  our  ex-
perience  that  there  has  never  been  a  problem  of  one
set  of  employees  intimidating  another  set  of  employees.
There  have  been  charges  that  they  say  false  things  about
each  other  and  about:  each  other’s  candidates  and  all
that  sort  of  thing,  but  that  is  common.

Q  Now,  Dr.  Leiserson,  with  respect  to  the  bitter-
ness  and  unrest  which  you  have  described  among  em-
ployees  when  employers  commit  certain  acts  which  you
have  mentioned,  does  that  bitterness  and  unrest  some-
times  result  in  strikes?

A  Most  strikes  that  come  for  organization  pur-

‘poses  happen  as  a  result  of  that,  in  this  way:

When  an  organization  is  well  established  and  has
relationships  with  the  employer,  they  hesitate  very  much
about  calling  a  strike,  because  it  is  likely  to  upset  all
the  relations  and  the  agreements  they  have.

ee

Moreover,  they  know  it  costs  money  to:  support
the  strike.  And  so  we  will  find  practically  every  na-
tional  organization  prohibits  a  local  organization  from
calling  a  strike  by  itself.
It  must  get  the  approval  of
the  national  organization,  and  the  constitutions  of  the
unions  provide  that  they must  first  exhaust  their  efforts
with  the  employers,  through  committee  and  so  on,  and
then  if  they  can  not  get  any  further,  they  must  inform
the  national  organization,  and  the  national  organization
sends  a  national  officer  to  negotiate  for  it.  Then,  as
the  very  last  resort,  they  may  or  may  not  approve  a
strike.

But  when  the  issue  is  whether  or  not  a  group  of
employees  who  have  no  organization  before  but  have
tried  to  have  an  organization,  and  the  employers  op-
posing  it,  then  the  usual  thing  is  for  the  leaders  to  be
discharged,  or  the  committee  that  came  to  see  the  em-
ployer  to  be  discharged  or  discriminated  against.

Very  often  very  many  of  the  strikes  we  have  had  in
the  last  few  years  are  because  the  whole  plant  went  out
to  support  those  few—often  thoughtlessly  gone  out  be-
cause  they  have  not  thought  how  they  were  going  to  be
supported  on  strike,  and  how  they  were  going  to  live.

But  that  illustrates  the  bitter  feeling  that  they  have
and
the  resentment  they  have  against  discrimination
against  the  leaders  or  active  members,  because  they  be-
come  so  enraged  and  so  fearful  of  their  own  job  that  a
whole  plant  will  walk  out  to  support  these  few.

Very  many  of  the  strikes—I  can  not  give  num-
bers—but  we  have  had  in  recent  years,  one  after  ap-
other,  have  happened  in  that  particular  way  that  I have
described.
Take  the  big  rubber  strike  that  just  hap-
pened—I  think  about  sixty  people  being  involved—and
they  could  not  somehow  settle
Then,
when  they  tried  to  get  rid  of  those  sixty,  the  whole  plant
went  out,  and the  city  was tied  up  with all sorts of law
and  order  problems.

that  dispute.

They  did  not  have  any  facilities  for  calling  a  strike,

545

no  national  organization  with  a  treasury to support
them  or  any  of  that,  but  they  felt  they  had  to  support
and  back  up  those  few  men  who  were  being  picked
on—and  that  has  been  the  typical  form  of  these  unor-
ganized  strikes.
Thus  may  I say  that  most  of the  strikes
that  have  happened  in  the  last  two  or  three  years  have
been  this  type  of  unorganized  strike,  rather  than  where
an  organization  deliberately,  after  all  efforts  are  ex-
hausted,  called  a  strike.

.

BY  MR,  WIRIN:
Q  Now,  in  your  experience  and  the  experience  of
the Boards  of which  you  were  a  member  and  are  a
member,  does  the  protection  of  law,  or  the  right  of
workers  to  be  free  from  these  practices’  by  employers
which  you  have  described—or,  to  put.  it  differently,
does  the  prohibition  by  law  upon. employers  to  commit
these  practices,  tend  to  eliminate  the  strikes  which  are
caused  by  the  practices?

A  Well,  there  is  no  question  about  that  at  all.
We  don’t  on  the  railroads,  with  the  exception  of  a
few  small,  very  small,  roads  in  the  South—the  problem
never  arises  of  a  man  being  discharged  for  union. ac-
tivity.
It  is  not  done.  There  are  no  such  disputes  on
practically  all  of  the  railroads.

However,  most  of  the  bitter  strikes  of  the  past,  the
violent  strikes  of  the  70’s,  80’s,  and  the  90’s,  were  about
those  questions.
If  we  have  a  threat  of  a  strike  now,  it
-  might  be on  a  big fundamental  question,  like  wages  and
hours,  and  we  usually  find  we  can  settle  those  by  arbi-
tration  or  otherwise.

We  have  had  threats  of  strikes  recently,  for  in-
where  certain  wages  that  most  of the railroads

stance,

546

had  restored,  one  or  two  of  the  smaller  fines  that  felt
they  did  not  have  any  money  did  not  want  to  restore  it,
and  we  had a threat  of strikes.
That  was even  possible
where  the  company  was  in  the  hands  of  receivers  that
we  could  settle  those  questions  without  strikes.

But  if  the  issues  involved  were  discrimination  or
discharge  of  men  because  they  had  the  organization,  or
the  question  would  be  the  right  of  the  organization  to  °
represent  them,  we  could  not  have  settled  those  strikes.

leans  case.
Let  me  quote  to  you  the  following  language
of  Justice  Hughes  from-that  opinion:

“Congress  was  not  required  to  ignore  this
right  of the  employees”—referring  to  the  right
to  be  free  from  interference  from  employers
and  the  right  to  collective  action—“but  could
safeguard  it  and  seek  to  make  their  appropriate
collective  action  an  instrument  of  peace  rather
than  of  strife.”

.

A  Is  that  quotation  that  I.  had  in  mind  before

when I referred  to  Justice  Hughes.

;

Q  Yes.  Now,  Dr.  Leiserson,  in  terms  of  the  actual
experience  upon  the  railways  since  1926,  and  since  the
decision  by  Justice  Hughes,  has  the  actual  experience
demonstrated  that  the  protection  of  this  right  by  Con-
gressional  action  in  the  1926  Railway  Labor  Act  made
collective  action  on  the  railways  av  instrument  of  peace
rather  than  strife?

A  There  is  no  question  about  that,  and  the  car-

riers as well  as  employees  would  so  testify.

When I go  out  to  mediate  disputes  and  we  get  on
that  subject,  the  conversation  would  invariably  turn  on
that  question.
In  the  old  days  we  would  not  answer  a
letter  that  came  from  a  union.  Now  we  have  learned
to  see  that  these  men  are  just  about  like  other  men.
They  try to  represent  the  interests  of  the  employees,  and
we  get along  with  them  on  a  businesslike  basis.

Jo

There  is  no  question  that  the  experience  since  1926
and  since  the  amended  Act  of  1934  bears  out  the  pur-
pose  that  Congress  had  in  mind  in  maintaining  peace
arough  these  methods  and  promoting  good  will,  I
should  say.

Q  Now  the  language  of  Justice  Hughes  in  the  Texas
and  New  Orleans  case  referred,  of  course,  to  the  rail-
roads?

A  Yes.
Q  Is  there  anything  in  your  experience  which
would  bring  you  to  the  opinion  that  the  experience  of
industries  that  are  in  commerce  or  whose  activities  sub-
stantially  affect  cammerce  would  be  different  in  so  far
as  the  recognition  of  this  right  by  workers  by  law?

A  No; I think  the  experience  in  the railroad  in-
dustry  is  just  typical  of  the  experience  throughout  in-
dustry  where  collective  bargaining  was  developer,  and
you  want  to  know  there  are  very  many  industries  that
have  had  that  experience.

Professor  Berman  mentioned  some  of  those  in-
dustries.  We  have  had  them  in  the  coal,  we  have  had
them  in  the  clothing,  we  have  had  them  in  the  foundry
industry,  we.  have  had  them  in  the  printing  industry,  and
we  have  had  them  for  years  in  the  pottery  industry—-in
ene  industry  after  another.
As  soon  as  the  question  of
the  employees’  fundamental]  right  as  American  citizens
is  out  of  the  way,  from  that  time  on  the  peaceful  rela-
tions  develop  and  the  questions  in  dispute  begin  to  be
settled  in  a  businesslike  way,  as  business  men  settle
their  own  disputes  among  themselves.

There  is the  right  of  working  men  to  be  considered

busines  men  in  their  own  affairs.

|

As  soon  as  the  employer  recognizes  that  they  have
the  right  to  be  considered  business  men  in  their  own  af-
fairs,  and  won’t  just  take  arbitrary  orders  for  a  fore-
man  or  manager,  from  that  time  on  you  establish  over
a  large  part  of  the  field  of  labor  relations  peace  and
good  will,  and  you  eliminate  the  cause  of  strife.

ernment  intervention  in  labor disputes,  I take  you  con-
cur  in  the  findings  as  set  forth  in  that  quotation?

A  Well,  the  government  has  always  intervened  in
I don’t  mean  by that  that  they  have  in-
But  in  labor  disputes  from

labor  disputes.
tervened  in  every  dispute.
the  very  beginning,  the  government  has  had  a part.

I quoted  yesterday  from  baek  in  1802.  The  govern-
ment  came.  in  there  as  a  prosecutor  for  conspiracy,  and
we  saw  apparently  in  the  40’s  that  the  government  was

courts.

ees

Now,  some  people  don’t  think  it  is  government  in-
tervention  until  Congress  or  a  state  legislature  passes  a
law.  As  a  matter  of  fact,  early  in  the  history  of  this
country,  as  early  as  the  first  decade  of  the  nineteenth
century,  states  had  to  pass  laws  and some  of  them  did,
as  to  whether  these  labor  disputes  were  conspiracies  or
not.  That  is  government  intervention.  You  can  not
avoid  it  and  it  is  too  fundamental  a  question  for  the
government  not  to  be  in  it.

Now,  when  the  government  intervenes  in  the  sense
of  Congress  or  a  Board  coming  in,  you  call  that  gov-
ernment  intervention.
But  when,  as  in  the  early  days,
or  prior  to  this,  these  disputes  became  things  for  police
authorities  or  militia  or  judges  to  handle  under  common
law,  that  is  not  government  intervention.
If  you  want
to  say  that,  why  then  you  are  just  confused  about  the
issue.

The  government  has  always  had  to  intervene  in
these,  because  they  are  important  fundamental  economic
and  social  problems.  And  the  time  has  come  when

550

Congress  sees  that  you  can  not  let  this  go  by different
judges,  differently  interpreting  common  law  on  the
thing,  when  the  common  law  did  not  develop  on  the
basis  of  modern  labor  relations.
And  so statutes  are  now  adopted  to  deal  with  them
in  an  intelligent  twentieth  century  way,  and  there  is  no
question  of the  need  for  intervention.

However,  this  is  true.

If  the  government  could  do
its  real  job,  guarantee  the  right  of  wage  earners  as
American  citizens  to  associate  themselves  and  keep  em-
ployers:  who  have  power  over  these  employees  from
trespassing  on  the  right—to  the  extent  that  the  govern-
ment  can  accomplish  that  purpose,  it.  will  make  less
necessary  direct  government  intervention  in  handling  of
terms  of employment,  such  as  hours  and  wages  and  other
questions.  Why?
if wages  are  too  low,  as  they  are  in
these  sweater  industries  where  people  are  not  organized
and  can  not  get  the  value  of  their  labor  through  col-
lective  bargaining,  then  you  have  demands  for  mini-
mum  wage  laws.  |
If  they  can  not  get  decent  hours  of  labor  by  asso-
clated  action,  then  you  have  the  demand  and  the  fixing
by government,  and  here  you  have  the  situation  that'I
mentioned  yesterday.  There  will  be  a  demand—and
there  is  a  bill  in  Congress  now—to  set  up  a dismissal
wage,  when  railroads  are  consolidated.
But  here you  have  an  industry  where  both  parties
are  anxious  to  avoid  that  legislation  and  to try  to  settle
it by an  agreement  through  collective  bargaining.
Now,  government  intervention  has  a  great  many
disadvantages  when  a legislature  or  a  state  tries  to  deal
with  the  details  of  the  terms  of  employment  and  con-
ditions  of  employment  in  industry:
It  is  desirable  to
avoid  that  kind  of action  on  the  part  of  the  government
as  much  as  possible.  The  best  way  to  avoid  it  is  to
equalize  the  bargaining  power  of  ‘employer  and  em-
ployee  and  heve  them  have  respect  for  each  other  as
equals.  Then,  when  you  do  that,  through  collective  bar-

.

gaining,  you  can  avoid  government  intervention  by
statute  fixing  terms  of  employment.

Where  people  are  not  permitted  or  are  unable  to
organize,  you  have  more  and  more  demand  for  inter-
vention  by the  government  by direct  statute.

|

;

the  bill.  -

Q  Now,  just  one  more  question,  Doctor.

In  your
experiences,  as  mediator,  for  instance,  have  you  found
commerce  affected  in  any  way  in  industrial  disputes?
A  Of  course,  on  the  railroads  it  is  all  affected.
Q  Other  than  the  railroads?
A  Well,  we  had  the  question  up,  both  in  the  petro-
leum  industry  and  when  I  was  with  the  original  Na-
tional  Labor  oard.  Many  employers  would  claim  they
were  not  engaged  in  interstafe  commerce.
Of  course,
that  is a  question  of  fact  in  the  case.

But  it always  seemed  to  me  that  the  argument  that
was  made  in many  of the  cases,  that  the  process  of man-
ufacture  is not  commerce,  does  not  have  anything  to  do
with  the  case,  Of  course,  if you  looked  upon  manufac-
turing  as a process  alone,  it has to be  done  in  one  place,
and  it is done within  a  state  or a  locality.

As  I understand,  the  Supreme  Court  has  ruled  man-
is not commerce.  Maybe  I  am wrong  about

ufacturing
it.

MR.  CARMODY:  That  is  not  quite  true,-Doctor,  is
it?  Is  it  not  an  actual  fact  that  automobiles,  for  in-
stance,  could not be put on the market  if  it  were  not for

uously?

THE  WITNESS:  Well,

of course  where  the  manu-
facturing  in order  to carry  on the process,  has to  cross
the  state  lines,  it is obvious.

But the point  I  want  to make  it  this.  In

labor  relations  we  find  ourselves  in  this  situation.  For
example, we had a case in Hammond,  Indiana,
or East
Chicago,  Illinois,  and  when  we  went  to deal  with  the em-
ployer  in  that. manufacturing  or  refining  company,  we
had to  go to  a  man in New York.  That is where  the em-
ployer  was.

Now,  it seems  to  me  that  the  relations  between  em-
ployer  and  employee,  or  the  relationship  between  them,
has to  be inquired  into these  things  as to  whether  it is
interstate  commerce  or  not.  True  the  Clayton  Act  says
that  labor  is not  an  article  of commerce,  and  it is not.

But  let  me  cite  an  actual  example.  There  was a bit-
ter strike  at  New  Albany,  Indiana,
across  from  Louis-
ville,  a  shirt  factory,  so  bitter  that  the  Governor  of  In-
diana  sent  the  Militia  there,  The  company,  of  course,
claimed  that  it was  just  a  manufacturing  company  with-
in the state,  and I don’t recall that this Board took any
jurisdiction  over  it.

_  Finally  the  mayor  of  the  town  got  the  Militia  out,
and  he  offered  an  arbitration  arrangement  fo  the  em-
ployer  and  to  the  employees.  The  employees  accepted
his  proposal  to  arbitrate  and  the  arrangement  they
would  make.

The  manager  of  the  plant  that  was  claiming  he  was
engaged  in  intrastate  manufacturing  in  Indiana,  said  “I
can  not  tell  you  whether  we  can  accept  this.
I have  to
send  it to  the  headquarters  of  the  company  in  New York
and they will  tell us whether we can enter  into this ar-
bitration  arrangement.”

Well,  now,  in  my  experience  as - an  arbitrator  I
would  not fuss with  this manager  in  New  Albany.
I
would  say,  “Now,  who  has  final  authority  to settle  this?”
I have  had that  happen  again and again in such
cases.  I once had to settle  a  big gasoline  station  strike
in Cleveland.  We didn’t  go to Cleveland  to settle this
at all. I went to New  York  to meet the president of the

¥ gompany,  where  their  headquarters  were.

od

Now,  when  you  have  the  employer, who  has  final
authority  to  enter  into  an  agreement,  with  headquarters
in one  state,  and  the  plant  over  in  this  other  state,  even
though  that  plant  does  nothing  but  manufacture  right
within  the  state—and,  of  course,  if  it  had  to  run  across
state  lines  to  manufacture  it  is clear  that  it is  interstate
commerce—but  assume,for  the  moment,  as  in  New  Al-
bany,  all  they  were  doing  was  making  shirts  right  there.
Even  if  the  shirts  were  sold  only  in  Indiana,  which  is
not  true,  but  even  if it were  true;  but  with  the  employer
in  New  York,  he  is  the  one  that  fixes  the  wages
Whatever  the  law
and  hours  and  everything  else.
may  be  on  that,  the  fact  in  industrial  relations  and  eco-
nomics  is  that  you  have  a  situation  that  manufacturing
can  not  be carried  on  except  by communication  and  com-
merce  between  the  headquarters  of  the  company  in  New
York  and  the  little  shirt  factory  in  New  Albany,  Indiana.
Now,  the  argument  goes  on  as  if  there  were  an  em-
ployer  in  Indiana  or  New  Albany;  but  there  is  not  one.
If  there  were  one,  then  that  is  intrastate  commerce.  But
when  you  have  the  employer  with  headquarters,  we  will
say,  in  Pittsburgh,  and  plants  in  West  Virginia  and  Ohio

This  Board  is  a  National  Labor  Relations  Board.
Our  Board  is  a  labor  relations  Board.  What  do  I mean
“labor  relation?”  . The  relation  between  the  employer
and  the  employee.

.

Who  is the  employer?  The  one  that  was  final  au-
thority  to set  wages  and  hire  and  fire,  and  set  the  policy
‘  or the terms  of employment.  Those  are  the  relations

with  which  we  are  dealing.

556,

In most  of  these  large  plants  the  personnel  manager
who  carries  out  the  corporation  policy  is  not  at  the
places  where  the  manufacturing  is going  on,  in  the  sense
of  the  detailed  making  of  things.  He  is  at  some  central
office  determining  the  policy,  issuing  the  orders,  and
whenever  a  labor  relations  problem  comes  up  within
one  of  these  plants  in  a  locality,  one  of  two  things  has
to  happen.  Either  we  have  to  go  there  across  the  state
line  to  get  it settled,  or  the  personnel  manager  has  to  go
over  there.

~  Let  me  give  you  an  example.  One  of  the  first  com-
pany  union  plan  was  the  International  Harvester  Com-
pany.  Now,  the  International.Harvest  Company  had  its
office—or  it  had  when  I looked  into  this  thing,  and  I
was  quite  familiar  with  it  in  the  earlier  days—on  Mich-
igan  Avenue,  in  Chicago.  They  set  up  a  representation  ~
plan  that  was  worked  in  the  Michigan’  Avenue  Office.  It
was  the  taken  by  a  director  of  personnel  or  industrial
relations,  who  handles  labor  relations  for  the  manage-
ment.  It was  taken  to  one  plant  after  another  of  the  In-
ternational  Harvester  Company—some  in  Illinois,  some
in  New  York,  one  down  in  New  Orleans,  one  in  Canada
—and  they  asked  the  employees  to  vote  on  it.  Most  of
them  voted  on  it  and  accepted  the  plan  and  set  up  rep-
resentatives.

Then  the  representatives  from  all  these  individual
plants  scattered all over  the  country  set  up  what  they
call  an  industrial  council  of  the  fifteen  plants,  we  will
say  there  were.

Now,  the  personnel  manager or  director  of  indus-
trial  relations  of  the  International  Harvester  Company
would  travel  from  week  to week  to  the  meetings  of  these
representatives  at  the  various  plants.

;

When  wages  had  to  be  fixed,  they  were  fixed  on
Michigan  Avenue  for  the  twine  plant  down  in  Louisiana,
and  when  wages  were  to  go  up  or  if  they  were  to go
down,  ‘and  whether  a  policy  as  to  promotion,  training,
and  anything  else,  or  what  terms  of  employment  were
to  be  fixed,  that  was all done  in  Chicago.

If  you  look  upon  making  twine  in  New  Orleans  as
@  process,  why  that  is  done  in  the  State  of  Louisiana.

pany  making  twine  in  Louisiana,  it  is  the  International
Harvester  Company  in  Chicago  making  twine  in  Louis-
iana,  and  it can  not  make.  it any  realistic  way  except  as
commerce  among  the  states.
It cannot  operate  its  labor
relations  except  as  commerce  among  all  the  states  in
which  these  fifteen  plants  or  the  foreign  country  is  lo-
cated.

That  is  why  I think  it is a  mistake  to  talk  about  the
process.  The  process  is mechanital  and  not  human.  La-
bor relations  are  relations  between  human  beings.  Why
even  in  you  lawsuits,  is one  man  is  a  citizen  of  one  state
and  another  man  is a  citizen  of another  state,  you  recog-
nize  that  is  a  Federal  question  and  then  you  can  refer

Well,  when  you  have  got  a  dispute,  we  as  a  media-
tor  or  a  labor  relations  man,  it  is  the  same  situation  as
with  the  judges  in  the  courts.  You  have  got  a  dispute
with  employees  in  one  state  and  employers  in  another.
The  relation  is  among  them.  You  cannot  settle  those
disputes  by remaining  in one  state.
It is a  Federal  ques-
tion  in  the  same  way  as  you have  it in  transferring  cases
on  account  of  diversity  of citizenship  to  a  Federal  court.

THE  WITNESS:  That,  of  course,  is  a  very  difficult

Too  many  factors  enter  into  the

iency.
All  of  the  irregularities
of the  methods  of  handling  labor  relations  cause  a  great
deal  of  disturbance,  and  they  also  cause  soldiering  and
laying  down  on  the  job  when  their  grievances  are  not
properly  handled.
I  am  familiar  with  that  because  |
was  connected  with  an  investigation  that  was  made
about  restriction  of  output  in  unorganized  plants,  in
plants  where  there  were  no  unions. We  found  uni-
versally—and I may  say  that  the  report  was  published
under  that  title,  “Restriction  of  output  in  unorganized
plants,”  under  the  authorship  of Stanley  B.  Matthewson,
published  by  the  Viking  Press  a  few  years  ago.

Now,  it  was  found  there  that  when  the  employees
were  unorganized  and  dissatisfied,  their  grievance  is  not
taken  care  of, that  they  soldiered  on  the  job.  They  fixed
the  limit  beyond  which  they  would  not  produce.  There
were  some  rough  estimates  attempted  in  the  book  as  to
how  much  in  the  way  of  losses  that  made  to  the  industry
in  increased  costs.

Now,  when  you  have  collective  bargaining,  that  sort
of  individual  guerilla  warfare  on  the  part  of  the  em-
ployees—who  are  helpless  in  protecting  themselves  in
any  other  way  except  by  laying  down  on  the  job—that
disappeared  and  it comes  out  in  the  oper.
For  example,
in  the  clothing  industry,  standards  of  production  are  set,
indicating  if  a  man  is  to  get  the  standard  union  wage
he  has  got  to  produce  for  it,  and  when  he  does  not,  he  is
disciplined  by the  organization.  Now  that  is  the  second
part.

|
When  you  have  employees  in  an  association,  you
have  through  their  officers  a  responsibility  of  all  of  the
employees  for  meeting  their  obligations  under  the  agree-
ment,  and  you  get  a  certain  esprit  de  corps  which  is  ap-
pealed  to  by  an  sfficer  when  the  man  is  not  doing  the
work  he  ought to do.  And  in  the  sense  of  that  kind  of  |

?

1

Similarly,  in  conclusion  on  that  question,  the  T.  V.
A.,  the  Tennessee  Valley  Authority,  found  itself  with  a
big problem  of  hiring  great  numbers  of  employees,  and
with  the  problem  of  how  to  manage  them.  It, of  course,
guaranteed  the  right  to  these  people  to  organize;  but  it
felt  that  just  leaving  it  that  way  was  not  enough,  So  it
made  a  study  of  the  best  management  of  labor  relations
that  it knew  about.
It called  in experts  to  find  out  about
it,  an  then  announced  and  printed  a  Tennessee  Valley
Authority  labor  policy,  which  not  only  sets.down  the
actual  right  that  is  in  your  law  and  in  our  law,  but  goes
on  from  there  as  to  what  is  intended  in  a  concrete  way.
And  so,  for  instance,  when  it  needed  to  revise  its
wage  schedules  and  so  on,  it called  in  the representative
of  the  employees  and  they  have  had  some  very  fine
meetings,  the  president  or  the  chairman  of  the  T.  V.  A.
told  me.

In  some  cases  the  men  agreed  that  certain  péople
were  overpaid  as  compared  to  others,  and  their  wages
should  be  reduced.  In  other  cases  there  were  inequalities
that  could  be  fixed  by  raising  upward.  And  there  has
been  a  development  of  cooperation  and-  confidence  as
the  result  of  that  policy—or  at  least  the  beginning  of
that—where  the  management  and  the  men,  both  through
representatives,  are  cooperating  to  further  the  purposes
of the  T. V. A. in  a way  that  when  the  management  alone
does  it, without  that  cooperative  help,  it  could  not  pos-
sibly  accomplish  it  because  there  is  always  the  balking
of  the  employees.

Here  you  have  got  the  will  to  work  and  the  will  to
-  cooperate  of the  employees  harnessed  to  the  same  will  of
the  management,  and  they  start  right  in  at  the  beginning
in  their  agreement,  in  that  policy.  An  obligation  rests
on  the  employees  as  well  as  on  the  management  to  fur-
ther  the  purposes  of  the  T.  V.  A.,  and  both  are  held  re-
sponsible  through  their  respective  organizations  and  rep-
resentatives.

government-created  entity  that  we  call  a  corporation.

Now,”  in

ager  who  is  trying  to  hire  him?

From  an  economic  point  of  view  the  economists

have  analyzed  it  this  way:

That  is  why  no  economist  of  any  reputation  holds
that  there  is  equality  of  bargaining  power  between  in-
dividual  employee  and  a  modern  employer  such  as  a

Even  though  economists  that  hold  that  wages  are
determined  by the  value  of  the  product  that  the  margin
of  the  last  hired  laborers—or  the  last  to  be  hired—or
the  least  necessary  labor  is  contributed—even  they  will

nomic  Theory”,  by John  Bates  Clark,  who  is the  leading
economist  of  the  orthodox  school.
He  said  that  his
theory  can  not  work  out  unless  there  is  collective  bar-
gaining;  that  while  the  employer  might  be  able  to  pay

then  they
could  get  what  they  produce.

ining,

In  effect  that  means  that  when  employees  bargain
individually—that  is,  most  employees,  although  I should
not  say  employees  who  have  a  special  kind  of  profes-
sional  skill  and  who  are  in  great  demand—but  labor,
-  manual  and  clerical  workers,  when  those  people  bargain
individually,  they  tend  invariably  to  get  less  than  they
produce.  And  only  when  they  bargain  collectively  can
they  get  what  they  produce  under  any  scheme  that  the
most  conservative  economists  consider  what  eed pro-
duce.

Now,  that  same  fact  is  recognized  generally  by the
public  and  it  has  been  recognized  by  the  courts;  and  I
-  want  to  léave  that  with  you.

562

For instance,  in a case  in  which  the  state  legislature
of Utah—I  think  it was—tried  to pass  an  eight-hour  law
with  respect  to mines  and  smelters,  because  the  people
were  not  able  to get it by collective  bargaining,  the  court
in upholding  the  law,  the  United  States  Supreme  Court,
said  this:
“The  legislature  has  also  recognized  the  fact”—and
I would  underline  that  word  “fact”;  it is not  underlined
in  this—“which  experience  of legisla  ures  in  many  states
-  has  corroborated,  that  the  proprietors  of  these  establish-
'  ments”—and  that  is  the  employers—“and  their  opera-
tives  do  not  stand  upon  an  equality,  and  that  their  in-
terests  are  to  be a  certain  extent  conflicting.  The  for-
mer”—that  is the  employers—“naturally  desire  to  obtain
as.much  labor  as  possible  from  their  employees,  while
the  latter  are  often  induced  by fear  of  discharge.”
The  Court  itself  knows  that  the  fear  of  discharge  is
an  important  element  in  this  situation.  To  continue—
“To  conform  to  regulations  which  their  judgment,
fairly  exercised,  would  pronounce.  to  be  detrimental  to
their  health  and  strength.  In  other  words,  the  proprie-
tors  lay down  the  rules,  and  the  laborers  are  practically
constrained  to  obey  them.
In  such  instances,  self-in-
terest  is often  an  unsafe  guard,  and  the  legislature  may
properly  interpose  its  authority.”
Here  you  have  a  statement  of  the fact  that  econom-
ists have  recognized  and  the  public  has  generally  recog-
nized  it  and  the  courts  and  legislatures  have  general
recognized  that  it is true,  that  the  employers,  if they  are
dealing  with  individuals,  lay  down  the  rules  and  the  in-
dividuals  have  to  accept  them.
Whereas,  if they  could  exercise  fai?  judgment,  they
would  not  accept  them.  How  can  they  exercise  fair
judgment?  By collective  bargaining.
An  employer  makes  a  proposition  to  an  individual
employee.  The  employee  says,  “Wait  and  I will  have  to
take  that up  with  my  experts,  the  officials  of my  organ-
ization,  who  know a great  deal  more  about  this  than  I

vd

|

|

2

do  whether  it  is  a  thing  I ought  to  accept  or  ought  not
accept,”—just  the  same  as  when  an  employee  asks  for
something..  When  an  employee  asks for  something,  the
foreman  does  not  ordinarily  decide  it:  He  says,  “TI  will
have  to  take  it up  with  the  personnel  manager.”
;
If you  have  individual  bargaining,  the employer  can
not  exercise  his  judgment.  If he  has  collective  bargain-
ing,  the  employees  can  get  an  expert  and  get advice and
counsel  and  they  get  it that  way.

hire  an  attorney.  You  attorneys  charge  too  much.  But  -
if he  can  chip  in a little’  money  through  an  organization,
he  can  get  a  good  lawyer.

recognize  something  that  we  on  these  Boards  always
find.

And  the  court  says:

Now,  it is very  interesting  that  Chief  Justice  Hughes
had  to  make  the  same  kind  of  remark  in  the  Texas  and
New  Orleans  case  where  he  had  to  say  that  the  com-
pany,  which  was  furthering  the  company  union  in  that
case,  complained  because  their  motives  in  sponsoring
the  company  union  were  questioned.

Chief  Justice  Hughes  had  to  say  that  they  can  not
complain—I  am  not  quoting  the  exact  language,  but  you
will  find  it—that  they  can  not  complain,  when  their  mo-
tives  are  questioned,  when  they  are  promoting  an  or-
ganization—althought  they  say  it  is  for  the  benefit  of
their  employees—when  they  are  promoting  it  in  opposi-
tion  to  the  employees’  own  efforts  to  select  their  own
organization  and  their  own  representatives.

Again  and  again  the  issue  comes  up  in  that  way,
that  the  employer  says,  “I  know  what  is  better  for  the
employees  and  I am  trying  to  protect  their  rights.”

The employee,  if he is to be an American  citizen,
must  be presumed  to  know  what  is best  for  himself,  and
when  he  wants  to  associate  and  run  his  own  organiza-
tion  and  choose  his  own  representatives,  he  is  entitled
to it.
CHAIRMAN  MADDEN:  Thank  you  very much,  Dr.

Leiserson.

(Witness  excused.)

,  ABRAHAM  BERGLUND

was  produced  as  a  witness  for  and  on  behalf  of  the  Na-

the  reporter?

ginia?

A  Abraham  Berglund.

A  In  Charlottesville,  Virginia.
Q  You  are  connected  with  the  University  of  Vir-
}

A  With  the  University  of  Virginia,  yes.
Q  What  is  your  position?
A  Professor  of  Business  Economics.  The  position

of  full  professor  I have  held  since  1926.

Q  While  at  the  University  have  you  had  occasion

to  study  the  economics  of  the  iron  and  steel  industry?

A  Yes,  I have.  I had  a  year’s  leave  of  absence  and
worked  at  the  Brookings  Institution  in  the  academic
year  1926  to  1927,  and  my  particular  work  was  the  tariff
on  iron  and  steel.

Then  I have  written  articles  on  the  price  policy  of
the  Steel  Corporation  and  the  policy  with  reference  to
industrial  stabilization.
These  articles  have  been  pub-
lished  in  the  Quarterly  Journal  of  Economics.  That  is
a  Harvard  publication.

of  Chicago  and  a  Doctor  of  Philosophy.  from  Columbia.

we  come  to  define  steel,  you  are  up  against  a  proBlem.

(Testifying  from  paper.)

Steel  is  a  form  of  iron,  differing  from  other  forms
like  ordinary  cast  iron  and  wrought  iron,  both  phys-
Ordinarily  it  is  less  brittle  and
ically  and  chemically.
tougher  than  cast  iron,  and  has  greater  elasticity  and
hardness  than  wrought  iron.

The  carbon  content  ranges  from  about  zero  to  as
high  as  22  per  cent.  Zero  is where  it has  practically  no
carbon  content.

With  a  small  carbon  content,  roughly  from  0.1  per
cent  to 0.3  per.  cent  or  0.4  per  cent,  that  constitutes  so-
called  soft  steel.

Much  or  most  of  the  large  tonnage  of  steel  is  what

.

is called  soft  steel.

Much  of  this  steel  is  manufactured  into  plates,

sheets,  wire  products  and  the  like.

High  carbon  or hard  steel  ranging  from  0.7  per
cent  to 2.2 per cent carbon  is largely  used  in the  manu-
facture  of high priced  cutlery,  watch  springs  and like
constitutes  for the most
articles.  Such  manufacture
part a  different  industry  from  the industry  engaged  in
by, we will. say,  the United  States  Steel Corporation.
MR.  CARMODY:  Crucible  steel,  is  it  not?
THE  WITNESS:  It is often  called  crucible  steel,

571

but today  most crucible  steel  is not made  in the
ible  furnaces  but  in  electric  furnaces.

,

(Continuing  with  statement.)
In addition  to  variation  in carbon  content,  steels  or-
dinarily  contain  at  least  a  small  amount of alloy  metal.
if the per cent of  alloy is large enough  to give  it  cer-
tain  characteristics  suitable  for  special  uses,  as  tungs-
ten in the  production  of  high  speed  steel,  it  is known
as  an  alloy  steel.

MR.  CARMODY:  When  you  say  “high  speed,”  ‘you
mean  steel  used  to  make  tools  that  are  operated  at  high
speed?

THE  WITNESS:  Yes.
Thus  we  have  chromium  steel,  vanadium  steel,

It  is known  as  alloy  steel.

manganese  steel  and  the  like.

The  great  bulk  of  the  world’s  steel  output  consists
of  ordinary  carbon  steel  with  low  or  medium  carbon
content  and  limited  amounts  of  other  ingredients  like
manganese.  Now,  ordinary  tonnage  steel  will  contain
roughly  one  half  of  one  per  cent  of  manganese.  Man-
ganese  is  important,  but  only  a  very  small  amount  ap-
pears in ordinary steel.  This  is the steel which  is the
cheap  or  “tonnage”  steel,  with  which  we  are  all  famil-
jer in the form of rails, structural  shapes,  steel wire,
ship and other  structural  plates,  and the like.
It is the
steel  produced  by  our  great  steel  corporations  and  in

Steel  production  for  the  most  part,  is  an  indus

furnishing  materials  utilized  by  other  producers.
That
is,  rails  are  sold  to  railroads;  structural  shapes  and

ere;  tin  plate  to  canning  companies  and  similar  con-

The  nianufacture  of  tonnage  steel  is  large-scale;
that  is, the producing  process  is most  economical  when
ormed  by  units  whose  individual  output  is  large.
For example,  the  most  economical  blast  furnace  is  one
whose  output  is about  1000  tons  every  24 hours  and re-
quires  an investment  of some  three  or four  million  dol-
lors.

Now,  steel  manufacture  is a  succession  of  processes,
but  all  under  one  great  control.
The  successive  stages
representing  these  processes  have  become  one  vast  uni-
fied system  where  each  step is part of one great machine
operation—that
is, from  the mining  of the ore in Minn-
esota
and Michigan  to the rolling of hot  steel  into rails,

vania,  and  a  very  small  percentage  from  Ohio.
had  been  the  second  most  important  source  of  iron  ore.

from  the  Upper  Peninsula  of Lake  Michigan.

cago  and  Gary,  Indiana.

‘to  various  blast  furnaces  on  the  southern  shore  of  Lake

as Japan.  If you  consult  the  trade  figures of

Japan,  you  will  find  that  Japan  has  a  very  heavy  im-

port  trade  in coal  and  a  very  heavy  export  trade  in
So  the  question  would  naturally  be  raised  as  to

why Japan has  the  heavy  trade  figures  in  coal,  both  ex-

ous  furnace  operations;  but  she  exports  a  low  grade

which  is  used  in  various  furnace  operations  which  the

A  Yes,  sir
Q  Now,  you  spoke  this  morning  with  reference
to  the  integration  of  leading  steel  companies.
Would
you  describe  more  specifically  what  that  integration
consists  of?

A  If  you  take  certain  figures  with  reference  to
output,  with  reference  ta  capacity,  with  reference  to
capitalization,  you  will  find  that  under  a  very  limited
number  of  concerns  you  have  the  greater  part  of  the
industry  controlled.

_Now,  here  are  some—well,  I  had  better  begin  first

with  the  tendency  toward  large-scale  production.

_  Now,  if  you  take  blast  furnaces,  you  will  find  that
in  1899,  the  number  of  establishments  was  something  .
like  228;  by  1983,  they  had, declined  to  72,  Now  that,  =
of  course,  was  partly  caused  by  the  depression,  but  —
even if  you take 1929,  which  was the banner  of steel
production  in  the  United  States,  the  number  has  been _
cut  down  to  105,  and  that  means  that  each  establish- eS

‘

ment  is producing  a  great  deal  more  than  had  been  the
case  in  1899.

In  other  words,  production  is  being  more  and
more  centered  in certain  large  establishments.  That
is, you  are  having  much  bigger  blast  furnaces.

Q  Would  you  state  the  source  of  those  figures,

Professor?

A  Those  are  the  United  States  bi-annual  census
of  manufacturers,  1921,  and  the  census  of  manufac-
turers,  1983,  summary.

Now,  if  you  take  steel  works,  you  don’t  have  such
a  marked  decline  in  the  number  of  establishments,
but  you  do  have  a  rather  small  number  as  compared
with  the  enormous  increase  of  steel  output.

Now,  if  you  take  1899,  you  have  reported  445  steel
works  and  rolling  mills.  In  1929,  you  have  486,  al-
though  production  in  that  period  tripled  or  quad-
rupled,  but  just  a  slight  increase  in  the  number of es-
tablishments.  You  have  some  decline  in  the  years
from  1929  to  1933,  and  the  number  given  here  is  394.
Now,  with  reference  to  the  number  of  wage-carn-
ers,  in  the  case  of  blast  furnaces,  in  1899,  you  have  the
total  number  given  as  39,241,  and  in  1929,  the  number
had  declined  to  24,960,  although  the  output  had  been
enormously  increased.

Of  course,  that  was  due  to  various  labor  econom-

ies effected  in  blast  furnace  operations.

The  number  of  1933  is  only  12,098,  but  of  course
part  of that  decline  is  due  to  the  business  depression.
Now, if you take  wage  earners  in steel mills,  you
have  some  increase.  Now,  in  1899,  you  have  183,249
of the wage earners,  and  in  1929,  the  number  was  394,-
574  Thas  is  more  than  double  the  figure  for  1899.
jw,  there is some  decline  in the years following  1929.
Psa here  is  276,847.

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  With  respect  to  the  products  of  the  iron  and
steel  industry,  would  you  say  that  the  market  was  a
national  market  or  that  it  was  local  in  nature?

A  It  certainly  isn’t  local.

It  is  decidedly  nation-
al,  and  when  things  are  pretty  prosperous,  it  tends  to
be international.  —

Q  Would  you  describe  very  briefly

the

basis

point  system  in  this  connection?

‘A  Roughly  speaking,  when  speaking  about  the
basing  point  system,  you  have  a  price  policy  where
you  have  certain  centers  of  production,  being  the re-
gion  from  which  you  name  your  prices.  That  is,  now,
Pittsburgh  has  been  a sort of  a  base  for  many  steel  pro-
ducts.

Theoretically,  the  price  of  other  regions  will  be
the  Pittsburgh  price  plus  the  cost  of  transporting  the
steel  to  other  regious.
Now,  of  course,  that  leads  to,
if  you  take  such  a  corporation  as  a  steel  corporation,
that  means  if it should  sell  some  material  to  some  con-
sumer  in  Iowa,  or  Nebraska,  instead  of  having  the
product  manufactured  in  Pittsburgh,  they  would  have
it  perhaps  at  Chicago,  or  Gary,  or  some  other  western
mill,  but  instead  of  fixing  the  price  there  at  that  par-
ticular  mill,  and  then  simply  charging  the  cost  for
transportation  the  product  to  its  ultimate  destination,
they  make  a  charge  from  Pittsburgh  to  the  consump-
tion  point.  But  that  has  been  due  to  the  fact  that  in
the  development  of  the  steel  industry,  Pittsburgh  was
really  a  base  for  steel  production, and  in  other  regions
the  cost  of  production  would  be  materially  higher.
Today that is not the same, and that  is not  so true.

Does  the  basing-point  system  prevail  in  the

industyy

at  the  present  time?

A\  I  believe  the  Federal  Trade  Commission  has
issued  Orders against  it, or  at  least  they  have  ordered
a  modification  of  it,  so  I don’t  think  that  it  prevails
.
to  the  same  extent,  although  you  will  have  prices  ~

—

|

quoted  from  certain  points,  at  so  much  at  Pittsburgh,
and  so  much  at  Gary,  or  Chicago,  or  so  much  at  the
far-west.

'

The plants  are  dependent  upon  that...

DIRECT  EXAMINATION  |

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  Miss  Carr,  will  you  give  your  name  to  the

porter?

A  Charlotte  Carr.
Q  Miss  Carr,  you  testified  here  in  response  to

subpoena  issued  by  the  Board?

A  Yes.
Q  What  is  your  residence  at  the  present  time?
A  45  Park  Avenue,  New  York  City.
Q  You  were  at  one  time  connected  with  the

Pennsy!vania  State  Department  of  Labor?

A  Yes,  for  four  years,  between  1925  and  1929,  |
was  the  Director  of  the  Bureau  of  Women  and  Chil-
dren  in  the  Labor  Department,  and  I was  Deputy  Sec-
retary  of  Labor  from  that  point  until  January  of  1935,  _

Q  Did  your  duties  in  that  Department  bring  you
in  contact  with  labor  and  industrial  conditions  in  the
State  of  Pennsylvania?

A  It  did.
Q  And  you  are  familiar  with  conditions  in  the

iron  and  steel  industry  in  the  State  of  Pennsylvania.

A  Yes.
Q  Has  it  been  the  practice  in  the  State  of  Penn-
sylvania  for  certain  employers  to  employ  special  po-
lice,  commissioned  by  the  State,  known  as  coal  and
iron  police?

A  Yes,  that  has  been  a  practice  for  many  years,

in  Pennsylvania.

:

In  1931,  Governor  Pinchot  refused  to  give  com-
missions  to  the  coal  and  iron  police,  although  the  law
permitting  such  commissions  was  still  on  the  statute
books.

:

|

Q  Who  paid  these  coal and  iron  police?
A  The  coal  and iron  police  were  paid  by  the

steel  and coal owners.

Q  Bat they were commissioned
by the State Gov-

ernment?

A  ‘They  were  commissioned  by  the  State  Govern

ment.

;

in  1981?

Q  And  these  commissions,  you  say,  were  revoked
A  Yes.
Q  Now,  did  the  employers  still  obtain  men  for
that  purpose,  despite  the  revocation  of  the  commis-
sions?
A  With  the  revocation  of  these  commissions  it
was  found  that  the  sheriffs  of  the  various  counties,
parficularly  in  the  western  part  of  the  State,  in  the
coal  and  steel  areas,  would  deputize  deputy  sheriffs,
who  in  personnel  as  well  ds  in  responsibilities,  were
identical  with  the  people  who  had  been  previously  coal
and  iron  police.

organization  of  workers?

of  property,  and  the  prevention  of  picketing  in  times
of  strike.
Q  What  methods  did  they  employ  to  prevent  the
A  Well,  that  is  an  age-old  story,  in  the  history
of  the  struggle  for  organization  of  labor  in  the  steel
and  soft-coal  areas.
They  were,  to  all  intents  and
Purposes,  officers  of  the  law  in  uniform,  with  arms,
and ‘they  used  physical  force  to  see  that  gatherings
of individuals  were

di

Q  Was  the  commission  appointed  by  the  Gover-
nor  of  Pennsylvania  to  investigate  the  special  policing
in  the  industry  of  Pennsylvania?
A.  Yes,  in  February,  of  1934,  Governor  Pinchot
“ppointed  a  commission  to  study  this  question.

600

Q  Did  the  Department  of  Labor  cooperate  in  the

work  of  that  commission?

A  The  commission  had  no  funds,  and  it  was  ap-
pointed  without  salary,  or  without  appropriation,  and
the  Department  of  Labor  and  Industry  served  in  a
secretarial  capacity,  and  its  mediator  and  its  Bureau
of  Mediation  assisted  the  commission  - its  investiga-
tions.

Q  Would  you  state  briefly  the  manner  in  which

the  commission  made  the  investigation?

A  The  commission  concentrated  its  investigation
on  two  localities,  Fayette  County  and  Beawer  County.
The  reasons  for  the  concentration  of  its  investigation
in  these  two  points  were  that—if  I  refresh  my  mind
as  to  the  date—in  July  of  1933,  there  was  a  coal  strike
in  the  western  part  of  the  State,  and  in  Fayette  Coun-
ty  the  activities  of  the  deputy  sheriffs  to  all  intents
and  purposes,  the  old  coal  and  iron  police,  were  s0
definitely  against  the  civil  rights  of  the  people,  includ-
ing  their  rights  to  organize,  that  Governor  Pinchot
was  obliged  to  order  the  National  Guard  into  the  coun-
ty  to  preserve  order.

The  reason  that  Beaver  County  was  rw  for
special  investigation  by  the  commission  was  that  in
October,  in 1983,  at  the  time  of  the  steel  strike,  in  Am-
bridge,  Beaver  County,  the  sheriff  of  Beaver  County
had  recruited  about  150,  in  addition  to  the  regular  one
hundred  deputy  sheriffs  in  the  county,  for the  purpose
of  stopping  picketing.  A  riot  followed  the  activities
of  these  deputy  sheriffs,  resulting  in  the  loss  of  one
life,  and  I believe  a  number  of  injuries,  and  the  com-
mission  reported  very  fully  upon  all  of  the  circum-
stances  of  that  trouble.

Q  The  commission  made  a  report  to  the  Gov-

ernor?

A  Yes.
Q  Was  that  report  published  by  the  Department

of Labor?

601

A  The  report  of  the  commission  is  a  special  bul-
letin  No.  38  of  the  publications  of  the  Department  of
Labor  and  Industry.

This  is  a  copy  of  that.

Q  You  are  referring  to  the  document  now  hand-

ed  me?

A  Yes.
MR.  EMERSON:  I wish  to  offer  in  evidence  this
report  of.  the  commission  on  special  policing  in  in-
dustry,  published  by  the  Pennsylvania  Department  of
Labor  aand  Industry.

mission’s  Exhibit  26.
