BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  While  you  were  serving  as  Secretary  of  the
Pennsylvania  State  Department  of  Labor  and  Indus-
try,  did  complaints  come  to  the  Department  from  em-
ployees  of  Jones  &  Laughlin  Company  at  Aliquippa?
A  Yes,  a  particularly  large  number  of  complaints

came  in  the  summer,  and  early  fall  of  1934.

Q  What  was  the  nature  of  those  complaints?
A  The  complaints  which  were  sent  to  the  Gov-
ernor  and  sent  to  me  as  Secretaary  of  Labor  and  In-
dustry,  were  to  the  effect  that  the  workers  were  not  be-
ing  permitted  to  organize  in  accordance  with  Section
7A  of  the  N.  R.  A.,  and  asking  assistance  from  -the
State  Police  to  protect  their  civil  liberties.

—

Q  What  action  did  the  State  Department  of  La-

bor  take?

A  The  State  Department  of  Labor  sent  its  medi-
ators  to  investigate  the  situation,  and  at  the  same  time

+  @&

-

602

the  Federal  Labor  Department  sent  a  mediator  to  in-
vestigate,  and  the  National  Steel  Board  had  asked  for
a  report  from  the  Department  of  Labor  and  Industry,
regarding  the  whole  situation.

Q  Did  the  Department  of  Labor  and  Industry
conduct  an  investigation  and  make  a  report  to  the  Na-
tional  Steel  Labor  Relations  Board?

A  Yes,  sir.
Q  Would  you  state,  first,  the  nature  of  the  in-

vestigation  that  was  made  at  that  time?

A  The  investigation  was  made  intensively,  fol-
lowing  a  hearing  before  the  Steel  Labor  Board  in  Oc-
tober  of  1934,  at  which  time  on  a  petition  from  the
lawyers  of  Jones  &  Laughlin  Company,  the  hearing
was  postponed,  and  the  workers  who  had  come  to
testify  at  the  hearing  expressed  themselves  as  being
afraid  to  go  home,  because  of  the  physical  danger  that
they  felt  that  they  would  be  in,  because  of  their  dar-
ing  to  testify.

Following  that,  following  that  information,  and
with  verification  of  the  possibility  of  that  danger  which
was  evident  to  the  mediators  of  the  Labor  Department,
the  Governor  of  Pennsylvania  sent  the  State  Police  into
the  town  of  Aliquippa  for  the  protection  of  the  civil
liberties  of  the  community.

The  evidence  all  leads  to  the  conclusion  that  were  it
not  for  the  presence  of  the  State  Police,  and  their  pro-
tection,  that  the  information  that  was  available,  made
available  to  our  mediators,  could  not  have  been  given
because  of  the  fear  of  the  workers  in  giving  that  testi-
mony.

;

Q  Was  that  investigation  conducted  under  your

immediate  supervision?

-  A  Yes.

Q  Will  you  state  briefly  the  findings  that  were
made  as  a  result  of  that  investigation  by  the  Depart-
ment of Labor?

A_  Well,

the findings  indicated,  first,  that.union  or-

603

ganizers  who  would  come  to  Aliquippa  to  attempt  to  or-
ganize  the  employees  of  the  Jones  & Laughlin  Company,
had  been  treated  with  such  force  and  vigor  by  the
deputy  sheriffs  and  the  company  police,  one  in -particu-
lar  instance  is reported  here  in an  affidavit  of the  repre-
sentative  of  the  union  being  beaten  up  so  badly  that  he
had  to  leave  town.  The  organization  in  Aliquippa  at
that  point  ceased,  but  organization  in  Ambridge,  a  steel
town  a  few  miles  away  from  Aliquippa  on  the  other  side
of  the  river,  continued.
;
The  employees  of  the  Jones  &  Laughlin  Company
attempted,  some  of  them,  to  go  to  Ambridge  to  attend
meetings  at  Ambridge.  When  they  did  this,  they  were
followed  over  to  Ambridge  by  the  Jones  &  Laughlin,
Aliquippa,  police,  and  threatened  for  going  to  the  meet-
ings,  and  there  is  evidence  to  the  effeet  of  actual  phys-
ical  harm  that  had  come  to  them  because  they  had  at-
tended  these  meetings.
There  is also  a  very  definite  evidence  of the  discrim-
ination  against  them,  dismissal  from  the  company,  less
time  permitted  to  work  because  they  were  members  of
the  union.

The  company  police,  always  armed,  not  only  fol-
lowed  employees  who  went  to  the  meetings  in  a  com-
munity  outside  of  Aliquippa  itself,  but  there  is evidence
in  this  record  of  a  company  police  entering  the  home
of  one  of  the  workers  in  Aliquippa,  searching  his  trunk
without  a  warrant  to  enter  his  room,  getting  from  his
trunk  the  cards  of  union  members  which  he  had  in  that
trunk,  and  taking  them  away  with  him.

There  are  about  eighteen  exhibits  in  this  report,  giv-
ing the  sworn  statements  of  a  number  of  workers,  as  to
situations  such  as  these  that  I have  described.

Q  Did  the  report  contain  conclusions  or  recom-
mendations  with  respect  to  what  action  should  be taken?

A  Yes.
Q  What  were  they?
A  The  report—one  statement  in  answer  to  the

604

question  that  was  asked  before  that  perhaps  should
be  put  in  the  record,  is  that  none  of  the  union  mem-
bers  in  the  community  were  permitted  to  rent  any
hall  for  the  meetings  of  the  union  workers,  in  the  town
of  Aliquippa,  up  until  the  point  that  the  State  Police
came  into  town.  At  that  point  they  were  permitted  to
rent  a  hall,  and  with  the  renting  of  the  hall,  and  with
less  duress  because  of  the  presence  of  the  State  police,
the  organization  of  the  workers  developed.

The  workers  had  asked  for  an  election  from  the
Steel  Labor  Board  to  determine  the  right  to  bargain  col-
lectively  with  the  Jones  and  Laughlin  Company,  and  the
report  which  was  made  by  the  Labor  Department  defi-
nitely  recommended  that  an  election  be  held,  as  the  one
way  to  settle  the  question,  as  to  whether  or  not  the
workers  were  organized  and  might  bargain  collectively
with  their  employers,  and  that  is the  recommendation  of
this  report  to  the  Steel  Board.

Q  Did  the  Department  of Labor  find  that  the  prac-
tices  of  the  Jones  & Laughlin  Company,  as  you  have  in-
dicated  them,  result  in  unrest  in  the  plant?

A  There  is  no  question  but  what  there  was  con-

|

siderable  unrest  and growing  unrest.

Q  And  the  Department  of Labor  felt  that  the  hold-
ing  of  an  election  would  tend  to  alleviate  a  certain
amount  of that  unrest?
A  It would  tend  to  settle  the  question,  in  the. fair-

est  possible  way,  it seemed  to  the  Department.

Q  Now,  from  your  experience  in  the  Department
of Labor  could  you  state  generally  the  attitude  of  other
steel  companies  in  the  State  of  Pennsylvania,  with  re-
ference  to their  employees  and  organization  by their  em-
ployees?
A  We have  reason  to believe,  and many  reasons—
and  we  have  no  reason  to  believe  that  the  situation  as
it  was found  in  Aliquippa  was  very  different  from  that
of the situation  in almost  any  company  steel  town  in  the
western  part of the State.

605

Q  Upon  what  sort  of  evidence  do  you  base’  that

statement?

“1  The reports  which  are  in  the  files  of  the  Penn-
syivania  Department  of  Labor,  and  Industry,  from  the
mediators  of  the  Labor  Department  who  were  called  in,
in  times  of  disputes  or  labor  troubles.

Q  Would  you  say  that  the  result  of  these  practices
by the  steel  companies  was  to  create  unrest  in  the  plant,
in  the  plants?

:

;

A  There  is  no  question  but  what  unrest  started  at
the  plant  at  which  there  was  any  indication  on  the  part
of  the  workers  to  organize.

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  From  your  experience  in  the  Department  of  L:.-
on would  you  say  that  there  was  generally  among  the

A  There  was  unquestionably  an  increased  or  more
articulate  desire  of  the  employees  to  organize  in  1934  or
following  the  Section  7A  of  the  N.  R.  A.

town,  are  very  limited,  without  doubt.

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  Would  you  explain  what  is  meant  by  company

town?

.

Ns

A  A  company  town  would  be  a  town  in  which  the
company  itself  owned  or  controlled  most  of  the  stores
or  businesses,  and  the  policing  and  even  the  utilities  of
the  town.  There  are  degrees  to  which  that  control  would
hold;  some  towns,  the  company  would  own  all  of  the  fa-
cilities  in  the  town  and  other  company  towns  they  would
control  but  not  own.

Q  They  ordinarily  own  the  houses?
A  Yes.
Q  Are  these  towns  incorporated  or  unincorpo-

.

rated?

A  I don’t  know  the  answer  to that  question.

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  Will  you  give  your  name  to  the  reporter?
A  John  A.  Fitch.
Q  What  is your  present  residence?
A  New  York  City.
Q  What  is  your  present  position?
A  Iam  a  member  of  the  faculty  of  the  New  York

School  of Social  Work.

|

Q  Would  you  state  the  experience  you  have  had
in  dealing  with  the  problems  of  labor  relations  in  the
iron  and  steel  industry?  ~~ 7

A  Why,  it goes  back  to  1907.  In  that  year,  I be-
came  a  member  of  the  staff  of  an  organization  called
the  Pittsburgh  Survey,  which  made  a  study  of  working
and  living  conditions  ir.  the  City  of  Pittsburgh,  and  my  |
particular  assignment  was  conditions  in  the  steel  indus-
try.

That::went  on  for  about  a  year,  and  I published  a
book  entitled,  “The  Steel  Workers,”  dealing  with  hours,  .
wages,  union  conditions  and  so  on,  and  after  an  interval
of about three years, I again was retained  by the Sur-
vey  to make  a  study  of conditions  in  other  steel  centers
in the United  States  and  I spent  about  a  year  doing  that.

609

,

That  was  in Bethlehem,  Pennsylvania;  Gary,  Pueblo,  Col-
orado,  Birmingham,  Alabama,
and  a few other  centers
‘not quite  so important,  and  I published  a series of ar-
ticles  in the  Survey  Magazine  in 1911.

:

I also visited  England  at  that  time  and  made  a  brief

study  of  conditions  in  the  British  steel  industry.

After that I became

the industrial  editor
of the Sur-
_  vey  Magazine,  and  studied—it  was  my  business  to  study
labor  conditions  of  all sorts,  and  I continued  to keep  in.  |
touch  with  the  steel  industry  from  time  to  tine occasion-
ally  events  occurred  that  took  me  into  the  field  as  a
_,  strike  in Youngstown, Ohio, a  few years ago, after I had
become  interested  with  the  Survey,  and  the  1919  strike,
the  big strike  in  the  steel  industry,  and  I also  followed
that  up  with  a  special  study  made  in  the summer  of
1920,  in  Pittsburgh,  and  other  centers,  in  order  to  dis-
cover  what  the effect  of the  strike  had been,  and  at
other  intervals,  other  periods,  I have  dropped  into  the  —
steel  districts,  in  order  to  discover  what  was  going  on,
and  my  most  important  excursion  of  that  sort  was  last
fall when  I spent  about  six  weeks  in  Chicego,  and  Gary,
and Pittsburgh,  bringing  my  information  up  to  date.

Q  Would  you  state  what  books  you  have  published,

and  you  are  the  author  of?

:;

A  The  “Steel  Workers”  published  in  1910  or  1911;
and  the  book  entitled  “The  Causes  of  Industrial  Unrest”
published in  1924,  and  a recent  book  which  is  perhaps  ir-
relevant,  “Vocational  Guidance  and  Action.”

Q  Have  you  been  connected  with  any  university  es

lecturer?

A  I  was  lecturer  in  economics  at  Columbia  Uni-
versity  for  two  years;  and  in  two  or  three  summer  ses-
sions;  I also  lectured  at  the University  of Minnesota,  in
one  summer  session.

Q  Now,  would  you  describe  the  labor  relations  in
the  steel  industry,  in  the  early  period  of  the  iron  indus-
try,  and  up  to  1892,  particularly  the  relations  of  the
union  to  the  companies?

610

A  That  was  a  period  in  which  the  iron  industry
reached  its  peak,  and  began  to  decline  before  the  rising
The  Amalgamated  Association  of  Iron
steel  industry.
and  Steel  Workers  came  into  being  in  1876,  and  grew
fairly  rapidly,  until  it  became  the  dominant  factor,  so
far  as  labor  was  concerned,  in  the  iron  mills  in  the  Pitts-
burgh  district,  and  to  a  considerable  extent  in  other  dis-
tricts.

During  the  80’s,  the  union  established  itself  pretty
thoroughly  in  those  mills  and  the  relations  with  the  em-
The
ployers  seemed  to  have  been  rather  amicable.
union  had  an  annual  picnic,  for  example,  and  the  iron
companies  used  to  suspend  operations  on  the  day  that
the  union  had  their  picnic,  so  that  the  men  could  all  go
to  it, and  the  union  sometimes  invited  the  offices  of  the
companies  to  go  to  the  picnic  also,  and  sometimes  they
did,  and  made  addresses  to  the  workers,  and  the  steel
industry,  one might  say,  really  got  under  way  in  the  late
_  70's,  and  the  unions’  greatest  suecess  in  that  area  was  in
the  Homestead  plant  of  what  became  the  Carnegie  Steel
Company.

That  became  pretty  thoroughly  organized,  and  other
steel  plants  began  to  be  organized,  the  South  Works  of
the  Illinois  Steel  Works  in  Chicago.were  pretty  well  or-
ganized,  and  Jones  & Laughlin,  in  Pittsburgh,  recognized
the  union,  and  there  were  quite  a  number  of  indepen-
dent  companies  of  lesser  significance.

Q  Did  the  Amalgamated  Association  have  agrec-
ments  with  the  members  of  the  iron  industry  at  that
time?

“3

A  Do  you  mean  with  individual  plants?
A  Yes,  very  generally  in  the  iron  industry.

._  Q  To  what extent  did those agreements  exist  in  the

steel  industry?

That  is, previous  to 1892?
A  To  a  lesser  extent,  but there  was  a  continuous
‘at  the  Homestead  ‘plant,  which  was  the  lead-
ing plant  of the  steel  industry,  from  about  1882,  or

611

thereabouts,  until  1892,  when  the  famous  Homestead
strike  eliminated  the  union.

Q  Would  you  say  that  in  that  period,  up  to  1892,
the  relations  of  the  Amalgamated  with  the  companies
that  it had  agreements  with  were  in  general  harmonious
relations?

A  In general,  I would  say  so,  with  the  exception  of
those  differences  of  opinion  that  do  exist.  frequently  at
the  time  of  the  expiration  of  a  contract,  and  sometimes
there  would  be  strikes  before  a  new  contract  could  be
negotiated.

Q  Now,  there  was  a strike  at  the  Homestead  plant
of  the  Carnegie  Steel  Company  in  1892,  was  there  not?

A  Yes.
Q  Would  you  state  the  causes  of that  strike?
A  Initially,  that  strike  came  about  because  of  the
demand  for  an  increase  in  wages  which  the  company  re-
fused,  but  it became  evident  in  a  short  time  that  it was
the  intention  of  the  company  to  do  away  with  the  union,
and  before  the  strike  had  been  on  for  more  than  a  week
or  so  everything  else  was  lost  sight  of, and  the strike  was
carried  on  for  the  purpose  of  preserving  the  union.

Q  Would  you  describe  briefly  the  events  of  that

strike?

5

A  Well,  H.  C.  Frick  was  the  president  of  the  Car-
negie  Steel  Company,  and  he  seems  to  have  been  de-
termined,  and  an  independent  sort  of  employer,  who
was  determined  to  have  his  way  with  regard  to  labor
condition.  One  would  gather  that  he  intended  at  the  be-
ginning  of  the  strike  to  eliminate  the  union.

He  made  arrangements  with  the  Pinkerton  Detec-
tive  Agency  to  supply  him  with  a  large  number  of
guards,  several  hundred,  who  were  brought  into  the
Homestead  plant  in  the  night,  on  barges,  on  the  Monon-
gahela  River;  and  when  they  attempted  to  land,  the
strikers  met  them,  and  a  battle  ensued,  which  resulted
in some  loss  of life,  and  a considerable  degree  of injury,
‘and  resulted  in  the  surrender  by  the  Pinkertons,  and

,

re)
S

>,
Tepes.
ee

Sa.

.

612

they  went  out  of  town,  and  an  agreement  that  had  been  |
made  by the  union.

After  that  the  Staté  Militia  was  called  in,  and  the
town  was  under  military  _— from then  om, to  the  end
of  the  strike.

Q  Can  you  state  how  many  employees  were  in-

volved in  that  strike  at  the  Homestead  plant?

My  best  recollection  is  about  2,500.  I  couldn’t  be

sure  of  that  at  this  time.

Q  Could  you  estimate  the  duration  of  the  strike?
A  It began  some  time  in  June,  and I think  it  went

on  until  August  or  September.

Q  Now,  what  was  the  result  of  that  strike  so  far

as  the  union  was  concerpéd?

A  The  strike  was  lost  by  the  union,  and  the  plant
was  operated  as  a  non-union  plant,  and  at  the  same  time
it  lost  out  in  several  other  Garnegie  mills,  smaller  Car-
negie  mills,  and  the  situation  seemed  to  have  an  effect
upon  the  other  steel  companies,  and  the  union  began  to
go  down  hill  from  that  time  on.

Q  Were  there  strikes  in  other  Carnegie  plants  at

the  same  time  as  this  Homestead  strike?

A  Yes.
Q  Do  yoy  know  about  how  many  employees  were

involved?

A  I couldn’t  say  now,  but  a  smaller  number  than

in  the  Homestead  plant.

Q  Were  there  efforts  to  revive  the  union  in  the

Homestead  plant  after  the  strike?

A  There  were  several  efforts  made  to  reorganize,
and  they  were  defeated  in  every  case  by  the  discharge
of  the  men  who  were  active  in  organizing  the  union.

Q  Now,  what  was  the  situation  so  far  as  the  union
was  concerned  in. other  parts  of  the  industry,  up  to  the
period  ending  the  Homestead  strike,  and  up  to  the  for-
mation  of the United  States  Steel  Corporation?

A  The  union  held its ground;  that  is,  it maintained
a  lodge,  which was recognized in the  South  Works  of the

613

Illinois  Steel  Company,  but  in  the  Pittsburgh  district  it
was  on  its  way  out,  and  it  wie -Saane  down  hill
through  that  period  of  time.

Q  Did  the union  exist  in the  Jones  &  Laughlin

plant  at  that  time?

A  Yes;  and  that  company  ceased  to  have dealings

with  them  in  1897.

Q  Now,  when  was  the  United  States  Steel  corpora-

tion  formed?

A  In  1901.
Q  What  policy  did  the  corporation  adopt  with  re-

spect  to  labor  organizations?

A  The  position  of  the  Steel  Corporation  as  indi-
cated  by a  resolution  adopted  by the  Board  of  Directors
early  in  its  career,  in  1901,  was  that  they  would  not  re-
cognize  trade  unionism  in  any  of  their  plants  where  it
was  not  already in  existence,  and  they would  oppose  any
extension  of  it.

Q  Was  there  an  attempt  shortly after  the  forma-
tion  of  that  company,  by  the  union,  to  organize  the
plants  of  the  United  States  Steel  Corporation?

A  Yes,  the  Amalgamated  Association  of  Iron,  Steel
and  Tin  Workers  attempted  to  secure  an  extension  of
recognition  to  other  plants  of  the  Steel  Corporation,  and
when  the  Corporation,  or  when  the  subsidiary  companies
of the  corporation  refused  to  sign,  they  went  on a strike,
and  that  strike  lasted  through  a  considerable  part  of  the
summer  of  1901,  and  ended  largely  in failure.

Instead  of  extending  the  scope  of  the  union,  the
company  finally  signed  for  a  smaller  number  of  mills
than  it  had  before.

Q  Did  that  seike  involve  other  plants  than  the

United  States  Steel  Corporation?

A_  I don’t  think  it  did.  It  was  entirely  a  Steel Cor-

poration  matter.

Q  How  long  efter  the  strike  af 1901  did  the  Steel
Corporation  continue  to  deal  with  the  workers  that  it
made  agreements  with?

614

A  It  continued  to  deal  with  several,  in  several  of
It  continued  to  deal  with  the
its  sheet  and  tin  plants.
union  until  1909,  and  then  notified  the  union  that  it
would  not  deal  with  them  any  further  in  any  of  ils
plants.

:

Q  Was  there a strike  at  that  time?
A  The  union  called  a  strike,  in  an  effort  to  main-
tain  itself,  and  the  strike  was  lost,  and  from  that  time
on  the  Steel  Corporation  had  no  further  dealings  with
Amalgamated  Association.

Q  That  is  true  down  to  the  present  day?
A  Well,  not  entirely  true,  not  in  the  sense  that  it
was  true  up  to  the  time  of  the  adoption  of  the  Recovery
Since  that  time,  trade  unionism  having  gotton  un-
Act.
der  way  to  some  extent  again,  the  company  has  had
what  you  might  call  conversations  with  representatives
of  the  union,  but  it  hasn’t  dealt  with  them  or  recognized
them.

Q  Now,  going  back  to  the  period  after  the  forma-
tion  of  the  United  States  Steel  Corporation,  in  the  per-
iod  between  1901  and  1910,  what  was  the  situation  in
Did  the  union  deal  with
other  parts  of  the  industry?
any  other  industry  during  that  period?

A  It  was  not  recognized  in  any  of  the  large  inde-
pendents  such  as  the  Jones  & Laughlin  or  the  Bethlehem
Steel  Company,  but  it  did  continue  to  have  agreements,
and  to  have  recognition  in  a  number  of  independent
sheet  and  tin  plants,  as  well  as  a  number  of  the  remain-
ing smaller  iron  mills,  in  Ohio,  and  Indiana.

in the  Pittsburgh  area,  with  a  plant  on  the  South  Side  of
Pittsburgh,  and  a  plant  at  Girard,  Ohio.

A  In  1907,  at  the.  time  of  the  Pittsburgh  Survey,

there  were  union  men  in  the  Beyers plant,  but  whether

A  I don't  recall  any.
Q  Was  there  a  strike  at  the  Bethlehem  Company

|

A  Yes,  there  was  a  strike  at  the  Bethlehem  Com-
pany  at  its  Bethlehem  plant.  That  came  about  because
the  machinists  had  been  working  pretty  steadily  on  Sun-
day,  and  they  appointed  a  committee  to  go  to  the  man-
agement  to  object  to  Sunday  work,  and  the  management
dismissed  the  committee,  instead  of  negotiating  with
them,  whereupon  the  machinests.  went  out on  strike  in
support  of  their  committee,  and  then  other  departments
came  out  also,  and  the  strike  was  on  for  quite  a  period
of  time.

MR.  CARMODY:  You  mean,  discharged  the  mem-

bers  of  the  committee?

THE  WITNESS:  Yes.
BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  What  was  the  result  of  that  strike?
A  It  was  not  successful,  and  the  men  finally  came
—  back  to  work  without  havipg  secured  any  concessions  at
an

all.

Q  Would  you  say  that  that  attitude  of  the  Beth-
lehem  Steel  Company  was  similar,  or  typical  of  the  at-
titude  of  the  large  steel  companies  at  that  time?

A  I should  say so,  yes.  When  committees  were
sent  to  the  management,  particularly  around  that  period,

616

if the  men  were  witbout  any  organization,  and  that  was
the  case  at  Bethlehem,  they  had  no  organization.

If  a  group  of  men  appointed  a  committee  to carry
a  petition  to  the  management,  they  were  in  danger  of
discipline,  not only. Bethlehem,  but  at  other  plants.

Q  Now,  would  you  describe  the  labor  policy  of  the
large  steel  companies  in  the  period  from  1910,  up  to  the
strike  of  1919?

What  was  their  attitude  toward  labor  organizations?
A  They  were  wholly  opposed  to  labor  organiza-
tions,  and  labor  organizations  made  no  headway.  That
was  a  period  when  the  attitude  toward  inaction  con-
tirlued  to  be  of  the  sort  that  I have  described,  not  only
opposition  to  trade  union  organization,  but  opposition  to
any  sort  of  collective  action,  such  as  the  appointment
of  a  committee.

Q  What  methods  did  the  companies  use  at  that

:

time  in  resisting  the  organization  of  employees?

A  Well,  a  characteristic  institution  of  the  steel  in-
dustry  was  a  spy  system,  which  brought  reports  to  the
management  of the  attitudes  on  the part  of the  men,  and
indicated  their  interest  in  unionism,  in  meetings  that
they  might hold,  and  when  it was  known  that  they  were
going  to  have  a  meeting,  the  foreman  would  usually
stand  in  front  of  the  place  of  meeting  to  see  who  was
going  to  attend,  and  take  the  names,  if  possible,  of  men
who  were  attending,  and  sometimes  there  were  cases_
where  men  held  a  meeting  on  a  vacant  lot  at  night,  and
flashlight  photograhs  were  taken,  in  order  to  acquaint
the  management  with  the  personnel,  of  the  persons
present.

Q  Coming  now  to  the  strike  of  1919,  would  you

state  the causes  of  that  strike?

A  The  original  cause  was the effort  on  the part
of the American  Federation  of Labor  to  organize  the  in-
dustry.  The  A.  F.,of  L.  Convention  of  1918,  I think  it
resofution  to  organize  the  industry,  and

that  was  adopted  in  1918.

was—a  resolution  was  adopted  to  organize  the  imdustry
and create  a  committee  fo  the purpose  of carrying  dn

A  There  were  twenty-four  of  them.

A  Yes,  affiliated  with  the  American  Federation  of

Labor.

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  Your  testimony  is  that  the  strike  was  due  to

this  effort  to  organize  the  industry?

A  Having  succeeded  in  organizing  the  industry,
iY  the  officers  of  this  Committee  addressed  a  letter  to
Judge  Gary  asking for  a  conference  for  the  purpose  of
dealing  with  grievances  and  negotiating  an  agreement,
and  Judge  Gary  and  the  other  officials  of the  steel  com-
panies,  refused  to  grant  such  a  conference.

They  replied  ‘that this  organizing  committee  didn’t
'  represent  their  men,  and  that  they  didn’t  have  any  or-
ganization  in  the  mills  of  the  United  States  Steel  Cor-
poration,  and  therefore  there  wasn’t  anyone  to meet.

John  Fitzpatrick  in  his  reply  said  that  if that  was
their  attitude,  there  would  be  no  way  by which  he  could
prove  his  leadership  except  by  putting  a  strike  into  ef-
fect,  and  so  the  strike  came  shortly  after  that,  and  in
the  neighborhood  of  three  hundred  and  fifty  thousand

618

men  went  out  on  strike  in  the  various  mills  of  the  coun-

Q  Going  back  just  a  moment,  would  you  say  that
this  effort  on  the  part  of  this  committee  comprising
twenty-four  unions,  met  with  ready  response  among  the
employees  of  the  industry?
A  -Apparently  it  did.

The  movement  was  very
rapid,  and  very  successful,  so  far  as  bringing  men  into
the  Union  was concerned.

Would  you  state  the  working  conditions  in  the  in-
dustry  with  respect  to  which  the  employees  and  the
unions  wished  to  bargain,  with  the  companies?

A  First  of  all  they  wanted  collective  bargaining,
and  one  of  the  grievances  that  they  listed,  after  the
strike  began,  they  drew  up a list  of  grievances,  was  the
refusal  of  collective  bargaining.

They  asked  for  an  increase  in  wages,  but  the  pri-
mary  grievance  was  the  existence  of  the  twelve  hour
day  and  the  seven  day  week;  and  aside  from  the  move-
ment  to  organize  which  was  a  step  preliminary  to  deal-
ing  with  that  situation,  I  should  say  that  the  primary
cause  of  the  strike  would be  the  —  conditions,
and  long  hours.

Q  Now,  will  you  describe  briefly  the  events  of
that  strike,  and  the  number  of  employees  involved,  and.
the  duration  of  the  strike.

A  The  strike  began  about  the  middle  of  Septem<-
ber,  1919,  and  was  called  off  sometime  in  January,  1920,
and  at  the  heighth  of  it there  were  probably  more  than
three  hundred  and  fifty thousand  men  on  strike.

It  resulted  in  some  of  the  very  large  mills  of  the
country  being  practically  idle,  and  the  plant  at  Johns
town,  Pennsylvania,  was  entirely  down,  the  plants  of  the
Carnegie  Steel  Company  in  the  Pittsburgh  District  were
very  seriously  crippled,  and  the  steel  industry,  I should
say,  was  very  seriously  effected  in  September  and  Oc-
tober,  and  then  the  men  began  to  drift  back  to  work,
until  the  situation  was  not  so  serious  for  their  compa-
nies.

619

But  probably  one  hundred  thousand  men.  were  out

at  the  time  the  strike  was  called  off.

Q  Were  the  plants  at  Gary,  in  South  Chicago,

closed  down?

A  I  couldn’t  say  they  were  closed  down,  but: I

wasn’t  there  at  the  time.
I was  in  the  Pittsburgh  dis-
trict,  but  I know  that  Gary  was  very  seriously  affected
-—I  don’t  know  that  it was  closed  down.

Q Would  you  describe  the - methods  which  the

steel  company  used  in  resisting  the  strike?

)

A  I suppose  the  most  important  thing  that  can  be
attributed  directly  to  the  steel  companies  was  the  ad-
vertisements  that  they  put  in  the  papers,  and  he  gene-
ral  publicity  designed  to  convince  the  public  that  the
strike  was  revolutionary  in  character.

It  was  claimed  at  the  time  that  the  purpose  was  to
set  up  a  soviet  form  of  government,  and  that  was  very
generally  circulated  and  believed  to  a  very  considerable
extent  by  newspaper  readers.
Full  page  ads  appeared
in  all  of  the  Pittsburgh  papers,  making  allegations  of
that  sort.

Now,  there  was  a  very  active  cooperation  with  the
steel  companies  on  the  part  of  public  officials.  The
sheriff  of  Alleghany  County  issued  a  proclamktion  that
no  street  meetings  could  be  held  by  more  than  three
people,  and  if  there  were  as  many  as  three  people
gathered  on  the  street  corner,  the  deputies  were  au-
thorizd  to  disperse  them.

Whether  that  actually  included  inside  meetings,
there  was  some  question  about,  but  I called  on  the  head-
quarters  of  the  Union,  in  MacKeesport,  at that  time,  and
found  an  organizer  there,  and  a  secretary,  and  because
my  presence  made  three  people  they  were  considerably
ill  at  ease,  and  were  afraid  that  a  trooper  would  come
in  from  the  street  and  disperse  us  at  any  moment,  so  I
wasn’t  able  to  talk  with  them  very  effectively,  and  I
.  had  to  leave  without  getting  all  of  my  questions  an-

swered.

620

The  mayors  of  towns,  or  the  Burgesses,  as  they  are
called,  in  the  village  form  of  government  in  Pennsyl-
vania,  took  an  active  part  in  preventing  the  Union  from
holding  the  meetings,  and  the  famous  statement  of  the
Mayor  of  Duquesne  is  well  known,  I believe,  when  he
said  that  if  Jesus  Christ  wanted  to  hold  a  meeting  to
organize  a  union,  in  Duquesne,  he  couldn’t  hold  it.

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  In  general,  would  you  say  during  this  period,
you  would  say  that  the  practice  of espionage,  and  anti-
union  practices,  continued  on  the part  of  the  steel  com-
panies?

A  Yes,  sir;  there  have  been  several  publications
that  would  indicate  that.  Sidney  Howard’s  book  “The
Labor  Spy,”  and  the  study  made  by  Frank  Palmer,  of
conditions  in  the  iron  mines,  in  Minnesota,  indicated
that  there,  too,  in  the  mines,  operated  by the  steel  com-
panies,  the  same  system  of  espionage  existed.

I  might  state  that  I  got  acquainted  with  the  head
of  the  spy  system  of  the  Carnegie  Steel  Company  in
1907,  and  he  told  me  that  he  was  the  head  of  it,  and
that  he  maintained  a  wide-spread  organization  for  the
purpose  of discovering  any  agitation  or  union  activity.
That  organization  continued  for  a  long  period  of  time.
Q  Now,  was  there  a  further  effort  to  organize  the

steel  industry  in  the  period  from  1933  to  1934?

622

A  After  the  passage  of  the  recovery.  Act,  with  its
Section  7  (a),  there  was  an  impetus  in  the  direction  of
organization.  I might  mention  one  thing  to  indicate  that
the  attitude  in  general  in  the  steel  -,listricts  has  not
changed,  that  just  precedes  this  a little  bit.

This  story  had  appeared  in  the  press  at  the  time,
just  before  the  adoption  of  the  steel  code,  that  Secre-
tary  Perkins  went  to  Pittsburgh,  to  acquaint  herself
by  first-hand  inquiry,  with  conditions  existing  in  the
mills.  And  she  had  a  conference  in  the  office  of  the
Burgess,  I believe  it  was,  with  steel  company  officials
and  some  others,  and  while  she  was  there  a  telegram
was  placed  in  her  hands  that  had  been  sent  to  her  by a
man  in  the  street,  who  hadn’t  been  able  to  get  in,  saying
that  he  represented  a  large  group  of  workers,  who
wanted  to  confer  with  her  about  conditions  prevailing,
and  that  they  had  all  been  prevented  from  coming  in,
and  would  she  come  out  to  them.

She  did  and  found  quite  a  group  of  men  in  the
street,  and  the  Burgess  hurried  out  after  her  and  said
that  they  couldn’t  have  a  meeting  on  the  steps  of  the
Municipal  Building.  She  noticed  a  little  Park  across  the
way  and  suggested  that  they  go  over  to  the  Park,  and
the  Burgess  said  that  she  couldn’t  have  a  meeting  with
the  steel  workers  anywhere  in  the  Borough  of  Home-
stead.

And  then  it  occured#to  someone  that  the  Post  Of-
fice  was  United  States  property,  so  the  Secretary  of  L1-
bor  went  to  the  Post  Office,  and  the  men  came  with  her
and  they  had  a  meeting  in  the  Post  Office.

Q  Would  you  describe  briefly  the  events  leading

;

up  to  the  threatened  strike  of  1934?

A  Well,  the  impetus  given  to  the  Union  movement
by  the  passage  of  the  Recovery  Act,  particularly  with
itself  in  the  steel  industry  as
Section  7  (a),  manif
amated  Association  increased
elsewhere,  and  the
its  membership  to  a considerable  degree.

Q  Would  you  for  a  moment  explain  in  what  way

section  7  (a)  gave  an  impetus  to  the  organization.

623

A  Well,  | didn’t  know  that  I would  have  to  state
here  what  was  implied  in  Section  7  (a),  but—and  yeu
don’t  want  me  to quote  it.-  But  in  view  of  the fact  that
that  section  7  (a)  declared  that  the  workers  do  have  a
right  to  organize,  and  that  they. do  have  a  right  to  select
representatives  and  through  them  to  bargain  with  their
employers,  and  that  it  is  illegal  for  anyone,  employers
or  others,  to  interfere  with  that  right,  they  felt  that.
they  were  now  freed  from  these  restrictions,  and  that
the  steel  companies  and  other  companies  imposed  on
them,  and  now  it  would  be  safe  to  organize,  and  they
wouldn't  lose  their  jobs.

So  the  Amalgamated  Association  considerably  in-
creased  its  membership  at  that  time,  and  there  began
to  be  a  good  deal  of  feeling  on  the  part  of  the  member-
ship  that  some  action  should  follow,  and  that  they
should  get  an  understanding  with  the  steel  industry.

This  pressure  became  so  active  on  the  part  of  a
certain  group  in  the  Union,  that  they  called  themselves
-  the  Rank  and  File  Movement,  in  order  to  differeatiate
.  themselves  from  the  officers  of  the  Union,  who  were  not

moving  very  much,  as  it seemed  to  them.

Now,  as  a  result  of  this,  it  was  at  a  special  con-
vention  held  in  Pittsburgh,  by  the  Union,  it  was  de-
cided  to  make  a  demand  of  all  of  the  steel  companies,
for  recognition,  and  so  in  one  way  or  another,  either
by  personal  word  or  by  communication  through  the
mail,  most  of  the  managers  of  most  of  the  plants  in
the  major  steel  companies  of  the  country,  were  com-
municated  with,  and  asked  to  meet  a  committee  of  the
Union.

That  demand  was  refused  in  every  case,  and  as  a
result  of  that  the  Union  called  a  convention,  and  voted
to  have  a  strike.

The  Administration  in  Washington  interested  it-
self  in  the  matter,  and  it  was  about  that  time  that  Res-
.  Olution  No.  44,  that  is  the  number  I recall,  authorizing
the  President  to  appoint  special  boards  to  deal  with

v

624

particular  industries  was  passed,  and  as  you  know,  a
Steel  Labor  Relations  Board  was  created.

In  view  of  that,  the  Union  decided  not  to  call  a
strike,  and  instead  to  place  its  demands  and  its  griev- .
ances  before  the  Board.

Q  Will  you  state  whether  or  not  at  this  time
there  was  an  increase  in growth  of  employee  represen-
tation,  and  what  the  cause  of  that  was.

A  The  Company  movement  grew,  I suppose,  more
rapidly  that  the  union  movement,  affiliated  with  the
American  Federation  of  Labor,  and  the  reason  for  it
was  that  the  companies,  recognizing  that  they  would  be
called  upon  to  deal  with  their  employees  collectively
as  required  under  Section  7  (a),  felt  that  they  could  do
sO  more  advantageously  to  theniselves  if  they  had  an
organization  of  their  own  employees  alone,  and  not
influenced  by  any  outside  agency,  and  therefore  with-
out  leaders  whg  were  not  their  own  employees.

The -movement  was  very  wide-spread  throughout
the  industry,  and  I  suppose  in  all  of  the  important
plants,  and  the  important  steel  plants  of  the  country,
such  company  unions  were  set  up,  entirely  on  the  ini-
tiative  of  the  employer,  and  in  the  majority  of  cases,
I should  say,  set  up  under  conditions  as  to  leave  a  con-
siderable  part  of the  control  of  the  organization  in  the
hands  of  the  companies.

Q  Would  you  describe  more  specifically  the  ex-
tent  to  which  those  casi unions  were  dominated  by
the  companies?

A  Well,  it  was  indicated  first  of  all  by  the  noti-
fication  that  went  out  from  the  President  of  the  com-
It  was,  curiously  enough,  the  same
pany  to  the  men.
letfer  in  identical  terms,  in  agreat  many
,  and
not—

THE  WITNESS:  Yes,  not  only  were  they  identical

acceptance  of  the  Union  or  of  the  plan,  but  in  most
cases  there  never  was  a  formal  vote  to  accept or  reject.

charging  men  because  they  presented  agreements,  the

Q  Would  you  say  that  these  plans  as  drawn  up  by
the  company  were  effective  mediums, or constituted  ef-
fective  mediums  for  collective  bargaining?
A  I should  say  no.  They  are  effective  as  agencies
for  petition,  but  the  final  decision  rests  entirely  with
the  company.
Q  Would  you  say  that  in  order  for  it  to  be  ef-
fective  it would  have  to  be  free  from  domination  or  in-
terference  by  the  company?
A  Of  course,  no  arrangement  between  employers
and  employees  can  be  free  from  what  might  be  called
dominance  or  interference,  because  the  employees  are
bargaining  with  the  employer,  and  the  employer  has  his  —

631

interests  to  protect’ and  he  will  protect  them,  just  as  ef-
fectively  as  he  can.

“+

The  question  is  not  that  of  whether  or  not  the  com-
pany  is  capable  of  getting  its  way,  but  the  question  is
whether  there  be  bargaining  strength  on  the  part  of  the
-  employees,  so  that  if  they  are  unable  to  get  their  way,
they  may  choose  the  alternative  of  exercising  their
strength  which  ultimately  of  course  would  be  in  the
form  of a strike.

:

|

‘Now,  I should  say  that  one  tremendous  difference
between  the  company  union  and  the  independent  union,
or  the  outside  union,  the  union  which  is  part  of  a  gen-
eral  labor  movement,  is  that  the  company  union  is  not
in  a  position  to  resort  effectively  to  the  weapon  of  a  ~
strike  and  therefore  it  is  a weak  bargaining  agency,  and
the  employer  is  in  a  much  better  position  to  have  his
way.

Now,  whether  you  call  that  dominance,  I  see  no
reason  for  characterizing  it  as  a  vicious  thing,  as  one
might  think  it  is  if  you  call  it  dominance.
The  strong
bargainer  always  dominates  to  a  certain  extent,  and  I
should  say  that  in  the  presence  of  the  company  union
the  employer  is  pretty  apt  to  get  his  way  on  everything
that  is  important.

If  there  be  a  strong  union,  with  affiliations  with
_  other  bodies  on  the  outside,  it  is  in  a  position  to  bar-
gain  very  mych  more  effectively,  and  therefore  to  cause
the employer  who  is  in  that  position  to  hesitate  about
putting  into  effect  a  decision  which  he  has  the  legal
power  always  to  put  into  effect.

Q  What  efforts  are  being  made  at  the  present  time
to  organize  the  employeers  in  the  steel  industry  for
purposes  of  collective  bargaining.

the  American  Federation  of Labor,  adopted  a  resolution
directing  the:  Executive  Council,  to  proceed  in  the  di-
rection  of  organizing  the  steel  industry.

That  action  was  held  in  abeyance,  partly  because  of

ous,  and  that  there  have  been  re-current  strikes  or  other

DIRECT  EXAMINATION

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Your  name  is  John  A.  Lapp?

~  638

_  A  Yes,  sir.
“  Q  And  you  are  now  resident  in  Washington,  D.  C.?
A  Washington,  with  legal  residence  in  Chicago.
Q  Were  you  at  some  time  a  member  of  the  Pe-

troleum  Labor  Policy  Board?

A  Iwas  a  member  of  the  Petroleum  Labor  Policy
Board  from  October  1,  1934,  until  the  ist  of  April,
1936.

Q  Did  that  Board  consist  of  so-called  “Impartial

Persons”  or  was  it  a  bi-partisan  board?

A  It  consisted  of  three  members,  impartial  mem-

bers.

Q  Who  were  the  other  two?
A  In  the  period  when  I  was  on  the  Board,  Dr.
George  W.  Stocking  was  Chairman,  and  James  Mullen-
bach  was  the  third  member  until  his  death  in  April  of
1935,  after  which  there  were  only  two  members  serving.
Q  Prior  to  your  appointment  and  assuming  the
duties  of  a  member  of  the  Petroleum  Labor  Policy
Board,  were  you  a  member  of  any  other  governmental
agency  dealing  with  labor  relations?

A  For  almost  a  year  I  was  Chairman  of  the  Bi-
tuminous  Labor  Board,  Division  I,  consisting  of  Indi-
ana,  IHinois,  and  Iowa,  and  as  Chairman  of  that  Board.
I was  a  member  of  the  National  Bituminous  Coal  Labor
Board.

Q  Now  state  what  experience  you  had  in  dealing

with  labor  relations  prior  to  that  appointment?

A  For  some  weeks  I  was  Secretary  and  Director
of  the  Chicago  Regional  Labor  Board,  and  I  organized
it  and  managed  cases  for  some  three  months,  I  think,
‘  approximately.

MR.  SMITH:  That  was  under  the  old. National  Labor

Board?

_  THE  WITNESS:  Yes.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  What  experience  prior  to  that  time?
e
A  Prior  to  that  time  I had  been  a  student  of  the  =

&:

subject  and  an  observer  of  labor  cases  and as  early as
1920  — was  a  member  of  a  special  committee  that  in-
vestigated  the  street-car  strike  in  Denver,  Colorado,  the
one that  caused  so  much  bloodshed.  ~

'

Q  Were  you  connected  at  any  time  with  the  Social
Action  Department  of  the  National  Catholic  Welfare
‘Conference?

A  For  about  seven  years,  1920  to  1927.
Q  And  during  that  connection,  were  you  inter-

ested  in  labor  relations  and  problems?

A  As  a  member  and  active  promoter  of  the  Cath-
olic  Conference  on  Industrial  Problems,  I was  constant-
ly in  touch  with  the  situation,  and  attended  some  four-
teen  of  the  regional  conferences  of  that  organization,
and  listened  to  the  discussions  of  many  experts  in  that
period.

Q  What  was  the  purpose  and  nature  of  that  or-

«

ganization?

A  The  purpose  was  trying  to  find  means  of  har-
monizing  the  relationships.  between  employers  and  em-
ployees,  by  common  understanding.

Q  Have  you  been  connected  with  the  faculty  of

Marquette  University?

_

A  For  approximately  five  years  at  Marquette  Uni-

versity,  from  1927  to  1932.

Q  In  what  department?
A  Head  of  the  Department  of  Social  ‘Sinan:
Q  Do  you  have  a  Doctor’s  degree  from  any  uni-

versity  ?

;

.

A  I have  an  L.  L.  D.  degree  from  Alfred  Univer-

sity.

Q  All told,  how  many  years  have  been  spent  deal-

ing with  problems  of  labor  relations?

_A  In one  time  or  another,  from  the  beginning  of
¥ activity  when  I became  director  of  the  Legislative
Bureau  of  Indiana,  I have  had  contact  with
s le problems, dealing  first  with  the  formation  of  legis-
n  in various fields,  and  as  early  as  1915  I instituted

in  Indiana  a  study  of  industrial  arbitration,  and  that
study,  which  was  developed  for  the  Bureau  of  Legis-
lative  Reference,  became  a  book  published  by  Carl  H.
Mote,  the  man  who  developed  the  study  for  me,  and
‘that  book  was  one  of  the  first  complete  reviews  of  arbi-
tration,  labor  disputes,  in  this  country.

nomics,  and  texts  on  sociai  sciences,  and  in  civics,  and
covering,  and  in  each  book  almost  covering  in

is

general  the  field  of  labor  relations.

Q  Will  you  state  briefly  what  the  duties  and  func-

tions  of  the  Petroleum  Labor  Policy  Board  were?

A  The  Petroleum  Labor  Policy  Board  had  entire
jurisdiction  of  all  labor  problems  arising  under  the
Petroleum  Code;  instead  of  the questions  going  origin-
ally  to  the  National  Labor  Board,  the  old  National  La-
bor  Board,  all  labor  problems  came  to  the  Petroleum
Labor  Policy  Board,  and  throughout  its  career  it  han-
died  all  of  the  questions  relating  to  collective  bargain-
ing  and  had  jurisdiction  and  enforcement  of  the  labor
provisions  of  the  Code.

Q  Do  you  have  with  you  the  figures  as  to  the

number  of  cases  that  the  Board  handled?
I  will  have  to
A  I  have  not  the  exact  figures.
find  that  here:  On  the  complaint  side,  alleging  viole-
tions  of  the  wages  and  hours  provisions  of  the  Code,
there  were  3,045  complaints;  2,862  of  these  had  been
closed  at  the  time  of  the  Schecter  decision,  and  1,085

osed,  1,458  were  adjusted  and  the
respondent  brought  in  compliance
no  violation  was  found  in  922  cases,

on the labor provisions  of  the Code, and

641

;

the  question  of  compliance  under  Section  7.0f  the  Code,
the  complaints  regarding  discrimination  against  union
men  were  received  in  77  cases,  involving  charges  of
discrimination  and  coereion
against  union  men  or
charges  that  company  unions  were  being  forced  upon
employees  by  employers.

The  charges  in  seven  of  these  cases  were  with-
drawn,  and  in  22  of  the  cases  the  Board  found  no  evi-
dence  of coercion  or  discriminatign.

Settlement  was  obtained  in  35  cases;  13  cases  re-
mained  unadjusted  at  the  time  of  the  termination  of
the  powers  of  the  Board  on  the  Schecter  decision.

Total  number  of  men  involved  in  all  of  these
charges  of  discrimination  was  6,613;  and  in  these  cases
the  Board  held  twenty-three  formal  hearings,  issued

ty-two  formal  decisions.
Q  Did  the  Board  find  that  any  of  the  practices  of
employers  which  you  have  just  now  mentioned  in  the
form  of  discrimination  against  employees  for  union  ac-
tivities  and  in  coercion  by  the  employers  upon  the  em-
ployees—did  the  Board  find  that  those  practices  led  to
unrest  among  workers?  -

:

A  The  Board,  as  a  result  of  the  cases,  had  to  deal
with  twenty  strikes,  and  with  fifteen  threatened  strikes,
during  the  period,  and  the  statistics  of  the  Board  on
these  strikes  indicated. that—or  perhaps  I can  read  the
formal  report,  a  paragraph  from  the  formal  report:

“Strikes  which  were  instituted  principally  because
the  employer  or  employers  refused  to  bargain  collec-
tively,  six.
Strikes  which  were  instituted  principally
because  the  employer  or  employers  refused  to  meet
union  demands,  although  they  did  meet  and  bargain
with  the  employee  representatives,  four.
Strike  which
was  instituted  principally  because  the  employer  co-
erced  and  discriminated  against  union  members,  one.
Strikes  which  were  instituted  principally  because  the
employers  failed  to  abide  by  a  previously  negotiated
agreement,  two.
Strike  which  was  instituted  principal-

642

)

°

ly because  the  employers  had  started  a  practice  of  leas-
ing  stations,  one.”
The  above  figures  would  indicate  that  the  predomi-
nant  reason  for  strikes  was  the  failure  or  refusal  of
employers  to  bargain  collectively  with  the  employees
through  .chosen  representatives,  or  discrimination  on
account  of  membership  in  the  unions.
While  statistics  are  not  available  for  the  threatened
strikes,  our  analysis  in  general  indicates  that  the  pre-
dominant  cause  of  the  sixteen  threatened  strikes  was
approximately  the  same  as  the  strikes  themselves.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Now,  did  any  of  the  elections  taken  by  the
Board  result  in  any  disorder  during  the  conduct  of  the
election?

A  We  have  no  evidence  whatever  of  anything  but

harmony  in  elections.

Q  State  whether  or  not  the  experience  of  the
Board  showed  elections  to  be  devices  to  allay  indus-
trial  unrest,  and  if  so,  under  what  circumstances,  and
what  are  the  facts?

A  I  would  say  from  the  experience  of  the  Board
that  it  was  almost  100  per  cent  perfect  as  a  means  of
allaying  controversy  on  a  particular  point.
The  only
exceptions  to  this  statement  is  that  in  two  or  three  in-
stances,  the  companies  failed  thereafter  to  abide  strict-
ly by  the  results  of  the  election,  in  that  they  attempted
by  various  subterfuges  to  get  around  the  necessity  of
dealing  with  the  unions,  and  they  did  this  as  they
thought,  legally,  and  within  their  proper  rights,  but  it
did  result  in  two  or  three  instances  in  the  failure  to
put  iato  effect  the  results  of  the  election.

However,  as  a  matter  of  producing  harmony,  |
think  that  there  is  no*  question  about  the  almost  per-
fect  results  obtained.

Q.  Is  there  any  instance  of  a  strike  following  an

election  conducted  by  the  Board?

A  I know  of  none  in  the  Petroleum  Industry.

and  throughout  the  other  twenty-five  contracts  there
were  not  half  a  dozen  instanges  when  the  Board  was
called  in.

In  other  words,  the  collective  agreement  worked
so  perfectly  that  there  was  no  need  for  any  final  arbi-
tration  in  the  case.

Q  Now,  changing  the  subject  to  the  experience  of
the  Bituminous  Coal  Labor  Board,  will  you  please
state  how  the  Bituminous  Labor  Board  was-set  up,  and
what  were  its  duties  and  functions?

A  The  Bituminous  Coal  Labor  Board,  Division  II,
was  one  of  six  divisions,  handling  the  labor  problems
of  the  Coal  Industry  throughout  the  country.
Division
II  covered  Indiana,  Illinois,  and  lowa,
and each  of  these
Boards  is  made  up  of  an  Impartial  Chairman,  a  repre-
sentative  of  the  miners,  and  a  representative  of  the
operators.

The  six  chairmen,  later  reduced  to  five,  constituted
the  National  Bituminous  Coal  Labor  Board,  which  was
a  Board  of Appeal  from  any of the  decisions  of the
regional  boards.

_

Let me add there,  that  the Board  had  jurisdiction,
Rather  complete  jurisdiction,  over  the  labor  problems
«  dm the Coal Industry,  and  was expressly  directed  to
‘held elections  whenever  there was a  dispute  as to  the

representation.
_-**  Q*+  Were  these

Divisional

Boards

bi-partisan

646

Boards,  with  a  representative  of  the  public  as  Chair-
man?

A  Yes.
Q  And  were  you  the  representative  of  the  public -

on  Division  II?

A  Yes,  on  Division  II.
Q  Did  you  have  an  experience  with  respect  to

the  holding  of  an  election  at  Merritt,  Illinois?

A  That  was  the  only  election  that  we  held  under
the  Bituminous  Labor  Board  Division  II,  the  reason
being  the  collective  bargaining  existed  quite  universally
throughout  the  area  of  Indiana,  Illinois  and  Iowa.

We  did  hold  an  election  in  Mark,  Iflinois,  in  1934.
Q  Will  you  let  us  have  the  benefit  of  your  experi-

ence  in  the  course  of  that  election?

A  That  election  was  injected  into  a  very  tense
situation  at  the  Mark  mine.  The  mine  had  been  a  non-
uni6én  mine  all  of  the  years,  and  in  the  fall  of  1933,
after  the  Coal  Code  was  in  operation,  the  operator  de-
cided  to  unionize  the  mine,  and  he  entered  inio  an
agreement  with  the  United  Mine  Workers  of  America.
Immediately,  the  Progressive  Miners  of  America,
which  was  an  opposing  faction  in  Illinois,  struck  the
mine,  and  the  situation  became  very  tense.

to  send  troops  there.

So  tense  was  it  that  the  Governor  was  called  upon
|
It  happened  that  I  was  in  Springfield,  Lllinois,  at
the  time  the  strike  broke,  and  I  was  in  the  office  of
the  Governor,  and  he  was  exceedingly  disturbed  By  the
situation.
He  called  on  the  long-distance  to  find  out
the  tensity  of  it,  and  agreed  with  them  that  instead  of
sending  troops  he  would  ask  the  Bituminous  Coal  Labor
Board  to  go  in  and  make  an  investigation.

The  Board  went  in  the  following  week,  and  the
mine  had  been  down  for  more  than  a  week,  and  a
I  have  held  many  hearings,  and  I
hearing  was  held.
know  of  none  that  was  quite  as  tense  as  that  one.
The
feeling  was  running  very  high  at  that  time,  between  the

647

Progressive  Miners  of  America,  ard  the  United  Mine
Workers.

We  held  a  hearing,  as  some  said,  almost  in  an
armed  camp.
The  hearing  resulted  in  the  conclusion
that  we  could  not  determine  which  union  had  the  right
to  represent  the  men  in  that  particular  mine,  and  that
an  election  was  the  only  way  to  determine  that  point.

We  announced  an  election  one  week  thereafter,
and  the  Board  went  as  a  whole  to  hold  the  election.
The  election  was  conducted  in  the  usual  manner:  in
which  such  elections  are  conducted,  with  a  check  of
the  pay  rolls  as  of  a  certain  date,  and  at  the  close  of
the  day  when  the  men  were  leaving  the  mines,  they
came  to  this  voting  place  for  the  purpose  of  casting
ballots.

They  filed  through,  and  practically  every  miner  in
the  mine  voted,  and  465,  I  believe,  cast  their  ballots;
245  of  those  voted  for  the  Progressive  Miners  of  Amer-
ica,  and  approximately  125  for  the  United  Mine  Work-
ers  of  America,  and  thereupon,  the  Board  declared  that
the  Progressive  Miners  of  America  had  carried  the  elec-
tion,  and  were  entitled  to  the  contract  in  that  mine.
We  ordered  the  operator  who  had  entered  into  this
contract  just  previously  with  the  United  Mine  Workers
to  cancel  his  agreement  wit
e  United  Mine  Workers,
and  enter  into  a  contract  with
the  representatives  of
the  majority  of  the  men  in  that  mine.
The  operator  was  adverse  to  this, but  he  did  it, and
the  United  Mine  Workers  accepted  it,  and  the  mine
opened  immediately,  and  as  far  as  the  conflict  was
concerned  thereafter,  it  was  resolved  completely.
Q  Was  the  strike  terminated  upon  the  holding  of
the  election,  and  the  declaration  of  the  result,  and  the
new  agreement  with  the  Progressive  miners?

A  Immediately.

ators  in  general  in  that  area,  and  one  member  was  a

A  Yes,  Mr.  Carmody.

A  Yes.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Now,  Dr.  Lapp,  are  there  collective  agreements
between  unions  and  operators  in  the  Bituminous  Coal
Mining  Industry?

A  Throughout  that  region,  I  speak  only  for  that
region,  Illinois,  Indiana,  and  Iowa,  almost  complete
unionization.

In  Indiana,  and  Iowa,  entirely  with  the  United
Mine  Workers  of  America,  and  in  Illinois,  partly  with
the  United  Mine  Workers,  and  partly  with  the  Progres-
sive  Mine  Workers  of  America;  with  very  few  excep-
tions,  if any  now,  excepting  some  of  these  small  dog-
holes,  the  entire  area  is  unionized,  and  moreover  prac-
tically  all  of  it  has  been  unionized  or  practically  forty
years,  without  any  interruptions—no,  strike  that.  There
has not  been  a  time  when  they  have  not  been  actually
unionized,  in  approximately  forty  years.

‘Q  Are  the employers  organized  in  any  association

dealing  collectively  with  unions?

A  In  each  State  there  is  an  operators’  association,
which  deals  collectively  for  the  operators  with  the  rep-
resentatives  of  the  miners,  in  making  their  bi-annual

649

In  the  State  of  Illinois,  there  is  an  addi-
agreements.
tional  operators’  association  which  deals  with  the  Pro-
gressive  Miners,  in  the  mines  which  they  have  jurisdic-
tion  of.

And  then  there  is  another  organization  of  oper-
ators  of  so-called  strip  mines  in  Indiana,  which  deals
with  the  members  of  the  United  Mine  Workers  who
have  been  operating  strip  mines.

Q  In  other  words,  is  it  fair  te  say  that  both  sides,

on  the  other,  are  organized  into  an  association  for  the
purpose  of  collective  bargaining?

A  Almost  completely  in  those  three  States.
Q  And  in  dealing  with  each  other  in  collective
bargaining,  each  group  is  represented—or  the  workers
are  represented  by  the  union  on  the  one  hand,  and  the
employers  by  an  employers’  association  on  the  other?

A  Yes.

ployers’  representatives  in  dealing  with  the  union,  and

agement  of  the  thing.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Are  these  collective  agreements  in  writing?
A  Yes,  and  published,  and  widely  distributed.
Everybody  has  a  copy  of  the  agreement  in  his  posses-
sion,  almost;  I think  almost  every  miner  has  a  copy.

Q  Do  these  agreements  provide  that  during  the
pendency  of  the  agreement  there  shall  be  no  strikes  or
stoppages?

A  Yes.

In  these  agreements,  in  all  three  states,
there  is  provision  that  there  should  be  no  stoppage  of
work,  that  the  disputes  shall  be  settled  by  a  system  of
arbitration,  and  in  two  of  the  States,  Illinois’  and  Indi-
ana,  there is an  umpire  who  is  the  final  authority  on
deciding  disputes.

In  the  meantime  the  work  goes  on,  and  if  any  party
violates  the  agreement  he  is  fined  a  sufficient  amount
so  that  it  rarely  happens  that  anybody  ever  actually
interferes  with  production.
[ do  not  know.  of  any  in-
stance  where  there  has  been  any  material  interruption
of  work  due  to  disagreement  over  the  terms  of  the  con-
tract.

They  have  chosen  in  both  of  those  States,  Indiana
and  Illinois,  very  competent  men  as  umpires.
John
White,  former  president  of  the  United  Mine  Workers,
was  for  many  years  umpire  in  Indiana,  and  William
B.  Wilson,  former  Labor  Secretary,  was  the  umpire  in
,
Illinois  for  many  years.

gard,  that  the  experience  of  forty  years  has  accumu-
lated  such  a  foundation  of  what  you  might  call  the
common  law  of  labor  relations  in  that  industry,  that
practically  every  question  that  could  possibly  arise  is
settled  on  the  basis  of  the  existing  experience  and  exist-
ing law.

Q  Now,  what  advantages,  in  your  opinion,  have  —
come  to  employees  as  a  result  of  the  practice  of  col-
lective  bargaining,  if  any?

A  The  main  advantages  are  that  they  approach
their  employers  on  more  nearly  equal  terms  than  they
possibly  could  have  approached  them  any  other  way.
You  take  the  Oil  Industry  as  an  example,  a  large  por-
tion  of  the  oil  executives  lived  in  New  York  City,  and
elsewhere  in  the  financial  centers  of  the  country,  anda  .
small  group  of men  down  in  Texas  would  have  a  pretty
difficult  time  trying  to  impress  absentee  owners;  not
only  that,  but  if  they  did  impress  them  they  could  not
necessarily  secure  any  agreements  on  equal  terms.

Even  small  unions  are  handicapped  in  that  respect,
and  it  is  only  when  the  unions  are  organized  on  a
lagge enough scale so that they can sit at  the council —
table  and  in comparative  equality  with  the  employers,
that
fully...

the  employees  have  a  chance  to  bargain  success-  ‘

z  A  Decidedly,  in  general,  and.  especially  so  in  the
:*-  Petroleum  Industry.  While  some  branches  of  the  Pe-
troleum  Industry  always  paid  fairly  good  wages,  even
before  collective  bargaining  was  introduced,  some  other

wages.  The  one  branch  of  the  Industry  where  that  has
been  proven  beyond  question  is  in  the  marketing  end
of  the  Petroleum  business,  where  wages  were  deplor-
ably  low,  and  where,  in  the  case  of  the  real  organiza-

Cleveland  was  one  of  the  best  examples,  ‘where
©  previously  wages  had  been  on  the  average  less  than

panies,  for  the  top: men,  and  the  wages  under  collectiv
agreements  steadily  are  advanced  to  a  point  where  at

tinued  collective  agreements,  and  they  will  no  doubt
lift  that  a  little  higher,  making  approximately  a  million

filling  stations  of  Cleveland  alone,  lifting  it: from  this
wage  which  was  a  sweated  wage,  up  not  to  what  we
call  a  living  wage,  because  $11200  would  not  be  even
that,  but  it  is  a  tremendous  advance  over  that.

As  for  working  conditions  in  all  branches  of  the
Industry,  my  observation  and  experience  would  lead
me  to  conclude  that  unions  have  decidedly  advanced
and  bettered  working  conditions.

experience  to  determine  what  better  things  can  be

I have  known  of  two  or  three  instances  where  men
went back  on  their  agreements  and struck,  but out af
all of the hundreds  of  instances  of a similar  charaeter,

anes, and they were,  as a rule, in those  cases,  beaten.

tatives  of  both  parties,  after  a  full  discussion  in  ad-

A  I know  of  no  instances,  except  where  there  is

Q  Now,  Dr.  Lapp,  what  has  the  general  public
gained  as  a  result  of  these  practices  of  collective  bar-
gaining  that  you  described?

A  The  very  fact  that  these  collective  bargaining
agreements  make  sure  that  we  have’  an  uninterrupted
flow  of  commerce  is  the  answer.

;

|

Namely,  the  public  can  be  assured  that  for  two
years,  in  the  case  of  the  Coal  Industry,  of  the  Middle
West,  there  is  going  to  be  peace  and  harmony  and
no  interruption  of  trade  in  coal.  They  know  in  the
case  of  the  other  industry,  every  other  industry  that  af-
fects  them  directly,  there is  going  to  be  no  interruption,
and  that  they  can  determine  their  business  for  a  year
or  two  years  on  the  basis  of  there  being  no  interruption
of  the  trade  in  which  they  are  engaged.
I think  that  is the  greatest  advantage  that  comes  to
the  public.  The prevention  of  strikes  naturally  is  a
great  benefit  to the  public,  since  the  public  must  suffer
directly  in every case  of  a  strike.

Now,  Dr.  Lapp,  are  you  familiar  in  a  general
way  with  the  provisions  of the  National  Labor  Relations
Act?

i

A  Yes,  fairly  familiar.

Q  Icall  your  attention  to  Section  8 of  the  National
Labor  Relations  Act,  which  describes  certain  practices
by  employers,  and  declares  such  practices  to  be  unfair
I will  ask  you  whether  or  not  in  your
laber  practices.
experience  such  practices  by  employers  have  affected
commerce.

A  These practices  in  the  industries  in  which  I have
worked  and  been  familiar,  decidedly  do  affect  com-
merce;  in  my  previous  testimony  upon  the  Oil  Industry,
as  well  as  the  Coal  Industry,  would  bear  that  out.

Q  Do  you  have  a  short  statement  of  analysis  of
the  National  Labor  Relations  Act  that  you  have  made,
or  rather,  of  the  problems  involved  in  the  Act?

A  I have  prepared  briefly  a  statement  about  ob-
structions  to  commerce,  and  I  would  like  to  say  that
my  experience  indicates  that  strikes  are  a  direct  obstruc-
tion  of  commerce,  and  not  an  indirect  obstruction.

Commerce  stops  when  a  strike  occurs,  or  com-
merce  is  very  heavily  burdened  when  a  strike  occurs,
and  therefore  the  causes  that  bring  on  strikes  are  the
direct  causes of a  direct  effect  upon  commerce.

In  this  connection,  I would  add  that  physical  ob-
structions  to  commerce  are  as  plain  as  direct  effects.
If  you  obstruct  a  river,  you  obstruct  commerce,  ever
so  little,  but  it  is  an  obstruction  to  commerce.

If  you  conspire  to  contro!  the  products,  and  prices
in  commerce,  you  obstruct  commerce,  and  you  directly
burden  commerce;  and  if  you  pass  State  Laws  which
interfere  with  commerce,  such  as  taxation  laws,  as  the
one  ruled  upon  the  other  day,  you  obstruct  commerce,
and  in  this  case  you  obstruct  interstate  commerce,  in
that  particular  case.

You  obstruct  commerce  also  by unfair  trade  prac-
tices,  which  impose  burdens  in one  way  or  another,
but  most  of all you  more  directly  obstruct  commerce
when  through  strikes,  you stop the flow  of commerce,

I have  had  considerable  experience  in

icular  field  of  labor  relations.

Q  Will  you  tell  us  in  a  general  way  what  that
experience  has  been?  I was  referring  to  the  union  man-
agement  cooperation.
A  I  understand.

The  experience  goes  back  as
far  as  1918,  when  in  the  Federal—or  United  States
Army  Arsenals,  we  tried  out  an  arrangement  of  hav-
ing  the  labor.  organizations  in  those  particular  arsenals,
take  part  in  furthering  the  programs  of  the  arsenal,
by  way  of  improving  production,  helping  to  utilize
the  services  and  products  of  the  arsenals  by  various
departments  of  the  Government,  increasing  the  effi-
ciency  and  the  quality  of  the  workmanship  of  the
products  of these  institutions,  and  in  general  being help-
ful  in  accomplishing  the  purposes  for  which  those  par-
ticular  manufacturing  plants  were  established.

Q  What  was  your  connection  with  the  arsenal?
A  At  that  time  I was  in  the  service  of  the  United
States  War  Department,  first  as  a  captain,  and  subse-
quently  as  a  Director  of  the  Bureau  which  sought  to
bring  about  a  greater  utilization  of  these  arsenals,  and
the  manufacturing  facilities  of  the  arsenals  by  all  De-
partments  of  the  Government.

Q  What  did  you  find  the  conditions  at  the  plant

to  be,  with  respect  to  efficiency  and  morale?

A  Well,  more  or  less  the  conventional  picture,
which  one  finds,  where  there  is  a  misunderstanding  as  .
to  the  true  place  that  organizations  of  labor  and  the
‘spokesmen  of  labor  might  possibly—or  the  true  func-
tions  of  the  organization  of  labor  and  the  spokesmen  of

I  might  say  that  immediately  prior  to  my  advent
to  the  Pittsburgh  district,  there  were  certain  labor  dif-
ficulties  that  had  arisen,  which  had  culminated  in  a
strike,  and  it  was  subsequent  to  that  strike  that  I came
into  the  picture.

~

-

-  The  strike  primarily  centered  around  many  of  the
difficulties  with  which  industry  is  confronted  today  by
virtue  of  a  misunderstanding,  or  an  unwillingness,
rather,  to  have  or  grant  men  that  type  of freedom  of as-
sociation  that,  for  example,  the  National  Industrial
Disputes  Act,  contemplates.
The  strike  turned  largely  around  what  in  the  :
estimation  of  the  men  were  unfair  labor  practices.
However,  the  strike  was-settled,  and  in  that  particular
atmosphere,  it  was,  that  I came  into  the  plant.

/

I say  that,  because:  that  only  made  the  task  all  the

But  all  that  it  asks  is  that  there  be  an  understand-
ing  as  to  how  it  shall  proceed,  and  that  labor  for  its

Consequently,  in  connection  with  thise
whole  program,  it  developed  very  early  that  one  thing

whole  program,  it  developed  that  one  of  the  serious
things  was  stabilization  or  regularization  of  employ-
ment.  It was  not  reasonable  to  expect  men  and  women
to  take  any  interest  in  furthering  efficiency,  for  exam-
ple,  if  tomorrow  somebody  was  going  to  be  laid  off.
But  it  so  happened,  as  the  situation  was  canvassed,
that  there  was  a  great  deal  of  opportunity,  if  the  man-
agement  was  willing,  to  really  and  truly  do something
about  regularizing  employment,  in  other  words,  stabil-

line,  and  indicated  labor’s  willingness  to  really  be  help-
ful  in  the  process.
Q  And  did  this  so-called  hostile  attitude  of  labor
to  improved  methods  become  converted  to  a  welcom-
ing by labor  of  more  efficient  methods  of  production?
_
A  Yes,  as  a  matter  of  fact  it  was  very  interesting
to  me  because  in  my  contact  with  the  situation  on
the  ground,  I was  struck  by  the  interest  and  the  pride

*

that  individual  men  themselves  took  in  concrete  prop-
ositions  that  had  been  the  result  of  their  suggestions,
and  the  pride  that  they  manifested,  for  example,  in  .
the  reduction  of  defective  cars  and  locomotives,  and  in
the  conversion  of  materials,  and  in  that  connection,  all
of  those  matters,  and  this  whole  program  is  utilized  to
acquaint  the  rank  and  file  with  the  economics  of  the
shop,  that  is,  with  the  costs,  and  other  items  which  are
usually  confined  or  are  supposed  to  be  of  interest
only  to  the  management.

The  minute  that  that  was  done,  the  men  took  a
much  greater  interest  in  the  purposes  for  which  the  in-
stitution  existed.

benefits  as  nearly  as  they  could  be  determined.

It  has  given  the  employees  a  new  reason  for  want-
ing  to  join  and  play  a  constructive  ‘part  in  the  organiza-
tions  of  labor,  and  by that  token,  in  the  conduct  of  the
railroad.

Q  What  about  the  management?
A  From  the  viewpoint  of  the  management  of  the
railroads,  the  policy  has  reduted  the  petty  annoyances
and  labor  disputes  to  a  minimum,  and  improved  rail-
road  service  by  bettering  service,  and  decreasing  loco-
motive  defects,  and  so  reducing  train  delays.

_

It  has  through  its  insistence  on  regularization  of
employment,  also  regularized  production  of shops,  there-
by  increasing  efficiency.

It  has  reduced  material  expenses,  through  the  in-
terest  ‘developed  on  the  part  of  the  Men  in  material
conservation.

*

It  has  improved  shop  efficiency  and  economy  not
only  through  the  interest  of  the  men  in  production
but  also  through  the  mobilizing  of  thousands  of  useful
practice  ideas  latent  among  the  employees.

It  has  stimulated  the  managements  to  devise  and
introduce  improved  methods  of  shop  operation,  work |
planning  and  the  like.

|

It  has  greatly  improved  the  morale  of the  rail-
road,  nd  has  greatly  improved  the  confidence  of  the

—

679

 ~™

in  those  roads  that  have  seen  fit  to  adopt  a  pro-
of  that  particular  kind.

It has increased  the  prestige  of  the  carriers.

of  the  public,  what  advantages  briefly  has the  public
derived  as  a  result  of  the  operation of these  plans?

A  Which  incidentally,

A  I  might  preface  what  I  have  to  say  on  that
score,  that  obviously  you  might  label  cooperation  -be-
tween  labor  organizations  and  management—it  can  only
be  sound  if  it  contemplates  benefits  to  the  public  as
well  as  to  the  railroads  and  their  owners  and  the
employees.
.  . The  plan,  in  short,  might  be  referred  to  as  co-

nt.

In  that  connection,  there  is  no  doubt  that  the  plan,
because  of  the  factor  of  greater  safety,  and  for
greater  concern  on  the  part  of  the  rank  and  file  in
the  quality  of  service  which  the  railroad  renders,  are
all matters  of  decided  public  interest,  and  it  is  along
that  general  line  that  the plan  can  be  said  to  have
justified  itself  from  the  public  point  of  view.
.
I would  say  that  that  is,  generally  speaking,  evi-

enjoyed,  that  have  seen  fit  to  adopt  a  program  of  this
particular  kind.

it will become  very difficult  if not impossible  to bring
about  the  constructive  relationship  between  labor  and
management  which  I have  described.

Q  Now,  I call  to  your  attention  a  reference  on  page
30  of  your  Wertheim  lecture,  the  following  language:
“With  the  elimination  of  the  major  cause
for  disputes—namely,  differences  about  the  sta-
tus  of  the  employees’  unions—and  with  the  em-
phasis  on  the  prevention  of  grievances,  the  ener-
gy  of  management  which  was  formerly  dissi-
pated  in  adjusting  labor  disputes  is  devoted  un-
der  cooperation  to  better  planning  and  per-
formance.”

With  particular  reference  to  the  phrase  “differ-
ences  about  the  status  of  the  employees’  unions”  state
whether  or  not  it  has  been  your  experience  that  the
question  of  the  status  of  employees’  unions  ordinarily
is  an  important  cause  of  unrest  among  employees.

A  As  long  as  there  is  doubt  about  the  status  of
the  organizations  of  employees,  there  is  unrest,  and
there  are  all  kinds  of  difficulties,  misunderstandings,
and  there  is  a  distinct  lowering  of  morale  in  the  en-
terprise  as  a  whole.

Those  doubts  must  be  resolved,  and  should  be  re-
solved,  preferably  by  peaceful,  orderly  methods,  such
as  an  election,  rather  than  by  a  show  of  force.

Q  Now,  I  think  that  I  have  one  more  question,
Does  this  plan  of  cooperation,  or  these
and  it  is  this:
plans  of  cooperation  which  you  have  been  describing,
do  anything  to  the  employee in so  far  as  his  dignity  or
responsibility  in the  industry  is  concerned?

A  It  is my observation,

and it has been  my  obser-
vation,  that  with  the  acceptance  or  with  the  resolution
of  the  misunderstandings  and  about  the  right  of  men
to organize,  the acceptance  on the  part  of the  man-
agement,  for example,  of the organization  of men,  the
making  and  maintaining  of  agreements  between ‘such
orgenigations,  and  finally  the growing  of  these  agree-
ments  into.a  general  cooperative relationship,  the  pur-
pose.of:  which is  to serve  the public, has a very  stimu-
lating  effect,  upon  thé rank  and file in its enthusiasm,

685

in its  feeling  of security,  in  its  attitude,—in  other  words,
in  its  concept  of  the  part,  and  the  constructive  part,
that  it  is  playing.
It  makes,  as  I like  to  expréss  it—it
‘makes  for  a  genuine  concept  of  citizenship  in  industry,
and  employees  do  not  feel  merely  as  a  wage  earner  who
is  there  to  get  what  he  can  for  a  few  hour  that  he
works,  but  he  feels  that  he  is  playing  a  dignified,  help-
ful,  constructive  part  in  a  useful  enterprise.

Q  Do  you  think  that  you  can  have  permanent
and  ultimate  peace  until  the  worker  is  in  that  psycho-
logical  mood?

;

A  I think  that  as  long  as  we  do  not  have  a  rela-

concern  for  which  he  works,  he  isn’t  giving  of  the  best,
and  that  in  turn  may  result  in  serious  breakdowns,  in
the  way  of  industrial  unrest.

outer-garments,  coats  and  suits.

A  When  I  entered  the  industry,  I  went  into  the

factory.

There  were  no  labor  relations  except  direct  ones
between  the  employer  and  the  employee,  and  there  was
no  established  union  in  Cleveland.

I  think  that  perhaps—I  can’t  give  you  the  exact

ored  the  formation  of  an  interim  organization,  asking

‘were  periodic  meetings  between  the  manage-
