ment

Q  How  did  the  wages  paid  by  your  company  com-
pare  with  the  wages  paid  by  the  rest  of  the  Cleveland
‘market?

A  Well,  there  was  no  exact  basis  of  knowledge.
I  think  that  the  workmen  regarded  this  factory  as  a
preferable  place  to work,  but  there  were  a  number  of
reasons,  but  I  wouldn’t  be  able  to  tell  you  positively
just  what  the  difference  of  the  rates  was.
I think  that
the preference,  if there  was  a  preference,  was  based
on  a  lenger  continuity  of  employment,  rather  than  a
marked  differential  in  wage.

695

There  may  have  been  some  differential  in  wage,
but  there  were  no  exact  statistics  kept  of  those  matters
at  that  time.

Q  Wete  there  other  reasons  why  your  shop  was
preferred  to  those  of  other  members  of  the  industry,
working  conditions,  or  other  reasons  of  that  nature?

A  Well,  I  think  that  there  were  perhaps  pleas-
anter  surroundings,  and  I  think  that  there  was  more
or  less  general  recognition  that  the  management  was
eager  to  do  anything  that  it  could  to  forward  the  in-
terests  and  well-being  of  the  workers,
I  think  that  we
had  a  number  of  features  that  I  think  we  were  among
the  first  to  install  a  nurse  in  the  factory,  a  welfare
worker  in  the  factory,  to  look  into  the  home  conditions

_  of  different  people.

I  remember  one  case  that  showed  us  a  good  deal,
and  unless  we  had  provided  somebody  who  put  all  of
her  time  into  that  effort,  we  would  never  have  found |
it  out,  but  one  of  the  cutters  was  rather  lax  in  his
work,  and  finally  we  determined  to  investigate  his
home  conditions,  and  we  found  that  he  had  in  his  home
an  imbecile  child  whose  existence  he  and  his  wife  were
concealing  from  their  neighbors  and  from  everybody
else,  and  they  were  sensitive  about  the  fact  that  their
child  was  an  imbecile,  and  this  child  was  growing  up,
and  he  was  five  or  six  years  old  by  that  time,  and  he
was  a  constant  worry  to  the  parents,  of  course,  and  it
resulted  in  neglect  of  the  child,  I mean  intelligent  care
was  lacking.

So  as  a  result  of  that  investigation,  this  child  was
put  in  an  institution,  and  the  parents  said  for  the  first
time  they  had  reason  to  feel  that  they  were  doing
everything  they  could  for  this  child,  and  before  that
they  were  impeding  its  existence.
That  is  a  marked  example.
I think  that  a  good  many  of  the -girls came  from
more  or  less  ignorant  homes,  and  their  own  sanitary
were—and  it  was  necessary  to  tell  them  many

Z

v

rs

a

696

things  that  better  instructed  households  would  have
cared  for  by instructions  from  their  parents,  and  it  was
a  surprising  thing.  When  we  built  a  new  factory,  at
first  the  condition  of  the  lavatories  was  deplorable,  but
many  people  hadn’t  learned  the  ordinary  decencies  of
life,  but  in  a  comparatively  few  months  that  condition
was  entirely  corrected.

Q  Now,  Mr.  Black—
A  If  any  of  this  is  not  germane,  don’t  hesitate  to

limit  it.

Q  I  just  wanted  to  bring  out  the  general  attitude
and  labor  policies  of  your  company,  toward  its  em-
ployees,  as  a  matter  of  background.

Now,  previous  to  1911,  did  the  garment  manufac-

turers  in  Cleveland  deal  with  the  unions?

No.
Was  there  a  strike  in  that  year?
In  1911  there  was  a  strike.
What  was  the  purpose  of  that  strike?
Well,  this  union  that  had  endeavored  to  form
itself  in  New  York  in  1898,  gathered  new  strength  unto
itself,  and  new  leadership,  and  in  1910  again,  after  a
prolonged  struggle,  succeeded  in  establishing  itself  as
an  important  factor  in  the  New  York  market,  and  in
fact  that  union  came  to  an  agreement  with  the  manu-
facturers’  associations  of  New  York,  which  were  formed
to  surpress  this  strike,  to  have  union  recognition,  and
union  working  agreements,  and  in  those  settlements  it  -
was  stipulated  by  the  New  York  manufacturers,  read-
ily  acceded  to  by  the  union  leaders,  that  they  would
establish  similar  conditions  in  Cleveland  in  the  short-
est possible  time  so the following  year  this  same  move-
ment  came  to  Cleveland,  and  resulted  in  a  strike  in
Cleveland  that  lasted  five  months,  but  was  not  success-
ful  in  Cleveland.

Q  The  purpose  of that  strike  then  was to organize

the  Cleveland  market?

A  Ye.

697

Q  How  many  employees  would  you  say  were  in-

volved  in  the  1911  strike?

|

A  In  the  whole  market?
Q  Yes.
A  There  were  several  thousand,  but  just  how
many,  whether  there  were  over  10,000,  I  don’t  know,
but  somewhere  between  five  and  ten  thousand.

Q  How  long  did  that  last?
A  Five  months,  from  June  to  October.
Q  You  stated  that  it  was  not  successful?
A  No,  it  was  not  successful.

It  was  completely

unsuccessful.

Pe
Q  Now,  was  there  a  subsequent  effort  to  organize

the  Cleveland  market  at  a  later  time?

A  There  was  stationed  there  an  agent  of  this
union  who  carried  on  a  very  active  campaign  through
literature,  meetings,  and  so  on,  and  he  was  a  constant
campaigner  from  1911,  or  preceding  1911,  to  1917.

Now,  in  1917,  the  War  broke  out,  and  a  number
‘of  the  manufacturers  in  Cleveland  wanted  to  help  in
the  war,  and  therefore  they  expressed  their  willingness
to  improvise  the  manufacture  of  soldiers
uniforms.
The  War  Department  stipulated  the  price  that  could
be  paid  for  these  coats,  that  were  to  be  made  for  the
use  Of  the  soldiers,  and  I  think  the  price  set  was  89
cents  a  coat.
_
Well,  I don’t  think  any  manufacturer  in  Cleveland
was  able  to  make  them  for  less  than  $1.75  or  $2.00,  or
more,  but  yet  they  were  willing  to  undertake  this,  not
to  make  money  out  of  it  but  to  do  their  share  of  win-
ning  the  War.

|

Now,  I should  say  of  the  twenty-six  manufacturers
in  Cleveland,  I  remember  that  there  were  seven  or
eight  that  volunteered  this  kind  of  service.

Q  Was  there  an  effort  at  this  time,  during  the

War,  to  organize  the  Cleveland:  market?

A  Yes,  the union then called  a  strike  in all of the
factories  in  Cleveland,  particularly  in  these  factories

A  Well,  it  was  settled  with  an  agreement  between

Q  Now,  wouid  you  explain  the  adjustment  which

came  out  of  that  strike?

A  Well,  we  appealed  to  the Secretary  of  War  to
appoint  an  Impartial  Committee  to  decide  upon  the
questions  that  might  arise,  and  we  said  that  .we  would
make  an  agreement  with  the  Secretary  of  War,  and
any  Commission  that  he  chose  to  appoint.  We  would
determine  the  matters  according  to  the  judgment  of
these  referees,  as  they  were  called;  and  Mr.  Baker  was
a  Cleveland  man,  and  the  Cleveland  manufacturers  had
a  high  degree  of  confidence  in  him,  and  they  were
willing  to  abide  by  any  appointees  that  he  might  make,
and  he  appointed  three  of  the  best  men  connected  with
the  Labor  Department,  or  the  War  Department,  at  that
time,  that  was  Professor  Hopkins,  who  was  president
of Dartmouth,  Major  Rosenson,  of New  York,  and a
lawyer,  and Mr.  McLean,  of  New  Hampshire,
also  a
lawyer,  and they  volunteered  their  services,  and acted
in the capacity  of referees,  to whom  all matters  might

Q  Was  there  at  a  subsequent  time  an  agreement
entered  directly  between  the  union  and  the  manufac-
turers’  association?

A  Well,  Mr.  Carmody  reminded  me  of  this  this
morning,  and  I think  his recollection  of this  is Gorrect:
That  after  a  year  and  a  half  or two  years  of  this  in-
direct  contact  with  the  union,  through  this  partial  gec-
ognition,  a  direct  agreement  was  reached,  and  that  dif-
ference  was  more  apparent  than  real.

Q  Would  you  state  some  of  the  subsequent  mem-
bers  of  the  Board  of  Referees,  after  the  original  board?
A  After  service  of  perhaps  six  months  or  more,
Mr.  Hopkins  felt  that  he  could  no  longer  serve,  and
that  as  president  of  Dartmouth,  his  duties  were  too
onerous  to  allow  him  to  continue  this  activity,  and  so
he  resigned,  and  Judge  Julian  Mack  was  appointed  in
his  stead.

Judge  Mack  served  for  two  or  three  years,  and  per-
haps  a  longer  period  than that,  and  I think Judge  Mack
was  active  as  long  as  I was  active,  and  perhaps  for  a
longer  period,  and  then  I understand  that  he  resigned,
and  in  his  place  was  appointed  Dr.  Hollander,  Profes-
sor of Economics  at  Johns  Hopkins,  and in more  recent
years,  Morris  Cook  of Philadelphia,  was  appointed, I
think,  in conjunction  with  Dr.  Hollander,  but in place
of Major  Rosenson.

the Rural  Electrification,  is that  the  same  man?

A  I dare  say  it is, but I am not positive.  He was

BY  MR.  CARMODY:
Q  Would  you  say  that  that  would  be  justification
for  the  creation  of  Boards  such  as  fhis,  not  perhaps
this  particular  Board,  but  Boards  such  as  this,  which
under  the  general  supervision  of  the  Congress  and  by
appointment  of  the  President,  does  undertake  to  de-
velop  better  relationships  between  these  two  contend-
ing  groups?

A  I  certainly  do,  and  I  think  that  unless  that  is
undertaken,  the  mere  recognition  of  collective  bargain-
ing  which  is  an  important  step  forward  in  this  move-
ment,  doesn’t  bring  about  enough  progress  to  make  it
worth  while.

Now,  you  can’t  get  along  without  that  element,  but
if  people  don’t  realize  that  that  is  only  part  of  the  pre-
cess,  and  I  think  that  that  is  why  the  labor  movement
in  America  has  made  such  slow  progress  as  it  has,  or
one  of  the  important  reasons  why  it  has.

:

Mr.

Black.

Did  the  employees  of  H.  Black  &  Company,  did
they  become  members  of  the  union  and  deal  with  you
through  their  own  organization?

A  Well,  let  me  see.

When  this  strike  in  1911
took  place,  I  think  that  there  were  not  over  thirty-five
avowed  unionists  in  the  factory,  but  when  the  strike
was  made  effective,  two-thirds  of  the  employees  stayed
away  from  work,  and  the  other  third  came  to  work
every  day,  running  the  gauntlet  of  militant  picket  lines
every  night  and  every  morning.
Q  This  was  in  1917?

|

705

A  The  1911  strike.  Now,  in  1917,  no  larger  pro-
portion  of  confirmed  unionists  were  present,  a  very
small  proportion,  but  our  own  management  seized  this
opportunity  to  make  what  they  believed or what  I be-
lieved  was  a  step  forward  towards  the  right  kind  of
relationship.  We  were  willing  to  entrust  our  business
to  an  experiment,  and  if  at  least  the  fundamental  cen-
ditions  were  such  as  we  believed  might  ultimately  work
out  on  the  basis  of  justice.

.
In  your  opinion,
from  your  experience  in  these  matters,  are  elections
conducted  by  an  Impartial  Agency  such  as  a  Govern-
mental  Board,  practical  and  satisfactory  ways  of  deter-
mining  the  representatives  of  the  employees?

Q  I  have  one  other  question.

A  Well,  I think  that  that  is  the  greatest  step  for-
ward  that  has  been  made  in  industrial  relationships
that  I am  cognizant  of  in  the  last  twenty  years.  That
is  only  a  beginning  of  it,  but  I believe  that  that  makes
possible  a  true  expression  of  choice  on  the  part  of  em-
ployees,  no  matter  how  it. may  fall  out,  and  no  matter
how  it  may  develop.
I  think  that  that  is  an  omen  of
great  promise,  and  it  is  little  more  than  a  promise  as
yet,  but  the  fact  that  there  is  machinery  that  may  be
set  up,  and  that  demonstrates  a  method  by  which  that
very  important  element  can  be  determined,  is  of  tre-
mendous  importance  and  valwe.

eral  months.

Q  In  any  event,  is  it  true  that  as  a  result  of  the
intervention  of  the  Secretary  of  Labor,  this  general
strike  in  the  rubber  tire  industry  was  averted?

A  Yes.
Q  Now,  what  was  the  occasion  for  the  appoint-
ment  of  the  fact-finding  board  in  accordance  with  the
provisions  of  the  agreement  calling  for  the  appoint-
ment  of  a  fact-finding  board  under  certain  circum-
tances?

A:  It  was  due  to,  primarily,  the  announced  pur-
pose  on  the  part  of  the  Goodyear  Company—-may  I
_  thange  “primarily”?  I would  like  to  change  that  word
_  —but  it  was  due  to  the  announcement  of  the  company

718

of  a  change  in  the  working  day  from  what  had  been
in  effect  since  October  of  1930,  of  the  6-hour  day,  to
the  8-hour  day,  with  flexibility,  and  also  due  to  alleged
discrimination  against  the  United  Rubber  Workers  of
America.

Q  And  now  just  so  I may  understand,  what  is  the
phrase  “with  flexibility”  in  terms  of  the  discussion  we
are  having?

A  Under  the  plan  which  had  been  in  effect,  they
had  the  6hour  day,  which  the  company  had  adopted
in  October  of  1930,  and  if  the  demands  for  their  prod-
uct  increased,  they  increased  the  length  of  the  day,  but
what  the  company  was  asking,  and  what  in  effect
was  an  8-hour  day,  with  flexibility  downward,  if  pro-
duction  demands  decreased  but  the  company  was  anx-
ious  to  get  their  employees  on  a  36-hour  rate,  and  they
had  worked  an  average  of  31  and  a  fraction  during
1935.

Q  Now,  did  someone  make  a  request  of  the  Sec-
retary  of  Labor  for  the  appointment  of  a  fact-finding
committee?

A  The  request  was  made  by  letter  under  date  of
October  25th,  a  letter  from  the  Goodyear  Local  of  the
union,  of  the  United  Rubber  Workers  of  America,  and
by  telegram  under  date  of  October  28th,  from  the
Lodge  208  of  the  International  Association  of  Ma-
chinists.

Q  Is  there  an  organization  at  the  Goodyear  plant

known  as  the  Assembly?

A  Yes.
Q  What  is  the  nature  of  that  organization?
A  .It  consists  of  a  House  of  Representatives  of
40  members,  and  a  Senate  of  2)  members,  and  they
are  chosen  by  employees  of  the  Goodyear  Akron  Com-
peny,  and  anyone  is  eligible  to  vote  who  is  on  the  pay
roll  the  day  before  the  election  date.

plaining  his  four-square  plan,  which  he  sold  to,  or  got

year  plant  was  one  of  them,  and  I am  not  too  sure  to-
day.

field.

mediately  after  the  War.
BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Now,  as  I  understand  it,  upon  this  request  of

A  Yes.

conduct  an  inquiry?

A  Yes,  we  began  our  hearings  on  the  22d.

A  We  completed  our  report,  and  the  report  was

1935.

were  members  of  the  industrial  assembly,  and  man-
agement  of  the  union?

A  We  did.

Of  course,  the  request  was  wholly
from  the  United  Rubber  Workers  of  America,  and  the
‘International  Association  of  Machinists.
The  Secretary,
before  the  appointment  of  the  committee,
had  con-
ferred  with  the  company,  and  indicated  that  she  was

tion.

720

Upon  reaching  Akron,  a  telephone  call  awaited  ma
from  the  Chairman  of  the  Industrial  Relations  Com-
mittee  of  the  Industrial  Assembly,  and  upon  communi-
cating  with  them,  they  asked  the  privilege  of  present-
ing  such  facts  as  they  had  to  the  Board.

Every  opportunity  was  given  them  to  present  those

facts.

Q  Did  you  listen  to  those  facts?
A  We  did;  and  of  course  they  had  a  great  deal
of  information,  because  of  their  close  contact,  person-
ally,  with  the  workers.

Q  Did  you  then  make  a  report  to  the  Secretary

of  Labor?

A  We  made  a  report  under  date  of  December

16th.

mittee  of  the  Assembly  a  joint  committee  of  manage-
ment  and  employees,  or  just  a  committee  of  em-
ployees?

THE  WITNESS:  Employees.

Q  Prior  to  the  making  public  of  the  report  which

tary  communicate  with  the  management  or  Mr.  Litch-
field?

A  The  Secretary  did,  and  invited  Mr.  Litchfield

and  conferences  were  held  on  two  occasions.

Q  And  then  subsequently,  was  the  report  made

A  The  report  was  released  early  in  January.

721

A  Yes.

;

ence?

te  WITNESS:  He  was  not.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  What  were  the  findings  of  the  committee  as  to
whether  or  not  the  Goodyear  management  had  failed
to  enter  into  negotiations  with  its  employees  or  their
representatives?

.

Please  state  whether  or  not  the  Board  made  any
findings  as  to  whether  the  management  had  entered
into  negotiations  with  employees  or  their  representa-
tives  fairly?

;

A  May  I read  the  paragraph?
Q  Yes.
A  “The  Fact-Finding  Board  is  of  the  opinion  that
in  the  present  controversy,  involving  hours,  wages,  and
lay-offs,  Goodyear  management  did  not  fairly  enter
into  negotiations  with  employees  or  with  any  of  their
representatives,  but  rather  promulgated  a  policy  and
then  sought  endorsement  by  representatives  of  em-
ployees  or,  in  effect,  merely  gave  notice  concerning
such  policy.”

Q  What  was  the  finding  of  the  Board  as  to  wheth-
er  or  aot  the  Goodyear  management,  in  dealing  with
representatives  of  the  employees,  had  discriminated
in  favor  of  a  certain  group  or  against  another  group?
A  “The  Fact-Finding  Board  is  of  the  opinion
that  Goodyear  management,  in  dealing  with  represen-
tatives  of  employees,  has  discriminated  between  A.  F.
of  L.  unions  and  fhe  Goodyear  Industrial  Assembly  in
reference  to  notices  concerning  changes  in wage  rates
and  conditions  of  employment,  in  freedom  of  contact
with  employees,  in  allowances  for  time  spent  on  com-
mittee  work,  and  in  failure  to  give  frank  recognition
to  representatives  of  the  unions  as  ‘chosen  representa-
tives  of  any  group  of  employecs.’”

Q  What  were  the  factual  bases  for  these  findings,

722

now,  with  particular  reference  to  freedom  of  contact
with  employees?

A  The  Industrial  Assembly  had  frequent  contact
with  the  employees,  and  with  representatives  of  the
management,  the  foreman,  or  all  of  the  people  up  the
line.

Q  When  you  say  “contact”  you  mean  the  right  to

confer  and  discuss  problems  of  employment?

,

A  Yes;  while

the  United  Rubber  Workers  of
America  were  somewhat  limited,  and  they  were  not
allowed  to  enter  the  plant  except  on  the  shift  on  which
they  were  working  and  making  their  contacts.

Q  I notice  in  the  findings  of  the  committee  with

respect  to  the  following,  you  say,—I  am  quoting—

“Failure  to  give  frank  recognition  to  representa-
tives  of  the  unions  as  chosen  representatives  of  any
group  of  employes,  that”—that  the  phrase  “chosen  rep-
resentatives  of  employees”  is  in  quotation  marks.

Is  that  borrowed  from  the  agreement?
A  Yes.
Q  What  were  the  facfs  as  to  that?
I  mean,  the  failure  of  the  company  to  give  frank

recognition  to  chosen  representatives?

A  Based  largely  upon  the  two  clauses  preceding
that,  freedom  of  contact,  and  then  failure  to  give  no-
tices,  and  members  of  the  Indystrial  Assembly  were
given  notices  of  lay-offs  24  hou in  advance  of  the
board,  and  during
three-day  posting  on  the  bulletin
that  twenty-four  hours  they  were  free  to  take  up  any
question  with  representatives  of  the  management,  and
the  A.  F.  of  L.  did  not  have  that  24-hour  notice.

Q  Now,  what  was  the  condition  among  the  em-
ployees  in  so  far  as  peacefulness  or  unrestfulness—or
what  was  the  reaction  of  employees  to  these  matters
that  you  found  in  your  report?  What  did  you  find  the
state  of mind  of  the  employees  to  be?

A  Well,  they  didn’t  know  what  was  going  to  hap-
pen.  They  felt  that  the  management  had  been  evasive

723

in  its  explanations,  and  they  felt  that  with  the  increase
in  the  length  of  the  day  that  they  would  be  expected
to  keep  up  the  same  speed  for  an  eight-hour  day,  or
for  the  longer  day.  They  seldom  used  the  term  “eight
hours”  as  they  would  for  the  shorter  day,  and they
had  speeded  up  with  the  shorter  day,  and  the  em-
ployees  felt  that  they  couldn’t  maintain  the-speed.

MR.  CARMODY:  You  mean  when  the  hours  were
reduced  from  eight  to  six  in  1930,  that  the  workers  had
speeded  up  so  that  they  were  producing  more  per  hour
during  the six  hours  than  they  had  been  per  hour  in
the  eight  hours  previously?

THE  WITNESS:  Of  their  own  accord.
MR.  CARMODY:  And  then  they  were

afraid
that  if  the  management  changed  from  the  six-hour  day
to  the  longer  day,  that’  the  management  would  expect
them  to  turn  out  as  much  per  hour  in  the  eight  hours
as  they  had  been  turning  out  in  the  six  hours?

THE  WITNESS:  Correct.
BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  What  was  their  state  of  mind  or  reaction  to

that,  in  terms  of  content  or  unrest?

A  Unrest.  They  felt  that  the  older  men  in  par-

ticular  would  not  be  able  to  keep  up  that  pace.

They  felt  also,  that  with  the  increased  hours  that
the  result  would  be laying  off  of  a  considerable  number
of employees,  and  the estimate  ran  from  15 to 2 per cent,
and  the  expression  was  often  used  by  the  employees,
that  it probably  meant  everyone  who  had  been  there
less  than  five  years  would  be  dropped  unless  produc-
tion  should  be  increased  beyond  the  estimates  of  the
‘company.

Q  I call  your  attention  to  the  findings  of  the  com-
mittee,  that  in  putting  this  change  in  hours  into  effect,
the  committee  found  that  the  Goodyear  management
did  not  fairly  enter  into  negotiations  with  their  em-
ployees,  but  rather  promulgated  a  policy  and  then
sought  enforcement  by  representatives  of  employees,  or

.

in  effect  merely  gave  notices  concerning  such  policy.

;

workers  with  respect  to  this  conduct  by  the  manage-
ment  to  be?

attitude  towards  this  announcement  by  the  manage-
ment,  without  conference  and  consultation,  and  work-
ing  the  matter  out  with  them?

A  Very  greatly  disturbed,  both  members  of  the
the  United  Rubber

industrial  assembly  as  well

A  On  October  1,  1985,  the  Goodyear  Industrial
Assembly  adopted  what  was  known  as  Joint  Bill  No.
1,  House  and  Senate  in  joint  session.

Q  It  sounds  like  an  Act  of  Congress.
A  They  are  a  Congress.  “By  the  Rules  and  Regu-

lations  Committee.

“Subject:  Standard  Six-Hour  Working  Day.
“Whereas:  Rumors  are  prevalent  through-
out  the  factory  with  regard  to  a  return  to  the
eight-hour  working  day,  and

“Whereas:  These  rumors  are  causing  con-
siderable  unrest  among  Goodyear  employees,  and
“Whereas:  A  large  majority  of  these  em-
ployees  are  demanding  the  retentior  of  the  six-
hour  day, and

“Whereas:  The  Goodyear  Tire  &  Rubber
Company  has  been  a  pioneer  in  the  six-hour
movement.

érefore:  Be  it  enacted  by the  Sixteenth
Goodyear  ‘Industrial Assembly,  both  bodies  con-
curring,  that the  six-hour  working  day  be  adop-
policy in all divisions  of the Good-

nounced—

committee  called  on  Mr.  Sjusser,  on  the  25th,  and  he

Q  Some  time  in  March,  did  you  return  to  Akron?
I am  jumping  ahead  now,  and  I will  come  back  in

A  On  March  17th,  the  Secretary  of  Labor  tele-
phoned  me  in  Columbus,  and  asked  me  if  I would  again
perform  a  service,  and  to  go  to  Akron,  and  confer,  par-
ticularly  with  the  company  officials.

Q  At  that  time,  was  a  strike  in  full  force?
A  The  strike  was  in  full  force,  and  negotiations
had  been  under  way  the  previous  week,  but  had  not
It  was  a  very  hazardous
been  continued  that  week.
situation,  she  felt,  and  I  proceeded  at  once  to  Akron
and  joined  the  two  Federal  mediators  who  were  there,
and  I was  to  go  without  any  information  being  given  to
the  president,  nor  did  I  make  the  best  contacts.

729

Q  After  you  arrived,  did  you  learn  whether  there
had  been  sporadic,  or  spontaneous,  or  so-called  sit-
down  strikes?

A  There  had  been  a  number  of  sit-down  strikes,
some  in  other  piants  in  Akron,  and  several  in  the
Goodyear  plant.

Q  Will  you  describe  what  a  sit-down  strike  looks

like?
'
when  the  workers  sit  down  by  their  machines.
very  effective  picketing.

A  It  is one  of  the  most  aggravating  forms  of strike,
It  is

;

Q  Do  they  work?
A  Not  at  all.  The  machines  are  all  ready  to  go,

but  the  worker  is  not  there.

I may  add  that  some  of  my  neighbors  asked  me  to
describe  a  sit-down  strike,  and  I  said,  “Well,  if  you
came  home  from  work  and  found  food  on  the  stove,
and  the  wife  sitting  by  the  stove,  and  the  gas  not  lighted,
you  would  understand  what  a  sit-down  strike  is,”  and
then  the  question  was  asked,

“Why  do  they  let  the  next  shift  in?”  and  well,  of
course,  they  don’h  know  whether  the  next  shift  is  going
to  sit  down  or  not,  and  if  they  don’t  let  them  in,  it
would  be  a  lockout,  so  it  is  a  very  telling  method.

Q  These  sit  down  strikes,  were  they  called  for-
mally  by  the  heads  of  any  union  or  were  they  more  or
less  spontaneous?

A  The  sit-down  strikes  were  spontaneous,  at  least
the  earlier  ones,  and  on  February  3rd  I  understand
there  was  a  sit-down  strike  of  Goodyear  workers,  against
what  they  felt  was  a  reduction  in  pay.

It  was  probably  due  to  an  adjustment  of  piece-
rate  prices.
That  was  spontaneous,  and  there  was  no
one  back  of  that,  the  ?ndustrial  Assembly,  or  the  United
Rubber  Workers.

On  February  14th,  on  what  they  know  is  the  fourth
shift  in  Plant  2,  where  the  longer  hours  had  been  in
effect,  there  was  a  sit-down  strike,  and  they  charged

730

that  70  tire  workers  who  had  received  a  lay-off  notice,
against  the  lay-off  notice  which  70  workers  had  received.
MR.  CARMODY:  In  other  words,  the  longer  hours
had  been  put  in  effect  in  that  department,  but  the
men  hadn’t  yet  been  laid  off  who  would  lose  their
jobs  as  a  result  of  lengthening  the  hours,  but  they  had
been  notified  that  they  wouldn’t  be  needed.

THE  WITNESS:  It  is  just  a  little  bit  different.
With  the  let-down  in  tire  demand,  due  to  the  earlier
automobile  season  put  on  last  fall,  there  had  been  a
reduction  in  demand,  and  the  company  said  that  they
were  letting  off  the  men.

MR.  CARMODY:  But  that  came  at  a  time  when
they  were  letting  the  men  go,  and  they  couldn't  pro-
vide  six  hours’  work,  and  it  was  against  that  that
these  men  sat  down?

THE  WITNESS:  And  these  sit-down  strikers  left

the  plant.

On  February  15th,  there  was  another  group  sat
down,  and  they  were  given  30.minutes  to  return  to
work;  137  of  them  were  fired,  and  some  went  back  to
work,  and  then  the  Industrial  Assembly  appeared  and
secured  the  reinstatement  of  these  men  within  a  day
or  two.

MR.  CARMODY:  Of  the  137?
THE  WITNESS:  Yes.
BY  MR.-WIRIN:
Q  Are  you  through  with  that?
A  On  February  17th,  ‘the  tire  workers  in  Plant  2

decided  to  picket—shall  I  lead  up  to  the  strike?

Q  Yes.
A  On  the  19th  there  was  a  sit-down  strike  in  Plant

1,  and  then  the  pickets  surrounded  the  main  plant.

About  the  22d  to  the  24th,  the  United  Rubber  Work-
ers  of  America  officially  sanctioned  the  strike,  and  be-
fore  that  it  had  been  a  spontaneous  strike.

Q  In  other  words,  the  strike  was  actually  in  ef-
fect  some  time  prior  to  its  endorsement  or  approval
by  the  union.

A  It  was.

in  ours,  without  conference?

A  While  there  may  have  been  an  immediate  cause,

general  unrest.

How  many  workers  were  on  strike?
A  The  plant  was  completely  shut  down,  and  sev-
eral  hundred  were  shut  in  the  plant,  and  had  been
there  since  the  time  the  strike  began.

Q  What  were  the  number  of  employees?
A  Approximately  14,000  employees  were  out  of

work.

Q  You  say  that  the  éntire  plant  was  shut  down.
Do  you  know  whether  or  not  the  rubber  that  goes
into  the  rubber  tires,  comes  from  anywhere  in  the
United  States,  or  what  part  of  the  -world  it  does  come

732

A  A  great  deal  of  it  comes  from  their  plantations

abroad.

;

Q  And  do  you  know  whether  or  not  the  manu-
factured  tires  are  sold  throughout  the  country  qyite
generally?

A  .They  appear to be,  from  the  advertisements.
Q  You  read  the  Saturday  Evening  Post?
A  Yes.
Q  Now,  what  happened  to  the  movement  of  raw
rubber  into  the  plant,  and  the  movement  of  manufac-
tured  tires  out  of  the  plant,  during  the  course  of  the
strike?

A  I understand  that  for  a  part  of  the  time,  at  any

rate,  there  was  lack  of  movement  of  supplies  in,  and
there  was  a  request,  on  February  19th,  the  police  chief
asked  permission  of  the  union  for  coal  trains  to  enter
the  plant.

Q  And  was  that  granted?
A  They  agreed  on  the  condition  that  no  further
work  on  production  would  be  carried  on  in  the  plant,
during  the  strike,  and  there  were  about  one  thousand
men  in  the  plant  at  that  time.

The  management  would  not  agree  to  that,  and  the

blockade  went  on  for  a  time.

Q  So  far  as  you  know,  from  your  investigation,
there  was  no  movement  in  or  out  during  that  period?
A  I  understand  that  later  there  was  some  move-
ment  in  and  out,  but  this  was  given  me  at  the  time
I was  in  Akron,  the  last  days  of  the  strike.

After  the  sit-down  strike  developed

pes

‘

of  the  strike?
into  a  conventional  one?”

)

A  Yes,  there  was  mass  picketing  to  start  with,  and
of  course  the plant  is  very
térge  and  it  was  often  re-
ferred  to  as  the  eleven-mile  picket  line.  On  February

.  ers  from  entering  the  plant  and  they  set  up  picket

A  Yes.

!

A  Yes,  it  did.  The  sheriff  undertook  to  carry  out

with  him  when  it  came  to  the  final  mass  attack  on  the
strikers.

.

|

BY  MR.  CARMODY:
Q  You  mean  the  City  Police?
A  Yes,  but  after  the  attack,  or  the  proposed  at-
tack  was  called  off,  the  United  Rubber  Workers  of
America,  their  leaders,  put  the  plan  of  ten  pickets  per
gate  into  effect  of  their  own  accord.

Q  Now,  was  there  a  threat  of  a  general  strike  dur-

|

ing  the  course  of  the  strike?

A  There  was.
Q  What  are  the  facts  about  that?
A  I can’t  give  you  the  exact  date  but  the  Central
Labor  Union  set  up  a  Committee  of  twenty-five  to  call
a general  strike,  throughout  the  area  there,  in  the  event
that  an  attempt  was  made  to  break  the  picket  line  at
the  Goodyear  Company.

;

_

Q  Was  the  picket  line  ever  broken?

734

A  No.
Q  Just  one  or  two  questions  more.  Were  so  called
Vigilante  Committees  organized  during  the  height  of
the  strike?

A  There  was  a  law  and  order  league  organized,

which  claimed  about  forty  thousand  members.
Q  And  did  they  take  to  the  air,  the  radio?
A  Yes,  the  leader  did  speak  over  the  radio.
Q  In  a  general  way,  what  was  their  program  of

action?

,

A  As  far  as  could  be  determined,  it  was  to  create
sentiment  back  of  the  non-strikers,  and  there  was  a
group  of  non-strikers.

Q  Now  ultimately  was  the  strike  settled?
A  The  strike  was.settled  on  March  21.
Q  And  as  one  of  the  issues  in  the  settlement  of
the  strike  was  the  question  of  the  re-hiring,  or  re-em-
ployment  of  those  who  had  been  on  strike  without  dis-
crimination  involved?

A  It  was.
Q  And  how  was  that  finally  determined?
A  “All  employees  of  February  12,  1936,  shall  re-
turn  without  discrimination  or  interruption  of  service
record.”  That  was  in  the  agreement.

Q  What  was  the  agreement  in  so

far,  or  the

settlement  in  so  far  as  the  problem  of  the  six  or  ~*~
hour  day  involved?

A  The  Company  will  observe  in  the  tire  and  nib

division  a  thirty-six  hour  week,  six  hour  daily  shifts.
Before  any  change  is  made  in  these  hours,  either  per
week  or  per  day,  below  thirty  hours  per  week,  it  will
be  arranged  for  by  vote  of  the  employees  in  the  de-
partment  or  division  effected.

the  employees  re-

as

quested  or  wanted  prior  to  the  strike.

week.

Q  Now  as  a  result  of  your  experience  which  you

”  gaining  tends  to  eliminate  certain  industrial  unrest,

agement,  and  of  management  on  the  part of the  work-
ers,  and  thereby  it  does  help  to  eliminate  strikes.

fires.
-
There  had been  an  under-current  of  revolt  in  that

tempts  had  been  made  by the  various  groups  to  organ-
ize  the  lumberjacks,  with  a  considerable  amount  of
fighting  on  both  sides.

tion  came  into  effect  in  the  form  of  the  LW.W.,  the  in-

ahead  and  established  our  own  Federal  Employment

years  before  a  contract  which  was  about  the  size  of  a

clearly  what  happened:

It  expressed  pretty

When  I  went  down  to  the  ILW.W.  headquarters

Q  Did  you  find  as  a  result  of  your  experience  that
conferring  with  workers,  and  understanding  their  griey-
ances  was  more  helpful  to  getting  work  done  than  sup
pression  and  persecution?

A  I  would  say  that  it  was  utterly  impossible  to
have  handled  the  situation  with  eight  thousand  men  if
you  aitempted  to  exercise  a  disciplinary  control  your-
self.  That  by  putting  the  entire  disciplinary  contro!  on
the  men,  they  assumed  the  responsibility,  and  there
was  a  very  loosely  organized  group,  made  up  of  a
hetrogeneous  bunch  of  migratory  workers  who  assumed
the  responsibility  when  it  was  put  on  them.

I  went  even  farther  than  that,  and  when  they  had
complaints  in  the  camps,  I  went  into  the  camps  and
said,  “I  know  nothing  of  your  complaint,  you  fellows
take  and  set  up  your  committee  and  try  the  case  and
give  me  the  answer.”

The  net  result  of  it  was  that  they  leaned  backwards
to  be  fair,  and  were  much  more  severe  in  the  handling
of  the  case  than  I  could  possibly  have  been,  because
their  attitude  was  that  they  wouldn’t  let  any  of  these
fellows  put  anything  over.

But  the  experiences  were  definitely  convincing  to
me  that  even  under  those  circumstances,  remote  from
the  Mais  of  control,  packing  their  food  to  these  men,
and  a  hetrogeneous  group  of people  with  no  background
or  traditions  of  organization,  in  a  state  of  revolt,  would
respond  to  an  opportunity  to  assume  responsibility,  and
did.

I  mean  there  is  no  question  about  that—it  is  4

matter  of  record.

Q  Are  you  acquainted  with  any  more  difficult,
hazardous  or fatiguing  work  than  fighting  forest  fires?
A  No,  sir;  there  is  nothing  more  dangerous.  1
had  one  hundred  and  fifty  men  burned  to  death  in

753

Northern  Idaho  in  1910,  and  two  hundred  and  fifty
of  them  burned  seriously.

ployment  of  any  employee.

A  Yes,  their  lungs  are  filled  with  smoke,  and  the
tops  of  trees  are  falling  down,  and  if  you  get  a  high
wind  you  are  liable  to  be  trapped.

Q  Now,  turning  to  another  experience,  were  you
in  Seattle  in  connection  with  labor  problems  some  time
after  that?

A  ‘Yes,  I was  in  Seattle,  for  the  Joint  Commission
for  the  Department  of  Labor  and  the  United  States
Shipping  Board.

Q  Before  you  went  to  Seattle,  were  you  connected

with  the  United  States  Army?

A  I had  a  commission  of  Captain  in  the  engineers,

in  France  of  1917.

Q  Is  that  your  present  title,  in  the  United  States

Army?

A  I  never  got  to  the  Army,  and  I  merely  got  a

commission.

Q  What  is  your  latest  commission?
A  I was  Captain  of  the  Engineers.
Q  And  were  you  ordered  or  requested  by  Secre-

tary  Wilson  to  proceed  to  Seattle?

A  Yes,  I got  a  request  after  the  Commission  came
through,  somebody  took  the  story  to  Secretary  Wilson,
who  was  then  Secretary  of  Labor,  about  this  situation
in  Northwestern  Montana,  and  I was  then  requested  by
the  Secretary  and  Mr.  Hurley,  who  was  then  head  of
the  Shipping  Board,  to  go  to  Seattle.
I told  them  that
I would  go  if they  made  a  request  for  my  release  from
the  Army,  which  they  did.

I then  went  to  Seattle.

Q  What  year  was  that?
A  In 1918.

I think  that  it  was in the  fall of  1918,
or  it was  in  the  fall  of  1917  and  the  early  part  of  1918.

Q  What  did  you  find  there?
A  The  problem  that  they  put  up  to  me  before

755

I left  here  was  to  deal  with  four  of  the  big  steel  ship-
yards  and  that  was  the  total  number  in  Seattle  at  the
time,  all  unionized  and  under  closed  shop  agreements
with  the  employers,  and  an  attempt  to  work  out  a  cen-
tralized  control  of  the  in  and  out  flow  of labor  into
these  yards.

That  is  through  central  employment  set-ups.
Secondly,  to  adjudicate  certain  types  of  disputes,
that  were  dead-locked,  and  certain  processes,  and  the
ships  were  lying  in  Seattle  Harbor,  at  the  heighth  of
the  submarine  warfare,  completely  finished  in  all  de-
tails  except  the  turbine  engines.
7
It  was  a  problem  to  see  whether  or  not  we  could
get  the  processes  going,  and  the  coppersmiths  to  pro-
duce  the  pipes  necessary  to  connect  them  up.
Then  there  were  the  caulkers  in  the  wooden  ship-
building  situation,  that  was  another  group  that  was
dead-locked.  And  there  was  a  shortage  of  riveters  and
machinists  and  so  on,  and  in  other  words,  evolved  by
‘using  the  employment  bureau  as  a  focal  point,  and
the complete  problem  of  dealing  with  the  entire  set-up,
and  the  entire  labor  set-up  in  these  four  steel  ship-
yards,  and  of  providing  ways  and  means  for  the  ad-
judication  of  disputes.
When  I arrived  in  Seattle,  there  were  about  four
thousand  men  up  against  those  shipyard  gates,  and
they  were  standing  there  in  the  drizzling  rain,  and  the
problem  first  of  all  came  to  get  those  men  under  cover,
and  establish  a  central  employment  bureau  whereby,
by some  sort  of  test,  a  mechanical  test  of  some  kind,
of  checking  the  men  up  by  actually  going  on  certain
machines  in  the  yard,  with  someone  connected  with
that  profession,  to  check  up  their  abilities,
We  had  to  evaluate  the  labor  supply  that  was  there

I established  this  central

pment  bureau  with  the joint  consent  of  the  un-—

of  unions.

BY  MR.  SMITH:
Q  Were  they  organized  into  a  metal  trades  coun-

ceil?

A  Yes,  and  they  developed  something  there  in
Seattle  which  I  think  is  quite  significant.  When  these
ship-yards  were  first  underway,  the  unions  and  the
employers,  dealing  on  a  regular  union  set-up  basis,  sat
around  the  table  and  arranged  all  of  the  differentials
between  crafts,  and  jointly  agreed  on  a  blanket  agree-
ment,  both  the  employers  and  the  union,  so  that  the
whole  question  of  the  arrangement  of  the  differentials
between  crafts  was  settled  jointly,  and  that  became  ef-
fective  for  each  one  of  the  four  yards  so  that  you  had
a  uniform  type  of  agreement.

And  it  was  a  tremendous  stabilizing  influence  in

having  a  common  uniform  base  for  all  four  yards.

Q  Were  the  unions  a  party  to  this  agreement?
A  They  were  all  definitely  a  party.

All  of  the
local  unions,  and  underwritten  by  the  various  interna-
tional  unions.

Q  Were  the  employers  orgatized  in  any  kind  of

an  Association?

_  A  Yes,  they  had  an  association,  I  can’t  give  you
the  exact  name  of  it,  it  was  the  Seattle  Ship  Owners
or some  sort  of thing  like  that.
Association,

Q  Did  that  represent  substantially  the  majority

of  and  the  important  shipbuilders?

A  It represented  all of the four  big steel yards  ~

757

.  there  at  the  time.  There  was  some  contract  shops  that
were  doing  specialized  type  of  work  like  grey  iron
moulding  or  coppersmithing,  that  were  supplementing
the  specialist  shops  of  the  ship-yards  themselves,  that
were  on  the  outside,  but  they  were  all  union  shops.

Q  Was  this  agreement  signed  by  the  Association?
A  It  was  signed  by  the  individual  members  of  the
Association,  there  being  only  four  of  them.
I  mean
only  four  of  the  big  steel  ship-yards,  and  there  .were
some  other  -contract  shops,  and  most  of  them  signed
the  agreement,  too.

Q  Did  you  find  as  a  result  of  that  experience  that
the  situation  in  the  ship-building  industry  during  the
War,  at  a  critical  time,  was  stabilized  by  virtue  of  the
practice  of  collective  bargaining?

A  I  think  that  unquestionably,  every  form  of
que@jjon  that  came  up  in  the  handling  of  those  ship-
yards—I  will
give  some  specific  examples,  where
through  a  collective  channel  to  deal,  you  could  deal
with  certain  problems,  where  without  it  you  couldn’t.
Take  the  coppersmith’s  smithing  situation.

I  got
to  Seattle,  and  there  was  a  stack  of  telegrams  about  a
foot  high,  in  connection  with  this  coppersmith  problem,
which  was  then  the  specific  problem  that  was  giving
the  trouble.
The  net  evaluation  of  those  telegrams  was  largely

Q  What  is  the  present  membership  of  the  Typo-

graphical  Union?

A  Approximately  ten  thousand.
Q  Do  you  know  the  membership  of  the  Interna-

tional?

A  The  last  figures  that  I  saw  were  around  sey-
enty-five  thousand,  and  I  imagine  around  seventy  to
seventy-five  thousand.

Q  When  was  the  employers  association

organ-

ized?

A  In  1887.
Q  And  was  the  organization  set  up  for  the  pur-

pose  of  dealing  in  part  with  the  unions?

A  I should  say  historically  that  the  employers  or-
ganization  came  into  effect  to  set  up  a  counter  balance
to  the  union,  and it  was  originally  organi¥ed  on  a  defi-
nitely  anti-union  basis.

Q  Now,  was  it  changed?
A  Subsequently  as  time  developed,  the  employers
split  into  two  groups  within  their  major  organization,
and  evolved  a  plan  by  which  they  would  have  their
cost-finding  and  all  of  that  sort  of  work  underwritten
by both  the  open-shop  and  the  closed  shop  groups,  and
then  they  established  two  separate  bodies  within  their
major  organization,  one  called  the  closed  shop  group,
and  the  other  called  the  open  shop  group,  and  on  labor
questions  these  two  groups  voted  separately  through
their  own  channels,  so  that  they  took  the  opposing
views  within  the  Association,  and  kept  them  separate
as  to  labor.

Q  Can  you  tell  us  whether  the  group  called  the
open  shop  was  an  anti-union  group  and  refused  to
deal  with  labor  unions?

A  Well,  you  had  every  variation,  from  those  that
would  deal  with  the  union,  and  those  that  woudn’t
frankly  say  so,  up  to  those  that  were  definitely  anti-
anion,  and used  every  device  to  keep  them  out  of  their
plants.

You  have  in  those  contracts  what  we  call  the  Supreme
Court  of  the  industry,  and  what  is  a  joint  committee
of  five  each  from  the  employers  and  from  the  individ-
wal  union,  and  that  is  there  would  be  five  members  of

dustry  itself.

fon  in  that  machinery  for  the  adjustment  of  disputes,

A  No.

It  is  based  fundamentally  on  collective
ae “<Q  Would  it  be  possible  where  employees  -  dealt

orderly  set  up  on  the  matter  of  adjudication  of  com-

Q  Let  me  ask  you  further  if  this  machinery  could
function  if  employers  attempted  to  coerce  employees
in  their  own  organization  activities.

A  No,  I  think  that  the  thing  would  have  to  be

I  mean,  if  you  attempted  to  use
discipline  of  that  sort,  based  upon  coercion,  I  think
that  in  the  net  results  there  would  be  friction,  and  dif-
ficulties  and  eventually  a  clash.

Q  Now,  do  the  agreements  . between  employers
and  employees  provide  for  any  apprenticeship  training,
and  a  school  of  some  kind?

A  I  would  like  to  point  out  that  aside  from  the
adjudication  of  complaints and  friction,  that  there  is
a  field  of  opportunity  where  you  have  responsible  or-
ganizations  on  both  sides,  to  deal  with  some  of  the
major  problems  of  the  industry  like  the  education  of
apprentices.

Q  Is  that  an  important  problem  in  the  printing

industry ?

A  Very  definitely,  and  it  takes  you  five  years  to.
train  a  first-class  compositor,  and  something  like  seven
years  on  a  four  color  pressman.

Q  And  is  it  to  the  advantage  of  the  employer  to

get  well-educated  workers?

A  It is both  to  the  employer  and  the  union.
Q  What  is  the  program  or  the  plan  in  use?
A  Well,  the  program  in  New  York  now  goes  back
about  eighteen  years.
There  was  a  small  compositor
school started by  the  Hudson  Guild,  down  in  a  cellar,
on  Fifty-Sixth  Street.
.  Q  The  Hudson  Guild  was  one  of  these  organiza-
tions?

767

__

A  No,  that  was  just  a  charitable  organization,  and
Dr.  Elliott,  was  taking  some  way-ward  boys  off  the
street,  and  he  got  them  down  in  the ceHMar,  and  got
some type and got them  busy, and the thing  began to
grow,  and  Dr.  Elliott  came  to  the  employers  organiza-
tion  and  the  union,  and  said,  “Here  iy  =  chance  for
training  the  boys  for  the  industry.”

They  formed  a  Board  of  the  employin:-  printers,
dealing  with  the  unions,  and  the  Typographical  Union,
and  out  of  that  evolved  a  contribution  by  the  Typo-
graphical  Union  of  ten  thousand  dollars  a  year,  and  a
contribution  frem  the  boys  on  the  basis  of  so  much  per
week,  which  contributed  about  eleven  thousand  dollars
a  year.

The  school  was  taken  over  under  the  joint  aus-
pices  of  the  unions  and  the  employers,  including  the
commercial  branch,  and  the  newspapers.
Out  of  that
was  evolved  a  compulsory  system  of  education  where-
by  the  boys  were  sent  to  school  a  half  an  afternoon
each  week  on  their  own  time,  and  an  evening  on  their
own  time,  and  the  school  was  modernized,  and  com-
plete  in  detail,  with  a  corps  of  instructors,  and  they
went  so  far  that  the  union  and  the  employers  jointly
agreed  on  making  intelligence  tests  on  boys  before  they
could  get  their  apprenticeships  there.

The  old  apprenticeship  system  had  brokenfdown
completely  under  modern  mechanization,  and  the  un-
ion  was  having  difficulty  in  furnishing  competent  men
and  having  men  shunted  around  from  one  plant  to
another  to  get  their  training,  and  the  employers  were
carrying  the  burden  in  one  form  or  another:
___.  By the  establishment  of  this  school,  and  an  orderly
system  of  introduction  of  apprentices  worked  out,  we
were  able  first  to  select  the  material,  both  as  to  phys-
“teal  and  health  examinations,  and  mental  examina-
tions,  and  to  secure  a  quality  of  boy  of  exercising  joint
a @isciplinary  controls  both  over  the  employer,  the  union
and  the boy.

768

The  thing  grew  to  the  point  where  we  had  six  thou-

sand  boys,  with  a  complete  course  of  composition.

Out  of  that  grew  an  additional  school  which  is  now
operating,  and  in  which  we  take  up  linotype  work,  and
the  second  school  was  evolved  to  take  care  of  the  press-
men,  and  I should  judge  that  we  have  mobilized  about
two  hundred  and  fifty  thousand  dollars  worth  of  equip-
ment  out  of  the  machinery  manufacturers.

We  have  a  four-way  cooperative  agreement  cover-
ing  the  newspapers,  the  commercial  branch  of  the
printing  industry  and  the  various  unions  involved,  all
contributing,  and  then  finally  we  tied  it  up.  with  the
Board  of  Education,  where  they  furnish  rental  space,
and  paid  the  instructors,  and  we  furnished  the  materi-
als  and  the  equipment  and  the  machinery.

I  would  like  to  make  one  passing  comment  in  re-

gard  to  vocational  education  in  this  field.

One  of  the  great  difficulties  that  you  run  across  in
your  established  Board  of  Education,  is  both  physical
and  mental  obsolesence,  and.  through  the  slow  pro-
cesses  of  the  appropriations  and  so  on  that  it  is  nec-
essary  for  your  local  government  to  go  through,  your
machinery  would  be  obsolete,  and  the  industry  would
have  gone  by  it,  so  that  what  we  have  done  there  is
through  these  joint  contributions  and  cooperation  with
the  machine  manufacturers,  we  have  kept  the  ma-
chinery  up  to  date,  and  if  it  gets  partly  obsolete,  they
take  it  out  and  put  another  one  in.

Q  What  has  been  your  experience  of  the  unions

toward  the  taking  ont  of  obsolete  machinery?

A  My  experience  has  been  that  the  unions  have
been  resistent,  and  they  are  resistant  to  the  introduc-

away  the  independence  of  representation  on  the  part
of  labor  groups,  and  to  bring  them  under  control.
It  is  the  old  play  for  power,  and  the  focal  point  of
power  is  to  keep  it  on  one  side  or  the  other.
I am  not
in  this  testimony  eliminating  entirely  and  glorifying  the
union  type  of  organization,  and  I  am  not  saying  that
they  are  not  guilty  of  the  same  attempts  for  control  of

cess  and  it  is  accepted  as  such,  and  if  there  is  resistance

that  these  things  are  expressions  of.

gree  of  parallel  between  this  attempt  on  the  part  of

pression  on  the  part  of  the  individual  groups,  so  that

in  hand,  and  the  right  to  discharge  shouldn’t  be  used
It  shouldn’t  be  used  in

that  way.  There  should  be  a  safe-guarding  of  the  rights

A  Yes.
That  was  on  the  Pacific  Coast.
Q  So  that  in  spite  of  the  fact  that  these  organiza-
tions  were  suppressed  twenty  years  ago,  there  still  was

A  Yes,  there  was  a  combination  ‘there.

The  Pa-
cific  Coast  situation  goes  back  to  the  type  of  organiza-
tion  that  came  into  existence  around  about  1917,  and
developed  in  that  Pacific  Coast  situation  what  was
known  as  the  Loyal  Legion  of  Loggers  and  Lumber-
men,  and  it  was
a  psychological
with
which  they  provided  the  way  for  some  of  the  I.  W.  W.’s
to  become  respectable  by  identifying  themselves  with

with  spruce  production.

substitution,

Then  the  issue  came  up  with  that  organization  as

taries,  or  the  government  would  appoint  the  secretaries,
and  of  course  there  is  a  fundamental  issue.

The  government  representative  at  the  time  was  Mr.
Brice  B.  Disk,  and  he  permitted  the  men  to  elect  their

general  headquarters  of  that  North  Idaho  Region.

A  I should  say  that  the  best  way  to  avoid  getting
a good  settlement  is  to  get  the  lawyers  interested  in  it.
We  have  definitely  excluded  them  from  our  New  York

set-up.  We  knew  perfectly  well  that  when  you  at-

in  the  New  York  set-up  agreed  that  one  way  to  stymie
a  settlement  was  to  get  the  attorneys  to  represent
either  side.

Of  course,  my  reference  is  to  the  legal  profession
as  a  means  of  settling  these  things.  My  proposal  goes
definitely  to  the  formalized  legal  procedure  in  attempt-
ing  to  settle  this  type  of  thing,  and  not  to  lawyers.

transitory,  and  it  has  to  develop  evidently  into  a  strict-  _

eee.

BASIL  M.  MANLY
was  produced  as  a  witness  for  and  on  behalf  of  the
National  Labor  Relations  Board,  and  having  been  duly

DIRECT  EXAMINATION

BY  MR.  WIRIN:

Your  full  name  is  Basil  M.  Manly?
Yes,  sir.
Where  do  you  live?
Washington,  D.  C.
And  do  you  hold  any  government  position?
PO
OFrO
A  1am  Vice-Chairman  of  the  Federal  Power  Com-

mission.

Q  Some  time  in  1912,  were  you  connected  in  any
way  with  a  commission  known  as  the  Industrial  Com-
mission ?

A  Not  in  1912
Q  Was  that  Commission  created  by  an  Act  of

Beginning  in  1913  I  was.

Congress  in  1912?

A  Yes.
Q  In  what  capacity  were  you  connected  with  the

Commission ?

A  I  was  Director  of  Research  and  Investigation

of  the  Commission.

Q  How  many  persons  were  members  of  that  Com-

mission?

A  Nine  members  of  the  Commission.
Q  What  were  your  duties  as  Director  of  Research

and  Investigation?

A  Generally  to  direct  the  work  of  the  staff  of  in-
vestigators  and  experts  and  also  to  plan  and  direct
the  public  hearings  of  the  Commission.

Q  Prior  to  your  being  connected  with  the  Com-
mission  were  you  interested  in  Labor  Relations  in  any
way?

785

A  Yes,  I  have  been  interested  for  several  years.
Q  What  was  the  nature  of  your  interest,  and

what  had  you  done?

A  My  first  work  in  that  field  was  with  the  Bureau
of  Labor  of  the  Department  of  Commerce  and  Labor
as  it  was  at  that  time,  and  first  as  an  agent  of  that
Bureau  in  the  invesiigation  of  Women  and  Child  wage
earners,  in  1908  to  1910.

Then  I  was  put  in  charge  of  the  investigation  of
wages  and  working  conditions  in  the  Steel  Industry,
which  required  a  couple  of  years  and  resulted  in  the
publication  of  a  four  volume  report.

.

Following  that,  I  believe  that  I  made  the  investi-
gation  of  the  relation  of  the  increase  in  prices  of  anthra-
cite  coal  to  the  wage  adjustment  in  that  industry.

Then  I was  called  in  as  an  expert  in  the  first  arbi-
tration  that  was  held  in  the  Coat  and  Suit  Industry,
of  which  the  present  Justice  Brandeis  was  Chairman,
and  i believe  that  those  were  all  of  the  important  con-
nections  up  to  the  time  that  I  went  with  the  Commis-
sion  on  Industrial  Relations.

Q  What  was  the  occasion  for  the  appointment
of  the  Commission  and  for  the  Act  of  Congress  which
authorized  and  directed  the  appointment?

A  The  creation  of  the  Commission  arose  primarily
out  of  the  very  large  number  of  strikes  that  had  taken
place  during  the  preceding  two  or  three  years.

There  had  been  a  condition  of  Nation-Wide  In-
dustrial  unrest,  centering  most  largely  in  perhaps  the
Colorado  strike,  which  ran  over  a  period  of  several
years,  the  big strikes  in  the  textile  industry,  in  Lawrence
and  Lowell,  Massachusetts,  and  the  unrest  in  the  coal
industry  generally,  and  the  metal  manufacturing  indus-
tries  and  the  building  trades.

Q  Were  these  strikes,  many  of  them  of  national

consequence ?

A  They  were  of  extraordinary  violence,  I  should
3  ‘say, and  many  of  them  had  a  very  direct  effect  upon
f, Ser

786

interstate  commerce,  which  seemed  to  justify  Federal
intervention.

Q  By  whom  was  the  Commission  appointed?
A  The  Commission  was  appointed  by  President
Wilson,  and  I  might  clear  that  up a  little  because  as
a  matter  of  history,  President  Taft  had  appointed  a
Board  not  long  before  the  conclusion  of  his  term  of  of-
fice,  but  that  membership  was  not  confirmed  and  con-
sequently  it  became  the  duty  of  President  Wilson  to
name  a  new  board,  and  he  named  an  entirely  different-
membership.

Q  The  original  Act  of  Congress  which  authorized
and  directed  the  appointment  of  the  Commission  was
passed  during  President  Taft’s  administration?
A  Yes,  August  of  1912,  as  I  remember.
Q  What  was  the  extent  of  the  investigation,  how

-

long  a  period  did  it  last?

A  Oh,  the  investigation  extended  over  a  period  of
The  staff  was  organized  in  October,
some  two  years.
1913,  I  believe,  and  the  investigations  were  instituted
immediately  which  led  to  public  hearings.  subsequently,
beginning,  I  should  say,  in  January,  about  1914,  and
running  almost  continuously  for.a  year  and  a  half  in-
terims  between  the  hearings.

The  hearings  were  held  in  almost  every  important
industrial  center  in  the  United  States,  and  the  Com-
mission  went  to  the  scene  of  the  difficulty,  rather  than
attempt  to  bring  witnesses  to  any  central  point.

Q  Approximately,  how  many  witnesses  were  in-

terrogated?

A  My  memory  was  that  it  was  something  more

than  seven  hundred.

Q  Were  those  witnesses  workers  and  union  rep-

resentatives  and  representatives  of  labor?

A  My  memory  was  that  it  was  devided  equally
into  three  groups—the  witnesses  who  represented  the
employers,  most  of  whom  were  either  selected  by  or
suggested  by employers’  associations  and  other  organiza-  is

787

tions  of  employers;  witnesses  representing  labor,  who
were  usually  represented  by  the  American:  Federa-
tion  of  Labor  or  by  local  labor  organizations,:and  the
third  group  of  witnesses  who  were  not  connected  with
either  employers’  organizations  or  with  labor  organiza-
tions.
That  group  included  various  kinds  of <people,
students  of  the  subject  of  industrial  relations,  offi-
cials  of  State  Labor  Bureaus,  directors  of employment
~  agencies,  and  various  other  official  and  unofficial  per-

sons  of  that  kind.

Q  Did  the  Commission  publish  a  report?
A  The  Commission  published  a  report  on  the  sit-
uation,  and  the  situation  regarding  the  report  was
rather  unusual.

Earlier  in  the  year  of  1913,  the  Commission  directed
me  as  the  Director  of  Research  and  Investigation,  to
prepare  a  report  of  the  staff  for  presentation  to  the
Commission.

It  was  not  intended  as  a  Commission  report.
At
the  same  time,  Professor  Commons,  who  was  one  of
the  outstanding  members  of  the  Commission,  prepared
a  draft  of  a  report  of  his  own  dealing  primarily  with
the  machinery  which  he  believed  to  be  best:  adapted
for  carrying  into  effect  the  plans  which  he  had  in
mind  for  the  Adjustment  of  Labor  conditions.

Those  plans  centered  primarily  around  the  crea-
tion  of  industrial  commissions  within  the  severa)  states,
and  I  believe  also  a  plan  for  a  National  Industrial
Commission.

As  a  result  there  were  in  essence  two  reports

made  by  that  Commission.

Q  In  addition  to  the  two  reports,  was  the  testi-
mony  that  was  submitted  before  the  Commission  pub-
lished ?

|

A  Yes,  that  was  published  in  11.  volumes:
Q  How  many  volumes  was  the  entire  report?
A  Well,  the  testimony  made  up  11  volumes  and

-.
the report  made  up  the  twelfth  volume.

788

Q  Was  there  any  essential  difference  between  the
group  which  we  will  call  the  Commons’  group,  and  the
group  of  the  Commission  that  signed  the  report  which
you  prepared  insofar  as  the  advisability  of  recogniz-
ing  the  principle  of  collective  bargaining  was  con-
cerned?

A  There  was  no  difference  at  that  point  at  all.
The  Commission,  I believe  you  will  find,  if  you  will
examine  the  separate  reports,  was  unanimously  in  favor
of  proper  protection  for  the  right  of  organization  and
collecitve  bargaining.

Q  Was  there  any  difference  of  opinion  as  to  the
two  groups  as  to  the  inequality  of  the  individual  worker
as  such  in  negotiating  with  his  employer  concerning
wage  agreements?

A  Well,  if  there  was  any  difference,  it  was  e  dif-  —

ference  of  degree,  rather  than  a  difference  of  character.
I think  that  the  Commons’  group  was  not  perhaps
as  definitely  determined  toward  the  protection  of  that
right,  as  the  staff  which  I had  the  honor  to  head  at  that
time,  felt  to  be  necessary  under  all  circumstances  and
conditions.

Q  Who  was  the  Chairman  of  the  Commission?
A  Frank  E.  Walsh.
Q  Did  he  sign  the  report  which  you  prepared?
A  Yes.
Q  Who  else  signed  the  report  that  you  prepared,

do  you  remember  offhand?

A  John  B.  Leonard,  Austin

B.  Garretson,  and

James  O’Connel.

Q  What  conclusions  did  your  group  arrive  at
concerning  the  denial  of  the  right  of  organization  by
employers  to  employees,  and  the  necessity  or  advise-
bility  of  collective  bargaining?

A  Well,  the  Commission  found  throughout  the
-  United  States  in what  you  might  call  organized  industry,
large  scale  industry;  a  general  denial  of  the  right  of
organization  and  collective  bargaining.

789

-
They  found  that  in  some  cases, in quite  aggravated

trol  within  the  commynity  and  not  only  inhibit  the right
of organization  but  so  completely  would  such  industry
dominate  the  communities  that  we  would  forbade  any
persons  from  coming  in  to  the community  for the pur-
pose  of  organizing  or  talking  with  the  employees,
Q  Would  there  be  any  interference  of  the  meet-
ings  of  the  employees  called  for  the  purpose  of  organ-
izing?

A  Oh,  yes.
Q  What  else,  if  anything,  did  the  Commission
find  with  respect  to  the  problems  of  collective  bargain-
ing?
~~ A  We  found  about  one  hundred  and  seventy-five
pages  of it, as  I remember,  and  we  found  quite  extensive
interference,  with  the  right  of  collective  bargaining
and  the  right  of  organization,  in  large  scale  industry.
They  found  generally,  as  I  recall  it,  that  the  or-
ganization  was  not  permitted,  “permitted”  is  not  quite
the  word,  but  was  interfered  with  throughout  the  major
part.  of  organized  industria]  production.

A  I would  rather  use  large  scale  industrial  pro-
duction,  that  was  what  I  meant  to  say,  instead  of  or-

which  purports  to  be  a  quotation  from  the  testimony

Brandeis.

:

In  the  course  of  that,  Justice  Brandeis  discusses  the
problem  of  what  he  calls  industrial  absolutism,  namely
thé  denial  to  employees  of  the  right  to  participate  in
the  conduct  of  industry,  and  in  determining  their  own
lot  in  industry.

I  will  ask  yeu  at  this  time  whether  or  not  your
group  was  substantially  in  accord  with  the  testimony
of  Mr.  Brandeis.

A  Yes,  that  was  incorporated,  and  his  testimony,
I  believe,  was  incorporated  with  approval  in  the  re-
port  of  the  Commission.

Q  Now,  Mr.  Manly,  what  did  your  group  find  the
facts  to  be  with  respect  to  the  advantages  of  the  worker
of  being  a  member  of  a  National  Union  as  distinguished
from  a  local  or  a  company  union?

A  Well,  we  found  generally  that  the  advantages  to
the  worker  were  dependent  upon  the  strength  of  the  or-
ganization  with  which  he  was  affiliated  and  its  free-
dom  from  control  by  the  employing  interests.

That  freedom  from  control  was  in  large  measure
in  inverse  proportion  to  the  dependence  of  the  mem-
bers  of  the  organization  upon  employment  by  that  par-
ticular  employer.

A  local  organization  employing  other  persons  than-
the  employees  of  that  particular  establishment  was
stronger  in  its  bargaining  power  than  a  company  union
and  by  the same  token,  a  national  union  equally  well
managed  was  stronger  in  its  bargaining  power  and  in  its
power  to  protect  the  workers  interests  than  a  local  -
union.

;

der  which  an  employee  who  -is  discharged  by  one  em-

BY  MR.  CARMODY:
Q  May  I stop  for  just  a moment.  Isn’t  it customary
in  American  Industry  for  a  prospective  employer  to
ask  an  applicant  for  the  names  of  his  previous  em-
ployers,  particularly  his  most  recent  employer?
A  Yes,  that  is  a  very  common  practice.
Q  And  then  isn’t  it  common  practice  for  the  re.
spective  employer  to  write  to  or  telephone  to  the  former
employer  and  ask  him  what  gprt  of  a  man  this  man  is?

A  Yes.
Q  Then  if  that  is  the  practice,  and  if  there  is  this
black  list  that  you  speak  of,  hasn't  it  far-reaching  conse-
quences  where  a  man  once  gets  on  some  list  of  that
sort?

A  Oh,  yes.
Q  Might  it  not  prevent  his  getting  employment  any-
where  within  the  region  in  which  he  normally  lives  over
a  longer  period  of  time?

A  It  would  at  any  rate  very  greatly  restrict  his
opportunity  of  employment.  As  a  result  of  it,  employ-
ees  go  under  assumed  names  and  deny  having  had  pre-
vious  employment  in  a  region,  in  the  hope  of  getting
by.

Q  That  was  brought  out  through  this  investigation

that  the  Commission  made?

A  Yes,  many  cases  of  that  kind.

or  not  those  workers  who  were  active  in  seeking  to

A  Oh,  yes,  they  were  the  ones  that  were  particu-

larly  on  such  lists.

BY  MR.  CARMODY:

A  The  Commirsion  felt  that  it  would  be  so.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:

BY  MR.  CARMODY:
Q  Do  you  think  that  otherwise  respectable  em-
ployers  persecute  applicants  for  employment  after  that
fashion,  is  that  the  record?
A  That  is  the  record.
Of  course,  the  employers  defense  of  such  practice
where  there  was  such  a  defense,  I believe  the  defense
was  made  in  the  city  of  Los  Angeles,  for  example,
where  the  practice  was  very  common,  and  they  had
what  they  called,  I  think,  Employment  Bureau  of  the
Employer’s  Association,  which  was  very  well  organ-
ized,  and  which  was  very  effective  in  keeping  men
who  were  known  as  trouble  makers  from  securing
employment.

The  defense  was  that  the  employer  did  not  want
to  have  in  his  employment,  men  who  might  subse-
quently  cause  trouble,  and  become  active  in  the  organ-
ization  of  unions,  and  they  were  justified  in  using  any
means  of  that  kind  in  acquainting  themselves  with  the
past  record  and  activities  of  the  employee.

Q  Now,  will  you  describe  just  what  the  facts  were
as  found  by  the  Commission  with  respect  to  the  ex-
istence  of  labor  espionage,  first  as  to  the  extent  and
second  as  to  the  methods  used?

A  Well,  the  general  system  of  espionage  consist-
ed  in  the  retaining  of  so-called  detective  agencies  to
supply  what  were  known  as  undercover  men,  to  work
in  the  plants  as  though  they  were  ordinary  workers,  to
mingle  with  the  workers,  to  report  upon  the  activities
both  of  individuals  and  of  groups,  and  to  advise  the
employer  of  any  attempts  to  bring  about  organization,
or  to  hold  meetings  for  that  purpose,  and  matters
of  that  kind,

The  practice  was  quite  wide-spread,  or  I  had  bet-
ter  put  it  the  other  way,  we  encountered  it  in  a  great
many  cases,  and- how  wide-spread  it  may  have  been  no-
body  knows  because  those  were  very  carefully  guarded
contracts,  and  the  worse  feature  of  that  situation,  of
course,  is  not  so.  much  fhe  espionage@as  the  fact  that  it
continued  employment  of  the  detectwe  agent,  and  the
spies,  as  dependent  upon  seeing  that  unrest,  and  other
things  as  continued  in  the  plant.

By  MR.  CARMODY:
Q  You  mean  that  these  spies  and  detectives  created
unrest  in  order  to  create  a  continuing  demand  for  their
services?

A  Yes,  sir.

Q  Now,  | believe. you  testified  that  your  Commis.
sion  reviewed  a  number  of  hundred  workers  or  rep-
resentatives  of  workers,  and  did  you  arrive  at  any

mind,  in  terms  of—in  other  ‘words,  did  it  lead  to  in-
dustrial  unrest  or  contentment  on
the  part  of  the
workers?

A  The  Commissions  finding  was  that  it  tended  to
promote  unrest,  and  dissatisfaction,  but  even  more
seriously  it  tended  to  create  a  feeling  of  desperation,
which  in  many  cases  led  to  violent  and  destructive
conflicts.

Q  Did  it  find  as  to  whether  or  not  the  existence
of  espionage  and  other  practices  you  have  mentioned,

worker?

three  years  ago,  gen

among  the  workers,  and  where  a  sufficient  group  found
it  possible  to  get  together,  it  usually  led  to  strikes  and
to  a  different  kind  of
es  ordinarily  from_the  strike

ng  well  organized  industries.
The sort  of strike  you get  when  you have  had that  con-
dition  of repression  existing  for  a  period  of  years as
an  explosive  strike.

The  norma!  condition  that  you  have  in  a  well-

of  the  two  sides,  and  sooner  or  later  a  conclusion  of
the  difficulties  by  negotiation.

Whereupor

BASIL  M.  MANLY

was  recalled  as  a  witness  for  and  on  behalf  of  the
National  Labor  Relations  Board,  and  having  been  pre-
viously  duly  sworn  by  the  Chairman  of  the  Board,
was  eXamined  and  testified  as  follows:

DIRECT  EXAMINATION  (Continued)

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Thereafter  were  you  Co-Chairman  of  the  Na-

tional  War-Lahor  Board?

A  Yes.
Q  Who  was  the  other  Co-Chairman?
A  Former  President  William  Howard  Taft.
Q  And  is  that  the  same  William  Howard  Taft  who
was  Chief  Justice  of  the  United  States  Supreme  Court?

A  Yes,  sir.
Q  Can  you  tell  us  the  personnel  of  the  Board?
A  The  personnel  of  the  Board  changed  somewhat,
and  the  two  joint  chairmen  originally  named  were
former  President  Taft,  and  Frank  P.  Walsh.
Q  Did  you  succeed  Frank  P.  Walsh?
A  Yes,  sir.
Q  Did  he  resign  during  the  work  of  the  Board?
A  Yes,  he  resigned  during  the  work  of  the  Board,
but  the  general  ‘constitution  of  the  Board  was  that  of
five  members,  representing  the  employing  interests,
whe  were  recommended  by  the  National  Industrial
Confereace  Board,  and  five  members  representing  the
Labor  interests,  who  were  nominated  by  the  American

|

801

+  Federation  of  Labor,  and  each  of  them  had  an  al-
ternate,  so  that  in  addition  to  the  two  joint  chairmen,
you  had  for  effective  working  purposes,  twenty-two
members  of  the  Board.

;

it  was  necessary  to  have  a  board  of  that  size  be-
cause  Of  the  very  great  volume  of  work  that  had-  to
be  handled  during  the  war  period.
Minor  cas@  we
usually  handled  by  a  section  consisting  of  one  repre-
sentative  of  the  employer's  interest,  and  one  repre-
sentative  of  the  labor  -interests,  and  if  they  could  get
together,  that  was  closed,  and  the  decision  was  auto-
matically  approved  by  the  Board,  and  if  they  couldn't
get  together  it  would  come  to  the  Board.

Q  By  whom  were  these  persons  appointed?
A  By  the  President  of  the  United  States,
Q  Did  the  President  also  appoint  concurrently
with  the  appointment  of  the  Board,  certain  members
as  a  panel  of  umpires?

mie

A  Yes,

t Board  met  under  the  joint  chairmanship  of—

BY  MR.  CARMODY:
Q  What  year  was  that?
A  1918.
The  Conference  Board  met  and  held  rather  pro-
longed  sessions  for  a  period  of  a  week  or  more,  and
then  submitted  to the  President  a  report  recommending
the  formation  of  a  National  War  Labor  Board  to  con-
tinue  throughout  the  period  of  the  war,  which  should
be  in  effect  a  Supreme  Court  of  Industrial  Relations.
and  also  recommended  for  the  President’s  consideration
the  promulgation  of  a  set  of  principles  which  should
govern  industrial  relations  during  the  period  of  the
war.

Those  recommendations  were  accepted  by  the  Pres-
ident  and  embodied  in  a  proclamation,  and  on  April
8,  1918,  in  which  the  President  named  as  members  of
the  War  Labor
the  two  joint  chairmen,  Messrs.
Taft  and  Walsh,  and
as  representatives  of  the  Employ-
ers.  Loyall  A.  Osborne,  Mr.  C.  E.  Michael,  W.  H.  Van
Dervoort,  B. L. Worden,  L.  F.  Loree.

Representing  the  Wage  Earners,  Frank  J.  Hayes,
William  L.  Hutcheson,  William  H.  Johnston,  Victor
A.  Olander,  and  T.  A.  Rickert.

Q  Did  this  board  or  the  personnel  that  you  have
described,  then  set  about  adjusting  labor  difficulties
throughout  the  country  on  the  basis  of  the  recognition

A  Yes.
Q  Could  yoy  tell  us  briefly  what  the  nature  of
the  work  of  the  Board  was,  and  how  many  cases  it
handled,  and  how  it  handled  them,  perhaps  to  give
precedence  for  this  Board?

A  The  Board  handled  something  like  more  than
twelve  hundred  cases,  during  its  life  time,  and  -  those
cases  consisted  of  two  classes—first,  what  was  known
as  Joint  Submissions,  in  which  two  parties  joined  in
asking  the  Board  to  sit  as  a  Board  of Arbitration  with
an  agreement  to  accept  whatever  award  was  handed
down,  and  the  other  cases  were  ex  parte,  in  which  either
the  employer  or  the  employees  would  bring  in  a  com-
plaint.

Those  complaints  were  immediately  assigned  to
what  we  call  the  Bureau  of Investigation,  which  inves-
tigated  the  facts  alleged  in  the  complaints,  and  if  they
found  them  to  be  substantially  as  stated  and  to  consti-
tute  something  material  in  the  way  of  a  grievance,
the  case  would  be  set  down  for  hearing,  and  the court
had compulsory  processes  so that it  was  able to secure
information  and  require  the  attendarice  of  witnesses,
and  as  a result  of those  hearings  awards  would  be
made  in  the same  way  that  they  would  be  made  in  a
case of joint  submission.

There  was  not  the  bjnding  effect  legally  for  those
ex parte cases that there was in the case  of  the joint

Q  Did  the  agreement  provide  elaborate  machinery
for  the  adjustment  of  disputes  as  they  arose  from  time
.to  time  during  the  pendency  of  the  agreement?

A  They  provided  complete  machinery,  but  it  was
a  simple  procedure.  Having  in  mind  that  the  industry
does  not  have  the  complexities  of  some  industries,  and
keeping  in  mind  also  that  in  this  particular  communi-
ty,  Cleveland,  at  this  time  there  was>a_  high  degree  of
confidence  on  both  sides,  and  therefore  there  was  no
need  for  an  elaborate  negotiating  machinery,  we  went
through  the  simple  procedures  of  giving  notice  and
presentation  of  cases,  first  locally  before  committees
and  then  before  the  board  or  the  arbitration  board  and.
the  arbitrator  who  was  brought  in  from  the  outside.

Q  Was  this  machinery  designed  for  the  primary
purpose  of  preventing  strikes  and  stoppages  of  em-
ployment  and  production?

A  That  was  the  sole,  or  the  one  cause  for  the
agreement,  namely,  to  get  away  from  this  expensive
and  oftentimes  bitter  controversy  involving  even  blood-
shed.

Q  Did  these  agreements  expressly  prohibit  strikes
and  stoppages  during  the  time  that  the  agreement  was
expected  to  run?
A  They  did.

of  the  stoppages  by  virtue  of  special  terms  which  they
were  able  to  reach  with  their  individual  ethployees.

It  is  a  road  market  community;  that
that  industry.
is,  they  send  out  road  salesmen  to  the  cities  all  over

of forty  weeks  employment  during  the  year.

Q_  Now,  moving  to  your  next  experience,  were  you
connected  in  any  way  with  the  printers  association  in
New  York  City,  and  if  so  in  what  way?
A  I  was  the  director  of  the  New  York  Employ-

ing  Printers  Association  in  1920  and  1921.

Q  Were  your  duties  similar  to  your  duties  in
Cleveland,  and  if  not,  in  what  way  were  they  differ-
ent?

A  The  duties  in  the  New  York  printing  Industry
were  broader,  and  we  not  only  had  a  defensive  program
to carry  on,  such  as  the  current  settlement  of  issues  with
individual  shop  disputes  and  the  periodic  adjustment
of  wage  scales  through  arbitration  but  we  also  had  a
program  of  a  constructive  character  to  improve  the
point  of  view  of  the  employers  in  the  industry  and  to
bring  their  staff  of  people  into  a  more  objective  frame
of  mind.
Q  Where  the  master  printers  organized  into  any

association  in  New  York?

A  Yes,  they  have  a  printers  league  section,  of
which  I was  secretary,  and  also  an  open  shop  branch,
of  which  I  was  secretary  at  the  same  time.
Q  Now,  the  printers  league  deals  with  unions?
A  It  is  a  closed  shop  employers  group.
Q  And  does  the  open  shop  group  have  so-called

-Sgon-union”  shops?

,

824

A  Some  call  it “open  shop,”  but  the  most  part  was

a  non-union  shop  group.

closely  together,  and  he  later  succeeded  me  in  New

about  the  printing  industry.

non-seasonal]  industry,  and  that  it  has  been  organized
for  over  a  century  and  has  ironed  out  many  difficulties
which  have  not  yet  been  solved  in  the  garment  industry.

a  non-profit  organization  in  New  York  State.

dustries,  particularly  manufacturers,  to  organize  into
