You  have  asked  a  number  of  questions  that  prompt
a  summary  statement  on  the  whole  employer  associa-
tion  movement  in  this  country.
I suspect  that  the  trade
union  association  movement  preceded  the  employer  or-
ganizations  by about  fifty  years.  The  trade  unions  were
organized  in  the  form  of  beneficial  associations  around
the  tarn  of  the  18th  to  the  19th  century,  around  1800.
Then,  with  the  flurry  of  activity  in  the  trade
union  field  in  the  30’s,  and  again  in  the  50’s  and  60's,
leading  to  the  organization  of  the.  A.  F.  of  L.  in  1881,
we  crossed  about  1880  a  certain  line  of  demarcation
in  the  thinking  in industry  in  this  country.
That  is,
not  only  on  the  labor  side,  but  on  the  management
side.
It  was  in  1881,  I think,  or  1880,  that  the  American
Society  of  Mechanical  Engineers  was  organized,  and
that  was  the  first  of  the  management  societies,  and  it
was  more  or  less  eclipsed  as  an  employer  organiza-

845

_.
tion,  and  it  isn’t  truly  now  an  employer  organization,
“  put  it  has  in  its  membership  men  who  for  the  most.
part  work  for  or  with  employers,  and  it  was  eclipsed  in
the  latter  years  by  this  conflict  stage  of  labor  relations
which  I referred  to  a  moment  ago.

Q  What  was  the  purpose  of  the  American  Society

'  of  Engineers?

‘was  organized,  in  other  words  than  those  which  I

and  in  addition  the  Personal  Research  Federation,  and

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  .Now,  are  the  employers  also  organized  into

Chambers  of  Commerce?

A  That  is  very  widespread.
Q  Do  the  employers  in  cities  organize  into  a  City

Chamber  of  Commerce?

A  There  are  local  Chambers  of  Commerce,  and
there  are  State  Chambers  of  Commerce,  and  there  is
the  National  or  United  States  Chamher  of  Commerce,
and  all  or  most  of  them  are  affiliated.

Q  What  kind  of employers,  in  a  general  way,  be-
long  to  the  Chamber  of  Commerce?  Is  there  any  dis-
tinction  between  the  membership  of  the  Chamber  of
Commerce  and  the  National  Association  of  Manufac-
turers?

A  The  Chamber  of  Commerce  has  a  large  body
of  its  membership  in  the  retail  trade  and  commerce,
and  professional  trades,  and  therefore  dilutes  to  a  very
large  extent  the  manufacturing  element  in  the  Cham-
ber  of  Commerce.

!

In  any  active  community,  in  any  community  where
there  is an  active  Chamber  of  Commerce,  the  important
elements,  employer  and  business  interests  of  the  com-
munity,  are  members.

MR.  SMITH:  You  will  frequently  have  in  the  same
large  industrial  State,  as,  for  instance,  Massachusetts,
both  a  State  Chamber  of  Commerce,  and  an
ation
‘such  as  the  Associated  Industries,  one  of  whfch  covers
primarily  the  people  engaged  in  mercantife  business,
and  the  other  covering  manufacturing,

THE  WITNESS:  That  is generally  true.  The  Cham-
bers  of  Commerce  locally  throughout  the  country,  and
in  the smailer  cities,  rarely have  anything
to do with

jabor  relations,  and  the  larger  cities  have  industrial

merce  of  Rochester,  and  other  larger  cities.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:  —
Q  Now,  do  the  Chambers  of  Commerce  locally,  in
State  organizations  and  nationally,  engage  in  activities
similar  to  the  National  Association  of  Manufacturers?

A  No,  I think  that  with  the  exception  of  the  pro-
gram  of  the—or  the  unit  in  the  United  States  Chamber
of  Commerce,  there  is  a  relatively  little  activity  in  the
Chamber  of  Commerce  in  this  labor  relations  field.

There  wes  for  a  time,  in the  20's,  an activity  on
the  part  of  some  Chambers  of  Commerce  in  the  Amer-
ican  Plan  Associations,  and  that  conspicuously  grew  oat
of  Chamber  of  Commerce  sponsorship  in  Cleveland,
and  Los  Angeles,  and  certain  other  cities.

Q  What  was  the  avowed  purpose  of  the  American

Plan  Association?

A  It  was  a  reaction  of  employers  in  the  post-war
period  against  organized—or  against  wide-spread  or-
ganized  dealings  of  the  war  period—they  were  open
shop  organizations.
'

Q  Was  it  anti-union?
A  Yes,  the  constitution  and  bylaws  of  the  Amer-
ican  Plan  Association  reads  like  a  Bible,  but  its  mem-
bership  was  conspicuously  composed  of  anti-union  or-
ganizations.

,

:

Q  Was  it  engaged  in  an  avowed  anti-union  cam-

paign ?

There  was  what  I understand  to  be  a  conscious
program  to  keep  the  American  Plan  movemertgon  a
local  basis,  and  therefore  there  was  no  organization.
I attempted  to  find  one  to  tie  them  together,  but  there
was  only  a  semblance,  a  chairmanship,  or  something
of that  kind,  and  they  did  have  meetings,  but  they  kept
it strictly  on  the  local  basis.

Q  Did  it  ever  express  itself  in  many  communities?
A  All  over  the  country,  but  not  so  conspicuously

Q  Do  they  arrive  at  decisions  after  conferring?
A  No,  they  are  quite  open  in  their  methods,  and
quite  frank  in  the  statement  that  they  have  been  assem-
bled  to  discuss  common  problems,  and  certain  individ-
uals  would  be  asked  to  speak,  and  they  wauid  hold  an
open  discussion,  and  there  would  be  no  conclusion  ar-
rived  at,  and  no  instructions  to  anybody.

Q  But  after  the  conference  is  over,  is  there  some-
times  uniformity  of  action  by  those  who  attended,  or
how  uniform?

A  The  pattern  in  industrial  relationships—I  mean
the  pattern  of  organization  both  in  trade  unions  and
in  employee  representation  programs,  the  pattern  is
very  uniform,  and  there  are  only  two  or  three  different
patterns,  so  that  when  you  speak  of  imitation,  or  influ-
ence,  it  is  in  the  form  of  imitation.

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Give  your  name  to  the  Reporter,  please.
A  Heber  Blankenhorn.
Q  Where  do  you  live,  Mr.  Blankenhorn?
A  Washington,  D.  C.
Q  What  is your  present  occupation?
A  Iam  an  industrial  economist  on  the  staff  of  the

National  Labor  Relations  Board.

Q  Prior  to  your  becoming  connected  with  the  Na-
ional  Labor  Relations  Board,  were  you  interested  in
the  field  of  labor  relations,  as  an  economist?

A_

I have  been  a  writer  on  labor-relations  subjects

for  about  20 years.

Q  And  have  you  published  any  publications  of  any

kind  or  any  articles  in  the  field  of  labor  relations?
A  Many  articles  and  a  couple  of  books.
Q  May  we  have  the  names  of  the  ‘books?
A  The  principal  books  that  bear  upon  this  sub-
ject  were  the  Interchurch  Reports  also  a  book,  “Strike
for  Unions”,  published  by  Wilson  Company  in  1922

Q  Were  you  connected  with  a  committee  move-
ment  or  a  committee  known  as  the  Interchurch  World
Committee  or  Commission?

A  I was  in  charge  of  their  field  investigations  and

later  acted  as  secretary  of  the  commission.

Q  During  what  years  were  your  activities  with  the

Commission ?

A  The  commission's  activities  went  from  the  years

1919  and  1920,  on  into  the  middle  of  1921.

BY  CHAIRMAN  MADDEN:
Q  How  large  a  commission  was  that?
A  The  personnel  of  the  commission,  as  listed  in

the  Reports,  there  were  seven  active  members  of  the
commission  and  two  advisory  members.
'-

the  commission?

sgt

eed

©  made  by  the  commission,  represented  by the  personnel  -
>  whom  you  have  just  mentioned?

A  A  congress  was held.in  New York  City im the
autumn  of  1919,  called  by  the  Interchurch  Wortd
Movement,  at  which  a  resolution  was  passed  to  set  up
an  independent  commission  of  inquiry  to  investigate
industrial  relations  in  general,  and,  because  of  the
steel  strike  that  had  just  been  started—because  of  the
steel  strike  in  particular—the  commission  then  con-
ducted  a  steel  inquiry  lasting  on.  into  the  spring  of
1920.  -

Q  Did  it  employ  experts  to  aid  in  the  conduct  of

that  inquiry?

A  It  hired  from  the  Bureau  of  Industrial  Rele-
tions  of  New  York,  which  was  a  privately  endowed
research  organization,  started  during  the  war  at  the  re-
quest  of  Governmental  officers—it  hired  them,  and  I,
as a  member  of it, was put  in charge  of the  field  work
of  the  commission.

In  that  work  I  had  under  me  certain  investiga-
tors  skilled  in  their  particular  lines.  The  list  is  print-
ed  in  the  report,  and  I can  name  them  over.  One  was
Joseph  Soule,  who  has  since  become  very  well  known
as  an  economist  and  author.

Another  was  Dr.  David  Saposs,  then  well  known
as  a  former  investigator  for  the  United  States  Com-
mission  on  Industrial  Relations.

|

Q  Is that  the same  David  Saposs  whose posiffon
is  now  industrial  economist  for  the  National  Labor
Relations  Board?
-

Q&  He  is  the  same  man,  and  his  study  on  the
-
*  “Mind  of  the  Immigrant  Communities”  was  very  much

‘A  “The  Mind  of  the  Immigrant  Communities”.

spect  to  its  findings  and  concerning  the  background

Q  What  is  the  name  of  that  publication,  and  the

publisher?

A  It  is  “Report  on  the  Steel  Strike  of  1919,  Com-
mission
of  Inquiry,  The  Interchurch  World  Move-
ment,”  published  by  Harcourt,  Brace  &  Company,  1920.
Q  Thereafter  was  another  publication  made  in

connection  with  the  experiences  of  the  commission?

A  In  the  following  year  what  was  called  the  sub-
reports,  made  by  the  expert  investigator  to  the  com-
mission  and  adopted  by  the  commission  were  pub-
lished  in  a  separate  volume,  entitled  “Public  Opinion
in  the  Steel  Strike,  Supplementary  Reports  to  the  Com-
mission  of  Inquiry,  The  Interchurch  World  Move-
ment”,  published  by  Harcourt,  Brace  &  Company  in
1921.

Q  In  answering  my  question  as  to  the  names  of
the  publications,  were  you  referring  to  two  ae,
which  are  the  publications?

A  Those  two  volumes,  yes.
Q  State  whether  or  not  these  volumes  are  con-
the  steel  strike  of
sidered  authoritative,  insofar  as
1919  is  concerned,  namely,  the  history  and  the  causes
of  the  strike.

A.  I  believe  they  are  regarded  as  standard,  and
they  have  been  used  frequently  in  economics  courses
in  colleges.

MR.  WIRIN:  At  this  time  we  shall  ask  the  Board
to  take  judicial  notice  of  these  two  volumes  which  the
witness  has  described.

acteristics  and  existence  of  the  system  of  espionage,

ly by  ofe  steel  company.

those  documents  contained.

of

reports

A  The  documents  were

industrial
spies  hired  by  this  steel  company  from  two  different

which  is  still  in  existence.
the  intention  of  the  commission  or  the  investigators  to
jook  for  espionage;  that  the  commission  had  been  ad-
vised  by  Judge  Gary,  of  the  Steel  Corporation,  that
they  had  evidence  of  violence  on  the  part  of  the  steel
strikers.  We  asked  for  that.

Q  Were  you  personally  a  party  to  those  confer-

ences  and  negotiations?

A  I  was.

On  invitation  of  Judge  Gary,  I  asked
President  Homer  D.  Williams,  who  was  then  president
of  the  Carnegie  Steel  Company,  who  assured  me  that
they  had  abundant  documentary  evidence  of  violence,
I sent  back  the  investigator  11  separate  times  for  that,
and  no  shred  of  paper  supporting  that  was  procured.
The  investigator,  who  was  Mr.  Robert  Littell,  then  un
der  my  direction,  then  went  on  to  other  steel  compa-
nies  asking  that  same  question.

The  Pittsburgh  Steel  Company  said,  “We  have
this  evidence”,  and  they  produced  what  they  called
the  “labor  file”  as  evidence  of  violence.  ‘This  “labor  —
file”  consisted  of  over  600  spy  reports,  from  five  or  six
operatives  hired  from  the  Corporation  Auxiliary,  and
letters  exchanged  with  half  a  dozen  other  steel  com-
panies,  including  other  spy  reports.

Incidentially,  as  I  say,  none  of  that  yielded  any

evidence  of  violence.

Q All  right;  proceed.
A  Then,  having  in our hands  the concrete  and
_  very abundant  evidence  of the operations  of the sys
tem—

Q  (interposing)  Let  me ask  you,  at this  point,

ond  volume,  “Public  Opinion  in  the  Steel  Strike.”

It also  contains,  for  the  first  time  so  far  as  I ‘knew,

blacklisting,  as  there  are  included  a  number  of  letters
exchanged  ‘between  steel  companies  to  which  lists
were  attached  of  employees  who  were  not  to  be  re
hired.  Some  employees  were  specifically  named  in
the  letters  as  being  radical  people  whom  nobody
would  want.

ers  who  had  been  active  in  the  unions?

BY  MR.  WIRIN:
Q  Now,  another  respondent  is  the  Wheeling  Steel
Corporation.  What  evidence  is  there  of  the  existence
of  espionage  at  any  of  the  plants  of  that  respondent?
A  At  the  Portsmouth  plant  there  have  been  two
Outstanding  examples  of  espionage,  one  implanted  and
discovered,  and  the  other  attempted.

Q  What  are  the  facts  pertaining  to  that?
A  One  concerns  the  secretary  of  the  joint  council
af the Portsmouth  Lodges,  J.  P.  Harris,  who,  in  the

|

:

_

- wammer  of  1954—

42°
eps  Tin  Workers.

.

Q  Yes.
te
A  (continuing)—-was  approached  by  a  mi  whe  —

introduced  himself  as  J.  C.  Boyer,  of  New  York,  re
senting  credentials—which  he  said  were  credentials—
His  conversation  with  Mr.  Harris,
from  New  York.
which  consisted  chiefly  of  an  offer  which  would  take
him  out  of  town  on  a  job,  was  interrupted  by  the  call-
ing-in of other  union  officials.
Mr.  Boyer  departed,
leaving  an  address,  which  was  Post  Office  Box  166,
Cleveland,  Ohio.

One  year  later  Mr.  Boyer  again  appeared  at  the
Portsmouth  Plant  and  definitely  offered  Mr.  Harris  a
job  out  of  town.  Two  days  afterwards  a  P.  W.  Wil-
son,  professing  to  represent  a  newspaper  system  in
Washington,  offered  Mr.  Harris  a  responsible  newspa-
per  job  in  Washington,  and,  leaving  left  as  an  address
Post  Office  Box  1661  Cleveland,  Ohio.

Q  Was  Mr.  Harris  active  in  the  work  of  the  Amal-

gamated  Lodge?

A  He  was  the  outstanding  leader  and  his  pres-
ence  was  essential  to  the functioning  of  the  joint  com-
mittee  of  the  lodges.

Subsequently,  within  the  past  two  months,  another
approach  was  made  by  Mr:  Boyer  to  discuss  an  offer
of  a  job.  Subsequently,  also,  Mr.  Boyer  was  identi-
fied,  not  as  a  New  York  man,  but  as  an  operative  em-
ployed  by the  Railway  Audit  &  Inspection  a
special  industrial  service,  of  Pittsburgh.

A  That  is  well  recognized  as  a  professional  ame a

THE  WITNESS.  It  has  been  found  by  numerous
tests,  that  the  statement  that  there  is a  spy  or  stool
pigeon  in  every  local  union  has  been  found  to  be  true  ©
in  certain  local  areas  where  tests  have  been  made,  no-
tably  in  Cincinnati  and  in  Philadelphia.
It is sufficiently  true  that  there  is in  a  certain  sense
-a  sort  of  axiom  among  labor  unions  that,  as  one  presi-
dent  of  a  large  railway  brotherhood  put  it,  he  never
knew  of  a  group  large  enough  to  be  called  a  meeting
that  was  not  small  enough  to  include  a  spy.
If,  then,  you  take  this  estimate  to  apply  to  every  lo-
cal union,  there  are  some  33,000  locals. directly  affiliated
with  the  American  Federation  of  Labor;  in  the  Interna-
fional  some  1,700 others,  and  about  8,000  independent
local  unions—and  that  is over  40,000.
And  if you  want  to  take  into  consideration  the  fact
that  most“of  these  agencies  always  have  a  stool  pigeon
to  check  on  a  stool  pigeon,  you  quickly  would  be  com-
pelled  to  double  your  estimate.

There  was  a  railway  strike  known  as the

Strike  of 1886,  which tied up  all the rail-
roads  running  south  and  west  of  Saint  Louis,  and  was
a  very  momentous  strike  in  the  sense  that  it  actually
crippled  all  traffic.  For  a  considerable  time  there  was
no  movement  of  freight  at  all.

Then  there  was  a  knitters  strike  of  20,000,  which
started  October  16,  1886,  in  the  State  of  New  York  and
ended  in  May,  1887.

There  was  also  a  coal  handler’s  and  longshoremen’s
strike  in  New  York  City  which  involved  28,000  workers
and  lasted  from  January  1,  1887,  to  February  of  that
year.

The  outstanding  strike,  however,  during  this  per-  .

ijod  was  the  general  strike  of  May  1,  1886,  in  which

sand  workers  and  tied  up  production  in  anthracite  coal

and  subsequently  to  fuel  oil?

Another  strike  which  attracted  a  great  deal  of  at-
tention  but  which  did  not  directly  involve  a  very  large
number  of  workers,  was  the  strike  of  teamsters  in  Chi-
cago  in  1905,  lasting  from  April  12  to  July  20,  involving
only  4,258  workers.
This  strike,  however,  because  it
tied  up  the  people  who  do  most  of  the  hauling  from
freight  yards  and  warehouses  and  so,  crippled  com-
merce,  both  in Chicago  as well as commerce  that is ship-
of  Chicago.  So  that  while  a  very  small  number
of  workers  were  involved,  it  is  regarded  in  the  history
of  strikes  as  an  exceedingly  important  and  vital  strike.

Q  Now,  specifically  with  reference  to  the  steel
strike  of  1919,  would  you  state  the  effect  that  that  strike
had  upon  production  in  the  industry  and  commerce  in
the production  of  the  industry?

A  The  steel  strike  of  1919  was  again  a  general
strike  of  the  entire  industry.
It  involved  at  its  peak
over  350,000  workers  directly  and,  all  in  all,  according
to  the  estimate  of  the  United  States  Senate  Investigating
Committee  of  the  steel  strike  industry,  about  500,000
workers.

I was  in  the  field  at  that  time,  visiting  the  important
steel  centers,  and  found,  for  instance,  that  around  the
Chicago  area—what  is  known  as  the  Calumet  Dis-
‘trict—the  industry  was  practically  at  a  stand-still.

In  the  Youngstown  area  the  same  situation  pre-

the Pittsburgh  area;  whereas  they did in these other

.  Q  Now, Mr. Saposs, referring  to the period from

|  Of these periods  of very intense activity,  and we find the

compared with the six years previous thereto, that is,

Q  Can  you  state  the  number  of  employees  in-

volved  in  labor  disputes  during  that  period?

.

A  The  number  of employees  involved  during  this
period,  with  incomplete  figures  for  the  year  1935,  as  fol-
lows:

In  1933,  812,137  employees  were  directly  involved

as  reported  in  disputes.

In  1934,  1,353,608  employees  were  involved  in  labor

disputes.

In  1935,  there  were  1,141,363  employees  involved.  |
So  that  during  this  period  of  three  years  there  were
over  three  million  employees  directly  involved  in strikes,
as  the  records  of the  United  States  Bureau  of Labor  Sta-
tistics  indicate.

ness  recorder  for  strikes.

days  lost.

Q  How  long  did  the  strike  last?
A  It  lasted  from  May  11,  1935,  to  August  27  of

the  same  year.

Q  Can  you  state  the  causes  of  that  strike?
A  The  cause  of  this  strike  was  the  refusal  of  the
New  York  Shipbuilding  Company  to  recognize  the
union,  to  recognize  the  union  which  is  known  as  the
Industrial  Union» of Marine  &  Shipbuilding  Workers,
as  the  exclusive  spokesman-for  the  workers  and  the
exclusive  bargaining  agency  for  the  workers  employed
by  the  company.

Q  Mr.  Saposs,  would  you  state  what  this  first  table,

A  The  first  tables  indicate  that  the  railroad  in
question  delivered  142  carloads  of  freight  to  the  New
York  Shipbuilding  Company  during  the  last  40  days
_  previous  to  the  calling  of  the  strike;  that  is,  through

That  during  the  109  days  of  the  strike,  that  is,  from
May  11  through  August  27,  only  47  carloads  were  de-
livered.

That  during  the  95  days  following  the  strike,  that
is, from  August  28  to  November  30,  these  same  railroad
lines  delivered  341  carloads  of  freight.

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  Now,  Mr.  Saposs,  was  a  study  likewise  made

-under  your  supervision  of  the  textile  strike  of  1934?

A  It  was.
Q  Would  you  state,  first,  the  number  of employees

,

involved  in  that  strike?

|

»

A  The  general  textile  strike,  which  occured  in
September,  1934,  was  the  largest  strike  in  the  United
States  since  the  steel  strike  of  1919.
In  the  aggregate,
~  .309,500  workers  were  involved,  as  reported  by  the
'.

United  States  Bureau  of  Labor  Statistics.

The  strike  was  called  on  September  3,  1934,  with
175,000  workers  responding,  and  then  gradually  more
appearing,  coming  out,  until  the  number  reached  the
total  I  have  given.

‘
The  United  States  Bureau  of  Labor  Statistics  also
\  Teports  that  about  60,000  workers  in  other  industries
. went  out  in  sympathy  with  the  textile  workers,  includ-
ing  clothing  workers,  miners,  and  hosiery  workers.
_»
The  description  of  the  strike  is  well  summarized
-  in  the  report  of  the  United  States  Board  of  Inquiry  for
the  Cotton  Textile  Industry,  appointed  by  the  Presi-
*  dent  and  popularly  known  as  the  Winant  Reports,  as

follows:

“The  present  strike  centers  primarily  around
troubles  in  the  cotton-textile  industry.
Never-
theless,  workers  in  the  wool-textile,  silk-textile,
throwing,  velvet,  upholstery,  and  drapery  indus-
tries in the wool trade and in  certain  parts of  the
hosiery  industry  have  been  called  out.”
=>  The  prime  issue  in  this  strike  was  the  right  to  col-

rective  bargaining,

as was  stated
by the union.

A The strike  occurred  over a labor dispute  between
the longshoremen  in San Francisco  and the waterfront
employers’

ating  the employers.

,  The strike

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  Now,  will  you  state  briefly  the  chief  events  of

that  strike?

A  While  the  strike  first  involved  only  the  long-
shoremen,  it spread  to  other  maritime  workers,  and,  on
May  17th,  8,000  seamen,  including  sailors,  firemen,  oil-
ers, cooks,  stewards,  and so forth,  struck  in sympathy
with  the  longshoremen,  demanding,  however,  in  addi-
tion,  union  recognition,  wage  increases,  shorter  hours,

o
y
nd  better  conditions.
mat

The  President  of  the  United.States  intervened and
appointed  a  special  board  to mediate  the strike.  In the

_ to handle the affairs of the city.

*

Q  How  long  did  the  longshoremen’s  strike  last?
A  It  finally  ended  on  October  12,  1934,
Q  Did you  state  when  it  began?
A  Yes,  I gave  it.
Q  How  long  did  the  general  strike  in  San  Fran-

cisco  last?

A  The  general  strike

in  San  Francisco  lasted
about  a  week,  I believe.  No;  it  was  called  on  July  16th
and  called  off  on  the  19th.
It  was  a  protest  strike,  in
sympathy  with  the  longshoremen,  who  were  denied  the
right  to  organize  by the  employers.

Q  Now,  in  addition  to  these  strikes  which  you
have  mentioned,  were  there  other  strikes  of  major  im-
portance  during  the  period  we  are  discussing  here,  1933
to  1935?

A  Yes,  there  were  a  number  of  other  strikes,  but
we  did  not  make  any  effort  to  study  them  in  the  same
way  that  we  did  these.

mierce?

A  No.

A  Thete  was  the  rubber  strike;  there  was  a  strike

in  the  garment  industry.  I  think  those  are  about  all.

A  No,  there  were  no  steel  strikes?

A  Yes;  there  was  a  strike  in the Wierton  plant,

Q  Would  you  state  whether  or  not  there  were  any

threatened  strikes  which  were  of  major  importance  and  .
which  were  prevented  by  Federal  intervention  of  one
sort  or  another?  ©

A  There  was  a  threatened  general  strike  in the steel
industry  in  1934,  which  was  only  averted  by  the  Presi-
dent’s  personal  intervention  and  meeting  with  the  rep-
resentatives  of  the  employers  and  the  representatives
of  the  workers  through  their  union  officials.

Q  What  were  the  issues  in  that  threatened  strike?
A  The  issue,  again,  was  the  right  to  organize,  al-

though  there  were  issues  of  wages  and  so  forth,  too.

The  President  finally  appointed  the  Steel  Labor

Relations  Board,  which  prevented  that  general  strike:

Q  Was  there  also  a  threatened  general  strike  in

the  automobile  industry?

:

-

~

A  There  was  a  similar  situation  in the  automo-
bile  industry,  where  the  workers  who  had  organized
themselves  into  unions  threatened  a  general  strike  be-
cause  the  employers  refused  to  recognize  them  as  the:
spokesmen  for  the  automobile  workers.  -

The  President  called

the  representatives  of  the
unions  to  Washington,  as  well  as  the  representatives
of  the  industry;  and,  after  a number  of  conferences,
the  threat  of  a  general  strike  in  the  automobile  indus-
fry  was  averted—the  President  succeeding  in averting
that  by  the  establishment  of  the  Automobile  Labor  .
Board.

7

ened strike  in  the  rubber  industry?

a  the representatives  of  the  industry,  and  the  Secretary

‘BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  Is  there  a  tendency  in  situations  in  these  basic
industries  about  which  we  have  been  talking  for  the
strikes  to  be  industry-wide  in  nature?

A  Yes!  these  is“a  growing  tendency  for  the  strikes
in  the  more  important  industries  to  be  industry-wide  in
nature.  —

Q  Now,  I  will  ask  you  whether  or  not  business
practice  commonly  takes  into  account  possible  inter-
ference  of  strikes  with  the  movement  of  commerce?

A  Yes;  business  practive  has,  for  a  long  time,
taken  into  consideration  the  fact  that  strikes  do  in-
terfere  with  the  movement  of  commerce  and  therefore
they  make  ita  point,  pretty  largely,  to  include  in  their
compliance  clauses  the  fact  that  if  strikes  occur  they
are  not  bound  to  fulfill  the contract.

Q  I will  ask  you  whether  or  not  you  have  pre-
pared  a  memorandum  on  the  subject  of  strike  clauses
in  contracts,  whether  such  a  memorandum  has  been
prepared  under  your  supervision.

that  memorandum  are,  to  the  best  of  your  knowledge,
accurate.

.

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  Will  you  state,  Mr.  Saposs,  whether  or  not  it
is  the  concesus  of  opinion  among  economists  that  un-
der  present  conditions  there  exists  inequality  of  bar-
gaining  power.  between  individiial  employees  and  the
ordinary  employer?

A  Yes,  there  is.
Q  Would  you  say  that  that  is  the  opinion  of

most  experts  in  this  field?

A  It  is  undoubtedly  the  opinion  of  the  large

number  of  experts  in  this  field?

-Q  Would  you  say  that  it  follows  from  that  that
there  are  powerful  incentives  arising  from  economic
necessity  for  employees  to  organize  in  their  dealings
with  employers?
‘
A  That  is  undoubtedly  the  underlying  motive  for
workers  to  organize  into  unions,  so  that  they  can  deal
on  an  approximate  equality  with  their  employers.

Q  And  if  employers  resist  this  effort  to  organize
through  discharge  of  employees,  and  other  methods  of  _
a  similar  nature,  what  recourse  do  employees  have  in
order  to  achieve  organization?

A  The  only  recourse  that  employees  have  in  a

situation  of  that  kind  is  the  strike?

Q  Now,  I will  ask  you  to  summarize  the  statistics
which  show  the  number  of  strikes  which  are  due  to  ef-
forts  on  the  part  of  employees  to  organize,  and  re-
sistance  by  employers  to  that  organization  through  an
attempted  denial  of  the  right.

A  We  have  prepared  from  the  publications  of  the
United  States  Bureau  of  Labor  Statistics  two  tables
Which  illustrate  the  growing  number of strikes  center-

_.
"ing  around  the  major  issue  of  union  recognition.
%

One  of  these  tables  gives  the  information  from  1881
to 1905;  the  other  one  from  1919  to  1934—there  being  a
gap  in  between  because  the  United  States  Bureau  of
Labor  Statistics  did  not  gather  statistics  on  this  subject

3 during  the  intervening  period.

.

ss

A  ‘They  are.

Labor  Relations  Act,  Legislative  History”  and  ask  you
whether  or  not  that  document  contains  a  complete  and
accurate  record  of  the  legislative  history  of  the  pres-
ent  National  Labor  Relations  Act.

A  Yes,  it  does  that.
Q  Does  it  contain  accurate  references  to  the

.

House  Report  and  the  Senate  Report?
A  From  my  knowledge,  it  does.
MR.  WIRIN:  We  will  offer  that  as  our  next  and
last  exhibit,  and  ask  the  Board  to  take  judicial  notice
of  the  legislative  history  of  the  National  Labor  Rela-
tions  Act  and  of  the  Reports  and  Congressional  records
referred  to  in this  exhibit.

'

by the  conference  method,  and

~s

THE  WITNESS:  It  has\been

found  that  disputes
over  substantive  conditions  can  be  arbitrated  and  can

.

_

if  I may  add,  of  the  more  important  industrial  States—
the  conciliation  divisions  of  those  States—have  found
that.  those  strikes  are  more  easily  adjustable.

MR.  SMITH:

Is  it  also  true  that  strikes  that  in-
volve  the  right  of  the  employees’  organizations  to  be
recognized  as  the  collective  bargaining  agencies,  are
less  easy  to  settle  by  the  method  of.  conference?

THE  WITNESS:  Yes;  inthe  history  of labor  leg-
islation  it  has  been  found  that  strikes  in  which  the  ma-
jor  issue  is  union  recognition  or  right  to  organize  for
collective  bargaining  are  the  most:  difficult  to  —
through  conference  or  through  mediation.
.
that fact  as  to  their  difficulty  was  one  of  the  reasons
which  prompted  Congress  in  enacting  this  Act to seek
-  the  elimination  of  the  practices  of  the  employers  in
.  ‘Yefusing  to  recognize  labor  organizations  for the  pur-
<..  Bese  of collective  bargaining?

MR.  SMITH:  And  is  it  not  true  that  recognition  of

THE  WITNESS:  The  legislative  history  of  this  Act
will show that the chief  concern  of Congress  was  to eli-
minate  these strikes, and,  therefore,  remove  that  type  of
obstruction  to commerce  by the  creation  of  this  Board.
MR.  SMITH:  Therefore  the  prevention  of interrup-
tion  of  commerce  by strikes  has  been  a  concern  of  the
Government  over’ a  long  period  of  time,  and  the  par
ticular  methods  set  out  in  this  Act  to  prevent  strikes
are  simply  adapted  to  strikes  caused  by  such  prac-
tices  of  employers ‘as are  set  forth  in  the  Act?

THE  WITNESS:  Yes,  that  is  true.  The  history  of
the  role  of Government  in  labor  relafions  would  show  ;
that  this  is  the  situation.

~  take  judicial  notice  of the  following:

The  Annals  of  the  American  Academy  of  Political
and  Social  Science,  for  March  1936,  which  volume,  No.
184,  deals  with  the  problems  of ‘organized  labor  and  so
entitled;  and  particularly  the  following  articles:

“Employee  Representation  by  Labor  Organization”

by David  J. Saposs,  the  witness,  at  page  192:

“Labor  and  Judicial  Interpretation,”  by  Alpheus

Thomas  Mason,  at  page  112;  and  an  article  entitled,

“The  National  Labor  Board,”  by Lloyd  K.  Garrison,

at  page  138.

MR.  EMERSON:  Perhaps  I had  better  ask  a  ques-

tion  of the  witness.

BY  MR.  EMERSON:
Q  Will  you  state  whether  or  not  the  Annals  of  the
American  Academy  of  Political  and  Social  Science  is re-
cognized  as  an  authoritative  source  of  information  on
social  problems?

A  It is one  of  the  oldest  scholarly  publications  is-
sued  in  the  United  States,  and  is  looked  to  by students
of economics  as  one  of  the  publications  where  they  can
expect  to  find  articles  by  leading  authors  of  the  sub-
jects  which  they  discuss  therein.
.  Q Is Professor  Mason  recognized  as  a  leading: au-

thority  in  his  field  of  labor  problems?

967

A  Profesor  Mason,  who  teaches  at  Princeton  Uni-
versity,  bas  been  regarded  as  an  outstanding  authority
on  labor  law.

:
Q  And  Mr.  Lloyd  K.  Garrison,  what  qualifications

does  he  have  in  the  field  of  labor  relations?

A  Mr.  Lioyd  K. Garrison  is now  the dean  of the
law  school  of  the  University  of  Wisconsin,  and  was  for-
merly  the  Chairman  of  the  first  National  Labor  Rele-

Q  Is  that  the  predecessor  of  the  present  Board?
A  The  predecessor  of the  present  Board,  and  he  has

been  accepted  as  an  authority  in  this  field.

MR.  CARMODY:  I realize  you  did  not  testify  with
reference  to  strikebreaking  activities,  but  I ask  you  this
question,  if  you  know:

Is  it  not  true  that  in  many  cases  where  employers
refuse  to  bargain  collectively  with  their  employees,  but
insist  on  individual  bargaining,  that  when  a  strike
comes,  they  make  a  collective  bargain  with  a  strike-
breaking  agency,  or  the  leader  of  a  strikebreaking
agency  for  all  of  the  employees  who  come  in  in  place
of  the  employees  that  go  out?  Is  that  not  true?

.

THE  WITNESS:  Yes,  it  is.

I  find  it  to  be  ‘the
ease  that  either  the  head  of  the  detective  agency  acts  as
a  drone,  who  does  all  the  hiring  of  the  detectives  and
guards  to  be  used  in  the  breaking  of  the  strike  and  who
very  frequently  collects  a  lump  sum  from  the  employer
and  then  pays  each  one  of  these  hired  individuals  what-
ever  wages  had  been  agreed  upon.

MR.  CARMODY:  Does  that  strike  you  as  anoma-

lous  position  for  an  employer  to  find  himself  in?

THE  WITNESS:  Yes,  it  very  clearly  is  a  contra-

diction  of  his  opposition  to  collective  bargaining.

“We  have  seen  that  the  word  ‘commerce’  is the  equivalent  of  the
Plainly  the  incidents
in the mining of coal do not constitute
such  intercourse.  The employment  of men,  the fixing of their  wages,
hours  of labor  and  working conditions,  the  bargaining  in  respect  of
these things—whether  carried  on  separately  or  collectively—each  and
all  constitute  intercourse  for  the  purposes  of  production,  not  of
trade.  The  latter  is  a  thing  apart  from  the  relation  of  employer
and employee,  which in all producing occupations  is purely local  in
character.  Extraction  of  coal from the mine is the aim and the
completed  result  of local activities.  Commerce  in the coal  mined
being  by  force  of  these  activities  but  by  nego-

to impede  or  destroy  interstate  commerce  by means  of  a  strike  in  a
manufacturing  plant,  or other  like direct  obstruction  to or burden
on interstate  commerce.  The  order  we  are asked  to enforce  is not

Jonzs  &  Lavoemun  Srexex  Corporation

This  cause came  on to be heard  on the petition  of  the National
Labor  Relations  Board,  for  the  enforcement  of  an  order  of  the
National  Labor  Relations  Board,  and  was  argued  by counsel;

On  consideration  whereof,  It  is now  here  ordered,  adjudged,  and
by this  Court,  that  said  petition  be.  and  the  same  is hereby,

Re:  Jones  &  Laughlin  Steel  Corporation
Dar  Sm:  In  accordance  with  the  request  set  forth  in  your  letter
of  September  22,  we  ate  returning  herewith  executed  original  of  a
stipulation  with  respect  to  the  exhibits,  to  be  lodged  with  the
Supreme  Court  of  the  United  States  in  National  Labor  Relations
Board  vs.  Jones  &  Laughlin  Steel  Corporation.  We  are  willing  to
of the  exhibits,  if  the Court  approves,  on

Steel  Corporation  to the  complaint  of the  National
Jones  & La
Board,  which  was  omitted  from  the  record  in  the
Labor
Circuit  Court  of  Appeals,  be  certified  to  the  Supreme  Court  as  a

vertently  omitted  before  and  we  think  it essential  that  it  be  made  a
part of the record  before  the  Supreme  Court.

We  also  wish  it understood  that  we  are  executing  this  stipulation,

expect  the  case  to be brought  before  the  Supreme  Court  and  argued
without  any delay, if certiorari  should  be. granted.

(c)  The National  Labor  Relations  Act, in’
to authorize
said  Board to hear and determine  the matters
in said com-
plaint,  denies  respondent  the  right of trial  by jury, in violation  of the
.  Constitution  of  the  United  States,  and  particularly  Article  VII  of

ing

ibed

-

the  Amendments  thereto.

(d)  Section  * of  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  upon  which
.  said  complaint  is based,  is invalid,  void  and  of no  effect,  in that  it is
directly  contrary  to  the Constitution  of the  United  States  of America
and  to decisions  of thie Supreme  Court  of the  United  States construing
said  Constitution,  and  said  Board,  in  asserting  jurisdiction  to  hear
said  complaint,  is  acting  unlawfully  and  in  defiance  of  the  Consti-
tution  and  said  decisions.
(e)  The  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  in  purporting  to  confer
jurisdiction  upon  said  National  Labor  Relations  Board  to  hear  and
determine  the matters  charged  in said  ocmplaint,  is illegal, void  and  of
no  effect,  by reason  of  the  fact  that  it  purports  to  confer  upon  said
Board  judicial  power  in  violation  of  the  Constitution  of  the  United
States,  and  particularly  Section  I and  Section  II  (1)  of  Article  II
thereof.
(f)  The  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  and  particularly  Section
7 thereof,  are  illegal,  void  and  of  no  effect,  in  that  they  constitate
an  arbitrary  and  unreasonable  interference  with  the  contractual  re-
lationships  between  the  respondent  and  its  employees,  and  with  the
-  Might and  freedom  of  the  respondent,  as  guaranteed  by the  Consti-
tution  of  the  United  States,  to  enter  into  contractual  relations  with
its  employees,  all  in  violation  of  the  Constitution  of  the  United
States,  and  particularly  Article  V of  the  Amendments  thereto.
(g)  The  matters  charged  in  said  complaint  and  over  which  said
Board  is assuming  to  exercise  jurisdiction,  concern  transactions  and
relations  between  employer  and  employees  which  are  local  and  intra-
state  in  character  and  are  not  subject  to  regulation  by the  Congress
of  the  United  States  through  said  National  Labor  Relations  Act
or  otherwise.

(h)  The  National  Labor  Relations  Act  can  not  confer  authority
on  said  Board  to  investigate  or  determine,  or  assume  jurisdiction
over  matters  or  controversies  between  employers  and  employees  en-
gaged  in  manufacturing  or  production,  for  the  reason  that  such
activities  are  not  and  do  not  affect  interstate  commerce,  and there-
fore  are  not  subject  to  said  Act, and  for the  reason  that-the  business
of the  respondent  and  its relationships  with  its employees  are  not in
and  do  not  affect  interstate  commerce,

and  being  the prosecutor  thereof,  is disqualified  from  exercising  or
purporting  to  exercise  a  judicial  function,  and  from  hearing  said
proceedings  and  making orders  therein.

(j) The  oy  Labor  Relations  Act,  under  any  construction
provisions  of  the  Constitution  of  the  United

NATIONAL  LaBor  RELATIONS  BOARD,  PETITIONER

Vv.

JONES  & LAUGHLIN  STEEL  CoRPORATION

PETITION  FOR  A  WRIT  OF  CERTIORARI  TO THE  UNITED
STATES  CIRCUIT  COURT  OF  APPEALS  FOR  THE  FIFTH
CIRCUIT

The  Solicitor  General,  on  behalf  of the  National
Labor  Relations  Board;  prays  that  a  writ  of  cer-
tiorari  issue  to  review  the  judgment  of the  United
States  Circuit  Court  of Appeals  for  the  Fifth  Cir-
cuit,  entered  in  the above  cause  on  June  15,  1936
(R. 977),  denying  a petition  by the  National  Labor
Relations  Béard  for  the  enforcement  of  its  cease
and desist  order  against  the Jones  & Laughlin  Steel
Corporation,  made  pursuant  to the  National  Labor
Relations  Act.

°

OPINIONS  BELOW

The findings  of fact and the order  of the National
Labor  Relations  Board  (R.  23-45),  are  not  re-
(1)

2

The  opinion  of  the  Circuit  Court  of
ported.
Appeals  (R. 975-977)  is reported  in 83 F.  (2d)  998.

JURISDICTION

The  judgment  of the  Circuit  Court  of Appeals
was  entered  June  15, 1936  (R.977).  A petition  for
rehearing  was  denied  July  15, 1936  (R. 980).  The
jurisdiction  of this  Court  is invoked  under  Section
240  (a) of the Judicial  Code  as  amended  by the  Act
of  February  13,  1925,  and  Section  10  (e)  of  the
National  Labor  Relations  Act.

QUESTIONS  PRESENTED
1.  Whether,  as applied  to  respondent,  the  Na-
tional  Labor  Relations  Act  is a valid  exercise  of the
power  of Congress  to regulate commerce  among  the
States  and  with  foreign  nations.

2.  Whether  the provisions  of the  National  Labor
Relations  Act  here  involved  violate  Article  III  of
the  Constitution  in  that  they  improperly  delegate
judicial  powers  to  the  National  Labor  Relations
Board.

3.  Whether  the provisivtis of the National Labor
Relations  Act  here  involved  violate  "the  Fifth
Amendment  to  the  Constitution  in  that  they  de-
prive  respondent  of  liberty  or  property  without
due  process  of law. :

4.  Whether  the provisions  of the National  Labor
Relations  Act  here  involved  violate  the  Seventh
Amendment  to  the  Constitution  in  that  they  im-
properly deprive respondent  of the right of trial by
jury.

3

STATEMENT

On  January  23,.1936,  a  written  charge  against
respondent,  the  Jones  & Laughlin  Steel  Corpora-
tion,  was  filed  with  the  Regional  Director  for  tke
Sixth  Region  of  the  National  Labor  Relations
Board;  pursuant  to  Section  10  (b)  of the  National
Labor  Relations  Act,  infra  (R. 6-11).  The charge
was  filed  by  the  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200,
Amalgamated  Association  of  Iron,  Steel  & ‘Tin
Workers  of  North  America,  and  alleged  that  the
company  had  engaged  in and  was  continuing  to
engage  in  certain  unfair  labor  practices  affecting
commerce  within  the  meaning  of  Section  8, sub-
divisions  (1)  and  (3),  and  Section  2, subdivisions
(6)  and  (7)  of  the  Act,  infra  (R. 6).

Pursuant  to  Section  10  (b)  of  the  Act,  the
Regional  Director  issued  and served  upon  respond-  t
ent  a  complaint  and  notice  of hearing  (R:  12-16).
The  complaint  alleged  that  respondent  had  en-
gaged  in  and  was  engaging  in  unfair  labor  prac-
tices  prohibited  by subsections  (1) and  (3)  of See-
tion  8,  in  that  respondent  was  interfering  with,
coercing,  and  restraining  its  employees  in  their
self-organization  and:  free  choice.  of  representa-
tives  for purposes  of collective  bargaining or other
mutual  aid  or  protection  and  had  discharged  and  —
demoted  employees  because  they  had  joined  the

4

above-named  labor  organization,
diseourage  membership  therein.

in  order  to

On  February  27, 1936,  respondent  filed  a  special
appearance  and  answer,  admitting  the  discharges
-and  demotion,  but  averring  that  in  all  cases  they
were  made  because  of inefficiency  and  violation  of
respondent's  rules.  Respondent  also  asserted  that
the Act  was  unconstitutional  upon  several  grounds,
and  that  its activities  were  intrastate  and  not  sub-
ject  to  regulation  by Congress  or  the  Board  (Bd.
Exh.  1).

Extensive  hearings  were  held  before  the  Board.
At  the  close  of  the  evidence  on  the  nature  of  re-
spondent’s  business,  respondent  moved  to  dismiss
the  proceeding  for  lack  of  jurisdiction  (R.  129).
The  motion  was  denied,  whereupon  counsel  for
respondent  withdrew  from  further  participation
in the  hearings  (R.  130).

On  April  9,  1936,  the  Board  handed  down  its
decision  setting  forth  its  findings  of  fact  (R.  23-
43)  and  entered  the  order  here  in  question  (R.
44-45).  “These  findings  may  be  summarized  as

On  the  basis  of  these  findings,  the  Board  con-
cluded,  as  a  matter  of  law,  that  respondent  was
engaging  in unfair  labor  practices  within  the terms
of Section  8, subdivisions  (1) and  (3), and Section
2,- subdivisions  (6)  and  (7)  of the Act  (R. 43-44).
It therefore  issued  an  order  requiring  respondent
to cease  and  desist  from  practices  tending  to inter-
fere  with  or  restrain  the  organization  of  its  em-
ployees;  to reinstate,  with  back  pay,  the ten  men
discharged  in the  commission  of such  unfair labor
practices;  and to post  notices  in the shops  and

authority  of Congress  under  the  commerce  clause
of the Constitution.  The court  did not  pass  upon
the  other  constitutional  objections  urged  by  re-
spondent.

SPECIFICATION  OF  ERRORS  TO  BE  URGED

The  Circuit  Court  of Appeals erred:
(1)  In  holding  that  the  order  of  the  Board
herein  was  not  shown  to  be  one  authorized  to  be
made  under  the authority  of Congress.

(2) In construing  the Board’s  order  in  this case
as  being  directed  merely  to  respondent’s  produc-
tion  operations  and  production  employees,  rather
than  to  the  protection  of  respondent’s  interstate
commerce  against  direct  and  substantial  burdens
brought  about by industrial  strife  among  any or
all of reapondent’s  employees,  as  well  as  others.

Sia Noee. fart Se toe of  Congress to
interstate  commerce  from  the burdens  and
of industrial strife is dependent upon

ae

a»

(4)  In holding  that a strike  or boyevtt  orighiat-
ing  among  respondent’s  production  empléyeess
would  result  merely  in  stopping  production  ‘with
an  effect  on  interstate  commerce
too remote  to War-
rant  Federal  regulation  to  prevent  such dispute,
contrary  to  the  findings  by  Congress.  based  on  a
study  of the  effects  of such  disputes,  and  contrary
to  the  facts  in  the  record,  both  of  which  factual
bases  justify  the  conclusion  that  such  a  strike
would  constitute  a direct  burden  upon  and  obstruc-
tion  of interstate  commerce  itself.

f

(5)  In holding  that  the  Act  as applied  hegein is
not  an  appropriate  exercise  of the  power  and  re-
sponsibility  of Congress  under  the commerce  clause
of  the  Constitution  to  protect  interstate  commerce
from  burdens  and  obstructions  from  whatever
source.

(6)  In  holding  in  effect  that  the  particular  ori-
gin of industrial  strife  characterizes  the  nature  of
the  resulting  burden  or  obstruction  upon  interstate
ecommerce,  and  in  not  judging  the  nature  of such
burden  or  obstruction  in  its  relation  to interstate
commerce,  from  the character  and  potentialities  of
the  strife  itself.

(7)  In  holding  that  the  decision  of  this  Court
in  Carter  v.  Carter  Coal  Company,  Nos.  636 and
651,  October  term,  1935, decided  May  18, 1936, in-
volving  the  Bituminous  Goal. Conservation Act of
1935,  was controlling  as to the non-applicability of
the  National  Labor  Relations.  Act  under  the  facts
of this  case.

ment  or any other  provision  of the Constitution.

REASONS  FOR GRANTING  THE  WRIT
1.  The  questions  presented  in  the  instant  case

are  of the  utmost  public  importance.

The  National  Labor  Relations  Act  is  the  culmi-
nation  of a long series  of attempts  by the  Federal
Government  to deal  with  the national  problem  pre-
sented  by labor  disputes  which  obstruct  and  burden
the  free  flow  of  interstate  and  foreign  commerce.
The-gravity  and  national  character  of the  problem
have  long been  realized.  Numerous  and extensive
studies  of the  causes  of disputes,  and  of  effective
ways  of  preventing  their  occurrence,  have  been
made by both Congressional  and Presidential  Com-
mittees  ‘since  1882.  On  many  occasions  Federal
intervention  in particular  disputes-has  been  found
.  necessary  to  protect  interstate  and  foreign  com-
merce  from  serious  harm,  existing  or  threatened.
The present  Act, an  outgrowth  of this  experience,
attempts  to go  beyond  ad  hoc  interference  and,  in
brief,  to  ‘apply  to  other  enterprises  in  inter-
state  commerce  the principles  successfully  applied
to railroads by the Railway  Labor  Act of 1926,  ap-
|  proved and 5 Eso in  Tezas  & New  Orleans  Ratl-

road  v.  Brotherhood  of Railway  Clerke,  281  U. 8.

1

The  court  below  has  held  that  the  application  of
this  Act  to respondent  is beyond  the  power of Con-
gress  under  the  commerce  clause  of  the  Constitu-
tion.  Whether  the power  of  the  Federa]  Govern-
ment  is thus  limited  is a  question  of  vital  interest
It is sub-
to the commercial  welfare  of the nation.
mitted  that  the  public  interest  will  be promoted  by
the  settlement  in  this  Court  of  the  questions  in-
volved.

Z.  The  decision  of the  Circuit  Court  of Appeals.
that  the  order  of  the  Board  was  not  within  the
power  of  Congress  is  not  in  harmony  with  the
principles  laid  down  by this  Court.

Congress  is  fully  empowered  to  protect  inter-
state  commerce  from  burden  and  injury,  however
local  may  be the effective  causes.
Stafford  v.  Wal-
lace,  258  U.  S.  495;  Board  of Trade  v.  Olsen,  262.
U.S.  1;  United  States  v.  Ferger,  250  U.  8.  199.
-  More  specifically,  it has  adequate  authority,  as  in-
voked  in this  Act,  to maintain  the  orderly  conduct
of interstate  commerce  by providing  for  the peace-
ful  settlement  of  disputes  which  recurrently  bur-
den  it or  threaten  so  todo.
Texas  & New  Orleans
Railroad  v.  Brotherhood  of Railway  Clerks,  281  |
U.  8.  548.  In  many  operations  besides  railroad
transportation,  labor  digputes  can,  and  Congress
has  correctly  found  im the recitals  of the  Act  that

.  merce.

~  gtructions  serves  the  purpose  of  a  specific  intent
to  interrupt  or  burden  interstate  commerce  in  the
Stafford  v.  Wallace,  supra,  pp.
particular  case.
520-521;  Board  of Trade  v.  Olsen,  262  U.S.  1, 37;
United  Mine  Workers  v.  Coronado  Coal  Co.,  259
U.  8. 344, 408.

A. L,  A.  Schechter  Corp.  V.  United  States,  295
U. 8. 495, and  Carter v. Carter  Coal  Company,  Nos.
636  and  651,  decided  May  18,  1936,  are  not  to  the
contrary.  In those  eases  the  Court  held  unconstitu-
tional  the  regulation  of wages  and  hours  in  activi-
ties  occurring  after  the  movement  of  interstate
commerce  has  ceased  or  before  it  has  begun,  but
made  it clear  that  where  the activity  occurs,  as  in
tHe present  case,  in the midst  of a  well-defined  flow
of  commerce,  it  is  still  governed  by the  rule  of
Stafford  v.  Wallace  and  Board  of Trade  v.  Olsen,
supra.

Moreover,  the  Schechter  and  Carter  cases  deal
with an  entirely  different  type of regulation.  They
concern  labor  provisions  which  sought  under  the
commerce  clause  to  regulate  hours  and  wages  of
production  employees  so  as  to stabilize  production
costs and  thus  to  stabilize  interstate  commerce  in
and coal.  The  pyesent  Act  does  not  regu-
poultry
late  hours or  wages.  It.is  directed  solely  te  the
protection  of  interstate  commerce  itself  frou:  une

3

actual  interruption,  burden  and  obstruction  caused
by  employer-employee  strife,  such  as  strikes  and
boycotts.  While,  as  a means  to such  protection,  the
present  order  restrains  the  conduct  of  a  corpora-
tion  engaged  extensively  in interstate  commerce  to-
ward  its  production  employees,  among  others,  this
does  not  make  the  order  a  regulation  of production
operations  rather  than  a  regulation  of  commerce.
The  relation  to  production,  if  it  may  be  said  to
exist  at  all,  is incidental  and  the  regulation  is per-
missible  because  necessary  to  prevent  employer-
employee  conflict  under  such  factual  circumstances
and  of  such  a  nature  as  would  immediately,  di-
rectly,  and  substantially  burden  or  obstruct  the
actual  physical  movement  of, and  continuous  trad-
ing  in,  commodities
in  interstate  commerce.
While  the  Carter  case  did  deal  with  a  provision  of
the  Bituminous  Coal]  Conservation  Act  relating  to
collective  bargaining,  that  provision  was  consid-
ered  merely  a  subordinate  part  of  the  wage  and
hour  provisions,  and  as  a  means  of fixing  wages
and  hours  in  production.
considered  or  passed  upon  the  issue  presented  here.
3.  The  record  meets  the  requirements  set  forth
in Borden’s  Co.  v.  Baldwin,  293  U.S.  194.  Exten-
sive  evidence  was  taken  (R. 399-970)  and  findings
made  by the  Board  (R. 27-31,  42-43)  regarding  the
steel  industry,  the  effect  of labor  disputes  upon  in-
terstate  commerce,  and  the  appropriateness  of the

Neither  case,  therefore,  .

.

14

means  provided  in  this  Act  for  the  elimination  or
mitigation  of such  disputes.

|

Wherefore,  it is respectfully  submitted  that  this
petition  for a writ  of certiorari  to review  the  judg-
ment  of the  Circuit  Court  of Appeals  for the  Fifth
Circuit  should  be granted.

The inequality  of bargaining  power  between  em-
ployees  who  do  not  possess  full  freedom  of  asso-
ciation  or  actual  liberty  of  contract,  and  employ-
ers  who.  are  organised  in  the  corporate  or  other
-  forms  of ownership  association  substantially  bur-
dens  and affects  the flow  of  commerce,  and  tends
to  aggravate  recurrent  business  depressions,  by
depressing  wage  rates  and  the  purchasing  power
of  wage  earners  in  industry  and  by preventing
the  stabilization  of  competitive  wage  rates  and
working  conditions  within  and  between industries.
Experience  has  proved  that  protection  by  law
to organize  and bargain
of the right of employees
collectively  safeguards  commerce  from  injury,  m-
pairment,  or  interruption,  and  promotes  the  flow
ef  commerce  by removing  certain  recognized
strife  and  unrest,  by  en-
sources  of  im@ustrial
couraging  practices  fundamental  to  the  friendly
adjustment  of  industrial  disputes  arising  out  of
differences  as  to  wages,  hours,  or  other  working
conditions,  and by restoring  equality of bargaining
power  between  employers  and employees.

It  is  hereby  declared  to  be  the  policy of the
United  States  to  eliminate  the  causes  of  certain
substantial  obstructions  to the free How  of  com-

DEFINITIONS

Sec.  2.  When  used in this  Aet—
(1)  The  term  “‘person” meludes  one  or  more
individuals,  partnerships,  associations,  corpora-
tions,  legal  representatives,  trustees,  trustees  in
bankruptcy,  or  receivers.

(2)  The  term  “employer”  includes  any person
acting  in  the  interest  of an  employer,  directly  or
indirectly,  but  shall  not  include  the United  States,
or  any  State  or  political  subdivision  thereof,  or
any  person  subject  to  the  Railway  Labor  Act, as
amended  from  time  to time,  or  any labor  organiza-
tion  (other  than  when  acting  as  an  employer),  or
anyone  acting in  the  capacity  of officer or  agent  of
auch  labor  organization.

(3) ‘The term “employee”  shall include  any  em-

of a particular employer,  untess.the  Act explicitly

whose  work  has ceased as a  consequence  of, or in

cause  of  any  unfair labor  practice, and  who  has
not  obtained  any  other  regular  and  substantially
equivalent  employment,  but  shall  not  include  any
individual  employed as  an  agricultural laborer, or
in the domestic  service  of any  family or  person at
his home, or any individual  employed by his parent
or  spouse.

.  individual  or  labor  organization.

(11)  The term  ‘‘old  Board’’  means  the National
Labor  Relations  Board  established by Executive.
Order  Numbered  67638  of  the  President  on  June

pursuant  to Title  I of the  National  Industrial  Re-
covery  Act  (48  Stat.  195)  as  amended.  and  con-

June  14, 1935.

NATIONAL  LABOR  RELATIONS  BOARD

Src.  3.  (a)  There  is hereby  created  a  board,  to
be  known  as  the  ‘‘National  Labor  Relations.
Board’’  (hereinafter  referred  to as  the ‘‘Board’’),
which  shal]  be  composed  of  three  members,  who
shall  be  appointed  by the  President,  by and  with
the advice  and  consent  of the  Senate.  One  of the
original  members  shall  be  appointed  for  a  term
of one  year,  one  for  a term  of three  years,  and  one
for  a  term  of five  years,  but  their  successors  shall
be  appointed  for  terms  of five  years  each,  except
that  any  individual  chosen  to  fill  a  vacancy.  shall
be. appointed  only  for  the  unexpired  term  of the
member  whom  he  shall  succeed.  The  President
shall designate  one  member  to  serve  as  chairman
of the Board.  Any  member  of the Board  may  be
removed  by the  President,  upon  notice  and  hear-
ing,  for  neglect  of  duty  or  malfeasance  in office,
but for no  other cause.

(b)  A vacancy  in  the  Boatd shall not  impair
the right of the remaining  members  to exercise  al?

'

.  3§o  in  original.

the powers  of  the Board,  dnd two  members  of  the
Board  shall, at all times,  constitute  a  quorum.  The
Board  shall  have  an  official  seal  whieh  shall  be
judicially  noticed.

(c)  The  Board  shall  at  the  close  of  each  fiscal
year  make  a  report  in writing  to Congress  and to
the  President  stating  in  détail  the  cases  it  has
heard,  the  decisions  it  has  rendered,  the  names,
salaries,  and  duties  of all employees  and  officers  in
the employ  or  under  the supervision  of the  Board,
and  an  account  of all moneys  it has  disbursed.

.

Sec.  4.  (a)  Each  member  of the  Board  shall  re-
ceive  a salary of $10,000  a year,  shall  be eligible  for
reappointment,  and  shall  not  engage  in  any  other
business,  vocation,  or  employment.
The  Board
shall  appoint,  without  regard  for  the  provisions  of
the  civil-service  laws  but  subject to  the  Classifica-
tion  Act of  1923,  as  amended,  an  executive  secre-
tary,  and  such  attorneys,  examiners,  and  regional
directors,  and  shall  appoint  such  other  employees
with  regard  to existing  laws  applicable  to  the  em-
ployment  and compensation  of officers  and employ-
ees  of the  United  States,  as  it may  from  time  to
time  find  necessary  for  the  proper  performance
of its duties  and as may be from  time  to time  ap-
propriated  for  by Congress.  The  Board  may  es-
tablish  or  utilize  such  regional,  local,  or  other
agencies,  and  utilize  such  voluntary  and  uncom-
pensated  services, as may from time to time be
needed.  Attorneys  appointed  under  this  section
EE  ee  ee  ee  ae
the Board  in any case  in court..  Noth-
in this Act shall be construed  tb authorize  the
to  appoint  individuals  for  the  purpose  of

:

conciliation  or  mediation  (or for statistical work),
where  such  service  may  be obtained  from  the De-
:
partment  of Labor.
(b)  Upon  the appointment  of the three  original
members  of  the Board  and the designation
of its
chairman,  the old  Board  shall  cease  to exist.  All
employees  of the old  Board  shall  be transferred  to
and  become  employees  of  the  Board  with  salariés
under  the  Classification  Act  of  1923,  as  amended,
without  acquiring  by such  transfer a permanent  or
civil  service  status.
All records,  papers,  and prop-
erty  of the old  Board  shall  become  records,  papers,
and  property  of  the  Board,  and  all  unexpended
funds  and  appropriations  for the  use  and  mainte-
viance  of  the  old  Board  shall  become  funds  and
appropriations  available  to  be  expended  by  the
Board  in the  exercise  of the  powers,  authority,  and
duties  conferred  on  it by this  Act.

(c)  All  of the  expenses  of the Board,  including
all  necessary  traveling  and  subsistence  expenses
outside  the  District  of  Columbia  incurred  by the
members  or  employees  of  the  Board  under  its
orders,  shall  be  allowed  and  paid on  the presenta-
tion  of itemized  vouchers  therefor approved  by the
Board  or  by any  individual  it designates  for that
purpose.

Sec. 5.  The principal office of  the Board shall be

in the District of Columbia,
but it may meet and
exercise  any or all of its powers  at any other place.
The  Board  may,  by one or  more of its members  or
by  such agents  or  agencies as it  may
prosecute  any  inquiry  necessary  to its functions  in
who par-
any  part of  the United States.  A member
ticipates  in such  an  inquiry shall  not be disqualified

» ee J ~

,

from  subsequently  participating  in  a  decision  of
the Board  in the same  case.

Src.  6.  (a)  The Board  shall  have  authority  from
time  to  time  to  make,  amend,  and  rescind  such
rules  and regulations  as may be necessary  to carry
out the provisions  of this  Act.  Such  rules  and reg-
ulations  shall  be  effective  upon  publication  in  the
manner  which  the  Board  shall  prescribe.  .

RIGHTS  OF  EMPLOYEES

Src.  7.  Employees  shall  have  the  right  to  self-
organization,  to  form,  join,  or  assist  labor  organi-
zations,  to  bargain  collectively  through  represen-
tatives  of  their  own  choosing,  and  to  engage  in
concerted  activities,  for  the  purpose  of  collective
bargaining  or  other  mutual  aid  or  protection.
~  Sxc.  8.  It shall  be  an  unfair  labor  practice  for
an  employer—

.

(1)  To  interfere  with,  restrain,  or  coerce  em-
ployees  in  the  exercise  of the  rights  guaranteed  in
section  7.

(2)  To dominate  or  interfere with  the  formation
or  administration  of any  labor  organization  or  con-
_  tribute  financial  or  other  support  to  it:  Provided,
That  subject  to  rules  and  yegulations  made  and
published  by the Board  sarge to  section 6 (a),
an  employer  shall  not  be  prohibited  from  permit-
ting employees  to confer  with  him  during  working
hours without  loss  of time  or  pay.

(8) By discrimination  in regard  to  hire  or  ten-
ure  of employment  or  any term  or  condition  of em-
ployment to  encourage  or  discourage  membership

—

_

in any labor organization:  Provided; That:nothing
in this Act, or in the National  Industrial  Recovery
Act  (U.  8S. C., Supp.  VII,  title  15, secs. '701-712),
as  amended  from  time  to time, or in  any code or
agreement  approved  or  prescribed  thereunder,  or
in any other  statute  of the United States,  shall pre-
elude  an employer  from making an agreement  with
a  labor  organization  (not established,  maintained,
or  assisted  by any  action  defined  in this Act  as an
unfair  labor  practice)  to require  as  a condition  of  |
employment  membership  therein,  if such  labor  or-
ganization  is the representative  of the employees  as
provided  in section  9 (a), in the  appropriate collee-
tive  bargaining  unit  covered  by  such  agreement
when  made.

(4)  To  discharge  or  otherwise  discriminate
against an  employee  because  he has filed  charges-or  -
given  testimony  under  this  Act.

-.

-  (5)  To  refuse  to  bargain  collectively  with  the
representatives  of his  employees,'  subject  to  the
provisions  of Section  9 (a).

REPRESENTATIVES  AND  ELECTIONS

Seo.  9.  (a)  Representatives  designated  or  se-
lected  for  the  purposes  of collective  bargaining  by  |
the majority  of the employees  in a unit  appropriate  —
for  such  purposes,  shall  be  the  exclusive  represen-
tatives  of  all-the  employees  in  such  unit’  for  the
purposes  of collective  bargaining  in respect  to rates
of pay,  wages,  hours of employment,  or  other  con-
ditions  of  employment:  Provided,  That.  any ,in-
dividual  employee  or  a  group  of  employees  shall
have  the  right  at  any  time  to  present  grievances  to  —
their  employer.

(b) The  Board shall decide  in each case  whether,
in order  to insure  to employees  the  full  benefit  of
their  right  to  self-organization  and  to  collective
bargaining,  and otherwise  to effectuate  the policies
of this  Act,  the  unit  appropriate  for  the  purposes
of colleetive  bargaining  shall  be the  employer  unit,
eraft  unit, plant unit, or subdivision  thereof.

(c)  Whenever  a  question  affecting  commerce
arises  concerning  the  representation  of employees,
the  Board  may  investigate  such  controversy  and
certify to the parties,  in writing,  the name  or  names
of the representatives  that  have  been  designated  or
In  any  such  investigation,  the  Board
selected.

der  section  10_or otherwise,  and  may  take  a  secret
ballot  of  employees,  or  utilize  any  other  suitable
method  to ascertain  *  such  representatives.

suant  to section  10  (c)  is based  in whole  or  in part

PREVENTION  OF  UNFAIR

Src.  10.

(a) The  Board  is empowered, ss het

inafter
péOvided, to  prevent  any person’ trom en-
gaging  in  any  unfair labor practice  (listed i in peC-
tion  8)  affecting  commerce,  This  power shall be
exclusive,  and  shall  not  be  affected  by any other
means  of adjustment  or  prevention  that has been
'  or  may  be  established  by agreement,  code, law, or

otherwise.

(b)  Whenever  it is charged that any  person  has.
engaged in  or  is engaging  in any such unfair  labor
practice,  the  Board,  or  any  agent  or agency  desig-
nated  by the  Board  for  such  purposes,  shall  have.
power  to  issue  and  cause  to  be  served  upon  such
person  a  complaint stating  the  charges in  that re-
spect,  and  containing  a notice  of hearing before the
Board or a member  thereof, or  before  a designated
agent  or  agency,  at  a place  therein  fixed,  not  less
than  five  days  after  the  serving  of said complaint.
Any  such  complaint  may  be amended  by the mem-
ber, agent, or agency  conducting the hearing or  the
Board  in  its  discretion  at any  time  prior  to the
issuance  of  an  order  based  thereon.  The  person
so  complained  of  shall  have  the  right  to file  an
answer  to. the  original  or  amended  complaint
to appear  in  person  or  otherwise  and  give  testi-
mony  at  the  place  and  time  fixed  in the  complaint.
In  the  discretion  of the  member,  agent, or agency
conducting  the  hearing  or  the  Board,  any other
person  may  be allowed  to intervene in  the said pro-
ceeding  and  to  present  testimony.
In’ any  such
proceeding  the  rules  of  evidence  prevailing  in
courts  of law  or  equity  shall  not be aces

EE

(c)  The testimony  taken  by such member,  agent,
or  agency  or  the  Board  shall  be reduced  to writing
and filed with the Board.  Thereafter,  in its discre-
tion, the Board  upon notice  may  take  further  testi-
mony  or  hear  argument.  If upon  all the testimony
taken  the  Board  shall  be  of  the  opinion  that  any
person  named  in the  complaint  has  engaged  in  or.
is engaging  in any  such  unfair  labor  practice,  then
the  Board  shall  state  its findings  of fact  and  shall
issue  and  cause  to  be  served  on  such  person  an
order  requiring  such  person  to  cease  and  desist
from  such  unfair  labor  practice,  and  to  take  such
affirmative  action,  including  reinstatement  of  em-
ployees  with or  without  back  pay,  as  will effectuate
the  policies  of  this  Act.
Such  order  may  further
require  such  person  to  make  reports  from  time  to
time  showing  the  extent  to  which  it  has  complied
with  the  order.
If  upon  all the  testimony.  taken
the  Board  shall  be  of  the  opinion  that  no  person
_hamed  in  the  complaint  has  engaged  in  or  is  en-
gaging  in any  such  unfair  labor  practice,  then  the
Board  shall  state  its findings  of fact  and  shail  issue
an  order  dismissing  the said complaint.

ja No  person shall be-exeused. from attending
and testifying  or  from  producing  boeks,  records,

obedience  to  the  subpena  of  the  Board,  on  the
ground  that  the  testimony  or  evidence  required  of
him-‘may  tend  to incriminate  him  or subject  him  to  °
a  penalty  or  forfeiture;  but  no  individual  shall  be

that  such  individual  so  testifying  shall-not  be  ex-
empt  from  prosecution  and  punishment  for
perjury  committed  in so  testifying.

.

(4)  Complaints,  orders,  and  other  process  and
papers  of the  Board,  its member,  agent,  or  agency,
may.  be  served  either  personally  or  by registered
mail or by telegraph  or  by leaving  a  copy  thereof  —
at  the  principal  office  or  place  of business  of the
person  required  to be served.  The verified  return
by the individual so serving the same setting forth
the  manner‘ of  such  service  shall  be  proof  of  the
same,  and  the  return  post  office  receipt  or  tele-
graph  receipt  therefor  when  registered  and  mailed
or telegraphed  as  aforesaid  shall  be proof  of serv-

jee of, the same.  Witnesses  summoned  before  the
its member,  agent,  or  agency,  shall be paid
Board,
the same fees and mileage that are paid witnesses
in  the courts  of  the United  States,
and witnesses
whose  depositions  ate  taken and the persons  taking
the same shall severally  be entitked to the same  feee
as  are  paid  for  like  services  in  the  courts  of  the
United  States.

(5)  All  process  of  any  court  to  which  applica-
tion  may  be made  under  this  Act  may~be  served  in
the judicial  district  wherein  the defendant  or  other
person  required  to  be  served  resides  or  may  be
found.

(6)  The several  departments  and  agencies  of the
Government,  when  directed  by the  President,  shall
furnish  the  Board,  upon  its  request,  all  records,
papers,  and  information  in  their  possession  relat-
ing to any  matter  before  the Board.

Src.  12.  Any  pérson  who  shall  willfully  resist,
prevent,  impede,  or  interfere  with  any  member  of
the  Board  or  any  of  its  agents  or  agencies  in  the
performance  of duties  pursuant  to this  Act  shall  be
punished  by a  fine  of  not  more  than  $5,000  or  by
imprisonment  for  not  more  than  one  year,  or  both.

ofthe Wnited States
Inthe Supremedourt

Ocroper  Term,  1936

No.  419

NaTIONAL  LaBoR  RELATIONS  BoaRD,  PETITIONER

v.

Jones  &  LAUGHLIN  STEEL  CORPORATION

ON  WRIT  OF  CERTIORARI  TO  THE  UNITR®  STATES  CIRCUIT
COURT  OF  APPEALS  FOR  THE  FIFTH  CIRCUIT

BRIEF  FOR  NATIONAL  LABOR  RELATIONS  BOARD

OPINION  BELOW

The  opinion  of  the  United  States  Circuit  Court
of  Appeals  for  the  Fifth  Cirenit  (R.  975-977)  is
reported  in  83  F.  (2d)  998.  The  findings  of  fact,
conclusions  of  law,  and  order  of  the  National

Labor  Relations  Board  (R.  23-45)  are  reported  in
1N.  L. R.  B. 503  (1936).

JURISDICTION

The judgment  of the  United  States  Circuit  Court
of  Appeals  for  the  Fifth  Circuit  was  entered  on
June  15,  1936  (R.  977).  A  petition  for  rehearing
(1)

2

was  denied  on  July  15, 1936  (R. 980).  Petition  for
a writ of certiorari  was  filed  on  September  30, 1936,
and  was  granted  on November  9, 1936.  The  juris-
diction  of this  Court  rests  on  Section  240  (a)  of the
Judicial  Code,  as.amended  by the  Act  of February
13, 1925,  and  Section  10  (e)  of  the  National  Labor
Relations  Act.

QUESTIONS  PRESENTED

1. Whether  the  Nationa]  Labor  Relations  Act,  as
applied  to  this  respondent,  is  a  valid  exercise  by
Congress  of its power  to  regulate  commerce  among
‘the  States.

.

2.  Whether  the  provisions  of  Section  8, subdivi-
sions  (1)  and  (3)  of the  National  Labor  Relations
Act,  as  applied  to  this  respondent,  are  consistent
with  the  Fifth  Amendment.

3.  Whether  the  provisions  of the  National  Labor
Relations  Act,  as  here  applied,  deprive  respondent
of  the  right  of  trial  by  jury  guaranteed  in  the
Seventh  Amendment.

|

4.  Whether  the  provisions  of the  Nationa]  Labor
Relations  Act,  as  here  applied,  deprive  respondent
of the  right  of free  speech  guaranteed  by the  First
Amendment.

5.  Whether  respondent  may  question  the validity
of provisions  or  applications  of the  Act  other  than
those  here  invoked  against  it, and  if so,  whether  the
validity  of  such  other  provisions  and  applications
is in  issue  here  because  of their  separability.

3

STATUTE  INVOLVED

The  National  Labor  Relations  Act  (Act  of J'ily
5, 1935,  ec.  372,  49  Stat.  449;  U.  8.  C., Sup.  I, Title
29,  Sec.  151  et  seg.)  is  printed  as  an  Appendix  to
the  brief  for  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board
in  The  Associated  Press  vy.  National  Labor  Rela-
tions  Board,  No.  365,  October  Term,  1936,  and  is
also  summarized  at  pp.  3-8  of that  brief.

STATEMENT

On  January  23,  1936,  a  written  charge  against
the  Jones  &  Laughlin  Steel  Corporation,  the  re-.
spondent,  was  filed  with  the  Regional  Director  for
the  Sixth  Region  of  the  National  Labor  Relations
Board,  pursuant  to  Section  10  (b)  of the  Act  and
Article  II,  Section  2  of  the  Board’s  Rules  and
Regulations  (R.  6-11):  The  charge  alleged  that
the  respondent  had  engaged  in  and  was  continuing
to  engage  in  certain  unfair  labor  practices  affect-
ing  commerce  within  the  meaning  of  Section  8,
subdivisions  (1)  and  (3)  and  Section  2, subdivi-
sions  (6)  and  (7)  of the  Act  (R. 6-7).  The  charge
was  filed  by  the  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200,
Amalgamated  Association  of  Iron,  Steel,  and  Tin’
Workers  of  North  America,  a  labor  organization
(R. 31,  142-143,  Bd.  Exh.  48).

Fhereupon,  pursuant  to  Section  10  (b)  of  the
Act  and  ‘Article  Lf, Section  5 of the  Regulations,
the  Board,  through its  Regional  Director  for  the
Sixth  Region,  issued  a  complaint.and  notice  of
hearing,  which  were  served  on  respondent  (R.

4

12-16).  The  complaint  alleged  that  respondent
had  engaged  in  and  was  engaging  in unfair  labor
practices  set  forth  in  subsevttions  (1)  and  (3)
of  Section  8,.in  that  respondent  was  interfering
with,  coercing  and  restraining  its  employees  im
their  self-organization  and  free  choice  of  repre-
sentatives  for  purposes  of  collective  bargaining  or
other  mutual  aid  or  protection,  and  had  discharged
or  demoted  employees  because  they  had  joined  the
above-named  labor  organization  in  order  to  dis-
The  complaint  al-
courage  membership  therein.
leged  further  that  these  were  unfair  labor  practices
affecting  eommerce  within  the  meaning  of  Section
2, subdivisions  (6)  and  (7)  of the  Act.

On  February  26,  1936,  respondent  filed  a  special
appearance  and  answer,  admitting  the  discharges
and  demotions,  but averring  that  they  were  made
because  of inefficiency  and violation  of respondent's
rules.  Respondent  also  asserted  that  the  Act  was
unconstitutional  upon  several  grounds  and  that  its
activities  were  intrastate  and  not  subject  to  regu-
lation  by @ongress  or  the  Board  (R.  982-987).

The  hearings  on  the  complaint¢were  held  before
the  Board  on  March  2 and  3, and  April  2, 3, 6,  7,
Following  the  introduction  of  evi-
and  8,  1936.
dence  on  the  nature  of  respondent’s  business,  re-
spondent  moved  to dismiss  the  proceeding  for  lack
of jurisdiction  (R.  129).  The  motion  was  denied
(R.  129),  whereupon  respondent  withdrew  from
further  participation  in the  hearings  (R.  130).

5

On  April  9, 1936,  the  Board  handed  down  its de-
cision  setting  forth  its  findings  of fact  (R.  23-43)
and  entered  the  order  here  involved  (R.  44-45).
These  findings  of  fact  may  be  summarized  as
follows:

1.  The  nature  of  respondent’s  business.—Re-
spondent  is  a  Pennsylvania  corporation  which,  to-
gether  with  its  19  subsidiaries,  transports,  mariu-
factures,  sejls  and  distributes  a  diversified  line  of
steel,  pig  iron,  and  other  materials  (R.  23-26;  Bd.
Exh.  4).
It  is  the  fourth  largest  producer  of
steel  in  the  United  States,  employing  ove?  22,000
persons.  About  10,000  persous  are  employed  at  the
respondent’s  plant  at  Aliquippa,  Pennsylvania
(R.  23-24;  88,  109,  Bd.  Exhs.  4, 5).

Like  all  large  units  of  the  steel  industry,  re-
spondent  is  completely  integrated.
It  owns  and
operates  ore,  coal,  and  limestone  properties,  and
transports  the  ore  and  coal  to  its  works  by its  own
lake  steamships,  tugboats  and  barges.
Respond-
ent  owns  and  operates  two  terminal  railroads,  one
at  the  Aliquippa  plant  and  another  at  the  Pitts-
burgh  plant,  over  which  it  receives  and  distributes
materials  and  products.
Each  system  of  tracks  is
connected  directly  with  all  parts  of  the  mills  and
yards  which  it serves,  as  well  as  with  an  important
interstate  trunk  line.
Cars  are  loaded  and  un-
loaded  by respondent’s  employees  with  the  help  of
respondent’s  cranes  and  loading  equipment.
Re-
spondent  alone  makes  97 per  cent  of the  shipments
from  Aliquippa  over  the  Pittsburgh  &  Lake  Erie

6

Railroad,  a  part  of  the
(R. 24-25 ; 99-102,  133, Bd.  Exhs.  4, 5).

New  York  Central  System

Many  of respondent’s  employees  are  engaged  in
the  distribution  of its  products  in  interstate  com-
merce.  Much  of the  steel  and _piy  iron  is shipped
from  Aliquippa  to respondent’s  warehouses  in New

and  Memphis,  the  shipments  to  the  last  two  cities
named  being  by  respondent’s  own  barges  and
transportation  equipment.
In  Long  Island  City,
New  York,  and  New  Orleans,  Louisiana,  respond-
ent  operates  structural  steel  fabricating  shops  in
connection  with  the  warehousing  of  semifinished
materials  sent  there  from  Pittsburgh  and  Ali-
Sales  offices  are  maintained  in  20  cities
quippa.
of  the  United  States,  and  there  is  a  wholly-
owned  subsidiary  devoted  exclusively  to the  distri-
Enor-
bution  of respondent’s  product  in  Canada.
mous  movements  and  transactions  in  interstate

In all, 80 per cent  by value  of the  material  received
