of Pennsylvania,  and  75 per  cent  of the  product  of
that  plant  is sold  and  shipped  in  interstate  com-
(R.  25-27;  88,  112,  132,  Bd.  Exhs.  4,  7.)
merce.
.  Most  of  the  production  at  the  Aliquippa  plant  is

according  to  special  order  (R.  30;  86-87).
elaboration  of  the  facts  as  to  the  character  and

2.  The  Unfair  Labor  Practices. __Beaver Valley
Lodge  No.  200  of the Amalgamated  Association  of
Iron,  Steel,  and  Tin  Workers  was  chartered  on
August 4, 1934  (R. 31-32;  73, Bd.  Exh.  48).  There-
after  the  union  sought  to  organize  the  men  in the
Aliquippa  Works.
Respondent  countered  by
systematic  coercive  efforts  to prevent  such  organi-  |
zation.
Officers  and  organizers  of the  union  were
followed  about  by respondent’s  police;  some  of the
more  important  union  officers  were  permanently
shadowed  and  followed  even  into  neighboring
towns;  a  house  in  which  a union  meeting  had  been
held  was  surrounded  by respondent’s  private  po-
lice,  and  respondent’s  employment  agent  sat  near
its  door  noting  who  entered;  some  persons  coming
out  of  the  house  were  questioned  and  even  beaten
(R. 31-32,  34-36;  135-136,  144-145,  167-169,  179,
254-256,  303,  334-335,  339).

Respondent  also  resisted  organization  by  vilifi-
cation  of the  union  and  its  leaders  (R. 36-40;  180-
181,  208,  224,  250,  337).  Employees  were  told  that
respondent  would  not  ‘‘stand  for’’  the  union,  and
that  employees  should  leave  it  alone;  that  they
should  not  attend  the  meetings  or  join  (R.  35-40;
160,  179,  293,  303).  One. employee. was told  that  if
he  continued  his  union  affiliation  the  company
could  not  tolerate  his  failure  to pay  back  rents  and
would  evict  him,  and  that  the  local  merchants
would  not  extend  credit  to him  (R. 40; 208-209).

In  June  1933  respondent  installed  at  its  Ali-
quippa  plant  a  so-called  Plan  of Employees’  Rep-

|

resentation,  providing  for  election  of  officers,
meetings,  and settlement  of grievances.  The  adop-
tion  of  the  plan  was  not  put  up  to  respondent’s
employees;  they.  were  invited  simply  to  elect  em-
ployee  representatives.

(R. 33;  160-162. )

The  12 employees  concerned  in  this  case  were
dismissed  during the period  from  July  1935  to Jan-
uary  1936,  The  Board  found  that  the  discharges
of 10 of these  men.were  the  result  of union  activity
and  were  intended  by  respoudent  to  discourage
All  of  them
membership  in  the  union  (R.  41).'
were  active  leaders  in  the  union;  several  were  offi-
cers,  and  others  were  leaders  of  particular  groups
(R. 42).  In most  cases  slight reasons  were  assigned
for  the  discharges—reasons  which  respondent  nor-
mally  had  not  considered  sufficient  for such  action.
Each  discharge  followed  hurriedly  upon  discovery
of some  possible  pretext  (R.  42).°  The  employees
discharged  were  engaged  in  various  duties  about
the  plant,  such  as  operating  cranes  or  nail  ma-
chines,  inspecting  motors,  driving  tractors,  and  the
like  (R. 33-42).

‘The  complaint  was  dismissed  as  to  two  of  the  men,  no
(R.

evidence  concerning  them  having  been  presented
42, 45).
° ae references  to  the  most  pertinent  evidence  on  the
anfair  labor  practices  with  respect  to  each  employee  are  as
follows:  Gerstner, R. 148-144,  150-153;  Phillips,  R. 166-168,
170-171,  173-174,  154-155;  Razzano,  R.  177-183,  190;  Volpe,
R.  826-333,  323-324;  Boyer,  R.  284,  285-292,  295;  Bozich,
R. 275, 278-280;  Dunn,  R. 226-282;  Maroll,  R.  302,  307-811;
Brandy, R. 247, 249-250,  258-262,  266;  Cox,  R. 208-208,  212.

9

Upon  the  basis  of these  findings,  the  Board  con-
cluded  that  respondent  discharged  the  ten  em-
ployees  because  of their  union  activity  and thereby
interfered  with  the  rights  of  its  employees  guar-  .
anteed  by Section  7 of the  Act ; discriminated  in re-
gard  to hire  and  tenure  of employment  cf said  ten
employees;  and  discouraged  membership  in  the
union ; all  of  which  constituted  unfair  labor  prac-
tices  within  the  meaning  of  subdivisions  (1)  and
(3)  of  Section  8  of  the  Act  (R.  43).  By  reason
of  the  nature  of  respondent’s  operations  and  the
nature  of  labor  disputes  in  the  industry,  the
Board  also  concluded  that  respondent’s  activities
were  unfair  labor  practices  affecting  commerce
withinmthe  meaning  of  subdivisions  (6)  and  (7)
of  Section  2 of the  Act,  in  that  they  tended  to  lead
to  labor  disputes  burdening  and  obstructing  com-
merce  and  the  free  flow  thereof  (R.  44).

The  Board  therefore  issued  an order  (R. 44-45)
requiring  respondent  to  cedse  and  desist  from
interfering  with,  restraining  or  coercing  its  em-
ployees  in the  exercise  of their  rights  to self-organ-
ization  and  otherwise,  as  guaranteed  in  Section  7
of  the  Act,  and  from  discouraging  membership:  in
the  union  or  any other  labor  organization  by dis-
crimination  in  regard  to  hire  or  tenure  of employ-
ment  or  any  term  or  condition  of employment;  to
reinstate  with  back  pay  the  10  men  discharged  in
the  commission  of  the  unfair  labor  practices;  and
to post appropriate  notices  (R. 44-45).

10

On  April  11,  1936,  the  Board  filed  in  the  court
below  its  petition  for  the  enforcement  of  its  order
(R. 972-974),  and  on  May  9, 1936,  filed  an  amend-
ment  thereto  (R. 45a-45b).  On June  15, 1936, the
court  entered  its  decree  denying  the  petition
(R. 977).  A petition  for  rehearing  was  denied  on
July. 15, 1986  (R. 980).

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

The  National  Labor  Relations  Act  is  an  exer-
cise  of the  power  of Congress  to  protect  interstate
-  commerce  from  injuries  caused  by industrial  strife.
Before  this  statute  was  enacted  experience  had
shown  that  industrial  strife  was  a recurrent  burden
upon  the  interstate  commerce  of  the nation.  Not
only  in  its totality  had  such  strife  produced  ob-
structions  to  commerce,  but  also  in  many  indi-
vidual  instances  such  strife  eventuated  in  con-
spiracies  to  restrain  commerce  or  imposed  such
substantial  burdens  upon  it  that  penalties  or  in-
junctions  were  applied  under  the  Sherman  Act.
These  facts  are  clearly  shown  by a survey  of  the
results  of  individual  disputes,  the  statistics  with
regard  to  the  total  number  of  such  disputes,  and
repeated  Federal  activities  in  connection  with
industrial  strife.

Congress,  in dealing  with this evil of industrial
strife, might have approached the problem in either
of two  ways.  It might have  enacted  a  statute  de-
signed  to  remove the  burden  on  interstate  com-
merce after it had evidenced  itself in a particular

a statute  to deal with the causes of the bur-  _

11
case,  as  the  Sherman  Act did; or,  it might have
enacted
den  in  anticipation  of their  probable  effect,  as  the
Packers  and  Stockyards  Act  did.  Congressional
power  to enact  this second  type  of statute  (herein-
after  called  the  preventive  power)  extends  at least
as  far as  the power  to enact  a statute  to control  the
burden  after  its appearance  (hereinafter  called  the
control  power).
Stafford  v.  Wallace,  258  U.  8.
495, 525.

|

The  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  which  is an
exercise  of this  pretentive  power,  does  not  attempt
to  eliminate  causes  of  strife  in  all  enterprises.
_The  statute  and  the  mandate  addressed  to  the
administrative  Board  are  specifically  directed  to
the  elimination  of  the  proscribed  practices  only
when  they  are  found  to  be  ‘“‘affecting  commerce”’
(See.  10  (a)).
This  phrase  “affecting  com-
merce’’  is  defined  in  Section  2  (7)  of  the  statute
as  “‘in  commerce,  or  burdening  or obstructing  com-
merce  or the free flow of commerce,  or having led or
tending  to lead  to a labor  dispute  burdening  or  ob-
_  Structing  commerce  or  the free  flow  of commerce.”’
These  words  are  plainly  patterned  upon  language
used  in  decisions  of this  Court in cases  arising
under  other  statutes  enacted  by  Congress  under
the  commerce  power.
This  jurisdictional  limita-
tion,  viewed  in  the light  of the legislative  history
elsewhere  set  forth  (see  Brief  for  the  Board  in
No.  365,  The  Associated  Press  v.  National  Labor
Relations  Board,  p. 114),  is manifestly  a direction

12

‘

\

to the  Board  to exercise  the  national  power  within
the limits  permitted  by the  Constitution.
In other
words,  Congress  has taken as  the ambit  of this  par-
ticular  preventive  statute  the  boundaries  to  which
it would  be  restricted  were  it exercising  to  the  full
its  control  power  under  appropriate  legislation.

Of  course,  in elimiggting  these  proscribed  prac-
tices  in  situations  which  are  likely  to  come  within
the  control  power  of Congress,  the  application  of
the  Aet  will  upon  occasion  result  in  the  prohibition
of  activities  which,  even  if  allowed  to  spend  their
force,  would  not  result  in  industrial  strife  which
did  in  fact  come  within  the  Federal  control  power.
But,  provided  that  when  the  practice  was  indulged
in  there  was  a  reasonable  likelihood  that  any  in-
dustrial  strife  which  resulted  would  be  within  the
control  power,  the  Board  may apply  the  preventive
measure.
To  hold  otherwise  would  deprive  Con-
gress  of  any  preventive  power,  since  no  one  can
predict  with  absolute  accuracy,  in  advance  of  the
complete  development  of the-effects  of a cause,  ex-
aetly  what  those  effects  will  be.  Compare  Stafford
v.  Wallace,  258 U.  8. 495;  Chicago  Board  of Trade
v.  Olsen,  262 U.S.  1.  This  lack  of predictability  is
particularly  true  of industrial  strife.  Moreover,  in
eliminating  the’ cause  of  an  evil  within  the  power
of  Congress,  the Act may on  occasion  eliminate  the
cause  of purely  local evils.  Indeed,
this is almost
inevitable,  since  a single  event  has  multiple  conse-
quences.  Yet  plainly  the existence of a granted
Federal  power cannot be limited  by the collateral

13
consequences  of  its  exercise.  Ashwander  v.  Ten-
nessee  Valley  Authority,  297  U. S. 288.

The  foregoing  epitome  of  the  statutory  scheme
makes  it clear  that  the question  of the  validity  of a
specific  application  of  the  statute  depends  upon
whether  industrial  strife  restfiting  from  the  prac-
tices  i»  the  particular  enterprise  under  considera-
tion  would  be  of  the  character  which  the  Federal
power  could  control  if  it  oceurred.  The  question
of  primary  #nportance  therefore  is  the  extent  of
the  Federal  power  of control.  Since  the  language
of this  statute  does  not  go beyond  the  ambit  of this
power,  whatever  it  may  be,  the  statute  is  clearly
constitutional  on  its  faee,  and  the  only  issue  is
whether  it has  been  constitutionally  applied.

This  Court  has  never  had  oceasion  to  define  the
full  extent  to  which  industrial  strife  in  the  manu-
facturing,  producing,  or  processing  divisions  of an.
enterprise  engaged  extensively  in  interstate  com-
merce  may  be subjected  to  the  exercise  of the  con-
trol  power  of Congress.  It has,  however,  held  that
certain  situations  in  these  divisions  are  clearly
within  the control  power,  and has further  laid  down
general  principles  which  furnish  standards  for  the
proper  appreciation  of the full  scope  of that-power.
It  is  well  settled  that  an  industrial  dispute  in
such  an  enterprise  involving  an  intent  to  affect
commerce  is within  the control  power  of Congress,
United  Mine  Workers  v.  Coronado  Coal  Co.,  259
U. S. 344; Bedford  Cut Stone  Co.  v. Stone  Cutters’
Ass'n,  274  U.  8. 37; Duplex  Printing  Co.  v.  Deer-

118606—37 2

14
ing, 254 U.  8. 443;  Loewe  Vv.  Lawlor,  208  U.  8. 274.
Consequently,  where  the  situation  in  a particular
enterprise  presents  a  reasonable  likelihood  that
industrial  strife,  if  it  occurred,  would  involve  an
intent  to affect  commerce,  the  Board  can  apply  the
statute  to that  enterprise.
This  Court  has also  recognized  the  principle  that
an  industrial  dispute  having  the  necessary  effect  of
substantially  burdening  commerce  would  be  within
Industrial  Asso-
the  control  power  of  Congress.
ciation  v.  United  States,  268  U.  S.  64,  81;  United
Mine  Workers  v.  Coronado  Coal  Co.,  259  U.  S.
Hence,  the  statute  1s  applicable  to
344,  410-411.
an  enterprise  which  is shown  by evidence  before  the
Board  to be  of  such  a character  that  strife,  if  it
occurred,  would  have  such a necessary  effect.  The
proper  application  of the  statute  to such  situations
-  depends,  of  course,  on  the  precise  scope  of  the
‘‘necessary  effect”’  principle.  Although  the  Court
has  recognized  the  principle  it has  not  heretofore
had  occasion  to define  it.  At  a  later  place  we  pre-
sent  our  views  on  the  definition  and  scope  of this
principle  on  which  the  scope  of the  jurisdiction  of
the Board  may  in a particular  case  depend.
Lastly,  the  scope  of  the  control  power  extends
“to  recurring  evils  which  in  their  totality  con-
This
stitute  a  burden  on  interstate  commerce.
Court  has  stated  that  such  recurrent  evils  may,
after  appropriate  findings,  be  subjected  by  Con-
gress  to national  supervision  and  restraint.  Staf-  -
ford  v.  Wallace,  258  U.  8.  495;  Chicago  Board

15

of Trade  v.  Olsen,  262  U.8.1.  This  principle  ex-.  .
tends  to  industrial  strife  in  enterprises  which  re-  .
ceive  a substantial  part of their  raw  rftaterial  from,
or  ships  a  substantial.  part  of  their  products
United  Mine  Workers
in,  interstate  commerce.
v.  Coronado  Coal  Co.,  259  U. 8. 344,  408;  Untted
Leather  Workers  v.  Herkert  & Metsel  Trunk  Co.,
265  U.  8.  457,  469;  Tezas  &  New  Orleans  R. Co.
v.  Brotherhood  of Railway  Clerks,  281  U.  8.  548.
Here  Congress  has  found,  and  experience  shows,
that  industrial  strife  in such  enterprises  constitutes
such  a  recurring  evil.  Under  this  view  of  the
control  power  the  statute  may  be  applied  to  the
individual  instances  of  this  recurring  burden—
that  is,  to  any  enterprise  which.  receives  a  sub-
stantial  part  of  its  materials  from,  or  ships  a
substantial  part  of  its  products  in,  interstate
"commerce,  and  is  dependent  upon  such  commerce.

for  the  successful  conduct  of  its  business.

We  believe  that  the  instant  case  falls  within  each
of these  three  situations  in which  the  control  power
of Congress  may  be exercised.

|

The reasonable  likelihood  of a controversy  with the
intent  to  affect  commerce  might  be  demonstrated
in  either  of  two  ways:  First,  evidence  might  be
presented  to  the  Board  that  in  a  particular  situa-
tion  the  intent  already  exists;  or  second,  evidence
might  be presented  to  the  Board  that  the  situation
was  comparable  to, and of the  same  general  type as,
others  out  of  which in the  past  there  had  evolved
controversies  with  intent  to  affect  commerce,  or

16
that  in  the  particular  situation  confronting  the
Board,  such  definite  intention  might  reasonably  be
expected  to  develop.  In  the  case  at  bar  it  is  not
claimed  that  there  is  evidence  of  the  first  sort;
but  there  is abundant  evidence  that  the  situation  is
fraught  with  the  risk  that. after  strife  developed  it
would  involve  the  purpose  to  curtail  the  movement
(See  particularly  pp.  40-
of  goods  in  commerce.
42, infra.)

As  previously  stated,  the  Court  has  not  yet  de-
termined  the  exact  scope  of the  phrase  ‘necessary
It
effect  of  substantially  burdening  commerce.”’
undeniably  includes  situations  in which  the  partici-
pants  cannot  be  charged  with  a  conscious  specrfic
United  States  v.
desire  to  interrupt  commerce.
Patten,  226  U.  S.  525.  The  statements  of  this
Court  make  it  clear  that  the  application  of  this
principle  may  depend  upon  the  magnitude  of  the
The  possible  use  of  such  a
effect  on  commerce.
standard  is entirely  consistent  with Carter’.  Carter
Coal  Co.,  298 U. 8. 238.  Three  possible  definitions
of the scope  of the “necessary  effect”  principle  may
be advanced : (1) a necessary  effect  on commerce  re-
sults  from  industrial  strife  occurring  in  an  enter-
prise which  lies within  a well-defined  stream  or  flow
of  commerce,  or  (2)  a  necessary  effect  on  com-
merce  results  from  industrial  strife  whieh  restrains
a  substantial  part  of  all  the  commerce  in  a  par-
ticular  commodity,  or  (3)  a  necessary  effect  on
commerce  results  from  industrial  strife  which  re-
strains  the  movement  of  a  substantial  volume  of

17

goods  which  would  otherwise  move  in  interstate
The  instant  case  falls  within  all  three
commerce.
of  these  aspects  of the  principle,  but  this  brief  ad-
dresses  particular  attestion  to the  first  and  second.
Lastly,  the  control  power  of Congress  extends  te
individual  instances  of  constantly  recurring  evils
which,  in  their  total  effect,  constitute  a  burden  on
interstate  commerce.
Such  a  burden  is  imposed
by  recurrent  industrial  strife  in  enterprises  which
receive  a  substantial  part  of  their  materials  from,
or  ship  a  substantial  part  of  their  products  in  in-
terstate  commerce.
The  statute,  therefore,  was
properly  applied  in  this  case,  for  the  facts  clearly
show  that  respondent’s  enterprise  is  largely  de-
pendent  upon  interstate  commerce  both  for  its  ma-
terials  and  for  the  marketing  of  its  products.

Neither  Schechter  Corp.  v.  United  States,  295
U.S.  495,  nor  Carter  v.  Carter  Coal  Co.,  298  U.S.
In  the  Schechter  case  it
238,  is  here  applicable.
was  urged  that  wages  and  hours  in  local  industry
bore  a  relation  to  interstate  commerce  by reason  of
*  an  intricate  chain  of  economic  causes  and  effects.
An  effect  on  commerce  which  occurred  in  such  a
manner  the  Court  characterized  as  indirect.  The
Act  involved  in  the  Carter  case  had  as  its  purpose
the  ‘‘stabilizing’’  of  the  bituminous  coal  industry.
through  regulation  of prices and  wages.
The effect
of wage  cutting  on  interstate  commerce  was  held  to
be indirect.on  the  basis  of the  Schechter  case.
The
collective  bargaining  provisions  of  that  Act  were,

18
as is clear  from  the face  of the statute  and  from  the
opinion  of the  Court,  ancillary  to  the  wage  fixing
provisions  and  furnished  the  means  through  which
regulations  with  respect  to wages  having  the  force
of law  were  to  be  arrivéd  at.  On  the  other  hand,
the National  Labor  Relations  Act  is designed  solely
to  eliminate  the  burden  on  interstate  commerce
caused  by industrial  strife.  Such  strife  constitutes
an  interruption  to  commerce  operating  directly
without  ‘‘an  efficient  intervening  agency  or  condi-
tion.”  Thus  it  deals  with  matters  closely  con-
nected  with  commerce,  does  not  go  beyond  what is
necessary  for  the protection  of commerce,  and  does
not attempt  ‘‘a  broad  regulation  of industry  within
the  State.’’

ARGUMENT

In the brief  filed  on  behalf  of the  National  Labor
Relations  Board  in  No.  365,  The  Associated  Press
vy.  National  Labor  Relations  Board,  we  have  shown
that  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act  is  a  valid
exeréise  of the  commerce  power  of  Congress.  We
have  also  shown,  in  that  brief,  that  it may  consti-
tutionally  be  applied  to  that  respondent,  and  that
as  there, and here,  applied,  it does  not  conflict  with
the Fifth, Seventh, or First Amendments.  Finally,
we  pointed  out  in that’ brief that  the  provisions  of
the  Act  there,  and  here,  before  the  Court  are  sep-
arable from other provisions  (particularly  Sections
8  (5)  and  9)  not  there,  or  here,  involved).  We
respectfully  refer  the  Court,  therefore,  to  that
brief  for  the  discussion  of those  questions.  In  the

19

present  brief  we  shall  consider  only  the  question
whether  the  Act  may  validly  be applied  to this  re-
spondent,  an  integrated  enterprise  engaged  in  the
manufacture  of steel  and  steel  products,  and  their
sale  in interstate  and  foreign  commerce.’

THE  NaTIONAL  LABOR  RELATIONS  ACT,  AS  ERE  APr-
PLIED  TO  THIS  RESPONDENT,  Is a  VaLip  EXERCISE
OF  THE  PowER  OF  CoNGRESS  OVER  INTEDJTATE
COMMERCE

The  National  Labor  Relations  Act  constitutes  an
exercise  of the  power  of  Congress  to protect  inter-
state  commerce  from  the burden  and  injury  caused
by industrial  strife.  As this  Court  stated  in Tezas
& New  Orleans  R.  Co. v.  Brotherhood  of Ratlway
Clerks,  281  U.S.  548,  570:

The  power  to  regulate  commerce  is  the
power  to  enact  ‘‘all  appropriate  legisla-
tion’’  for  its  ‘protection  and  advancement”’
(The  Daniel  Ball,  10  Wall.  557,  564);  to
adopt  measures  ‘‘to  promote  its  growth  and
insure  its  safety’’  (County  of  Mobile  v.

*On  the  merits  of  this  case,  the  Board  ‘nade  deeafbea

findings  (R. 33-42)  with  respect  to  the unfair  labor  practices
engaged  in  by  respondent  (pp.  7-8,  spra),  and  concluded
that  the  respondent  had  discharged  10  employees  under
circumstances  which  constituted  violations  of  Section.  8.
subsections  (1)  and  (3)  of the  Act.  Respondent  offered  no
evidence  on  this  question  at  the  hearing  before  the  Board.
The  Act  provides  that  the  Board’s  findings  as  to the facts,
if  supported  by  evidence,  shall  be  conclusive  (Section  10
(e)).  Here  the  record  clearly  supports  the  findings  and
consequently  respondent  is  not  in  position  to  overthrow
them.  Florida  v.  United  States,  202  U.S.  1,  12;  Federal
Trade  Commission  v.  Algoma  Lumber  Co., 291  U.  S.  67, 73.

20

Kimball,  102  U.  S.  691,  696,  697)  ; to  **  fos-
ter,  protect,  control  and  restrain’’  (Second
Employers’  Liability  Cases,  223  U.S.  1, 47).
For  many  years  before  this  statute  was  enacted
there  had been  a  recognition  of the  evil  of constant
industrial  strife  and  of  the  magnitude  of  its  ef-
fect  on  the  commerce  of  the  nation.  During  cer-
tain  periods  of  readjustment  the  evil  became  more
pronounced,  the  controversies  more  numerous  and
more  apt  to  spread,  through  sympathetic  action,  to
other  enterprises  in  the  industry  or  to  other  in-
dustries.

Moreover,  not  only  did  the  total  burden  of  a
multitude  of  strikes  bear  heavily  upon  interstate
commerce,  but  also,  in  many  instances,  industrial
strife  eventuated  in  conspiracies  to  affect  com-
merce,  or  strikes  which  otherwise  so  substantially
burdened  it that  in order  to protect  commerce  puni-
tive  sanetions  authorized  by  the  Sherman  Antl-
Trust  Act  were  applied.

The  statute  here  involved  cannot  properly  be
considered  apart  from  this  background  of  indus-
trial  disputes  and  of previous  attempts  by the  Fed-  .
eral  government  to  meet  this  problem.‘  For  that
reason,  we set  forth  at  the  outset  a  brief  statement
Following  that,  at  pp.  31-86,
of this  background.
‘The  non-legal  materials  in  the  present  brief  were  col-
lected  by  the  Division  of  Economic  Research,  National
Labor Relations  Board.  David  J. Saposs, Chief  Economist  ;
George  S.  Wheeler,  Associate  Economist;  George  Brooks,
Assistant  Economist;  Jean  M. Paton,  Jacob  Karro,  Louis  R.
Becker,  Elizabeth  T.  Bliss,  Junior  Economists.

21

infra,  we  will  explain  the nature  of the statute,  and
the  constitutional  basis  for  the  application  of the
provisions  of Section  8, subsections  (1)  and  (3)  té
this  respondent.

THE  BURDEN  AND  INJURY  TO  INTERSTATE  COMMERCE
RESULTING  FROM  INDUSTRIAL  STRIFE

_  The  paralyzing  effect  on  interstate  commerce  of
industrial  disputes  in enterprises  similar  to that  of
respondent  is a  matter  of  common  knowledge.  A.
strike  or  lockout  ordinarily  means  the  complete
cessation  of business.  No materials  flow  in;  no fin-
ished  products  flow  out.
Effects  are  immediately
felt  in other  enterprises  dependent  upon  the  plant
where  the  dispute  oceurs  for  their  materials  or
semi-finished  parts.  Markets  are  disrupted;  no
business  commitments  can  be made.

By  way  of  illustration  a  brief  sketch  may  be
given  of the  steel  strike  of 1919-1920.  That  strike  -
started  in  September  1919  and  lasted  until  Jan-
uary  1920.
For  many  weeks,  more  than  350,000
men  were  out.’  Plants  in  Pennsylvania,  Dlinois,
Ohio,  West  Virginia,  New  York,  Colorado,  and
other  States  suspended  operations.’

*U.  S.  Senate,  Hearings  before  Committee  on  Education
and  Labor,  /nvestigation  of Strike  in  Steel  Industries  (66th
Cong.,  ist  Sess.) , pp.  370-371,  411.
'
* For  general  accounts  of  the  strike  see  R.  616-620  (testi-
mony  of John  A. Fitch)  and  R. 927-932  (testimony  of David
J.  Saposs).
See  also  Monthly  Labor  Review  (December
1919), pp.  79-94, 369; Senate  Report  No. 289, 66th  Cong.,  ist
Sess.,  Investigating  Strikes  in  Steel  Industries.

22
The  pages  of  Iron  Age,  the  standard  business
publication  of  the  industry  (R.  928),  reveal  the
effect  of the dispute  upon  the  iron  and  steel  mill
In  the  issue  of  Sep-
operations  and  the  market.
tember  22,  1919,  published  three  days  after  the
strike  began,  it  was  reported  that  the  ‘‘veneral
effect”’  of the strike  ‘“was  to paralyze  the  iron  and
éteel  market.  Not  knowing  just  what  they  had  to
face,  some  mills  directed their representatives  to
cease taking orders,  and under  no  circumstances  to
promise  definite  deliveries  against  contraets  or
Cleveland  reported  a
specifications’  (R.  929).
cessation  of  orders  for  pig  iron  (R.  929-930).
Likewise  in  New  York,  business  in pig iron  was
reported  to  have  ‘“‘taken  a  decided  slump’’  (R.
930).  Few  offers  for  finished  iron  and  steel  ‘‘are
being  given  serious  consideration  by selling  agen-
cies.  What  business  has been  taken  is largely  by
mills  which  do not seem  to be seriously  threatened
by strike curtailment  of operations’’  (R. 930).  The
same  issue  of Iron  Age  reported  that  sympathetic
strikes of seamen,  cooks,  and firemen,  which  would
tie up lake iron  boats,  were  threatened  (R. 929).

Iron  Age  for  October  2 reveals  even  more
drastic  dislocations  of commerce.  Pittsburgh  re-
ported  that  ‘‘everything  in  the  steel  trade  is sub-
ordinate  to  the  strike,  and  neither  producers  nor
consumers  are  making  any  real  effort  to  do  busi-
ness’?  (R. 930).  Business  in  ferro  alloys,  struc-
tural  materials,  wire  products  and  scrap  were
_—  erippled  in that  city  (R. 930-931)  and  in Chicago

(R. 931-932).  The effect of the strike  was already
being  felt  in  the mining  and  shipment  of iron  ore
(R. 982).

industrial

to  industry-wide

The  strike  of  1919  is  not,  however,  an  isolated
example  of a  burden  on  interstate  commerce.  In
the  steel  industry  itself  major  upheavals  simi-
lar  to that  of 1919  have  taken  place at periodic  in-
tervals—1892,  1901,  and  1909-1910  (R.  611-624).
An industry-wide  strike in 1934 was averted  only by.
Federal]  intervention  (R.  623-624).  Smaller  dis-
putes  that  are  constantly  recurring  (R.  948-949)
must  also be taken  into account.  The present  criti-
cal situation  is a matter  of common  knowledge.
'  Nor  is  this  burden  upon  interstate  commerce
restricted
strife.
Without  detailed  description,  it  may  be  pointed
out  that  similar  disorganization,  obstruction,  or
even  paralysis  of  interstate  commerce  may  re--
sult  from  industrial  disputes  limited  to  par-
ticular  areas  of  an  industry,  as  in  the  case  of
the  Pacifie  Coast  lumber  strike  of  1934°  (R.
944-946,  Bd.  Exh.  99.  Monthly  Labor  Review
(September  1935)  pp.  656-659),  or  from  an  indus-
trial  dispute  in  a  particular  concern,  such  as  the
Chevrolet  Motor  Company  strike  of 1935  (R. 937-
943.  Monthly  Labor  Review  (July  1935)  pp.  83-
84).  Similar  burdens  upon  interstate  and  foreign
commerce  were  continually  disclosed  in  the  hear-
ings  before  the National  Labor  Relations  Board
in the administration  of the Act.  See First Annual

Cd

>

24
Report,  National  Labor  Relations  Board  (1936)
pp.  137  et-seq.

The  picture  of  disruption  of  commerce  caused  .

by industrial  strife  could  be enlarged  indefinitely,
and details  furnished  by studies  made  of the effects
of particular  strikes.  We  do  not  believe  it neces-
sary,  however,  to  expand  this  material  in order  to
illustrate  a  point  which  no  one  will  deny.  Like-
wise,  it  is  unnecessary  to  give  more  than  a
summary  statement  to  show  the  recurrent  and
continuous  nature  of the burden.

Industrial  strife  is  of  almost  daily  occurrence.
The  table  in  the  Appendix  (infra,  p. 95),  which
gives the number  of industrial  disputes during  each
year  from  1916  through  June  1936,  reveals  the
In  no  year  has  the  num-
enormous  total  burden.
ber  of disputes  fallen  below  650—about  two every
day—and  in  some  years  the  number  has  risen  to
over  3,500—ten  every  day.  On  the basis  of similar
statistics  the  Senate  Committee  on  Education  and
Labor,  in reporting  the  bill  which  became  the  pres-
ent  Act,  said  (Sen.  Rep.  No.  573,  74th  Cong.,  1st
Sess.,  pp.  1-2):

|

During  the  period  from  *915  through  1921
there  were  on  the  average  3,043  strikes  per
year,  involving  the  vacating  of  1,745,000
jobs and  the loss  of 50,242,000  working  days
every  12 months.  From  1922  through  1926
the  annual  average  totaled  1,050  strikes,
775,000  strikers,  and  17,050,000  working-
_  days  lost.  From  1927  through  1931  the
yearly  average  for disputes  was  763, for em-

25

-  ployees  leaving  their  work  275,000,  and for
days  lost  5,665,000.
In  1933  over  812,137
workers  were  drawn  into  strikes,  and  in
1934  the  number  rose  to  1,277,344.  In  this
2-year  period  over  32,000,000  working  days
were  lost  because  of  labor  controversies.
The  table  in the  Appendix  also reveals  that  dur-
ing  certain  periods  industrial  strife  becomes  par-
ticularly  acute.  Thus  in  the  four  years  from  1919
to  1922  there  occurred  more  than  10,000  labor  dis-
putes  involving  more  than  8,000,000  employees.
(See  also  R. 920-921).  Among  those  which  seri-
ously  burdened  interstate  commerce  may  be  noted
the  strikes  of  harbor  workers  in  1919;’  the strike
of  250,000  railway  trainmen  in  Augnst  1919  (R.
926) ; the  general  steel  strike  of  1919-1920  (supra
pp.  21-23);  the  strike  of  longshoremen  in  New
York  in  October  1919;*  the  strike  of  450,000
bituminous  coal  miners  in  November  1919,’  the
marine  workers  strike  in  May  1921:(R.  924-925)  ;
the  general  strike  in  the  ladies’  garment  ‘industry
in  November  1921;"  the  strike  of  meat  packing
employees  in  December  1921;"'  the  New  England
textile  strike  which  began  in  January  1922;  the
widespread  strikes  in  the  bituminous and  anthra-

|

A  similar  series  of  major  industrial  conflicts
with  equally  burdensome  effects  on  interstate  com-
merce  occurred  in the years  }933  to 1935.  Among
the strikes  of this period  which  seriously  burdened
commerce  may  be  noted  the  maritime  workers’
strike  on  the Pacific  Coast  which  began in May 1934
and  led  to  the  general  strike  in  San  Francisco
(R. 946-948)  ; the  Minneapolis  teamstera’  strike  in
May  1934;"*  the  national  textile  strike  in  Septem-
ber  1934  (R. 943-944)  ; the  strike  of enamel  work-
-ers  in Terre  Haute,  Indiana,  in March  1935,  which
led  to  a  general  strike  in  that  city  (R.  943-944) ;
the  strike  in the  Chevrolet  Motor  Company  at  To-
ledo,  Ohio,  in April  1935  (supra,  p. 23) ; the  Pacific
Coast  lumber  strike  in  May  1935  (supra,  p.  23) ;
and  the  strike  of shipbuilding  workers  in Camden,
New  Jersey,  in May  and  August  1939."  In  addi-
tion, major  disputes  threatening  to tie up  the  steel,
automobile,  and  lumber  industries  during  1934  and
1935  were  prevented  only  by Federal  intervention
(R. 949).

As  we  have  pointed  out  above,  industrial  strife
is a burden  on  commerce  not only in its totality,  but

14  Jd,  (December  1922),  p.  1-21.

i

also because  frequently  individual  instances  of such
strife  eventuate  into-conspiracies  to  affect  com-
merce,  or  into  burdens  so  great  in their  effect  upon
commerce  as  to be within  the  power  of Congress  to
control  under  fhe  Sherman  Act  or  other  appropri-
ate  legislation.  That  industrial  strife  often  leads
to  these  intentional  restraints  on  interstate  com-
merce  is  illustrated  by  the  situations  which  have
been  before  the  Federal  courts.’
Moreover,  in-

295;  Bedford  Cut  Stone  Co.  v.  Stone  Cutters’  Associa-

ported  cases  in the  lower  federal  courts  in which  injunctions

United  States  v.  E. J. Ray  (p. 101) ; United States  v.  Artery

The  cases  enumerated  constitute  only  a  partial  list  of  in-
junctions  which  have  been  issued  in  situations  involving
strikes, boycotts  and other industrial  strife.  In both the
texts  dealing‘ with  the  labor  injunctions  and  the  hearings  of

28

dustrial  disturbances  with  such  potentialities  are
apparently  increasing  in number  and  intensity,  due
both to the increasing  size and national  character.of
modern  business  units  and to the  increasing  growth
of labor  organization  on  a national  scale.  Such  in-
stances  have  arisen  in the cases  before  the  National
Labor  Relations  Board.  First  Annual  Report,  Na-
tional  Labor  Relations  Board  (1936)  p.  141.

The  magnitude  of this  burden  on  interstate  com-
merce  has  for  many  years  prompted  Federal  ac-
tion  by both  the  legislative  and  executive  branches
of the  government  in  efforts  to  bring  about  indus-

On  numerous  occasions  the  President  has  made
personal  efforts  to  bring  about  an  adjustment  (R.
440-441,  451-453,  460, 466-469,  623-624,  949-950).”°

15,900  industrial  disputes  involving  16,000,000  em-
ployees  (R.  434).”

Nor  has  Federal  intervention  in »  the  seat failed
to  consider  or  invoke  means  for  the  prevention  of
the  burden  on  commerce  resulting  from  industrial
strife  through  elimination  of  its  basic  causes.  As
early  as  1882  the  United  States  Senate  adopted  a
resolution  directing  its  Committee  on  Education
and  Labor  to  undertake  a  study  of  labor  disputes,
to ‘‘inquire  into  the causes  thereof  and  the agencies

Moreover,  in  several  fields  the  Federal  govern-
ment  has  dealt  with  industrial  strife  on  a  compre-
hensive,  long-range  basis.  In  the  railroad  indus-
_  try,  after  an  experience  in  legislation  dating  back
to 1888,  Congress  in the Railway  Labor  Act of 1926,
and  in  the  1934  amendments  to  that  Act,  applied
the  principles  which  it  subsequently  incorporated
in the National  Labor Relations  Act.  Id.,pp.54-09.
In war  industries:during  the  World  War,  the  Na-

years  immediately  preceding  passage  of  the  Na-

THE  NATURE  OF  THE  NATIONAL  LABOR  RELATIONS  ACT
Federal  legislation  designed  to  eliminate  or  re-
duce  burdens  or  obstructions  to  interstate  com-
merce  may  take  either  of  two  forms.
First,  Con-
gress  might  enact  a  statute  designed  to  mitigate
the  burdens  by dealing  directly  with  each  partic-
ular  obstruction  as  it arose.
The  Sherman  Anti-
Trust  Act  (Act  of July  2, 1890,  c.  697,  26 Stat.  209 ;
U.S.  C.,,  Title  15,  See.  1)  may  be  referred  to  as
illustrative  of  this  particular  type  of  legislation.
Or,  second,  Congress  might  have  approached  the
problem  by a statute  dealing  with  the  causes  of this
burden  in  anticipation  of their  probable  effect.  A
statute  of  the  first  type  will  be  referred  to  here-
after  as  an  exercise  by  Congress  of  its  ‘‘control”’
power  and  a statute  of  the  second  type  will  be
referred  to hereafter  as  an  exercise  by Congress  of
ts “*preventive’’  power.
Both  types  of legislation
are,  of  course,  merely  different  means  by  which
Congress  may  approach  the  problem  of  burdens
on  interstate  commerce.

This  power  of Congress  to enact  preventive  legis-
lation is  illustrated  by the  historical  setting  of  the
Packers  and Stockyards Act.  Many practices  prev-
alent  in  the  stockyards,  and  in  the  meat-packing
industry in  general,  had  been  seriously  burdening
interstate  commerce  in  both  livestock  and  meat

32
products.  In  Swift  &  Co.  v.  United  States,  196
U.  9. 375,  some of these  practices  were  effectively
punished  under  the  Sherman  Act.  Congress,  not
content  with  action  against  particular  individuals
or  combinations,  then  passed  the  Packers  and
Stockyards  Act  (Act  of August  15,  1921,  c.  64, 42
Stat.  159;  U.  S.  C.,  Title  7, Ch.  9),  designed  to
eliminate  the  causes  of  the  burdens  to  interstate
This  Court,  upholding  the  Act  in Staf-
commerce.
ford  v.  Wallace,  258  U.  8.  495,  stated  (p.  520):

The  language  of  the  law  shows  that  what
Congress  had  in mind  primarily  was  to pre-
vent  such  conspiracies  by supervision  of the
agencies  which  would  be  likely  to  be  em-
ployed  in it.  If Congress  could  provide  for
punishment  or  restraint  of such conspiracies
after  their  formation  through  the  Anti-
Trust  Law  as  in  the  Swift  Case,  certainly
it may  provide  regulation  to  prevent  their
formation.

Many other  examples  of the exercise  by Congress
of  this  power  to  prevent  evils  which  it  could
control  could  be  cited.  For  present  purposes,  it
will  be sufficient  to refer  to the dpi Futures  Act
309,  42  Stat.  998;
(Act  of  September  21,  1922,  c.
U.  S.  C.,  Title  7, Ch.  1)  (see  Chicago  Board  of
Trade  v.  Olsen, 262 U. S. 1) and the Railway  Labor
Act  (Act  of  May  20,  1926, c.  347,  44  Stat.  577;
U.S.  C., Title 45, Ch. 8, as amended)  (see  Tezas  ¢
New  Orleans  R.  Co.  v.  Brotherhood  of Ratlway
Clerks,  281  U.  8.  548).  In  any  event,  the  exist-

33

ence  of  the  power  cannot  be  doubted.  Moreover,
that it  may be utilized  in the field  of industrial  dis-
putes  has  been  indicated  by the  Court  not  only  in
Texas  &  New  Orleans  R.  Co.  v.  Brotherhood  of
Railway  Clerks,  supra,  but  also  in  United  Mime
Workers  vy.  Coronado  Coal  Co., 259  U.  8. 344,  408,
and  United Leather  Workers  v.  Herkert  & Meisel
Trunk  Co., 265 U. 8. 457, 469.

The  National  Labor  Relations  Act  is an  example
of  this  preventive  legislation,  based  on  the  ad-
mitted  power  of  Congress  to  control  certain  types
of  industrial  disputes  when  they  have  occurred.
-  The  Act  does  not  deal  with  injuries  to  interstate
commerce  after  they  have  occurred,  but  seeks  to
prevent  their  occurrence  by the elimination  of cer-
tain  specified  practices  which Congress  has found
lead  to such  disputes.

Although  Congress  thus clearly  has  a  power  to  .'

prevent  obstructions  to commerce,  it is equally  clear
that  the  preventive  measures  must bear a  reason-
able  relation  to the end  sought  to be achieved.  The
exercise  of  the preventive  power  must,  in  other
words,  bear  a reasonable  relation  to the elimination
of  evils  which  could  be  subjected  to  the  control
power of Congress  after  they  occurred.  Congress
cannot  make  the  existence  of  an  evil  of  national
concern  the  excuse  for  regulating  a  large  measure
of affairs  of purely  local  concern.

Nor  has  it  done  so  in  this  Act.  The  Act  does
not  attempt  to  eliminate  causes  of  strikes  in  a

34

enterprises.  The  statute,  and  the  mandate  ad-
dressed  to  the  administrative  Board,  are  specifi-
cally  directed  to  the  elimination  of  the  practices
proseribed  by Section  8 only  when  they  are  found
to be ‘‘affecting  commerce’’  (Section  10  (a)).  This
phrase  ‘‘affecting  commerce”’  is defined  in  Section
2 (7)  of the  statute as ‘‘in commerce,  or  burdening
-or  obstructing  commerce  or  the  free  flow  of  com-
merce,  or  having  led  or  tending  to  lead  to  a  labor
dispute  burdening  or  obstructing  commerce  or  the
free  flow  of commerce.’’  These  words  are  plainly
patterned  upon  decisions  of  this  Court  in  cases
arising  under  other  statutes  enacted  by Congress.
This  jurisdictional  limitation,  viewed  in  the  light
of  the  legislative  history  set  forth  in  the  Brief
for  the  Board  in  No.  365,  The  Associated  Press
vy.  National  Labor  Relations  Board,  at  p.  114,
is  manifestly  a direction  to  the  Board  to  exer-
cise  the  national  power  within  the  limits  per-
In  other  words,  Con-
mitted  by the  Constitution.
gress  has  taken  as  the  ambit  of  this  particular
preventive  statute  the boundaries  to Which it would
be restricted  were  it exercising  to the  full  its  con-
trol  power.  It has  not  endeavored  to  exercise  its
preventive  power  where  it could  not  have  exercised
its control  power  under  appropriate  legislation.

In  examining  the  nature  of the  evil  to be antici-
pated  before  it  requires  the  elimination  of  the
practices  the Board thus operates in a manner  simi-
lar  to  that  followed  by  Congress  itself  in  other
Just  as  Congress  found
preventive  legislation.

35

in  the  steckyards  and  the  packing ‘industry  eer
tain  evils  which  burdened  interstate  commereé,
and  undertook  to prevent  them  by the  Packers  and
Stockyards  Act,  arid  found  similar  -evils  on  the
grain  exchanges  and  undertook  to prevent  them  by
the  Grain  Futures  Act,  so  here  the  Board  deter-
mines  the  existence  or  the  probability  of the ocgur-
rence  of an  evil  which  Congress  could  control.
If
it so  determines  in  any  particular  case,  it applies
the  preventive  measures  provided  in  the Act, for
clearly  such  a  case  is one  in which  industrial  strife
would  constitute  a  burden  and  obstruction  to
commerce.

4

This  ad  hoc  application  of this  preventive  stat-
ute  is a  milder  exercise  of  the  power  of  Congress
than  either  the  Packers  and  Stockyards  Act  or  the
Grain  Futures  Act.  Those  statutes  apply  gener-
ally to every  person engaged  in the industries regu-
lated,  while  the  present  one  is  applied  only  after
specific  investigation  as  to the prohable  outcome  of
an  industrial  dispute  in  the  particular  enterprise
’  eoncerned.  Moreover,  those  statutes  prescribe  de-
tailed  regulation  of  a  meticulous  character  with
the  intention  of  governing  many  of  the  details  of
Here  Congress  seeks  merely  to
eliminate  the  causes  of  particular  evils,  and  has
made  no  attempt  to construct  a plan or  pattern  for
the  general  conduct  of business.

_  the  businesses.

One  further  characteristic  of preventive  legisla-
tion  should  be noted.
In eliminating  certain  prac-
tices  in situations  in which  the  resulting  industrial

36

strife  would  be  likely  to  come  within  the  control
power  of Congress,  the application  of the  Act  will
upon  occasion  result  in the  prohibition  of activity
which,  even  if allowed  to spend  its force,  would  not
result  in  industrial  strife  which  did  in  fact  come

within  the  Federal  control  power.
that when  the practice was  indulged  in there  was  a
reasonable  likelihood  that  any  industrial  strife
which  resulted  would  be within  the  control  power
under  appropriate  legislation,  the  Board  may
apply the preventive  measure.”  To hold  otherwise
would  deprive  Congress  of any  preventive power,
since  no  one  can  foretell  with  absolute  accuracy
what the  exact  effects  of any  cause  will  be.  Com-

The  record  also  contains  detailed  evidence  concerning
the  recurrent  nature  and  causes  of  industrial  strife  in
the  steel  industry  (R.  608  et  seg.,  877  et  seq.;  Bd.  Exhs.
93,  95).  See  also  H.  Rep.  No.  2447,  52d  Cong.,  1st  Sess.,
Employment  of  Pinkerton  Detectives,  p.  xi;  Sen.  Rep.
No.  289,  66th  Cong.,  Ist  Sess.,  /nvestigating  Strike  in
Steel  Industries,  p. 4.  In summary,  this history  shows  that
there  have  been  recurrent  efforts  on  the  part  of  the  em-
ployees  in  the  industry  to  organize;  that  since  1892  it has
been  the  established  policy  of  the  larger  steel  companies  to
oppose  such  efforts;  that  as  a  consequence  there  has’ been
continuous  unrest  and  recurrent  strife;  that  as  long  as  this
situation  exists,  such  unrest  and  strife  is “absolutely  inevita-
ble”  (R.  682-683).

vehicles  travelling  in  interstate  commerce.
tainly  it would  not  be  invalidated  simply  because
it would  result  in the  protection  of  many  vehicles
engaged  only  in local  commerce.

The  foregoing  discussion  makes  it clear  that  the
question  of the  validity  of a  specific  application  of
the  present  Act  depends  upon  whether  industria]
strife  in the particular  enterprise  under  considera-
tion  could,  if it occurred,  be  controlled  by the  Fed-
eral  Government,  under  appropriate  legislation.
The  question  of  primary  importance,  therefore,  is
the  extent  of the  Federal  power  of control.  Since
the  language  of this  statute  does  not  go  beyond  the
it of this  power,  whatever  it may  be determined
to
be,  the  statute  is  clearly  constitutional  on  its
face.  The  only  issue  is whether  it has  been  consti-
tutionally  applied.

This  Court  has  never  had  occasion  to  define  the
full  extent  to  which  industrial  strife  in  the  manu-
facturing,  producing,  or  processing  divisions  of an
enterprise  engaged  extensively  in  interstate  com-
merce  may  be subjected  to  the  exercise  of the  con-
It  has,  however,  held
trol  power  of  Congress.”

that  certain  situations  are  clearly  within  the  con-

to  state  three  situations  in  which  Congress  may
exercise  its  power  to  control  burdens  and  obstruc-
tions  on  interstate  commerce.  We  shall  first  briefly

he

(1)  It is  well  settled  that  an  industrial  dispute:
involving  an intent  to affect  interstate  commerce  is
within  the  control  power  of Congress.

(2)  This  Court  has  also  recognized  that  an  in-
dustrial  dispute  having  the  necessary  effect  of sub-
stantially  burdening  or  obstructing  commerce  is
within  the  control  power  of Congress.

(3)  This  Court  has  also  recognized  that  the  con-
trol  power  of  Congress  extends  to  recurring  evils
which in their  totality  may  constitute  a  substantial
burden  on  interstate  commerce.

essary  to  discuss  the  situation  which  arises  where  the  enter-

has  already  been  discussed  in  the  Board’s  briefs  in  No.  365,
The  Associated  Presa  v.  National  Labor  Relations  Board,  and
is fully  supported  by Texas  & New  Orleans  R. Co.  v.  Broth-
erhood  of Railway  Clerks,  281  U.S.  548.
Moreover,  the  lan-
guage  of Section  2 €7),  which,  together  with  Section 10  (a),
establishes  the  jurisdictional  limitations  of  the  Board,  dis-
tinguishes  between  lat~  practices  which  occur*“in  com-
merce”  (that  is,  in  enterprises  such  as  an  interstate  trans-
portation  system),  and  labor  practices  which  may  be  de-
scribed:  as  “having  led  or  tending  to  lead  to  a  labor  dispute
burdening  or  obstructing  commerce  or  the  free  flow  of  com-
merce”  (that  is, in enterprises  such  as  that  of respondent).

We  submit  that.in  the  application  of the  Act  to
respondent  each  of  these  three  situations  is  pre-
sented.  Of course,  the  existence  of any  one  of the
three  is sufficient  to sustain  the  jurisdiction  of the
Board.

THE  VALIDITY  OF  THE  ACT  AS  APPLIED  TO  THE
RESPONDENT

(1)  The  Power  of  Congress  With  Respect  to  Industrial
Strife  Involving  an  Intent  to  Affect  Interstate  Commerce

It  is  well settled  that  an  industrial  dispute’  in-
volving  an  intent  to  restrain  commerce  is  within
the  control  power  of Congress,  even  though  arising
out of local  activity.  Coronado  Coal  Co.  v.  United
Mine  Workers,  268  U.  S. 295;  Bedford  Cut  Stone
Co.  v.  Stone  Cutters’  Association,  274  U.S.  37; Du-
plex  Printing  Co.  v.  Deering,  254  U. S. 443;  Loewe
v.  Lawlor,  208  U.  S. 274.  Consequently,  where  the
situation  in a particular  enterprise  presents  a  rea-
sonable  likelihood  that  a  dispute,  if  it  occurred,
would  involve  an  intent  to restrain  commerce,  then
the  Board  can  apply  the  statute  to that  enterprise.
The  reasonable  likelihood  of  a  dispute  with  the
intent  to  affect  commerce  might  be  demonstrated
in  either  of  two.  ways:  first,  evidence  might  be
presented  to  the  Board  that  in a  particular  situa-
tion  the  intent  already  exists;  or second,  evidence
might be presented  to the  Board  that  the  situation
was  comparable  to and  of the same  general  type  as
those  situations  from  which  in the  past  there  had

41

evplved  controversies  with  intent  to  affect  com-
rece,  or  that  in the  particular  situation  confront-
9 the Board  such  definite  intention  might reason-
ably be expected  to develop  should  the strife  occur.
In  the  case  at  bar  is  it not  claimed  that  there  is
evidence  of the  first  sort ; but there  is abundant  evi-
dence  that  the  situation  is  fraught  with  the  risk
that  after  strife  developed  it  would  involve  the
purpose  to  curtail  the  movement  of  goods  in  com-
merece.  This  evidence  is of three  different  sorts.  ©

Ina

In  the  first  place,  respondent  conducts  a  highly
integrated  national  enterprise  operating  in  some
States  mines  and  quarries  which  are  the  source  of
its  materials,  in  other  States  steel  mills  and
other  manufacturing  plants,  and  in  still  other
States  wholesale  outlets  for  the  distribution  of  its
products.
Moreover,  it owns  and  operates  certain
transportation  facilities  operating  in  interstate
far-flung,  integrated  enterprise  of
commerce.
this  type,  controlled  ‘by  a  single  employer,  indus-

is  peculiarly  apt  to  involve  a  purpose  to  promote
collective  action  among  employees  in  other  divi-
sions  of the  enterprise  in order  to increase  the  bar-

common  employer.

situation  evolves  into  a  pattern  such  as  has  been

ations  presented  to  this  Court  in Loewe  v.  Lawler,

208  U.  8. 274; Duplex  Printing Co.  v.  Deering,  254

boycotts  in  those  cases  was  to  promote  collective
action  among  employees  other  than  those  engaged”
in  the  boycott,  and  the  local  action  taken  was  de-

Here  one  of the purposes  of industrial  strife  in any
division  of the  enterprise  would  be  to  promote  col-
lective  action  throughout  all of the  divisions  of the
enterprise,  and  such  local  action  as  might be taken
would  be  designed  io  effectuate  a  similar  or  more
aggravated  interruption  of  commerce.

ployees  are  engaged  in  interstate  commerce  or

if  it  did  not  originate  with  commerce  employees,
would  be  reasonably  likely  to  spread  to  them  in

purpose  of stopping  shipments  out  of all  plants  in
order  to  bring  pressure  to  bear  upon  the  industry
as  a whole.

peticies.

Thus  the  evidence  in this  case  warrants  the  con-
clusion  that  there  is  a  reasonable  likelihood . that
industrial  strife  in  respondent’s  enterprise  would,
if it occurred,  involve  an  intent  to affect  commerce.
This  conclusion  is particularly justified  in all  situ-
ations  where,  as  here,  there  is a probability  of strife
with  respect  to  employees’  basic  rights  to  organize
and  choose  representatives  for  collective  bargain-
ing.  Such  controversies  are  notoriously  bitter  and
_  prolonged  because  of  the  fundamental  issues  at
stake,  tend  to  involve  sympathetic  action  by  or-
ganized  employees  inthe  same  or  related  indus-
tries,  and  co‘sequently  are  most  likely  to  result  in
intentional  interferences  with sales  or  shipments  in
interstate  commerce.
Precisely  such  factors  are
manifested  in  the  cases  under  the  Sherman  Act  in
which  intent  was  found  to  exist.
Cf.  Coronado
Coal  Co.  v.  United  Mine  Workers,  268  U.S.  295,
297-298 ; Bedford  Stone  Co. v. Stone Cutters’  Ass’n,
274  U.  8. 37,  59-63  (Brandeis  and  Holmes,  J.  J.,

was  justified  in holding  that  the  preventive  meas-

(2)  The  Power  of  Congress  With  Respect  to  Industrial

It is not necessary,  however,  to support  the  order
of the Board  in the present  case  upon  the power  of
,  Congress  to  control  industrial  strife  involving  an
Alternatively,  we  sub-
intent  to affect  commerce.
mit that industrial  strife  in respondent’s  enterprise
would  be within  the control  power  of  Congress  be-
cause  it would  necessarily  have  the  effect  of  sub-
stantially  burdening  commerce.

This Court  has  recognized  that  Congress  may
exercise  control  over  some  industrial  disputes  even
where  intent  cannot  be  or  is not proven.  For  ex-
ample,  in  United  Mine  Workers  y.  Coronado  Coal
Co.,  259  U.  8.  344,  a  case  which  arose  under  the
Sherman Act, this  Court stated  (pp. 410-411)  :

*  coal  mining  is not  interstate  com-
*.  *
meree  and  obstruction  of  coal  mining,
though  it may  prevent  coal  from  going  into
interstate  commerce,  is  not  a  restraint  of
that  commerce  unless  the  obstruction  to
mining  is intended  to  restrain  commerce  in
it or  has  necessarily  such  a  direct,  material,
and  substantial  effect  to restrain  it that  the

45

|

intent  reasonably  musi  be inferred.  [Italics
supplied. ]

.
This language  was  expressly  approved  in Indus-
irtal  Association  v.  United  States,  268  U.  8. 64.
The  Court  there  pointed  out  that  the  strike  in the
first  Coronado  case  was  held  not to constitute  a con-
8p iracy  to restrain  interstate  commerce  ‘‘in  the ab-
sence  of  proof  ef  an  intention  to  restrain  it  or
proof  of such  a  direct  and  substantial  effect  upon
ti, that such intention  reasonably  must  be inferred”’
Further,  the  Court
(p.  81).
said  that  the  Sherman  Act  was  not  applicable  even
if it  were  conceded  that  the  natural  result  would
be  to  keep  the  preponderant  part  of the  output  of
the  mine  from  being  shipped  out  of  the  State,  be-
cause  ‘‘the  effect  on  interstate  commerce  was  not
of such  substance  that  a  purpose  to  restrain  inter-
state  commerce  might  be  inferred’’  (Ibid.).  See
also  United  Leather  Workers  v.  Herkert  d& Meisel
Trunk  Co., 265 U.  8. 457, 471.

[Italics  supplied.]

_

It  may  be  argued  that  in  these  cases  the  Court-
in  referring  to  such  direct  and  substantial  effects
upon  commerce  stated  that  an  tmtent  must  be  in-
ferred.
But,  this  is equivalent  to saying  that  that
Act  may  constitutionally  be  applied  to  a  situation
in which  there  is no  actual  specific  intent,  but  only
a  “necessary  effect’?  on  commerce.  And  it  must
follow  that  the power  of Congress:extends  at  least
as  far as  the  Sherman  Act has  been  enforced—that
is, to industrial  strife  which  has  only  a  ‘“‘necessary
effect’’  on  interstate  commerce.

*\

115696—37——_4

46
Thus,  the  cases  dealing  with  industrial  disputes
through  the  application  -of.  the  Sherman  Act
establish  the  proposition  that  Congress  may,  by
ad hoc  action,  control  industrial  strife  irrespective
of the  amount  of the  reduction  of the  supply  of the
commodity  involved  if an  intent  to stop  interstate
shipments  be  shown  (Cf.  Nash  v.  United  States,
999  U.  S.  373),  and  that  Congress  my  also  take
similar  measures  irrespective  of  a  showing  of
specific  intent  where  the  reduction  in the supply  of
the  commodity  is so large  that  an  intent  to burden
and  obstruct  interstate  commerce  may  be  inferred
(Cf.  United  States  Vv.  Patten,  226  U.S.  525).

The  existence  of this  power  of  Congress  to deal
with  industrial  disputes  which  have  only  a ‘‘neces-
sary  effect’?  of  burdening  interstate  commerce  is
borne  out  by  the  analogy  of  other  cases  un-
der the  Sherman  Act, in which  no  specific  intention
was  shown.  Thus  in  United  States  v.  Patten,  226
U.  S. 525, this  Court  held  that  a conspiracy  to cor-
ner  cotton  on  an  exchange  was  within  the  prohibi-
tion-of.  the  Sherman  Act  since  “‘by  its  necessary
operation  it would  directly  and  materially  impede
and  burden  the  due  course  of trade  and  commerce
among  the  States’’,  and  that  it was  therefore  im-
material  that  there  was  ‘‘no  allegation  of #specific
intent  to  réstrain  such  trade  or  commerce’’  (p.
543).  The  same  proposition  is  referred  to  in
Addyston  Pipe  &  Steel  Co.  V.  United  States,  175
U.S.  211,  243.

°

47

Indeed,  unless  the  power  of  Conggess  extended
to  disputes  with  a  necessary  effect  on  commerce,
the  boundaries  of  Congressional  power  under
the  commerce  clause  would,  in  this  respect,  be  °
measured  by the state  of mind  of the persons  whom
Congress  was  attempting  to  control,  and  it cannot
be  supposed  that  such  a  startling  proposition  is
sound.  See  Schechter  Corp  v.  United  States,  295
U.  8S.  495,  547-548;  United  Leather  Workers  v.
Herkert  &  Meisel  Trunk  Co.,  265  U.  8S.  457,  472.
The  comment  of the  Court  in  Railroad  Retirement
Board  v.  Alton  R.  R. Co.,  295  U.  S. 330,  is apt here
(p.  373):  ‘The  meaning  of the  commeree  and  due
process  clauses  of  fhe  Constitution  is not  so  easily
enlarged  by the  vohintary  acts  of  individuals  or
corporations.  ’’

The  opinions  of  this  Riad dis not  clearly  define
the  category  of industrial  strife  with  the  necessary
effect  of  substantially  burdening  interstate  com-
merce.
The  reference  in  the  first  Coronado  and
Industrial  Association  cases  (pp.  44-45,  supra)
to a “‘direct,  material,  and  substantial  effect”’  upon
commerce  indicates  that  one  of  the  factors  to  be
considered
in  determining  whether  industrial
strife  is of this  type  is the  magnitude  of the  actual
effect  upon  the  interstate  commerce  in  a  commod-
ity.  Although  we  do not  believe  this  need  be  the
sole  basis  of  decision,  we  believe  that  this  might
properly  be  actepted  as  one  of  the  criteria.
In-
deed,  in  International  Organization  v.  Red  Jacket

48
C.  C.  &  C.  Co.,  18  F.  (2d)  8389  (OC.  C.  A.  4th),
certiorari  denied,  275  U.  S.  536,  this  factor  was
chiefly  relied  on.  There  the  contention  was  made
that  ‘‘a  conspiracy  to  interfere  with  the  opera-
tion  of coal  mines  is not a conspiracy  to restrain  or
interfere  with  interstate  commerce.’’  The  court
pointed  out that  in the  second Coronado  case  (Cor-
onado  Coal  Co.  v.  United  Mine  Workers,  268  U.S.
295)  this  Court  had  held  that  evidence  that  the  ca-
pacity  of  plaintiff’s  mines  was  substantially  more
than  5,000  tons  per.  day  (as  against  the  capacity
of  5,000  tons  per  week  which  had  been  assumed
in  the  first  Coronado  case)  made,  with  other  evi-
dence  as  to  intent,  a  case  for  the  jury  as  to  con-
spiracy  to restrain  interstate  commerce.  The  Cir-
cuit  Court  of Appeals then  went  on  to say  (p. 845)  :
Here  it  appears  that  the  total  production
of the  mines  of complainants  is in excess  of
40,000,000  tons  per  year,  more  than  90  per
cent  of which  is shipped  in  interstate  com-
Interference  with  the  production
merce.
of  these  mines  as  contemplated  by  defend-
ants  would  necessarily  interfere  with  inter-
state  commerce  in  cod@f  to  a  substantial
degree.

It may  be objected  that  a distinction  based  upon
degree  or  magnitude  is  inconsistent  with  the  de-
cision  in Carter  v.  Carter  Coal  Co., 298  U. 8. 238.
We  submit  that  it is not.  Im the  Carter  case  the  —
question  was  whether  the  relationship  between
wage-cutting  and  interstate  commerce  in  coal  was

49

direct  or  indirect.”  The  Court  held  that  the  rela-
tionship  was  indirect,  since  the  effect  on-interstate.
commerce  resulted  through  ‘‘an  efficient  interven-
ing  agency  or  condition”  (pp.  307-308) ; that  is,
the  effect  of diverting  the  movement  of  interstate
commerce  resulted  only through  a causal  chain  pro-
ceeding  from  wage-cutting,  which  led  to  price-cut-
ting,  which  in  turn  led  to  diversion  of  interstate
shipments.
In  that  situation,  the  Court  made  it
clear  that  the  extent  of  the  effect  was  immaterial,
even  though  it might  be conceded  to be substantial,
since  it  could  not  be  direct.  The  Court  stated
(p. 308) :

:

It  is  quite  true  that  rules  of  law  are  some-
times  qualified  by considerations  of degree,
as  the  government  argues:  But  the  matter
of  degree  has  no  bearing  upon  the  question
here,  since  that question  is not—What  is the
extent  of  the  local  activity  or  condition,  or
the  extent  of  the  effect  produced  upon  in-
terstate  commerce?  but—What  is the  rela-
tion  between  the  activity  or  condition  and
the  effect?

[Italics  in  original. ]

In other  words,  where  there  is an  efficient  inter-
vening  agency  or  condition  between  the  cause  and
the  effect,  ‘“‘such  effect

sive  it may  be,  is secondary  and  indirect.  An  in-

Here,  on  the  other  hand,  industrial  strife  with  the

being  direct,  the  matter  of  degree  or,magnitude

may  become  significant.
ring,  in Schechter  Corp.  v.  United  States,  295  U.S.
