°

Magnitude  of effect  upon  interstate  commerce  in
a  particular  commodity  need  not,  however,  be  the
sole  criterion  upon  which  the  Board  is  to  decide
whether  industrial  strife  in  any  particular  enter-
prise  would  have  the  necessary  effect  of  burdening
The  investi-_
or  obstructing  interstate  commerce.
gation  by the  Board  may  cover  the  nature  of  the
enterprise,  including  its  size  in  relation  to  the  in-
dustry  as  a  whole,  whether  it is within  a  stream  or
flow  of  commerce,  and  the  volume  of  interstate
ecommerce  both  in  raw  materials  and  in  finished
products;  the  nature  of the  industry,  including  its
national  or  local  character,  the  size  of  its  units,
and  whether  one  person  or  corporation  owns  or
*  controls  widely  scattered  units;  and the  likelihood
of  sympathetic  strikes  or,  boycotts  by  unions  or

51

organizations  other  than  the  ones  directly  con-
cerned.

It  is  unnecessary  and,  indeed,  impossible  to list
all of the  factors  upon  which  the Board  might  rely.
However,  for  purposes  of  simplification,  and  with
no  intention  of  exhausting  the  possibilities,  three
possible  constructions  of “necessary  effect’’  may  be
suggested:  (1)  industrial  strife  has  the  necessary
effect  of  burdening  or  obstructing  interstate  com-
merce  when  it occurs  in an  enterprise  lying  within
a  well-defined  ‘‘stream  or  flow  of  commerce’’;*
(2)  industrial  strife  is of that  type  when  its effect
would  be  to  prevent  a  substantial  proportion  of all
the  commerce  in  the  particular  commodity ; or  (3)
industrial  strife  is  of  that  type  when  its  effect
would  be  to  prevent  interstate  sales  or  shipments
of  a  substantial  volume  of  goods  which  would
otherwise  move  in interstate  commerce.

We  believe  that  for  the  purposes  of  the  present
ease  a  discussion  of  the  construction  of ‘necessary
effect’?  may  be confined  to construction  (1)  or  (2),
although  a  fortiori,  the  enterprise  is  also: within
construction  (3).  We  turn,  then,  to  a  considera-
tion  of  the  nature  of  respondent,  with  particular
attention  to  its  position  in  a  stream  or  flow  of
commerce.

*  In defining  “affecting  commerce”  in  Section  2 (7)  of the
Act,  Congress  including  specifically  those  situations  where
there  was  a  “flow  of  commerce.”

52

The  nature  of respondent’s  operations
Respondent  is  the  fourth  largest  company  in
the steel  industry  (R.  109;  Bd.  Exhs.  4,5).  ‘The
consolidated  assets  of  respondent  and  its  19  sub-
aidiaries  totaled  $181,532,641  as  of  December  31,
It is  ‘‘completely  inte-
1934  (Bd.  Exhs.  8, 18).
grated’’,  and  is engaged  directly  in the  transporta-
tion,  production,  acquisition,  sale,  and  distribution
of  materials  and  products  (Bd.  Exh.  4).  One  of
its  two  main  plants  is located  at  Aliquippa,  Penn-
sylvania,  where  normally  about  10,000  of  respond-
ent’s  more  than  20,000  employees  are  employed
(R. 88, 110; Bd.  Exh.  4).

This  Aliquippa  plant is strategically  located ‘with
regard  to  river  and  railroad  transportation  facili-
It-stretches  approximately  five  miles  along
ties.
the banks  of the  Ohio River  (Bd.  Exh.  4) below  the
juncture  of the Monongahela  and  Allegheny  Rivers.
Respondent,  therefore,  has  direct  access  to  the
cheap water  transportation  afforded  by three  great
waterways,  the Mississippi,  Ohio, and Monongahela
Rivers  (Bd.  Exh.  7).  The  respondent  is  also
thoroughly  equipped  with  trackage,  locomotives,
freight  cars,  and  other  railruad  equipment  in  and
around  the  plant.  The  tracks  extend  through-
out  the  plant  and  into  and  out  of  the  various
(R.  100;  Bd.  Exh.  4.)  Respondent  —
buildings,
owns  and  operates  the  Aliquippa  &  Southern
Railroad  Company  (Bd.  Exh.  4; R. 99-100)  —43.20
miles  of standard  gauge  track—which  connects  the
Aliquippa  plant with  the Pittsburgh  and Lake  Erie
Railroad,  a part of the  New  York  Central  Railway

53

System  (Bd.  Exhs.  4, 5, 15,  50).  About  450  em-.
ployees  are  directly  employed  in  railroad  work
within  the plant  (Bd.  Exhs.  5 and 15; R. 101).  The
Aliquippa  &  Southern  Railroad  has  19  logpmo-
tives  and  614  freight  cars  (Bd.  Exhs.  5,15).  It re-
ports  to  the  Interstate  Commerce  Commission  as  a
common  carrier  (Bd.  Exh.  16).  Respondent  car-
ries  a  considerable  tonnage  of  its  products  to  the
South  and  Southwest  over  the  Ohio  and  Missis-
sippi  Rivers,  owning  and  operating  transportation
equipment  (Bd.  Exh.  4) consisting  of steamers  and
barges  (R.  120,  392).  The  barges  are  loaded  by
respondent’s employees  (R.  394).

|

It  is  evident  that  respondent’s  transportation
facilities  and  activities  are  essential  to and part of
respondent’s  completely  integrated  business.  Re-
spondent,  for  the  production  of steel,  is dependent
upon  three  basic  materials,  iron  ore,  coke, and lime-
stone  (R. 82, 83), and  it maintains  its own  reserves,
mines  and.quarries  at various  points  in the  United
States  from  which  it obtains  these  basic  materials.
Respondent  procures  its  iron  ore  from  its  ore  re-  -
serves  in the  Lake  Superior  region,  and  in connec-
tion  with  one  of  its  iron  mines  in  that  region,  —
respondent  operates  a  washing  and  concentrating
plant  (R.  98,  Bd.  Exhs.  4,5).  During  the  eight-  _
year  period  ending  December  31,  1930,  respondent
used  approximately  3,570,000  gross  tons  of iron  ore
annually  (Bd.  Exh.  5).  The  iron  ore,  en  route
from  the  reserves  in  the  Lake  Superior  region  to
Aliquippa,  is first  carried  to the ‘‘upper  lake ports”’

54

by industrial  railroads  doing  practically  no  other
business  (R. 594).  The  ore  is then  loaded  on  spe-
cially  constructed  lake  ore  carriers  and  is carried
by way  of the Great  Lakes  to respondent’s  docks  at
Ashtabula,  Ohio  (R. 99, 594;  Bd.  Exh.  4).  In order
to  avoid  interruption  in  the  transportation  of  its
supply  of iron  ore,  the  respondent  owns  and  oper-
ates  four of these  ore  boats  on  the Great  Lakes  (Bd.
Exh.  4).  Because  the  lakes  are  closed  to  naviga-
tion  by ice  from  November  to  April,  and  in  order
to  meet  anticipated  business  requirements  during
that  period,  some  of  the  ore  is stored  at  respond-
ent’s  lake  docks  at  Ashtabula,  Ohio,  but  the  bulk
of  the  ore  used  at  Aliquippa  is  shipped  from  the
lakes  by rail  without  storage  (R.  84,  85,  99,  107,
122;  Bd.  Exh.  4).

Respondent  obtains  its coal  from  its  properties
in  Pennsylvania  (Bd.  Exh.  4;  R.  84-85)  where
about  2,250  men  are  employed  (R. 236).  The  coal
is  transported  down  the  Monongahela  and  Ohio
Rivers  by a fleet of 10 towboats  and  165 steel  barges
owned  and  operated  by the  respondertt,  directly  to
respondent’s  plants  (R.  85,  89;  Bd.  Exh.  5).  At
the  Aliquippa  plant  barges  are  unloaded  by  re-
spondent’s  locomotive  cranes  (R.  102).  The  coal:
which  is  destined  for  the  respondent’s  blast  fur-
naces  is reduced  to coke in respondent’s  by- oe
plants  (Bd.  Exh. 4; R. 86).

Respondent’s  third  main  raw  material,  lime~
stone, is shipped by rail  from  its properties  in West
Virginia  (R. 85, 107).

55

Some  idea  of the  huge  bulk  of the  raw  materials
which  flow  into  the Aliquippa  works  may  be gained
from  the  fact  that  in a represeutative  month,  Octo~
ber  1935,  the  plant  received  6,222  full  carloads  and
237  less  than  carload  lots of materials,  76.5  percent
of  which  originated  outside  of the  State  (R.  133).
This  average  of  more  than  200  inbound  cars  per
day by railroad  is exclusive  of the heavy river  traf-
fic  m  coal.  Most  of  these  incoming  produets  are
used  directly  without  storage  (R.  84).  About  97
percent  of  all  the  business  of  the  Pittsburgh  and
Lake  Erie  Railroad  at  Aliquippa  is dependent  upon
the  continued  operation  of  respondent’s  plant  (R.
133).

This  great  bulk  of  material  which  flows  regu-
larly  in  interstate  commerce  to  Aliquippa  with
the  aid  of  respondent’s  transportation  facilities  is
unloaded  by  respondent’s  own  employees  and
equipment,  continues  its  course  through  respon-
dent’s  works  over  respondent’s  trackage  and  equip-
ment,  and  finally  moves  on  to respondent’s  cus-
tomers  throughout  the  world  with  the  aid  of
respondent’s  transportation  facilities,  from  one  or
another  of  respondent’s  departments  in the  form
of  steel  in  various  stages  of  fabrication.
spondent’s  product  is sold  in both  semifmished  and
finished  forms  (R.  88).  Each  of  respondent’s  de-
partments  has  its  own  shipping  department  to
handle  materials  which  are  forwarded  to customers
from  that  department.  Employees  are  engaged  in
these  various  shipping  departments  to  see  that

Re-  —

56
orders  of customers are carried  out  and  to  handle
goods which are being placed on  cars or  barges
ready  for  shipment  (R.  102).  Respondent  pro-
duces  steel  largely  in  accordance  with  orders  or
specifications  of  customers  (R. 86-87).

In the  production  of steel  the blast  furnaces  are
loaded  with  coke,  ore  and  limestone,  in successive
layers,  and  the  iron  ore  is reduced  to  molten  iron.
From  the  blast  furnaces  the  molten  pig  iron  is
poured  either  into  the  Bessemer  converters  or  the
open  hearth  furnaces  (R.  82).  It  is at  this  stage
that variations  in the steel, according  to customers’
specifications,  are  first  made  (R. 86).  At the  Ali-
quippa  plant  very  little  of  the  product  of  the
blast  furnaces  is  allowed  to  cool  as  pigs,  but  is
used  in molten  state  to produce  either  Bessemer  or
what  is called  ‘‘open hearth’’  steel  (R. 82).  From
the  open  hearth  or  Bessemer  converter  the  steel .is
poured  into  ladles,  and  from  the ladles into molds.
When  it is  cool  enough  the  mold  is removed  and
the ingot is moved  into  the soaking  pit.  The  soak-
ing  pit is  an  underground  furnace  where  the  in-
gots  are  brought  up to an  evenly  distributed  tem-
perature  proper  for rolling.  From the  soaking  pit
the  steel  is taken  to the blooming  mill  where  it is
rolled  into  rectangular,  flat  pieces  of  steel,  called
‘“slabs”’  or  square  pieces  called  ‘‘blooms’”’.  The
slabs go to the shear  and are  shipped outside  of the
plant  to  the  respondent’s  South  Side  Works  (RK.
83).  The bloomsare  reduced to billets, or sheet bar
or skelp  and  are  either  sent  to the  various  depart-

57
ments  for  further  falftication  or  shipped  to  cus-
tomers  outside  the  plant  (R.  83).  About 30  per
cent  of this  semifinished  steel  is sold by respondent
at this  stage  (R. 103).

The  operations  at  the  Aliquippa  works  are  “‘a
continuous  process’?  (R.  101).  They  are  based
largely  tipon  the  use  of molten  pig, “‘So  that  when
pig iron  is made,  it has  to be processed  as  quickly
as  possible’  (R.  118).  One  operation  furnishes
the  material  for  the  next,  and  at  each  step  it
is  necessary  to move  heavy,  bulky  materials  with
a minimum  waste  of time.  The  handling  of mate-
rials  as  they pass  from  one  department  to another
is  done  by rail  and  by cranes.  The  cranes ‘not
only control  the flow  of materials  from  one  depart-
ment  to  the  next,  but  if that  material  was  sold  at
one  given  point  the crane  men  would  load  it into  a
railroad  car  at that  point’  (R. 101).  In the  seam-
less-tube  department  there  are,  for  example,  22
cranes  and  52 crane  men  (R. 202).

Rapid  handljng  of  materials  and  expeditious
shipment  of the product  at whatever  stage it is sold
is  made  necessary  because  respondent  works
largely to customers’  specifications  and'orders.  On
the  average,  customers’  orders  can  be filled  within
one  week  (R. 118).  Customers  are  dependent  upon
and  expect  uninterrupted  operation  of  respond-
ent’s  Aliquippa  plant  (R.  104).  Respondent  em-
 ploys  trace  clerks to handle  requests  that  come  in
from  customers  for  assistance  in effecting  prompt
*  deliveries  (R. 116-117,  128).  The trace clerks fol-

58
low  up  the  movement  of  steel  in  shipment  until  it
is delivered  at the customer’s  place  of business  (R.
128).  Respondent  also  occasionally  traces  late  in-
coming  shipments  in  order  to  expedite  them
(R. 128).

Respondent  states  that  *‘100  industries  look  to
J.  &  L.  for  steel’’  (Bd.  Exh.  21),  and  that  its
metallurgists  ‘‘are  customer  men  in a double  sense.
They  exercise  strict  control  of quality  in the  metal-
for your  protection,  and  they are  also  available  for
consultation  on  any  steel problem  you  may  have’’
(Bd.  Exh.  20).  A  part  of  respondent's  product
is  shipped  by  railroad  to  its  customers;  for  this
purpose  the  Pittsburgh  &  Lake  Erie  Railroad
furnishes  an average  of  1,900  cars  each  month  or
more  than  60 ears  per  day  (R.  112).  This  consti-
tutes  about  97  per  cent  of  all  the  business  of  the
railroad  at Aliquippa  (R.  133).  As  previously  de-
scribed,  respondent  also  ships  a  considerable  ton-
nage  of its products down  the Ohio  and  Mississippi
rivers with  its own  transportation  equipment.

In order  to facilitate  the distribution  of its prod-
ucts  ‘‘the  corporation  has  large  steel  warehouses
located  at strategic  points over  the country  through
which  it markets a substantial  tonnage  of its prod-
ucts”  (Bd. Exh.  4).  These  warebouses  are  in part
located  in  large  cities  along  the  Ohio  and  Missis-
sippi  rivers  where  they  can  be  reached  by tne  re-
spondent’s  own  barges.  - In addition,  the  respond-
ent has warehouses  in Chicago,  Detroit,  and  Long
Island  City, N. Y.  (Bd. Exh.7).  From  the  ware-

59

houses  the  steel  products  are  transshipped  by rail
to customers  (R. 107).

In connection  with  two  of these  warehouse  points
the respondent  operates  structural  steel  fabricating
Here  the  semifinished  steel  is  further
shops.
adapted  to  customer  specifications  (Bd.  Exh.  5).
Through  one  of  its  subsidiaries,  the  Louisiana
Erecting  Co., respondent  ‘‘conducts  a general  erect-
ing business,  using  principally  materials  fabricated
by  the  [respondent]  at  its New  Orleans  Ware-
house’’  (Bd.  Exh.  5).
It  thus  appears  that  the
stage  of  ‘‘manufacture”’  is  not  completed  at  Ali-
quippa,  but  rather  that  the  operations  there  con-
stitute  steps  in  the  series  of  integrated  activities
involved  in  producing  and  distributing  steel.

According  to  the  respondent  its  ‘‘products  are
sold  to  its  customers  throughout  the  United  States
and  many  foreign  countries,  principally  through
its  own  sales  offices  located  in twenty  of the  larger
cities  of the  country.  Its sales  in Canada  are  mar-
keted  through  its  subsidiary  the  Jones  and  Laugh-
lin Steel  Products Company  with  its offices  in Pitts-
burgh  and  Toronto,  Canada”  (Bd. Exh.  4).  In
common  with  the other  large steel  corporations,  the
respondent  sells  its  product  only upon  the  basis  of
delivered  price  (Bd.  Exhs.  40,41).

From  this  record  it  is  evident  that  the  Jones
&  Laughlin  Steel  Corporation  is  a  natioriwide
It  regularly  draws  its  materials  in
enterprise.
well  defined  paths  of  commerce  from  ifs  prop-
erties  in  various  States  to  its  works  at  Ali-

and,  from  there

60
quippa.  At  the  Aliquippa  plant  the  bulk  of
these  materials  pass  first  into  the  blast  fur-
to  the  converters  or
naces
open  hearth  furnaces.  From  this  point  on  the
stream  differentiates  and  moves  into  and  from  the
various  departments  according  to consumer  orders.
In any  event,  both  technological  necessity  and  cus-
tomer  interest  make  the  movement  of  materials
and  fabrication  of  products  a  continuous  process.
The  respondent  markets  these  products  in  inter-
state  commerce  through  its  own  organization  and
performs,  or  retains  control  of,  the  transportation
of  the  products  to  the  point  of  consumption.  A
considerable  proportion  of respondent’s  employees
are  directly  engaged  in  this  work  of  transporting
materials  to the  plant,  moving  materials  within  the
works,  preparing  shipments  and  loading  them  ac-
cording  to  cubtomer  specifications,  and  finally  in
warehousing  and  transporting  products  to  cus-
tomers  in  respondent’s  equipment.  The  extensive
ownership  by the respondent  of the means  of trans-
porting this flow of goods to and  from  its Aliquippa
plant  is itself  conclusive  evidenete  of a  regular  and
Otherwise  such  pri-
definite  stream  of commerce.
vate  ownership  of transportation  equipment  would
not  be feasible.

We  believe  that  the Board  would  have  been  jus-  |

tified  in  concluding  from  the  facts  with  respect  to
respondent,  without  more,  that  industrial  strife  in
-  that  enterprise  would  have  the  necessary  effect  of
burdening  or  injuring  interstate  commerce  within
the  first  definition  suggested  above  (p. 51,  supra).
However,  the  Board-went  on to consider  other  fae-
tors  which  serve  to  reinforce  that  conclusion.

The  nature  of  the  steel  industry  as  a  whole,
which  was  brought  out  in  detail  in  the  hearings
on  this  case,  not  only  shows  that  respondent’s  en-
terprise  is  characteristic  of  the  industry,  but  also
sheds  some  additiona!  light  on  the  stream  or  flow
of  commerce  within  which  respondent  is situated.
In  1934  the  blast  furnaces  in  the  steel  industry
utilized  nearly  49,000,000  gross  tons  of  mate-
rials  (Bd.  Exh.  29).  These  materials  moved  in
interstate  commerce  in  a  definite  pattern.  Thus
the  sources  of  iron  ore  are  concentrated  in  the
Lake  Superior  region,  and  about  85  per  cent  of
all iron ore used  in  1934  came  from  the  mines  of
*  The  attached  map  illustrates  not  only  the  nation-wide

of respondent’s  materials  and  products.

two  States,  Minnesota  and  Michigan  (R.  572;  Bd.

this  ore  own  and  operate  ore  mines,  special
lake  carriers,”  and  the  ore  loading  and  unloading
Similarly,  all
‘equipment  at  the  docks  (R.  575).
of  the  important  steel  producing  States,  with  the
exception  of  Pennsylvania  and  Alabama,  are  de-
pendent  upon  other  States  for  their  supply  of cok-
ing coal, and  even  Pennsylvania,  with  its large  coal
deposits,  imported  one-fifth  of its coking  coal  from
other  States  (Bd.  Exh.  78).  Limestone  is  more
widely  deposited  than  suitable  ore  and  coal,  but
here  again  there  is a  large  interstate  movement  to
the  steel  plants,  as  in  the  case  of  the  respondent
(supra,  p. 54).

|

|

The  steel  plants  to  which  all  these  essential
materials  are  carried  are,  for  the  most  part,  con-
centrated  in  four  States,  Illinois,  Indiana,  Ohio,
and  Pennsylvania,  in the  path  of commerce  which
brings  the  ore  from  the  upper  lake  region  to meet
the  coking  coal  from  the  Pittsburgh  area  (Bd.
Exhs.  74,78).  With  few  exceptions  the  plants  are
at the junction  of cheap water  and rail transporta-
tion  facilities  either  along the  Ohio  River  system
or  on  the  southern  shores  of  Lake  Michigan  and

“The  integration  of  this  Great  Lakes  transportation
with  the  steel  industry  as  a  whole  is affirmed  not  only  by
the  facts  with  respect  to this respondent  (supra,  p.  54),  but
See  U.  S.  Senate,
also  by  the  Lake  carriers  themselves.

74th  Cong.,  Ist  Sess., Part  4, pp.  558-560.

Lake  Erie  (R.  576-577).  Over  a  period  of years
there  has been  a  considerable  movement  of  steel
plant  capacity  away  from  the  Pittsburgh  area  to
the  Lake  shores,  but,  significantly,  this  movement
has  not  been  out  of  this  path  of  commerce  but
rather along it in the  direction  of the  ore  reserves
and steel  markets  (R. 578; Bd.  Exh.  74).

In recent  years  the  steel  industry  has  been  char-
acterized  by  continuous  process  production  and

thus exhibits  (1) an enormous
and  regular  movement  of  materials  in  interstate

shipment  without  storage  of semi-finished  and  fin-

A  factor  which  renders

this pattern  of commerce
peculiarly  susceptible  to  disruption  by labor  dis-
putes  is that  control  is concentrated  in  large  and
completely  integrated  units.  In 1934  two corpora-
tions,  United  States  Steel  and  Bethlehem  Steel,
accounted  for  more  than  1 per  cent  of the  indus-
try’s  ingot  capacity.  Less  than  a  dozen  corpora-
tions control more than 80 per cent of the capacity
Bd. Exh 84).  This
of the industry  (R. 593-594,
tendency  toward  large  units  and the  merger  of
control  is not accidental,  but arises  from
corporate
the nature  of  the industry  and  its integration  of
the  successive  stages  of  production,  receipt  and
distribution  of raw materials  and finished  products

-  (R. 574-575

; Bd. Exhs. 73, 84).
limitation

This practical

of units
on the number
in  the  indugtry  necessarily  gives  greater  signif-
jieance  to  industrial  disputes therein.  Labor  dis-
putes  in an  industry  consisting  of  small,  auton-
omous,  conipeting  units, may be met by a  diversion

_

of  production  and commerce  from one unit to an-
other.  When,  however,  an industry  is character-
ized by a  few corporations  and each controls a large
part  of a vital  pattern  of commerce,  disruption  of
any one 0. these units necessarily  burdens  a  sub-
stantial  movement  of interstate  commerce.  If, in
addition,  the employers’  association  of the industry
speaks  for  the industry  on  important  matters  of
labor  policy,  as  the Iron and  Stee!  Institute  does
(Bd.  Exh.  44),  unfair  labor  practices  may  well
precipitate  industry-wide  strife  which  would  dis-
rupt  the  commerce  of  the  entire  industry  and  of
industries  dependent  upon  it.  See  supra,  pp.
21-23.

We  submit  that in  the light of these  facts,  indus-
trial  strife  in  respondent’s  Aliquippa  plant  would
have  the  necessary  effect  of  burdening  and  ob-
structing  interstate  commerce  even  if the  limits  of
the  ‘‘necessary  effect’’  principle  be  narrowed  as
suggested  in  the  first  definition  suggested  above
(p. 51, supra), t. e., industrial strife  which  occurs in
‘a well-defined  stream  or  flow  of  commerce.  And
ial this  very  limited  definition  of  *“*necessary
effect  of  burdening  or  obstructing  interstate  com-
merce’’  is not  too  broad.

In enterprises  within  a well-defined  stream  there
is  an  ever-present  likelihood  that“any  industrial
dispute  will  choke  off the  stream  completely,  or  at
least  seriously  decrease  its volume  or  force  it into  |
In such
different  and more  burdensome  channels.

enterprises,  at  least,  the  Court  has  already  sanc-
tioned  the  application  of  preventive  legislation,
even  where  the  activities  and  practices  burdened
commerce  only  through  the  operation  of  indirect
price  relationships.
Chicago  Board  of  Trade  Vv.
Olsen,  262  U.S.  1.  See  also  Stafford  v.  Wallace,
258  U.S.  495;  Tagg  Bros.  d&  Moorhead  vy.  United
States,  280  U.  S. 420.  A  fortiori,  such  preventive
legislation  may  properly  be  applied  to  these  enter-
prises  where  its  purpose  is  to  prevent  the  direct
physical  obstruction  to  the  stream  of  commerce
caused  by industrial  strife.

interstate.

enterprise’  involving

Respondent  is  engaged  in  a  highly  integrated
of
-  sources  of  supply,  interstate  transportation  facili-
ties,  manufacturing  plants,  and  outlets  for  distri-
bution.  This  enterprise  is dependent  upon  a  great
movement  of  iron  ore,  coal  and  limestone  along
well-defined  paths  to the  steel  mills,  thence  through

ownership

them,  and  thence,  in  the  form  of  steel  products,
into  the  consuming  centers  of the  country—a  “‘defi-
nite  and  well  understood  course  of  business.’’
Stafford  v.  Wallace,  258  U.  S. 495,  516.  Industrial
strife  in  the  focal  point  of  this  stream—the  Ali-
quippa  plant—would  cripple  the  entire  movement
of  this  great  stream  or  flow  of  commerce.

|

It  may  be  objected,  however,  that respondent’s
enterprise  cannot  be  in  a  “‘stream  or  flow  of  com-
merce’’  because  respondent  engages  in  manufac-
turing,  the  raw  materials  being  held  within  the
plant  for  a  time  and  then  sent  out  in  the  form  of

67
pig  iron  or  steel.  The  objection  is  in direct  con-
flict  with  the  decision  of this  Court  in  Stafford  Vv.
Wallace,  258  U.  8. 495.

The  Packers  and  Stockyards  Act,  ae valid  in
that  case,  defines  the  current  of commerce  there  in-
volved  as  including  purchase  or  sale  of  livestock
for  slaughter  within  the  State  and  shipments  out-
side  the  State  of the  products  resulting  from  such
slaughter.  Act  of  August  19, 1921,  c.  64, 42  Stat.
159,  Sec.  2; U.S.  C., Title  7, See.  183.  And  in the
Stafford  case,  supra,  the  Court  said  (p. 514):

The  object  to  be  secured  by the  act  is the
free  and  unburdened  flow  of live  stock  from
the  ranges  and  farms  of  the  West  and
the  Southwest  through  the  great  stockyards
and  slaughtering  centers  on  the  borders  of
that  region,  and  thence  in the  form  of meat
products  to  the  consuming  cities  of  the
country  in  the  Middle  West  and  Kast,  or,
still  as  live  stock,  to  the  feeding  places  and
fattening  farms  in  the  Middle  West  or  East
for  further  preparation  for  the  market.
The  Court  thus  recognized  that  stoppage  for pur-
poses  of processing  in the  packing  plant,  involving
a  definite  interruption  in  the  physical  movement
and  a very  distinct  transformation  in the  nature  of
the  commodity,  did  not  cause  a  break  in  the
“stream”?  or  ‘current’?  of commerce  in the consti-
tutional  sense.  See  also  Chicago  Board  of Trade  v.
Olsen,  262  U.  8S.  1.

Moreover,  operations  in the meat  packing  plants
are  as  essentially  manufacturing  as  are  the  opera-

68

tions  at  Aliquippa.  It  is common  knowledge  that
meat  packing  involves  a very  extensive  transforma-
tion  in  the  nature  of  the commodity ; this  is fully
brought  out in the authorities  upon  which  the Court
relied  in the  Stafford  case.”  There,  as  here,  the  es-
sential  purpose  of  the  processing  operation  is not
merely  to  facilitate  the  flow  of  commerce,  but  also
to halt  it and  to transform  the  commodities  in ques-
tion  into  things considerably  different,  by manufac-
turing  or processing  operations  of varying  degrees
of  complexity.”  Indeed,  due  to  the  seasonal  char-
acter  of the  supply  of raw  materials  and  the  stable
demand  for meat  products,  with  a consequent  neces-
‘sity for much  storage,  and  to the  necessary  slowness
of many  of the  operations  in  the  meat-packing  in-
dustry,  such  as  chilling,  curing  and  smoking,  the
delays  or  stoppages  of the  flow  of  products  in  that

.

It
performs  the  indispensable  function  of  converting  raw  ma-
terials  into  finished  products  and  then  distributing  these
products  to  thousands  of  retail  outlets  all  over  the  country
*  *.~In  most  industries  the  manufacturer  prepares  or
*
buys  his  raw  material
adds  a  certain  amount  of
*
labor  and  other  expenses,  and  finally  assembles  his  raw  ma-
*
terials  into  a  finished  product.  The  meat  packer
does  exactly  the  opposite.  He  takes  one  unit  of  raw  ma-
terial,  a  live  animal,  and  converts  it  into  a  great  many
products  differing  widely  in  character  and  marketability.”
Swift  and  Company,  Year  Book,  1935,  pp.  15-16.

*

*

*

industry  are  frequently  much  longer  than  those  in
the  operations  of  respondent  (R.  118).

In describing  the  total  effect  of the  flow  of live-
stock  and meat  products  from  West to East, though
interrupted  at the packing plants in the manner  de-
scribed  above,  this  Court  said  in  Stafford  v.  Wal-
lace,  258  U.  8. 495,  518-519:

The  application  of  the  commerce  clause
of the Constitution  in the  Swift  case  was  the
result  of  the  natural  development  of  inter-
state  commerce  under  modern  conditions.
It was  the  inevitable  recognition  of the great
c@ptral  fact  that  such  streams  of  commerce
from  one  part  of  the  country  to  another
which  are  ever  flowing  are  in  their  very  es-
sence  the  commerce  among  the  States  and
with  foreign  nations  which  historically  it
was  one  of  the  chief  purposes  of  the  Con-
stitution  to  bring  under  national  protection
and  control.

This  language  is equally  applicable  to respondent’s
’  vast  interstate  enterprise  already  described,  and  to
the  steel  industry  as  a  whole,  involving  a  great
movement  of  iron  ore,  coal,  and  limestone  along
well-defined  paths  through  the  steel  mills  and
“thence  in  the  form  of  steel  products  to  the .
consuming  centers  of  the  country—‘‘a  definite
and  well-understood  course  of business’’.  258 U.8.
The  relationship  of  industrial  strife
at  516.
at  the  respondent’s  Aliquippa  plant  to  re
spondent’s  entire  interstate  enterprise  is  no  less
direct  than  that  of  local  practices  in  the  live-

~~ f  —

70

stock  industry  at  the  point  where  the  commodities
are  halted,  become  part  of  the  mass  of  property
within  the  State  (Minnesota  v.  Blasius,  290  U.  8.
1) and  go through  extensive  processing  operations.
Nor  can  it be  said  that  the  packers  and  stock-
yards  illustration  is  peculiar  because  of  the  great
public  interest  involved  in the  free  and  unburdened
distribution  of  meat  products  in  interstate  com-  |
merce,  for  this  alone  does  not  create  constitutional
power  where  it does  not  otherwise  exist.
If  such
considerations  were  relevant,  it  could  readily  be
demonstrated  that  uninterrupted  commerce  in  the
steel  industry,  of  which  respondent’s  enterprise
forms  one  of the  large  units,  is as  important  to  our
national  commercial  and  economic  life  as  is  com-
merce  in meat  products.

However,  even  aside  from  the  stream  or  flow
aspect  of  the  respondent’s  enterprise,  we  contend
that  industrial  strife  in  respondent’s  enterprise
would  have  the  necessary  effect  of  burdening  and
obstructing  interstate  commerce.
If  ‘‘necessary
effect’’  be defined  as  suggested  in  (2), supra,  p.  51,
i. é.,  industrial  strife  which  has  the  effect  of  pre-
venting  or  disrupting  a  substantial  proportion  of
all  the  interstate  commerce  in  a  particular  com-
modity,  it  is  clear  that  respondent  is  within  this
second  suggested  definition.

This  definition  is  supported  by  the  considered
statements  in  United  Mine  Workers  v.  Coronado
Coal  Co.,  259  U.  8S.  344;  Industal  Associa-
tion  v.  United  States,  268  U.  S.  64;  and  Interna-

71
tional  Organization  v.  Red  Jacket  C. C. & C. Co.,
18 F.  (2d)  839  (C. C. A. 4th),  which  are  discussed
above  at  pp.  44-48.  Moreover,  the  extent  of  the
actual  burden  and  disruption  of  interstate  com-
merce  from  strife  in  an  enterprise  such  as  re-
spondent’s,  even  if its  location  in a stream  of -om-
merce  be ignored,  shows  the  realism  of such a defi-
nition.  A  strike  or  lockout  in  respondent’s  plant
would  mean  the  complete  cessation  of  business,
Materials
not  merely  the  cessation  of production.
in  the  amount  of  more  than  200  freight  cars  per
day  would  cease  coming  into  the  plant;  the  busi-
ness  of  the  Pittsburgh  &  Lake  Erie  Railroad  at
Aliquippa  would  be  curtailed  97  percent;  the  re-
spondent’s  enormous  lake  and  river  traffic  in ore,
coal  and  steel  products  would  come  to  a  halt.  The
functional  coherence  of the  entire  enterprise  would
be  destroyed.  The  dispute  might  involve  a  num-
ber  of  plants  or  even  the  whole  industry.  Em-
ployees  not  engaged  in  production  would  be  ‘¥
to become  active  participants  through  sympathetic
action.  Markets  would  be disrupted.  No  business
Orders would  be eur-
commitments  could  be made.
tailed,  and  if given could  not  be filled.  Movements
and  trading  would  stop.  The  effect  upon  com-
merce  would  be direct,  immediate,  and  substantial.
Therefore,  the evidence  in this  case  warrants  the
conclusion  that  there  is a reasonable  likelihood  that
industrial  strife  in  respondent’s  enterprise  would,
if it oceurred,  have  the  necessary  effect  of substan-
tially  burdening  interstate’  commerce.  Accord-

72

we submit,  the Board was justified  in holding
ingly,
that  the  preventive  measures  of  the  National
Labor.  Relations  Act  might  be  invoked  against
respondent  in  order  té anticipate  industrial  strife
affecting  commerce  which  would  be  within  the
‘control  power  of  Congress.

(3)  The  Power  of  Congress  with  Respect  to  Constantly
Recurrent  Industrial  Strife  which  Is  a  Burden  upon
Interstate  Commerce

This  Court  has  on  many  occasions  enunciated
the  principle  that  where  Congress,  after  investiga-
tion, directs  its legislation  to the  prevention  of cer-
tain  activities,  even  though  usually  only  of  local
concern,  which  recur  with  such  frequency  as  to
constitute  an  undue  burden  on  commerce,  those
activities  may  be subjected  to the  control  power  of
Congress.

This  principle  is most  forcibly  illustrated  by the
historical  setting  of  the  Packers  and  Stockyards
Act.  At  first,  where  attempts  were  made  to  bring
individual  practices  within  the  scope  of the Sher-
man  Act,  the  trade  practices  of  dealers  and  live-
stock  men  were  held  to affect  interstate  commerce
only  indirectly.  Hopkins  v.  United  States,  171
U.  S.  578;  Anderson  v.  United  States,  171  U.  5S.
604.  Later, however,  an  injunction  under  the Sher-
man  Act  was  sustained  where  directed  against  ac-
tivities  of  a  combination  of  packers  which  fixed
and  manipulated  prices  and  commission  rates  at

73

te  stockyards  and  engaged  in other  practices  in  “
interstate  rates  and transportation  proper.  Swift
& Co. v.  United  States,  196 U. 8. 375.

Thereafter,  in  1921,  a  preventive  statute  was
passed—the  Packers  and  Stockyards  Act  (Act  of
August  15, 1921,  c.  64, 42  Stat.  159;  U.  8. C., Title
7,Ch.9).  That  Act regulates  the rates  of commis-
sions of  market  agencies  and  various  unfair  dis-  .
eriminatory  or  deceptive  practices  of such  agencies
and  packers  ‘‘in  commerce”’  (Section  202).  Staf-
ford  v.  Wallace,  258  U.  S. 495, presented  the  ques-
tion  of  the  application  of  the  Act,  not  to  the
packers,  but  to  the  commission  men  and  dealers
in  the  stockyards.  Arguing  from  the  Hopkins
and  Anderson  cases,  supra,  these  dealers  and
commission  men  urged  that  their  activities  af-
fected  interstate  commerce  only  ‘‘indirectly”’,  that  —
the  Act  sought  to  obliterate  the  line  between  the
powers  of  the  States  and  those  of  Congress,  and
that  if it were  sustained,  there  would  be no  limit  to
the  scope  of the  power  of Congress  under  the  com-
merce  clause.  See  258  U.S.  at 503-504.  In reject-
ing  these  arguments,  the  Court  pointed  out  that
Congress had  found,  on  the basis of long experience
and  investigation,  that  the local  acts  of commission
men  and  dealers ‘‘constitute  a direct and undue bur-
den”’  on  interstate  commerce  (Jd.  at  521)  which
worked  an  ‘‘injurious  direct  effect’?  (Id.  at  525).
In  conclusion  the  Court  said  (pp.  520-521) :

The  reasonable  fear  by  Congress  that  such
acts,  usually lawful  and  affecting  only intra-

74

state  commerce  when  considered  alone,  will
probably  and more  or less constantly  be used
in conspiracies  against  interstate  commerce
or  constitute  a  direct  and  undue  burden
on  it,  expressed  in  this  remedial  legisla-
tion,  serves  the same  purpose  as  the  intent
charged  in  the  Swift.  indictment  to  bring
acts  of a  similar  character  into  the  current
of interstate  commerce  for  federal  restraint.
Whatever  amounts  to more-or  less  constant
practice,  and  threatens  to  obstruct  or  un-
duly  to  burden  the  freedom  of interstate
ecommerce  is within  the regulatory  power  of
Congress  under  the  commerce  clause,  and  it
is  primarily  for  Congress  to  consider  and
decide  the  fact of the danger  and  meet  it.
The  result  is  illustrated  graphically  by  other
cases  under  the same  statute,  as  compared  with  the
earlier  decisions  under  the  Sherman  Act.  Thus
the  Packers  and  Stockyards  Act  has  been  applied
to the  conduct  of a boycott  by a  dealers’  exchange
association  (United  States  v.  American  Livestock
Association  Co., 279  U.  8. 435)  and  rates  of com-
mission  for  brokers  in  the  stockyards  have  been
fixed  thereunder  (Tagg  Bros.  &  Moorhead  v.
United  States,  280  U.  S.  420)  without  regard  to
Yet  the
intent  to  affect  interstate  commerce.
application  of  the  Sherman  Act  in  like  circum-
stances  had  previously  been  denied  in  the  Ander-
son  and  Hopkins  cases.

The  principle  that  Congress  may  legislate  with
respect to recurring  practices  burdening  interstate
_  commerce  also  finds  expression  in  the  legislation

75
relating  to grain  exchanges.  In Chicago  Board  of
Trade  v.  Olsen,  262 U. 8. 1, the Court sustained  the
validity  of the Grain  Futures  Act in its application
to  trading  in  futures  which,  as  the  Court  made
abundantly  clear,  was  not  in itself  interstate  com-
Again,  as  in  Stafford  v.  Wallace,  supra,
merce.
the  Court  pointed  to  the  dangers  which  were  rea-
sonably  to  be  expected  from  speculation  in  grain
futures,  and  concluded  (p. 40):

:

By  reason  and  authority,  therefore,  in  de-
termining  the  validity  of  this  act,  we  are
prevented  from  questioning  the  conclusion
of  Congress  that manipulation  of the market
for  futures  on  the  Chicago  Board  of Trade
may,  and  from  time  to  time  does,  directly
burden  and  obstruct  commerce  between  the
States  in  grain,  and  that  it recurs  and  is a
constantly  possible  danger.  For  this  rea-  .
son,  Congress  has  the  power  to provide  the
appropriate  means  adopted  in  this  act  by
which  this  abuse  may  be  restrained  and
avoided.

In  the  Olsen  case,  as  in Stafford.  v. Wallace,  the
Court  relied  upon  a  decision  upholding  the  appli-
eation  of  the  Sherman  Act  in  the  same  field. .
United  States  v.  Patten,  226  U.  S. 525.  However,
other  prior  decisions  of the  Court,  such  as  Ware &
Leland  v.  Mobile  County,  209  U.  S.  405;  United
States  v.  Patten,  226  U.  S.  525,  544,  and  Untted
Mine  Workers  v.  Coronado  Coal  Co.,  259  U.  8.
344. 410,  show  that  in both  the Packers  and  Stock-
yards  Act  and  the  Grain  Futures  Act  Congress

76

hds  established  and  this  Court  has approved  a  —
-  geope  of jurisdiction  wider  than  that  required  for
the purposes
of the  Sherman  Act  and  more  ap-
propriately  designed  to  achieve  the  Congressional
purpose.”

|

Neither the Stafford  nor  the  Olsen  case  can  be
distinguished
on the ground  that  the evil  thus
aimed  at  was  monopoly,  and  that  Congress  does
not  possess  the  same  power  with  respect  to  other
evils  which  constitutes  obstructions  or  injury  to
interstate  commerce.  In summarizing  the  holding
of these  cases  in  United  Leather  Workers  v.  Her-
kert  & Meisel  Trunk  Co., 265  U.  8. 457,  the  Court
said  (p. 469) :

What  those  cases  decided  was  that  when
Congress  found  from  investigation  that
more  or  less  constant  ‘abusive  practices  and
a  course  of  business,  usually  only  within
state  police  cognizance,  threatened  to  ob-
struct  or  unduly  to  burden  the  freedom  of
interstate  commerce,  it could by law institute
supervision of  such  course  of  business  in
order  to  prevent  the  abuses  having  such
effect.

The  Stafford  and  Olsen  cases  show,  therefore,
that  Congress  may  legislate  with  respect  to  bur-

*  To the same  effect are the Federal  Trade  Commission  Act

(Federal
U.  S. 304,  810;  Federal

U.  S.  491,  427-429,  Brandeis,  J., dissenting),  and  various
seetions
of the Clayton  Act  (Standard  Fashion Co. v.  Ma-

77

dens  or  obstructions  to commerce,  even  where  the
burdens  develop  out  of local  practices,  where those
practices  and  burdens  are  reasonably  found  .con-
stantly  to recur.  See also  Tezas  & New’ Orleans  RB.
Co.  v.  Brotherhood  of Railway  Clerks,  281  U.8.
548.

It is, of  course,  obvious  that  this  principle  ean-
not be applied  to  all  recurrent  activity  whieh  is of
only  local  concern.
Such  an  application  of  the
principle  would  undoubtedly  conflict  with  our  dual
system  of  government.  Hence  it  is  neceadary  to
establish  that  the  injurious  activities, considered
individually,  have  a  close  and  substantial  relation  —
to  commerce.

At  the  outset,  it is to  be  noted  that  the  fact  that
the  injury  actually  arises  from  local  activities  is
of no  moment.
This  was  true  in the  Stafford  and
Olsen  cases,  and  in  Texas  & New  Orleans  Ry. Co.
v.  Brotherhood  of Railway  Clerks,  281  U.  8.  548.
And  it  is  well  established  as  a  general  principle
that  Congress  may  remove  burdens  and  obstruc-
tions  to  interstate  commerce  whatever  the  source
of  those  injuries  may  be.  Schechter  Corp.  v.
United  States,  295  U.  8.  495,  544;  United  States
v.  Ferger,  250  U. 8.  199.

For  the  purposes  of  the  present  case  it  is un-
necessary  to decide  how  close  this  relationship  be-
tween  commerce  and  the  individual  activity  must
be, for  it is abundantly  clear  that  industrial  strife
in  what  may  be  called  an  interstate  enterprise—
that  is, an  enterprise  which  receives  a  substantial  ©

115696—37-—_-6

part  of  its  raw  materials  from,  and  disposes  of  a
substantial  part  of  its  finished  products  in,  inter-
state  commerce—bears  a  close  relationship  to  in-
terstate  commerce.  Industrial  strife  in such  enter-
prises  bears  this  relation  to  interstate  commerce
because  it effectuates  an  actual  interruption  of com-
merce  without  ‘‘an  efficient  intervening  agency  or
eondition.’?  Carter  v.  Carter  Coal  Co.,  298  U.  8.
It  does  not,  in  the  words  of  the

Court,  the  observations  of  impartial  observers  on
‘the  effects  of  such  strife  in  these  enterprises  in
‘the  past,  and  the  findings  of  Congress  in  the
present  Act  are  in accord  on  this  conclusion.

This  close  relationship  is  demonstrated  by  the
eases  in  which  the  Sherman  Act  has  been  held  ap-
plicable  to boycotts  and strikes  called  or  conducted

trated  by Texas  & New  Orleans  R. Co.  v.  Brother-

which  affect  that  commerce.  We  believe  that  in

In the  first  place,  it is obvious  that the  validity
of the  Railway  Labor  Act  does  not  depend  on  the
fact  that  the  employees  with  whom  it is concerned
Actually,

v.  Ferger,  250  U. 8. 199;  Schechter  Corp.  v.  United

The  Tezas  case  can  be  distinguished,  then,  only
upon  a  showing  that  industrial  strife  on  the
agencies  of  transportation  can  reasonably  be  ex-
pected  to be more  injurious  to interstate  commerce
than  industrial  strife  in  an  interstate  enterprise.
We  believe  that  no  such  showing  can  be made.  A
simple  illustration  will  make  the  point  clear.  The
Pittsburgh  & Lake  Erie  Railroad  derives  97  per-
cent  of its  shipments  to  and  from  Aliquippa  from
respondent  (R. 133).  An industrial  dispute  on  that
railroad  could  be  dealt  with  under  the  commerce
power  not  merely  because  the  employees  involved
would  be  employed  by  an  interstate  carrier,  but
because  such a dispute  would  obstruct  the shipment
of materials  and  products  received  and  shipped  by
Obviously,  however,  an  industrial
respondent.
dispute  at respondent’s  Aliquippa  plant  would  also
obstruct  the  receipt  and shipment  of  respondent’s
materials  and  products  not  only  over  the  Pitts-
burgh  & Lake  Erie,  but  also  over  every  other  rail-  »
road  and  every  other  type  of  carrier  which
transports  articles  to and  from  respondent’s  plant.
Indeed,  it is true  that  a  strike  of the  employees  on
the  Pittsburgh  & Lake  Erie  might  result  in only  a
very.  slight  obstruction  to  interstate  commerce  if
respondent  could  arrange  to  receive  its  materials
and  to  ship  its  product  by  water,  motor  lines,  or
other  carriers,  while  a  strike  among  the  employeés

82

of Jones  & Laughlin  at Aliquippa  would  completely
stop  all  interstate  commerce  into  and  out  of  that
plant.

|

The  foregoing  cases,  we  believe,  prove  conclu-
sively  that  the  relation  between  industrial  strife
and  interstate  commerce  is  immediate  and  sub-
stantial,  and  consequently  within  the  principle
above  described.

This  Court,  indeed,  has  already  stated  that  the
power  to  subject  recurrent  local  activities  to  na-
tional  supervision  and  restraint  applies  to  recur-
rent  industrial  strife  arising  out  of  local  activity.
In United  Mine  Workers  v.  Coronado  Coal  Co.,  259
U. S. 344, it was  said  (p. 408)  :

Obstruction  to  coal  mining  is  not  a  direct
obstruction  to  interstate  commerce  in  coal,
although  it, of  course,  may  affect  it  by  re-
ducing  the  amount  of  coal  to  be  carried  in
that  commerce.  We  have  had  occasion  to
consider  the  principles  governing  the  va-
lidity  of  congressional  restraint  of  such
indirect  obstructions  to interstate  commerce
in  Swift  &  Co.  v.  United  States,  196  U.  8.
375;  United  States  v.  Patten,  226  U.S.  520;
United  States  v.  Ferger,  250  U.  8S.  199;
Railroad  Commission  of Wisconsin  v.  Chi-
cago,  Burlington  &  Quincy  R.  R.  Co.,  257
U. S. 563; and  Stafford  v.  Wallace,  258  U.S.
495.  It is clear  from  these  cases  that  if Con-
gress  deems  certain  recurring  practices,
though  not  really  part  of  interstate  com-
merce,  likely  to.obstruct,  restrain,  or  burden

NX

83

it,  it  has  the  power  to  subject  them  to  na-
ronal  supervision  and  restraint.  Again,  it
has  the  power  to  punish  conspiracies  in
which  such  practices  are  part  of  the  plan,
to  hinder,  restrain,  or  monopolize  interstate
commerce.  But  in the  latter  case  the  intent
to  injure,  obstruct,  or  restrain  interstate
commerce  must  appear  as  an  obvious  conse-
quence  of what  is. to be done,  or  be shown  by
direct  evidence  or  other  circumstances.”
The  relationship  to  interstate  commerce  of  in-
dustrial  strife  recognized  in  these  decisions  of  the
Court  may  be  said  to  be  a  matter  of  common
The  brief  survey  of  the  history  of
knowledge.
industrial  strife  in  this  country  set  forth  above
(supra,  pp.  21-31),  shows  the  disastrous  conse-
quences  to interstate  commerce  of such  strife.  The
survey  also  demonstrates  the  justification  for  the
view  of Congress  that  this  is a constantly  recurring
consequence  of  industrial  strife.

Congress  had  before  it, therefore,  when  it  was
ecasidering  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  an
extensive  background  upon  which  to  base its  legis-
lation,  all of which  had been  brought  before  its com-
mittees  during  hearings  extending  over  a period  of

‘tt As  the  context  shows,  the  obstructions  in  question  were
referred  to  as “indirect”  only  in the  sense  that  they had  first
impact  upon  local  activity.  The Court  here makes  clear,  and
Stafford  v.  Wallace  holds,  that  such  practices  when  found  to
be recurring  and  likely  to  obstruct,  restrain.or  burden  inter-
state  commerce  may  be subjected  to  national  supervision  and
restraint.

84

two  years.”  Its  finding,  therefore,  of  the  burden
of  industrial  strife,  is  peculiarly  deserving  of
weight.  Section  1 of the  Act  states:

The  denial  by  employers  of  the  right  of
employees  to  organize  and  the  refusal  by
employers  to  accept  the  procedure  of  col-
lective  bargaining  lead  to  strikes  and
other  forms  of  industrial  strife  or  unrest,
which  have  the  intent  or  the  necessary  ef-
fect  of  burdening  or  obstructing  com-
merece.

*

*

*

7

*

—

he

ee

Experience  has  proved  that  protection  by
law  of  the  right  of  employees  to  organize
and  batgain  collectively  safeguards  com-
merce  from  injury, impairment,  or interrup-
tion,  and  promotes  the  flow  of commerce  by
removing  certain  recognized  sources  of  in-
dustrial  strife  and  unrest

*.  —

*

*

Viewed  in the light of the facts which  Congress  had
before  it, this  finding  cannot  be held  unreasonable,

There  can  be no  doubt  that  if there exists, .as we
contend,  this control  power  of Congress  over  recur-
rent  industrial  strife  in  interstate  enterprises,  the
order  of the-Board  in this  case  was  within  its juris-
Respondent’s  operations  have  been  sum-
diction.
marized
(supra,  pp.  56),
in  the  statement
and  are  set  out  in  great  detail  at  pp.  51-61,
supra,  but  for  present  purposes  it  is  sufficient  to
point  out  that  respondent  is dependent  upon  three
basie  materials,  iron  ore,  coal  and  limestone  (R.
Its  iron  is  obtained  from  iron  reserves,
82,  83).
which  it owns,  in  States  other  than  Pennsylvania,
chiefly  in the Lake  Superior  region  (R.98).  The
coal,  it derives  chiefly  from  Pennsylvania  (R. 84—-
85).  The limestone  is obtained  from  its properties
in  West  Virginia  (R.  85,  107).  In  percentages,
the  record  shows  that  about  80  percent  by value
of  the  materials.  received  at  the  Aliquippa  plant
originates  outside  the  State  of  Pennsylvania,  and
75 percent  of the product  of the plant  is shipped  in
interstate  commerce.

With  respect  to the  distribution  of its  iron  and
steel  products,  it  is  obvious  that  its  market  is  a
national  one.  The  record  shows  that  respondent
maintains  large  warehouses  at  various  points
'  throughout  the  country,  from  which  it markets  a
“substantial  part”  of its  products  (Bd.  Exh.  4).

86
Respondent  itself  states  that  its  products  are  sold
to its customers  throughout  the  United  States  and
many  foreign  countries,  principally  through  its
own  sales  offices  located  in  twenty  of  the  larger
cities  of the country.  Its sales  in Canada  are  mar-
keted  through  its subsidiary  the  Jones and  Laugh-
lin  Steel  Products  Company  with  its  offices  in
Pittsburgh  and  Toronto,  Canada  (Bd.  Exh.  4).

We  submit,  therefore,  that  industrial  strife  in
respondent’s  enterprise  is of the  character  which,
by its recurrence,  comes  within  the control  of Con-
gress.  Consequently,  the  Board  was  justified  in
holding  that  the  preventive  measures  of  the  Na-
tional  Labor  Relations  Act  might  be  invoked
against respondent  in order  to anticipate  industrial
strife  affecting  commerce  which  would  be  within
the control  power  of Congress.
THE  CARTER  AND  SCHECHTER  CASES  ARE  NOT  CONTRARY
pote THE  CONCLUSION  OF  THE  BOARD-IN  THE  INSTANT

~

We  have  already,  in  dealing  with  various
points  in  the  argument  above  presented,  referred
to  the  decisions  in  Carter  v.  Carter  Coal  Co., 298
U. 8. 238, and  in Schechter  Corp.  V.  United  States,
995  U. 8. 495:  However,  the  reliance  of the  court
below  on  those cases  justifies  treating them  in an
extended  fashion.  —

The  distinction  between  this  case  and  the
Schechter  and  Carter  cases  goes  to  &  funda-
mental  distinction  in  the  scope  and  purpose  of
the  respective  statutes  involved.  The  statutes  in

|

87

those  two  cases  regulated  basic  manufactur-
ing  or  producing  faetors—wages  and  hours—
in order  to  stabilize  labor  costs  throughout  a par-
ticular  industry,  or  industry  generally.  Legal
justification  adduced  in support  of the  regulations
was  based  upon  the  intricate  econdmic  effects  of
disparate  labor  costs  upon  the  prices  and  move-
ment  of the product  in interstate  commerce.

|

In the  Schechter  case  the Government  attempted
to  sustain  the  validity  of  the  regulation  of wages
and  hours  in the  business  of slaughtering  poultry,
by showing  that  wages  and  hours  bore a relation
to  interstate  commerce  through  a  chain  of  eco-
nomic  causes and effects—the  effect  upon  the price  .
of  chickens  sold  in  local  commerce  which  in  turn
New  York
affected  the  shipment  of  chickens  into
and  other  States.  Similar  effects  could  be  traced
to  numerous  other  forms  of  activity,  local  in  na-
ture,  and  the  regulation  of wages.and  hours under  .
‘such  circumstances  could  not  have  been  upheld
without  also imposing  regulation  of virtually  every
other  factor  in the  operation  of the business  which
entered  into the price of chickens  sold in New  York
trade.  The  contrast  is marked  between  that stat-
ute  and  the  present  Act,  which  does  not  depend
for  its  validity  upon  any  such  chain  of  economic
causes  and  effects.  The  practices  to  which  it ap-
plies are‘brought  under  regulation  in order  to pre-
vent  the  immediate  injury  to  interstate  commerce
which  results  from  industrial  strife.

~

The  Bituminous  Coal  Conservation  Act  in-
volved  in the  Carter  case  was  enacted  for  the  pur-
pose  of  ‘‘stabilizing’’  the  bituminous  coal]  mining
industry  through  a  regulation  of prices  and  wages
by means  of a coercive  tax.  All  producers  of  coal
in  the  United  States  were  to  be  brought  into  a
Code.  This  Code  set  up  machinery  for  the  fixing
of minimum  and  maximum  prices and the machin-
ery  for  the  determination  of minimum  wages  and
maximum  hours  of employment.  The  prices  were
to  be  established  by governmental  agencies.  The
wages  and  hours  were  to  be established  by agree-
ment  between  the  producers  and  representatives  of
the mine  workers  and were  to have  the force  of law.
Thus,  wage  agreements  negotiated  by  collective
bargaining  in any  district  or  districts  of more  than
two-thirds  of  the  annual  tonnage  and  representa-
tives of the majority  of the mine  workers  in district
or  districts  were  to be accepted  by all  operators  in
such  district  or  districts.

It  may  be  contended,  however,  that  the  Carter
case  is directly  determintive  of the present  case  be-
cause  the Act  there  involved  contained  a  provision
for  collective  bargaining  which  was  among  the
However,
labor  provisions  held  unconstitutional.
it is apparent  that  the  scheme  outlined  above  for
the  determination  of wages  and  hours  by means  of
collective  agreements  could  nat  operate  without
some  statutory  protection  for the right of collective
bargaining  of the  mine  workers.  Thus  the  collec-
tive bargaining  provisions,  instead  of being directly
related  to avoiding  labor  disputes  interfering  with

interstate  commerce,  were  subordinated  to the main
purpose  of stabilization  of the  industry  and  were
in fact  the means  through  which  one  of the stabiliz-
ing  factors—the  establishment  of  uniform  wages
and  hours—was  to be effected.”

It is further  to be noted  that  the labor  provisions
of  that  Act  were  applicable  to  every  produeer  of
bituminous  coal  and  every  employee  in  the  indus-
There  was  no
try  within  the  United  States.“

the  Carter  case.  Thus  on  page  199  of  that  brief  it  is
stated  that  “The  Bituminous  Coal  Conservation  Act  regu-
lates  labor  relations  only  because  and  in  so  far  as  they
constitute  an  integral  and  essential  element  in  the  competi-
Producers  deny  to  their  employees  the  right

of  collective  bargaining  and  cut  wage  rates  not  because  of

order  to gain  an  advantage  over  others  in the sale of coal  in

commerce.
in  order  to  prevent  them  from  gaining  such  an  advantage.
in selling  coal.”

:

The  entire  argument  of the  Government  on  the  labor  pro-
visions  of  the Act is made in accordance  with  this keynote.
The  fact  that the collective  bargaining  provisions  would
have  the  independent  effect  of  preventing  obstructions  and
stoppages  of  interstate  commerce  is  stressed  (p.  281),  but
a  reading  of  the entire  argument  makes  it clear that this
feature  was  considered  as  incidental  to  the  main  purpose  of
stabilization  of  wages  and  hours.

*  In  its  brief  in  the  Carter  case  the  Government  argued
that the price provisions  were limited to sales in or  affecting

with  activities  which  occur  under  circumstances

the flow  of  goods  had  ceased;  in  the  Carter  case

The  argument  advanced  herein  does  not  carry
the  implication  that  the  Federal  power  extends  to
the  fixing  of wages  on  the  ground  that  differences
between  employers  and  employees  over  wages  cause
industrial  strife.  Wage  regulation  mighf  well  be
thought  not  reasonably  calculated  to  eliminate  the
causes  of industrial  strife,  and  hence  not  to  bear  a
reasonable  relation  to  the  protection  of commerce.
Wage  fixing  would’ merely  transfer  the  power  to
determine  wages  from  the  private  parties  to  the
Government,  and  differences  would  still  exist  over
the  wages  which  should  be  paid  in  excess  of  the
True,  the  number  of  conflicts
fixed  minimum.
might be somewhat  reduced,  but  it seems  clear  that
they  would  still  be  numerous.  On  the  other  hand
_  protection  of  the  right  of  employee  organization

93

and  pollective- bargaining  has  been  proved  by  ex-
perience  to  be  calculated  to  eliminate  one  of  the
most  serious  causes  of  industrial  strife.  In  the
railroad  industry,  for  example,  the  enactment  of
parallel  legislation  has  virtually  eliminated  indus-
trial  strife.  See  Brief  for  the  Board  in  No.  365,
The  Associated  Press  v.  National  Labor  Relations
Board,  pp. 56-58.  Moreover,  this Court  has held in
the  Teras  & New  Orleans  case  that  such  legislation  _
is reasonably  related  to the protection  of commerce
and  does  not  go beyond  what  is necessary  for  that
This  statute  eliminates  entirely  the
purpose.
heavy  burden  on  interstate  commerce  due  to  in-
dustrial  strife  traceable  to  the  opposition  of  em-
ployer  to  the  recognized  right  of  employees  to
organize  and  bargain  collectively  through  repre-
sentatives  of their  own  choosing.  The  recognition
and  protection  of that  right  provide  the  oper door
to  the  reasonable-and  just settlement  of differences
with  respect  to grievances,  wages,  and  other  condi-
tions  of  work,  thereby  also  greatly  reducing  or
mitigating  industrial  strife  over  the  substantive
;
terms  of the  employment  contract.
The  Act  now  before  the  Court  is an  exercise  of
the  “‘adequate  authority”  of  Congress  ‘‘to  main-
tain  the  orderly  conduct  of  interstate  commerce
and  to  provide  for  the  peaceful  settlement  of dis-
putes  which  threaten  it.”  Here  Congress  has  not,
as  in  the  case  of  the  Bituminous  Coal  Act,  used
“this  protective  authority  as  a  pretext  for  the  ex-
ertion  of power  to  regulate  activities  and  relations
within  the  States  which  affect  interstate  commerce

v4

only  indirectly” and  it  does  not  go  ‘‘beyond  any
proper  measure  of  protection  of  interstate  com-
merce  and  attempts  a  broad  regulation  of industry
within  the  State.’’
Justice  Hughes in  Carter  v.  Carter  Coal  C i

CONCLUSION
Wherefore,  the  judgment  of  the  court  below
should  be  reversed,  and  the  cause  remanded  with
instructions  to grant the  petition  of the  Board  for
the  enforcement  of its  order.
Respectfully  submitted.

CORPORATION.

«  OPINION  BELOW.

The opinion  of  the United  States Circuit  Court of
Appeals  for the Fifth Circuit  (R. 975-977)  is reported
of the petitioner,
in 88 F.  2nd 998.  The  decision
National  Labor Relations  Board,  appears  in the record
at pages 23 to 45.

:

.

2

Statement  of the  Case.

JURISDICTION.

Appeals  for  the  Fifth  Circuit,
the  National  Labor  Relations  Board,

The  deeree  of the  United  States  Circuit  Court  of
the  petition  of
entered  on
-  June  15, 1936,  and on July  15,  1936,  the petition
of the
Board  for a rehearing  was
National  Labor  Relations
denied,  without  written  opinion.
The  petitioner  filed
its Petition  for Writ  of Certiorari  on  September  30, 1936,
which  was  granted  on  November  9, 1936.  This  Court’s
jurisdiction  is based  upon  Section  240  (a)  of the  Judicial
Code,  as  amended,  and  Section  10  (e)  of  the  National
Labor  Relations  Act.

~

STATUTE  INVOLVED.

The  National  Labor  Relations  Act  (Act  of  July  5,
1935,  C. 372,  49 Stat.  449;  U.  S. C.,  Sup.  I, Title  29,  Sec.
151  et seq.)  is set  forth  in the  appendix  to  the  petition-
er’s  brief  in  The  Associated  Press  v.  National  Labor
Relations  Board  (No.  365)  and  its various  provisions  are
summarized  at  pages  52  to  54,  infra.

?

~

STATEMENT  OF  THE  CASE.

Although  the  essential  facts  are  not  seriously  dis-
puted,  the version  which  appears  in the petitioner’s  brief
cannot  be accepted  without  question  because  it neglects
important  elements
in the background,  which,  if given
proper  emphasis,
will clearly  expose  the fundamental
errors  of the petitioner’s  argument.  In approaching the
statute  which  is involved,  the  petitioner  has brushed
past  the real  purpose  of the National  Labor  Relations
Act in its effort  to convirite  this  Honorable  Court  that
Congress  had no legislative  object  other than the pro-
tection
Similarly,  in  setting

of interstate  commerce.

Statement  of the Case.

3

forth the facts of this case, the petitioner  has endeav-
ored  to hitch them  to the current  of commerce  by mag-
nifying  a  simple controversy  between  an employer  and
a  dozen employees  into a  national crisis,  threatening  the
very  foundations  of  the  movement  of  commerce.
Stripped  of  the  importance  which  the  petitioner  seeks
to  attach  to  them,  the  ultimate  facts  are  relatively
and obvious.
simple

1.  The  Pleadings.

This proceeding  had  its origin in an order issued
by  the  petitioner  against  the* respondent,  Jones  &
Laughlin  Steel  Corporation,  pursuant  to  the  National
is a quasi judicial
Labor  Relations  Act.  The petitioner
agency  constituted  under  the  Act,  with  authority  to
hear  and  determine  complaints  against  employers,  aris-
ing under Section  8 of  the Act.
Some  time  prior

to January  23,  1936,  thirteen  indi-
viduals  who  had  been  employed  in the  respondent’s  steel
plant at  Aliquippa,  Pennsylvania,  complained  to the peti-
tioner  of  alleged  discrimination  against  them  by the
respondent,  because  of their affiliations  with a  labor or
ganization,  and on that  date the petitioner  issued
formal  complaint  against  the Jénes &  Laughlin  Steel
Corporation  (R. 12)  in which  it  charged  that the re-
spondent  had engaged  in unfair labor practices,  in vio-
lation  of  Section  8 (3)  of the National  Labor Relations
Act, by the discharge  of twelve  of the complaining  em-
ployees  and the demotion  of the thirteenth.  It  was
alleged  that the respondert  had discharged  and demoted
these individuals,  because  of their union activities,  and
by reason of such ucts, the respondent  was charged  with
discrimination  against  a  certain  labor  organization
known  as the Amalgamated  Association
of Iron, Steel
and Tin Workers  of North  America,  Beaver  Falls  Lodge

4

Statement  of the  Case.

No.  200,  of  which  the  individuals  were  alleged  to  be
members.  In  order  to  support  the  jurisdiction  of  the
petitioner,  the  complaint  set  forth  that  the  acts  of  the
respondent  constituted  unfair  labor  practices,  within  the
meaning  of  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  and  had
burdened  and  obstructed  interstate  commerce  and  the
free flow  thereof,  and  had  led  and  tended  to lead  to labor
disputes,  burdening  and  obstructing  commerce  and  the
free  flow  thereof  (R.  14,  15).
In  connection  with  this
complaint,  the  petitioner  issued  a  notice  of  hearing,  re-
quiring  the  respondent  to  appear  and  answer  the
charges  set  forth  in the  complaint  (R.  16).
'
Upon  receipt  of the above  complaint,  the  respondent
filed  with  the  petitioner  a  pleading  which  it  designated
as  a  “Special  Appearance  and  Answer”  (R.  982 to 986),
under  which  the  respondent  appeared  and  answered  the
complaint,  with  full  reservation  of  its  objections  to  the
jurisdiction  of the  petitioner.

"The  respondent,  in its answer,  denied  the  averments
of  the  complaint  that  the  respondent  was  engaged  in
interstate  commerce  and  denied  that  its  activities  con-
stituted  ‘“‘a  continuous  flow  of commerce  among  the  sév-
eral  states,”  although  admitting  that  a  substantial  por-
tion  of  its  raw  materials  and  finished  products  were
shipped  to and  from  the  respondent’s  plant  in interstate
commerce  (Para:  2 of  Answer).  The  respondent  admit-
ted  that  it had  discharged  and  demoted  the  individuals
named  in  the  compisint,  but  denied  that  it had  done  so
because  of their  union  membership  or  activities,  averring
on.the  contrary  that  the  individuals  named  had  been  dis-
charged  and  demoted  because  of inefficiency,  violation  of
the  Employer’s  rules,  conduct  of the  delinquent  individ-
ual  which  endangered  his  fellow-workmen,  or  other
causes  which  made  it  necessary  for  the respondent  to  dis-
miss  the men  discharged,  and  would  have  necessitated
the discharge  or  dismissal  of any other  man in the re-

a.

Statement  of  the  Case.

5

spondent’s  employ  who  had  been  guilty  of  the  same  of-
fense  or  inefficiency  (Para.  4 and  5).  In addition,  the  re-
spondent  denied  that  it  was  guilty-of  any  unfair  labor
practices  within  the meaning  of the  National  Labor  Re-.
lations  Act;  denied  that  it had  discriminated  against  the
individuals  named  by  reason  of  their  union  member-
ship;  and  denied  that  it  was  discouraging  membership
in  the  labor  organization  named  in the  complaint.  Re-
spondent  also  denied  that  any  of  its  acts  burdened  or
obstructed  interstate  commerce  or  the  free  flow  thereof,
or  otherwise  affected  interstate  commerce,  or  consti-
tuted  unfair  labor  practices  affecting.  interstate  eom-
meree,  within  the  meaning  of  the  National  Labor  Rela-
tions  Act  (ara.  6, 7,  8 and  9 of Answer,  R.  982  to 986).
For  an  affirmative  defense  to  the  complaint  of  the
National  Labor Relations  Board,  the  respondent  averred
that  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board  was  without
jurisdiction  over  the  respondent  or  its  activities  or  the
subject  matter  of  the  complaint,  and  that  the  National
Labor  Relations  Act,  pursuant  to  which  the  Board  was
acting  in  the  premises,  was  unconstitutional  and  void,
in so far as it  was sought  to  be applied  to the respondent.
In this  connection,  the  respondent  averred  that the  com-
plaint  was  insufficient  in  law;  that  the  National  Labor
thereof
Relations  Act,  and  particularly  the  provisions
under  which  the  complaint  was  issued,  were  void
and.
of no  effect  in that  they  violated  the
Constitution  of the
United  States,  and  particularly  Article\V  of the  amend-
ments  theretes  that  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act
was  invalid  and  void  in  that  it  denied  respondent  the
right of trial  by jury in violation  of the  Constitution  of
the  United  States,  and  particularly  Article  VII  of  the
Amendments  thereto;  that  the  National  Labor  Relations
Act  was  invalid  and  void  in  that  it  was  contrary
to  decisions  of  the  Supreme  Court  of  the  United
States  and  that  the  Board  in  assuming  jurisdiction

Statement  of the  Case.
6
to  hear  the  complaint  was  acting  unlawfully  and  in
defiance  of  these  decisions  and  the  Constitution  of
the  United  States;  that  the  National  Labor  Rela-
tions  Act  was  invalid  and  void  in  that  it  purported
to confer  judicial  power  upon  the Labor  Relations  Beard
in violation  of the Constitution  of the United  States,  and
particularly  Section  I and  Section  II  (1)  of  Article  II;
that  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act  was  invalid  and
void  because  it  constituted  an  arbitrary  and  unreason-
able  interference  with  the  respondent’s  freedom  of  con-
tract;  that  the  transactions  described  in  the  complaint
were  not  subject  to  regulation  by  the  Congress  of  the
United  States  through  the National  Labor  Relations  Act,
or  otherwise;  that  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board
had  no  jurisdiction  over  controversies  between  the  re-
spondent  and  its  employees  in that  such  matters  did  not
affect  interatate  commerce  and  were,  therefore,  not  sub-
ject  to  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  that  the  Na-
tional  Labor  Relations  Board,  having  taken  an  active
part  in the  institution  of  the  proceedings,  was  disquali-
fied from  purporting  to exercise  a judicial  function  in the
premises;  and  that  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act,
under  any@nstruction  consistent  with  the  provisions  of
the  Constitution,  could  have  no  gpplication  to  the  re-
spondent  or  its  relations  with  its  employees  (Para.  10
of the  Answer).
After  the  foregoing  special  appearance  and  Answer
had  been  filed, the  National  Labor  Relations  Board  pro-
ceeded  to  entertain  the  complaint  and  to  hear  evidence
on  the  matters  therein  set  forth.  Because  of  the  re-
spondent’s  objections  to  the  jurisdiction  of  the  Board,
the  issue  of  jurisdiction  was  first  heard  and  evidence

.

Labor  Relations  Act  and  its  application  to  the  respond-
ent.  At the  close  of that  evidence,  the  respondent,  con-

The  jurisdiction  of the  Court  was  based  on  the  fact  that
the  respondent  was  qualified  to do business  in Louisiana.

2.  Facts  Relating  to  the  Nature  of  the  Respondent’s
Operations.

The  respondent  is a  Pennsylvania  corporation,  with
its  principal  office  in  Pittsburgh,  Pennsylvania  (Board’s
Exhibit  3).  It  is  engaged  primarily  in  the  business  of
manufacturing  iron  and  steel  in  plants  situated  in  the
vicinity  of  Pittsburgh  (Board’s  Exhibit  4).  The  trans-
actions  covered  by the  complaint  of  the  National  Labor
Relations  Board  related  solely  to  the  discharge  of  cer-
tain  employees,  who  had  been  employed  by the  respond-
ent  in  its  plant  at  Aliquippa,  Pennsylvania  (R.  13,  14).
Although  the
This  is  the  decisive  factor  in  the  case.
National  Labor  Relations  Board  offered  and  received
evidence  relating  to  a  multitude  of  other  activities  of
the  respondent  and  other  employers  and  employees,  an
examination  of  the  complaint  will  show  that  the  only
substantial  question  involved  is the  propriety  of the  dis-
charge  and  demotion  of  thirteen  employees  who  were
unquestionably  engaged  in aiding  the  respondent’s  man-

of the Case.
Statement

in the  Aliquippa  works  (R. 33 to
activities
41).  Two  of the employees  were  motor  inspectors;  one
was  « tractor  driver;  three  wére grane  operators;  one
was  a  washer  in the coke  plant;  and three were  laborers
R. 33 to 40).  Three  other employees were
(Decision,
mentioned
was with-
in the complaint,  but the complaint
drawn  as to one of them  and no evidence  was heard  or
action  taken  es to the other  two.

The  respondent’s  Aliquippa  plant  is  situated  in  @
community  of  approximately  30,000  persons,  which  is
located  about  fifteen  miles  below  Pittsburgh  on the
banks  of the Ohio  River  (R. 341).  The plant  in question
contains  complete  facilities  for  the  production  of  fin-
ished  and semi-finished  iron  and steel  products  from  raw
materials  (Boar’’s  Exhibit  4).  A  substantial  invest-
ment  in lands,  plants and equipment  is involved  (Board’s
Exhibit  4).  The  Aliquippa  works  consists  primarily  of
a by-product  coke  plant for the  production  of coke;  blast
furnaces  for  the  production  of  pig  iron;  open  hearth
furnaces  and  Bessemer  converters  for  the  production
of steel;  blooming  mills  for the reduction  of steel ingots
into  smaller  shapes;  and  a  number  of finishing  mills,
such  as structural  mills,  rod mills,  wire  mills  and the
like.  In addition  there are other buildings,  structures
and equipment,  storage  yards,  docks,  and an intra-plant
railroad  system.  Approximately  10,000  employes  are
engaged  in carrying  on the operations  at  the Aliquippa
-plant  (R. 31).

For  the most part,  the respondent's  operations  at

Statement  of the Case.

8

wrought  upon, which is apparent  from the nature  and
extent  of  the processes  to which they  are subjected.

In the first state  of respondent’s  operations,  the
principal  raw materials  used are iron ore of varying

important  materials  (R. 82).  The iror  ore is procured
from  mines  in Minnesota  and Michigan  and is trans-
ported  over the Great  Lakes to the lower lake  ports
(R. 99).  A subsidiary  of the respondent  is  engaged  in
the business  of lake transportation
and transports  ap-
proximately  25%  of  the ore used  by the respondent:  at
its various  plants,  as well as ore belonging  to other man-
ufacturers  (R. 123).  The ore is taken  from  the docks
on  the lower  lake  ports by independent  rail  carriers  and
shipped to the respondent’s  plant  at Aliquippa  (R. 85),
where  it is stored  in stock piles for future  use (R. 84).
‘The  amount  of  ore  in  storage  varies  with  the  season,
but  there  is usually  enough  ore  on  hand  to maintain
operations  from  nine  to  ten  months  (R.  121).  Coal  is
procured  from the mines of Vesta Coal Company,  a sub-
sidiary  of the  respondent,  situated  in  Pennsylvania  (R.
85),  and is transferred  by  barge  from  the mine to the
respondent’s  plant  at  Aliquippa  where  it,  like  ore,  is
for future  use (R.  121).  Approximately
two to
stored
three months’  supply  of coal is always  on hand  (R. 121).
Limestone,  which  is another  important  raw  material,  is
obtained  in  Pennsylvania  and  West  Virginia  (R.  85),
and is shipped,  by independent rail carriers  (R.  85), to
respondent’s  plant at Aliquippa,  where  it is stored.  The
amounts  of limestone  usually  on  hand  are  probably  suf-
ficient  to operate  the blast furnaces  for a  few weeks

(R.  108).

subsequent  operations  (R.  86),  which  is  also  the  case

|  chased  (R.  90).

The  respondent’s  operations  up  to  this  point  are
not  carried  on  with  reference  to  any  preexisting  orders
or  specifications,  although,  of  course,  the  amount  of  pig
iron  produced  does  reflect  the  existing  and  probable
future  demands  of  the  market  (R.  86).  Although  dif-
fering  grades  of  pig  iron  are  produced,  there  is  no  en-
deavor  to  manufacture  according  to  Geet  custom-
ers’  specifications.

The  second  stage  of  respondent’s  operations  con-
sists  of the conversion of pig iron  into  steel  and: the
fabrication  of  finished  and  semi-finished  steel  products
(R.  86).  To  a  large  extent,  the  second  stage  is  deter-
mined  by  the  orders  and  specifications  of  customers,
although  a portion  of respondent’s  products  is manufac-
tured

for stock  (R.  86).

The  pig iron  is transported  from  the  blast  furnaces
to a mixer  and  then,  with  scrap.  ores  and  various  metals,
converted  into steel  in the open  hearth  furnaces  or  by
the  Bessemer  process  (R. 82).  The steel ingots which
result  from this  operation  are then  placed  in soaking
pits to obtain  an evenly  distributed  heat  and cut  down
into  shapes  and  sizes  suitable  for  further  fabrication  in
the blooming mill  (R. 83).  After  this  operation is
completed,  the  resulting  blooms,  slabs,  billets  and  bars
(which  are  sizes  and  shapes  produced  by  the  rolling
operations
sold or sub-
of the blooming  mill)  are either
jected  to  other  manufacturing  processes  in  the  various
