of the  Case.  .

i

;

finishing  mills,  where pipes, structural shapes, tin plate,
rods,  wires,  tubes and a  variety  of products  are made
(R. 82).

The  greater  portion  of the  respondent’s  production
of  semi-finished  steel  is used  by it in  subsequent  oper-
ations,  but  some  is  sold  to  other  manufacturers,  prin-
cipally  in  the  Pittsburgh  district  (R.  88)  within  the
State  of Pennsylvania.  Of the  finished  products,  a por-
tion  is  carried  in  stock,  but  the  greater  percentage  is
immediately  shipped  by barge  or  railroad  to  customers
(R.  87).  In  some  instances  finished  and  semi-finished
products  are shipped  by barge  or  rail to warehouses  in
a  number  of  localities  and  then  shipped  from  there  to
cust@mers  (R.  107).  By far  the  greater  portion  is han-
dled  by rail  carriers  (R.  87).  It is admitted  that  prob-
ably  75%  of  respondent’s  finished  products  is normally
transported  to  states  other  than  the  State  of  Pennsyl-
vania  (R.  88).

The  respondent  does  not  own  or  operate  any  rail-
roads  but  does  control  a  subsidiary  corporation,  which
This
operates  an  intra-plant  railroad,  at  Aliquippa.
railroad  receives  cars  from  independent  rail  carriers  and
moves  them  about  the  respondent’s  plant  (R.  100).  It
likewise  delivers  cars  containing  finished  products  at
the  plant  to  independent  carriers  for shipment  to  cus-
tomers  (R.  100,101).  The  respondent  also  owns  a num-
ber  of barges  and  a  subsidiary  corporation,  Vesta  Coal
Company,  owns  and  operates  tow  boats  for  hauling
barges  laden with  coal,  and in some cases  with  manu-
factured  products  (R.  120).

The  Board  attached  great  significance  to  the  fact
that  within  the  last  year  the  respondent  claimed  a  s0-
called  “transit  privilege”  which  entitles  a  shipper  of
unfinished  material  to stop  it  in transit  for  further
fabrication  and  then  continue  the  shipment,  under  a
through  tariff (R. 367).  It  appears  that the respondent

3.  Facts Relating  to the Discharges  and Demotion  of
Employees.

As the summary  of the pleadings  and procedure  has
indicated
(supra,  pages  3  to  7),  the  gravamen  of
the complaint  against  the respondent  was that  it  had
discharged  or  demoted  thirteen  production  employees
at  its  Aliquippa  plant,  because  of  their alleged  union
activities.  In other  words,  although  the petitioner  was
nominally  the plaintiff,  as well as the judge,  the real
parties  in interest  were the thirteen  employees  who  were
represented  by the  Board.  Practically  all  of the  factual
in the case  (except  that  which  dealt  with  the
evidence
nature  of the  respondent’s  business)  related  to  the  cir-
cumstances  surrounding  the  relations  of  these  em-
The  petitioner  has  not
ployees  with  the  respondent.
reviewed  this  evidence  in  its  brief,  because  it considers
that  only  questions  of law  are  now  before  the  Court.
On the other  hand,  there  is. good  reason  to  hold  that
these  facts  are most  important  to  a  decision  on  the con-
stitutional  issues,  because  they  show  how  tenuous  is the
connection  between  the  movement  of  interstate  com-
merce  and the transactions  which  were  probed  and  de-
termined
by the petitioner.  More  than  that,  they  are
striking  proof  that  the provisions  of  the Act which  the
Board  has sought  to impose  upon  the respondent  are  a
continuing  menace  to  its freedom  of  operations  and
contract.

After the evidence  as to the nature of the respond-
ent’s  operations  had been  received,  the respondent,  con-

on the merits.
any evidence

The  procedure  for  hearing  which  the  National
Labor  Relations  Act  provides  does  not  require  the  appli-
cation  of  the rules of evidence  prevailing  in courts  of
law  or  equity  and the petitioner,  in this  case  at least,
has  accepted  this  as a  license  to  let down  the  bars  to
every  conceivable  kind  of  highly  prejudicial,  irrelevant
and  irresponsible  evidence.  Hearsay,  rumor,  suspicion
and  unqualified  opinion  were  received  in the course  of
the hearings  and given  full  consideration  in the petition-
er’s  decision.  The  respondent  was  blamed  for  the activ-
ities of  local police authorities  (see  e.  g.,  R. 139) and of
other  persons  who had  no  connection  with  the respond-
ent,  except  in  the  biased  opinion  of  hostile  witnesses
(see e. g. 169,  170;  356).  Transactions  which  occurred
long  before  the  enactment  of  the  National  Labor  Rela-
tions  Act were  probed  and used  against  the respondent.
It is noteworthy  that  much  of the testimony  which  dealt
with  events  occurring  before  the Act was heard  by the
National  Steel  Labor  Relations  Board  (R. 141,  212 and
360)  under  previous  legislation  (Public  Resolution  No.
44)  and  the respondent  exonerated  by the  findings of
that  authority.  Strikes  in the steel  industry  and else-
where,  occurring  within  the last quarter  century,  were

cism (R. 469, 470) and the Board was outspoken in its

16
that he got only one or two days’ wo.* a  week  while

where  more  work  was  available  (R.  234).  The  same
foreman  brought  him back  to a  crane.  In June,  1935,
he was suspended  for  ten days as a disciplinary  measure
(R. 222).  He was discharged  for leaving  the key to the
safety  lock to his crane on  the bench beside  the crane
(R.  229).

Dominic  Brandy,  8 coal  washer,  likewise  complained
that prior to his discharge  he  did  not  receive  as much
work  as  the  other  men  (R.  251),  but  when  it  came  to

he  considered  that  he  had  worked  five  days  a  week  for
five  months  before  his  layoff  (R.  264-265).  Again  a
member  of the Board  encouraged  the witness  to vilify
the Company  (R. 268).  He  was laid off in August,  1935,
because  when  a  sample  of his work  was taken,  it  was
found  that  the  coal  had  not  been  properly  washed  and
that  there  was  an  excess  of slate  present.  In November
he was discharged  because  another  bad  sample  was
found  (R. 258-262).

|

Eli Bozich  was  laid off one  week  in September,  1935,
because  he left his job before  it was finished.  His ex-
cuse  was that others  were  similarly  neglectful  (R. 272).
Later  he received  a  two weeks’  layoff  for not reporting
to work  but says  that  he was not instructed  to appear
for standing  idle
(R.  273).  He was finally  discharged
after he had finished  a  particular  piece of work (R. 280).
He  says  that other  workers  who loafed  on the job have
gotten  away with  it  (R. 281).

labor  gang  to a crane  in another  mill  at his-own  request

4.  Hypothetical  Evidence.

There  was  no  proof that  the respondent’s  operations
had  been  interrupted  by  a  strike,  or  that  the  effi-
ciency  of its operations  had  been  in the  slightest  respect
impaired  or  hampered  by  the discharge  of the complain-
ing witnesses.  Petitioner,  however,  offered  and  received
in evidence  a great variety  of documentary  and other  evi-
dence,  including  a  miscellany  of  economic  and  political
opinions,  none  of which  seemed  to  have  any  actual  rele-
vancy  to  the  issues  of  the  case.  This  hypothetical  evi-
dence  covered  such  a  wide  field  and  embraced  so  many
subjects  that  it  is  almost  impossible  to  give  an  intelli-
It ranged  from  magazine
gible  abstract  of  its  content.
articles  (see  e.  g. Board’s  Exhibits  25 to 35),  newspaper
-  clippings  (Board’s  Exhibit  36)  and  the  testimony  of
newspaper  readers  (R.  396)  through  numerous  ab-
stracts  and  summaries  of doctorate  and  other  theses  on
labor  relations  in general  (see  e.  g. Board’s  Exhibits  70,
71, 92,  94,  95)  to  a  best  seller  ‘Steel  Dictator,”  (R.  898)
and  comments  by Judge  Gary,  following  the  1892  strike.
The  petitioner  took  judicial  notice  of  collegiate  theses,
as  well  as  public  records.  Some  of these  documents  pre-
tended  to  be  factual  reports,  but  many  were  admittedly  ,
expressions  of  opinion.

In the  hearings  which  were  conducted  at Pittsburgh
and  which  involved  the  actual  discharges,  the  petitioner
made  some  effort  to  adhere  to  ordinary  standards  of
relevancy,  but  at  the  Mater  hearing  in  Washington,  the
bars  to irrelevant  and  prejudicial  material  were  entirely
dropped.  It is significant  that  at this  latter  hearing  the
respondent’s  case  was  combined  in  a  joint  hearing  with
the  cases  of  Wheeling  Steel  Corporation  and  Crucible
Steel  Company,  with  evidence  being  admitted  in  bulk
against  the  three  employers,  without  regard  to  dis-
similarities  in  their  position.

of the  Case.
Statement

The Board’s  evidence  in Pittsburgh  established  that
the  respondent  was  a  well  integrated  steel  company,
ranking  about  fourth  in the industry.  It  was also  shown
that  the industry  in general  was  of  substantial  char-
acter,  that  its  functions  were  important  to  the  nation
at  large,  and that  the use of steel  in other industries
was  widespread.  Evidence  was  also offered  to show  that
in slack-
strikes  had occurred  in the industry,  resulting
ening  of  operations,  or  at  least  a  transfer  of the bulk
of  business  from  non-operating  to  operating  units.
Witnesses  expressed  the  opinion  that  future  strikes  in
the  industry  might  affect  other  businesses,  such  as  the
manufacturers  of  automobiles  and  the  railroads,  be-
cause  of  the  widespread  use  of  steel.  The  1919  strike
was  dwelt  upon,  although  the  respondent’s  operations
were  not  affected  by it  (R.  267).

It is clear,  from  the  Board’s  own  procedure,  that  all

5.  The  Decision  of  the  Board.

In  the  last  analysis,  the  substance  of  the  decision
was  that  the  respondent  must  reinstate  ten  discharged
employees,  with  compensation  for  time  lost  d
their
period  of uneraployment.  However,  ip certain
the  decision  went  beyond  this  by  making  negative
orders,  requiring  the  respondent  to  cease  and  desist
“from  interfering  with  its  employees  in  their  Union
activities  and  from  discouraging  membership  in  the
Union  by discrimination  in  regard  to  hire  or  tenure  of
employment”.  These  negative  restraining  orders  were
based  upon  the  same  state  of facts,  that  is, the dis-
charges  of  the  ten  complaining  witnesses,  which  are
designated  as  “unfair  labor  practices”  in the  decision  of
the  Board  (R.  33  to 42).

Finally,  the  Board  has  ordered  the  respondent  to
take  affirmative  action  in posting  “notices  to its em-

SUMMARY  OF  THE  ARGUMENT.

The  respondent  contends  that  the  National  Labor
Relations  Act  is,  in  reality,  a  regulation  of  labor  rela-
tions,  and  not  of  interstate  commerce,  and  that,  as  a
consequence,  it  is  not  within  the  power  of  Congress  to
enact.  Even  if it should  be considered  a true  regulation
of  interstate  commerce,  it  still  has  no  application  to
the  respondent’s  relations  with  its  production  employ-
ees,  because  they  are  not  subject  to  regulation  by  the
Federal  Government.  In  addition,  the  provisions  of the
Act  which  the  petitioner  seeks  to  apply  in  the  present
case  are  invalid,  because  they  violate  Section ’2 of  Ar-
ticle  III  of  the  Constitution,  as  well  as  the  Fifth  and
Seventh  Amendments  to  the  Constitution.

The  respondent  is  a  corporation  engaged  in  the
In  this  con-
manufacture  of  iron  and  steel  products.
nection,  it owns  and operates  a  large  steel  plant  at  Ali-
quippa,  Pennsylvania,  in  which  are  employed  approxi-
mately  ten  thousand  men.  The  present  case  is,  in
reality,  a controversy  between  ten  individuals  who  were
formerly  employed  by  the  respondent  in  -production
These  indi-
work  at  this  plant,  and  the  respondent.
viduals,  with  three  others,  filed.a  complaint  with  the
petitioner,  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board,  charg-
ing that they had been  discharged  or  demoted  by the

ent objected  to the jurisdiction  of  the petitioner,  but
its motion  to dismiss  was overruled  and the petitioner,
after  hearing,  determined  that  the  complainants  had
been  wrongfully  discharged  and  ordered  their  imme-
diate  restoration,  with  compensation  for  lost  pay.

has  engaged  in  unfair  labor  practices  “affecting  com-
merce”  within  the  definition  of  the  National  Labor

Summary

POINT  I.

The  National  Laber  Relations  Act Cam Have  No  Appil-  .

cation  to  Transactions  Between  the  Respondent
and  Its Production  Employees.

|

 A-
‘THE  NATIONAL  LABOR  RELATIONS  ACT  IS  NOT  A  TRUE
REGULATION  OF  INTERSTATE  COMMERCE.

_

1.  The  problem  of  measuring  the  constitutional
validity  of  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act  is  simpli-
fied if the  inquiry  pays  true heed  to the literal  require-
‘  ments  of  the  Constitution.  The  power  of  Congress  is
clearly  defined  by the  Commerce  Clause  of  the  Consti-
tution  and  considerations  of  political  expediency  have
no  weight  in fixing  the  dividing  line  between  the  powers
of  Congress  and  the  reserved  powers  of  the  several
States.  The  argument  of  the  petitioner  is,  in  the  last
analysis,  a  plea  that,  from  an  economic  standpoint,
Congress  should  have  power  to  apply  its  legislation  to-
the  respondent’s  employment  relations.
This,  we  sub-
mit,  is entirely  beside  the  point,  if the  exercise  of such
power  would  run  counter  to  the  Constitution.

The  jurisdiction  of  Congress,  under  the  commerce
clause  of  the  Constitution,  includes  the  power  to  regu-
late,  restrict  and  protect  interstate  commerce,  but  it
does  not  include,  and  was  not  designed  to  include,  the
right  to  use  such  jurisdiction  as  a  pretext  for  legisla-
tion  whigh  interferes  with  the  local  sovereignty  of  the
separate  States.  Since  Gibbons  v.  Ogden; 9 Wheat.  1
(1824),  this  Court  has  adhered  to  the  principle  that
while  Congress  has power  over the  movement  of per-
sons  and commodities
among  the several  States,  it  has
no jurisdiction  to regulate  activities  which  may precede  |
or  follow,  but  are  not,  commerce.
The  use  of  an
admitted  power  of  Congress  as  a  pretext  to  interfere

Summary  of  the Argument.

r

with local  activities  which  ere  not subject  to its
jurisdiction,
is to be condemned.  The commerce  clause
will  not serve  as an excuse  for legislating  with  respect  —
to labor  relations,  which do  not constitute  a  part of
interstate  commerce.

2.  The legislative  history  of the National  Labor
Relations  Act  and  its  substantive  provisions  are  suffi-
cient  to  demonstrate  that  the  Act,  although  disguised
as a  regulation  of interstate  commerce,  is, in actuality,
a  regulation  of  labor.  The  terms  of  the  statute  apply
to almost every; employer and every employe (Section
2),  although  the  jurisdiction  of thé  petitioner  to enforce
it is somewhat  circumscribed..

The  provisions  of the statute,  if carefully  eh

will  indicate  that  Congress-is  primarily  con
with  the  protection  and  establishment  of  labor  organ-
The  provisions  condemning  plant.  unions
izations.
and  sanctioning  “closed  shop”  agreements,  bear  no
reasonable  relation  to  interstate  commerce.  Similarly,
the  express  preservation  of the  right  to  strike  indicates
that  Congress  was  not  interested  in  preventing  inter-
ruptions  to  the  movement  of  commerce.  The  interfer-
ence  with  the  employer’s  discretion  to  hire  and  fire  is
another  reason  for  believing  that efficiency  in  the  ship-
ment  of  products  in  interstate  commerce  has  not  been
the  object  of the  statute.

3.  Congress  has  endeavored  to  save  the  statute
from  the  taint  of  invalidity  by  confining  its  enforce-
ment  to  transactions  “affecting  commerce”,  which  the
Act defines  as transactions  which  burden  commerce  or
lead  or tend  to lead  to a  labor  dispute  which  burdens
commerce  (Section  2).  Despite  this  limitation  on  the
enforcement  of  the  Act,  the  substantive  provisions
‘make  no  such  exception  and  are,  in  fact,  broad  enough
to  cover  almost  every  employment  relation  (Section  2).

26

Summary  of  the  Argument.

The  petitioner  has followed  the same  involved  line
of reasoning  in endeavoring  to  “find”  that  the  respond-
ent’s  operations  “affect  commerce”.
Like  Congress,  it
has found  itself  faced  with the task  of piling  premise
upon  premise  and hypothesis  upon  hypothesis  to reach
the conclusion  that  the discharge  of  a  few  production
employees  at the  respondent’s  plant  has  a  vital  bearing
upon  the movement  of interstate  commerce.  We sub-
mit  that  the  ultimate  fact  remains  that  Congress  has
enacted  a  labor  law,  and  not  a  regulation  of  commerce,
and  it  does  not help  to sustain  the pretext  that  there
may  be an indirect  connection  between  the  two.  Carter
v.  Carter  Coal  Company  et al, 298 U.S.  238  (1936).

B.

THE  NATIONAL  LABOR  RELATIONS  ACT  CAN  HAVE  NO

APPLICATION  TO  THE  BESPONDENT'S  RELATIONS

WITH  ITS  PRODUCTION  EMPLOYEES.

1.  Although  the  respondent  purchases  raw  mate-
rials,  which  have  a  point  of  origin  in  other  States,  and
ships  a  large  portion  of  its  finished  products  across
State  lines,  its  production  activities,  including  its  em-
ployment  relations,  are  not  thereby  subjected  to  the
jurisdiction  of  Congress.  Howard  v.  MTlinois  Central  -
Railroad  Co.,  207  U.  S. 463  (1908).  There  is no  logical
or  legal  connection  between  fhe  respondent's  limited
participation  in interstate  commerce  and  the  union  affil-
iations  of  its  production  employees.  Adair  v.  United
States,  208  U.  S.  161  (1908);  Hammer  v.  Dagenhart,
247  U.  8S.  2561  (1918);  Railroad  Retirement  Board  v.
The  Alton  Railroad  Company  et al.,  295 U.  S.  330  (1935)
supra.

2.  The  principle  which  impeaches  the  validity  of
_  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  viz,  that  Federal
power  over  interstate  commerce  must  be confined  to

‘Summary  of  the  Argument.

27

bona  fide  regulation  of  the  movements  of  commerce,
likewise  prevents  the application  of the statute  in the
present  case.  An unbroken  line of decisions  under  the
_mmerce  clause  has  established  that  manufacturing
and production  activities are not in or a  part of inter-
state  commerce,  even  though  they  may  be preceded  or
followed
by the movement  of  materials  between  States.
Arkadelphia  Milling  Co.  v.  St.  Lowis  &  Southwestern
-Railways  Co.,  249  U.  S.  134;  Oliver  Iron’Co.  v.  Lord,
262  U.  S..172  (1923);  Industrial  Association  v.  United
States,  268  U.  S.  64  (1925).  Although  most  of  the
decisions  have  dealt  with  the  police  or  taxing  powers
of  the  States,  they  are  based  upon  “ie  fundamental
principle  that  manufacturing  activities  are  subject  to
the  exclusive  jurisdiction  of  the  separate  States.

|

The  distinction  between  the  local  manufacturing
activities  of  a  business  and  its  subsequent  or  precedent
participation  in  interstate  commerce  has  been  main-
tained  in  the  field  of labor  relations.  Hammer  v.  Dagen-
hart,  247  U.  S.  251  supra;  Industrial  Accident  Commis-
sion  v.  Davis,  259  U.  S.  182  (1922).  Even  though  an
employee  may  be  employed  in  indirectly  assisting  the
movement  of  products  in  interstate  commerce,  his  rela-
tionship  to  his  employer  is  a  status  existing  wholly
within  the  State,  whose  incidents,  such  as  wages,  hours
of  labor  and  the  like,  are  purely  domestic  in  character.
Carter  v.  Carter  Coal  Company  et  al,  298  U.  S.  238
(1936).

Congressional  regulation  of  the  labor  relations  of
interstate  carriers  furnishes  no  precedent  for  the  pres-
ent  Act.  Because  interstate  carriers  are  instrumental-
ities  of  the  movement  of  commerce,  and  because  they
are  public  utilities,  Congress  has.subjected  them  to  an
exhaustive  scheme  of  regulation, of which  the  labor  |
legislation  is  merely  an  incident.
As  a  result,  the  de-
cision  in  Texas  & New  Orleans  Railroad  Co.  v.  Brother-

Summary  of  the Argument.
23
hood of Raihoay  & Steamship Clerks et al, 281  U. S.
(1930)  is not  controlling.
@  3.  Decisions,  such  as  Stafford  v.  Wallace,  258 U. S.
495  (1922)  and  Chicago  Board  of Trade  v.  Olsen,  262
U.  S.  1  (1923),  which  sustain  Federal  regulation  of
Stockyards  and  Grain  Exchanges,  have  no  application
to  the  present  case.  Grain  Exchanges  and  stockyards
are  instrumentalities  of  interstate  commerce  in  much
the  same  sense  as  the  actual  carriers  of  interstate  com-
merce.  They  are  focal  points  through  which  the  stream
of commerce  in grains  and  cattle sweeps  on  its way  from
The  -activities
producer  to  the  ultimate  consumer.
which  were  regulated  in  both  cases  were  activities  in
the  stream  of commerce  and  exerted  a  direct  effect  upon
its  flow.  Neither  case  sanctions  the  extension  of  the
doctrine  to  production  activities  which  may  indirectly
affect  the  stream  of  commerce.  Carter  v.  Carter  Coal
Company  et al., 298 U.  S. 238  supra.

4.  The  petitioner  relies  upon  decisions  which  have
'  upheld  the  application  of the  Anti-Trust  Laws  to  indus-
trial  conspiracies,  such  as  Coronado  Coal  Company  v.
United  Mine  Workers,  268  U.  S.  295.  These  were  cases
involving  conspiracies  to  restrain  interstate  commerce
The  element  of  an
by  means  of  local  combinations.
intentional  interference  with  the  movement  of  inter-
state  commerce  was  essential  in  these  cases,  not  only
because  ‘an intent to restrain  commerce  was  a part  of
the  proscribed  offense,  but  also  because  the  existence
of  such  an  intent  made  the  effect  on  interstate  com-
merce  neceasarily  direct.  This is shown  by a comparison
of  the  first  Coronado  case  (United  Mine  Workers  v.
Coronado.  Coal  Co.  et  al, 259  U.  S.  344  (1922)  ), where
there  was  no  intent,  with  the  second  (Coronado  Coal
Co.  v.  United  Mine  Workers,  268  U.  S.  295,  supra),
where  there  was  both  direct  and  inferential  evidence  of

Summary  of  the  Argument.  —

we

;

(Cf.  Onited  —Leather  Workers  v.  Herkert  4

intent.
Meisel  Trunk  Co.,  265  U.  S. 457  (1924)  ).

The  petitioner's  efforts  to  show  an  intentional  re-
if a strike
straint  in the present  case are futile.  Event
should  occur  at the  respondent’s  plant,  the  respondent
could  not  be  held  responsible  for  the  voluntary  inter-
vening  act  of  outside  agencies.  The  suggestion  that
the  respondent  might  be charged  with  an  implied  intent
to  destroy  interstate  trade,  if  a  labor  dispute  should
occur,  is obviously  unsound.

The  conspiracy  cases,  such  as  United  Mine  Work-
ers  v.  Coronado  Coal  Co.,  259  U.  S.  344,  supra,  and
Local  167,  etc.  v.  United  States,  291  U.  S.  283  (1934),
although  primarily  concerned  with  the  application  of
the  Anti-Trust  Laws,  prove  the  fallacy  of  the  petition-
er’s  argument,  because  they  establish  that  Congress
cannot  regulate  local  transactions  or  relations  unless
they  exert  a  direct  effect  on  interstate  commerce.  The
definition  of  “affecting  commerce”  in  the  National
Labor  Relations  Act  is a  confession  of the  indirectness
of  the  connection  between  the  respondent’s  labor  rela-
See
tions  and  the  movement  of  interstate  commerce.
Schechter  Corporation  v.  United  States,  295  U.  S.  495,
supra;  Industrial  Association  v.  United  States,  268 U. S.
64  (1925).  The  petitioner  calls  attention  to  the  fact
that  strikes  may  and  frequently  do  produce  an  inhib-.
itory  effect  on  the  movement  of interstate  trade  to and
from  the  affected  area.  This  is  the  fundamental  error
in  the  petitioner’s  argument,  in  that  it assumes  that  it
need  only  establish  the  connection  between  strikes  and
the  stoppage  of  commerce.  There  has  been  no  strike
In  actuality, the
or  labor  dispute  in  the  present  case.
petitioner  means  that  the respondent’s  discharge  of ten
employees  might  have  led  to  dissatisfaction,  which
might  have led  to  a labor  dispute,  which  might have  led

30

Summary  of  the Argument.

to  a strike  and  a  consequent  interruption  of  interstate
commerce.

The findings  of  Congress  that  discriminatory  dis-
charges  may  lead  to  an  interruption  of  commerce,  are
unavailing.  A  declaration  by  Congress  of  the  need  of
regulation  will  not  automatically  justify  its  action,
where  its  legislative  findings  run  counter  to  estab-
lished  rules  of construction,  which  hold  that  there  is no
necessary  and  direct  connection  between  the  relations
of  an  employer  and  his  employees  and  the  movement
of  interstate  commerce.
In  this  respect,  the  present
case  is  clearly  controlled  by  the  decision  in  Carter  v.
Carter  Coal  Co.,  298  U.  S.  238,  supra.

There  are  dangerous  implications  in  the  petition-
er’s  argument.
If  it  be  accepted,  there  would  be  no
reason  why  Congress  should  not  use  its  power  over
interstate  commerce  as  a  pretext  to  stifle  the  sover-
eignty  of  the  States.  We  therefore  believe  that  the
Court  will  not  suffer  the  powers  of  the  States  to  be
whittled  away  by a  statute  which  piles  speculation  upon
speculation  to  attain  its  ends.
United  States  v.  Butler
et  al,  298  U.  S.  1;  Schechter  Corporation  v.  United
States,  295  U.  S.  495,  supra.

POINT  OL
THE  PROCEDURAL  PROVISIONS  OF  THE  ACT  VIOLATE  SEO-
TION  2  OF  ARTICLE  II  OF  THE  CONSTITUTION
AND  THE  SEVENTH  AMENDMENT  TO
THE  CONSTITUTION.

The  National  Labor  Relations  Act  confers  upon  the
petitioner  exclusive  original  jurisdiction  over  contro-
versies  between  an  employee  and  his  employer  as  to  the
propriety  of  the  employee’s  discharge,  and  the  Board
is  authorized  to  render  affirmative  relief  to  the  em-
ployee,  including  the  restoration  of his  employment  and

Summary  of  the  Argument.

$1

compensation  for  lost  wages.  The  findings of fact  of the
Board  are  conclusive  and  only  objections  made  before
the Board  will be heard.  The act does  not  make  any pro-
vision  for  a  trial  de  novo  in  the  constitutional  courts
on  constitutional  or  jurisdictional  issues.

We  submit  that  the  Act  is  invalid  because  it  au-
thorizes  the  Board  to  award  a  money  judgment,  depriv-
ing  the  employer  of  his  right  to  trial  by  jury  in  cases
involving  more  than  twenty  dollars.
If,  as  the  peti-
tioner  contends,  the  action  before  the  Board  should  be
considered-in  the  nature  of  a  suit  in  equity,  with
authority  in  the  Board  to  award  a  mandatory  injunc-
tion  restoring  employment,  with  incidental  damages  for
back  pay,  then  the  Act  violates  the  provision  of Article
II  of the  Constitution,  for  it deprives  the  constitutional
courts  of their  authority  to try constitutional  and  juris-
dictional  issues.

The  present  case  presents  a  controversy  between
employees  and  the  respondent,  in  which  the  petitioner
has  directed  the  restoration  to  employment  of the  com-
plaining  employees,  with  back  pay.  This  being  the  case,
it  is  a  controversy  between  private  citizens,  enforcing
private  rights.  The  failure  to  provide  means  for a trial
de  novo  of  jurisdictional  issues  in  such  a  case  is fatal.
Crowell v. Benson,  285 U.  S. 22  (1932).

The  proceedings  of  the  Board  are  not  comparable
to those  of the  Federal  Trade  Commission,  for the latter
acts only  in the  public  interest,  where  the  United  States
is,  in  effect,  the  plaintiff,  and  it  does  not  assume  the
_  right  to  enforce  or  redress  private  rights.  In  addition,
the  Federal  Trade  Commission  does  not  exercise  au-
thority  to  grant  any  affirmative  relief,  but  merely  en-
joins  the  continuance  of  unfair  competitive  practices
or  of violations  of  the  Anti-Trust  Laws.  Where  private
citizens  seek  relief  for  damage  under  such  laws,  they

Summary  of  the Argument.
32
are obliged  to resort to the District  Courts.  Cf.  Federal
Trade  Commission  v.  Kleaner,

280 U. 8. 19 (1929).

POINT  IV.
The  Provisions  of  the  Act  Which  the  Petitioner  Seeks
to Apply  to  This  Respondent  Are  Invalid  Under
the  Fifth Amendment to the  Constitation.

A.
THE DECISIONS  OF THIS COURT  IN ADAIR  v.  UNITED  STATES
AND  OCOPPAGE  v.  KANSAS  ARE  DETERMINATIVE.
The real issue  in the present  case is the alleged
commission  by the  respondent  of unfair  labor  practices
in  the  discharge  of  union  employees.  Section  8 (3)  of
the  Act,  which  prohibits  discrimination  in regard  to hire
or  tenure  of  employment  and  which  has  been  applied
to  the  respondent  by the  petitioner,  is  an  arbitrary  and
unlawful  interference  with  the  respondent's.  freedom  of
contract.  Adair  v.  United  States,  208 U. S. 161  (1908)  ;
Coppage  v  .Kansas,  236  U.  8.  1  (1914).  Section  8  (3)
of the Labor  Act presents  a much  greater  menace  to the
respondent’s  freedom  of  contract  than  the  enactments
which  were  denied  validity  in  the  Coppage  and  Adair
cases,  because  it does  not  merely  punish  discriminatory
contracts  or  discharges,  but  authorizes  the  Board  to
direct  the  restoration  of  employment,  thereby forcing
the  respondent  to  enter  into  a  contractual  relationship
against  its will

The  Adair  and  Coppage  cases  are  not  affected  by
the  decision  of this  Court  in Texas  & New  Orleans  Ratl-
road  Co.  v.  Brotherhood  of  Railway  and  Steamship
Clerks,  281  U.  S. 548  (1930).  That  case  did not involve
a  statutory  prohibition  against  discriminatory  dis-
charge,  but  merely  the  violation  by  the  carrier  of  an

of  the Argument.

3%

rder  of court  which  had  enjoined  interference  with  the
d
on  of  representatives  by its  employees  for  pur-
poses  of voluntary  mediation  under  the  Act.  The  power
of  the  Court  to  require  the  defendant  carrier to purge
itself  of  contempt  is  no  precedent  for  a  statute  author-
izing  an  administrative  agency  to  require  the  reem-
ployment  of  an  unsatisfactory  employee.

There  is  another  distinction  between  the  facts  of
the  Texas  &  New  Orleans  Railroad  Co.  case  and  the
issues  of  the  present  case.  The  former  dealt  with  an
interstate  carrier,  which  is  a  public  utility.  Congress
has  always  subjected  interstate  carriers  to  an  ex-
haustive  scheme  of  regulation  and  has  the  right  to
compel  them  to  continue  in business  to serve  the  public.
By reason  of its power  to compel  the continuity  of inter-
state  movement,  Congress  might  also  have  the  right
to  require  the  carriers  to  employ  certain  individuals.
The  respondent  is not  a  public  utility  and  Congress  has
no  authority  to  compel  it  to  continue  its  business.  ‘It,
therefore,  has  no  authority  to  make  it  enter  into  con-
tractual  engagements  with  its  employees  on  terms
which  ure  not  of  its  own  making.  A  comparison  of
Char'ss  Wolff  Packing  Co.  v.  Court  of Industrial  Rela-
tions,  262  U.  S.  522  (1923),  with  Wilson  v.  New,  248
U.  S.  332  (1917)  points  the  distinction.  It  should  also
be observed  that  the  mere  size  of the  respondent’s  oper-
ations  does  not  put  it  in  the  category  of  businesses
“affected  with  a public interest.”  Cf.  Williams  v.  Stand-

_  ard.O%  Co.,  278 U.  S. 235  (1929).

34

'

Summary  of  the  Argument.

B.

THE  PETITIONER'S  ORDER  OONSTITUTES  AN  UNLAWFUL
WITH  THE  RIGHT  OF  THE  RE-
INTERFERENCE
SPONDENT
TO  MANAGE  ITS  OWN
BUSINESS.

The  law  has  always  been  hesitant  to  interfere  in
questions  of  employer-employee  relationships.  From
the  standpoint  of  the  employee,  the  law  has  recognized
that  he  should  not  be  forced  into  a  relationship  which
may  be  distasteful,  and  from  the  employer’s  viewpoint,
the  courts  have  held  that  the  right  to  judge  the  capa-
bilities  of  employees  is  absolutely  essential  to  the  effi-
cient  management  of  the  employer’s  business.  The
question  of  retaining  or  discharging  an  employee  in-
volves  delicate  considerations  of  discretion  which  the
The  facts  of  the
law  is  loath  to  attempt  to  weigh.
present  case  show  the  dangers  of bureaucratic  interfer-
ence,  in  that  each  discharge  involved  some  admitted
fault  on  the  part  of  the  complaining  employee,  but  the
petitioner  determined  that  it was  better  qualified  to  de-
cide  and  that  the  respondent’s  action  had  been  too
drastic.  This  is clearly  an  interference  with  the  normal
its  own  business,
right  of  the  respondent  to  manage
because  it is a dictatorial  usurpation)  of the  respondent’s
discretion  to determine  the  capabilities  of its  employees.
Another  dangerous  implication  of  the  law  and  of
the  petitioner’s  decision  is that  it confers.a  kind  of civil
service  status  upon  union  employees,  which  will  inev-
itably  encourage  laziness,  insolence  and  inefficiency.
This  is  confirmed  by a  notice  which  the  petitioner,  in
its  decision,  has  ordered  the  respondent  to post  in  its
plants,  to  the  effect  that  it will  not  discharge  members
of  the  union.  It would  be  the  equivalent  of  informing
the  employees  that if  they  become  affiliated  with  the
union,  they  will  be thenceforth  immune  from  discharge.

Summary  of  the  Argument.

85

We  submit  that  the  underlying  philosophy  of  the
National  Labor  Relations  Act  is  a  constant  threat  to
the  respondent’s  normal  right  to  manage  its  own  busi-
ness.  Not  only  does  the  Act  provide,  in effect,  that  an
unqualified  bureau  will  sit 2s a  higher  court  over  the
respondent’s  employment  office,  but  it also  ordains  that
the  respondent  must  deal  with  whatever  union  may  be
selected  by  a  majority  of  its  employees  and  refuse  to
negotiate  with  other  employees  or  their  representatives.
The  power  which  it delegates  to  a  majority  of  the  em-
ployees  to bind  the  minority  is arbitrary  and  unfair  and
will  necessarily  lead  to  the  suffocation  of  minorities
and  to  the  closed  shop,  forcing  the employer  to herd  his
employees  into  an  organization  which  is  not  of  their
own  choice.  This  will  in  turn  seriously  disturb  the  dis-
cipline  and  morale  of  the  respondent’s  employees,  with
obvious  injury  to  them  and  to  the  respondent.
(Cf..
Carter  v.  Carter  Coal  Co.  et al, 298  U.  S. 238,  supra.)

Argument.

ARGUMENT.
POINT L
The Findings  of the Petitioner,  the National  Labor
Relations  Board,  on  Jurisdictional  and  Constita-
-
tional Issues,  Are Subject  to Review  by This Court.
The  National  Labor  Relations  Act  provides,  in Sec-
tion  10  (e),  that  “the  findings  of  the  Board  as  to  the
facts,  if  supported  by  evidence,  shall  be  conclusive”’.
in Section  10  (b),  the statute  declares  that “rules
Again,
of evidence  prevailing  in  Courts  of  law  or  equity  shall
not  be  controlling”.  It has  been  suggested  in  the  peti-
tioner’s  brief  that  the  Board  has  found  that  the  acts  of
the  respondent  in  discharging  the  employees  named  in
the  complaint  tended  to  lead  to  a  labor  dispute  which
would  burden  ‘or  obstruct  commerce,  and  therefore,
“affect  commerce”  as  a  matter  of  law.  Although  the
petitioner’s  decision  did contain  findings  which  are  sub-
stantially  to  this  effect,  these  findings  cannot,  of them-
selves,  justify  the  action  of the  petitioner  in  assuming
jurisdiction  over  the  respondent's  industrial  relations.
We believe  that this Court is entitled  to cut through  the
findings  of the petitioner  and to examine  the ultimate
facts,  and  that  such  an  examination  will  conclusively
show  that,  under  accepted  principles  of  constitutional
construction,  the  transactions  described  in  the  com-
were,  as a matter  of law, not  subject  to regulation
“under  the  Natfonal  Labor  Relations  Act,  from  which  it
must  follow  that the  petitioner  had  no  jurisdiction  over
the  respondent  or  over  the  subject  matter  of the  contro-
versy.

The decision  of this Court in Crowell  v.  Benson,  285
U.  S. 22  (1932)  entitles  the  respondent to a  reexamina-
tion of the fects upon which the jurisdiction  of the peti-

Argument.

|

aT

Uoner fe eeidte depend.  In holding
was entitled  to a  trial de novo  on a  question  of jurisdic-
tion  under  the  Longshoremen’s  and  Harbor  Workers’

commissioner,  appointed  pursuant  to  the  Act,  on, juris-
dictional  issues  were  not binding,  the. Court  said:

that an employer  —

“The  recognition  of the utility  and  convenience
of administrative  agencies  for the investigation  and
finding  of  facts  within  their  proper  province,  and
.the  support  of their  authorized  action,  does  not  re-
quire  the  conclusion  that  there  is no  limitation  of
their  use,  and  that  the  Congress  could  completely
oust the courts  of all determinations
of fact  by vest-
ing the  authority  to  make  them  with  finality  in its
own  instrumentalities  or  in  the  executive  depart-
ment.  That  would  be to sap the judicial  power  as
it  exists  under  the Federal  Constitution,  and to es-
tablish  a  government  of  a  bureaucratic  character

“In  the  present  instance,  the  argument  that
the  Congress  has  constituted  the  deputy  commis-
sioner  a fact-finding  tribunal  is  unavailing,  as  the
contention  makes  the  untenable  assumption  that
the  constitutional  courts  may  be  deprived  in  all
cases  of  the  determination  of  facts  upon  evidence
even  though  a constitutional  right may  be involved.
Reference  is also  made  to the  power  of the Con-
gress  to change  the procedure  in courts  of admir-
alty,  a  power  to which  we have alluded  in dealing
with  the  function  of  the  deputy  commissioner  in
passing  upon  the compensation  claims  of employees.
But when fundamental  rights are in question, this

Argument.

Court  has  repeatedly  emphasized  ‘the  difference  in
security  of  judicial  over  administrative  action.’”
(Italics  supplied)  (pp.  60-61)
It  might  well  be  argued  that  the  National  Labor
Relations  Act  is  fatally  defective  because  it  makes  no
provision  for  a  trial  de  novo  on  jurisdictional  issues.
Although  the  Circuit  Court  of  Appeals  and  this  Court
may  review  the  record  which  is made  before  the  Board,
a mere  re-examination  of the  recorded  evidence  is in no
sense the equivalent  of a trial de novo and the Act makes
no  provision  for  a thorough  judicial  inquiry  into  funda-
mental  questions  of  jurisdiction..  But  apart  from  this
point,  which  will  be  considered  later,  the  existence  of  a
causal  connection  between  the  transactions  over  which
the  Board  assumes  jurisdictidn  in  particular  cases  and
the  movement  of  interstate  commerce  is  necessarily  8
question  of  law  and  the  constitutional  courts  may  not
be  deprived  of  an  opportunity  to  make  a  determination
of  the  facts  upon  which  the  connection  is  said  to
depend.

It is no  answer  to this  to contend  that  the  petitioner
is a quasi-judicial,  rather  than  an  executive  agency.  If
such  a contention  could  grant  finality  to the  petitioner’s
decisions  on  issues  involving  its  own  right  to  assume
jurisdiction,  the  constitutional  powers  of  the  judiciary
would  be  seriously  impaired  and  the  right  of  a  citizen
to  resort  to  the  constitutional  courts  for  the  protection
of his  rights  completely  abrogated.  This  principle  has
been  reiterated  in many decisions  of this Court  and finds
its latest  expression  in 3t. Joseph  Stock  Yards  v.  United
States,  298  U. S. 38  (1936),  in the  statement  that

“When  the  Legislature  acts  directly,  its  action
is subject  to judicial  scrutiny  and  determination  in
of these limits  .
order  to prevent  the transgression
cannot  preclude  that
of  power.  The Legislature

Argument.

39

scrutiny  or  determination  by  any  declaration  or
legislative  findtmg.  Legislative  declaration  or finding
is necessary  subject  to  independent  judicial  review
upon  the  facts  and  the  law  by courts  of competent
jurisdiction  to  the  end  that  the  Constitution  as  the
supreme  law  of  the  land  may  be  maintained.  Nor
can  the  Legislature  escape  the  constitutional  limita-
tion  by authorizing  its agent  to make  findings  that
the  agent  has  kept  within  that  limitation.  Legisla-
tive  agencies,  with  varying  qualifications,  work  in a
field  peculiarly  exposed  to  political  demands.  Some
may  be  expert  and  impartial,  others  subservient.
It  is  not  difficult  for  them  to  observe  the  require-
ments  of law  in  giving  a  hearing  and  receiving  evi-
dence.  But  to  say  that  their  findings  of  fact  may
be  made  conclusive  where  constitutional  rights  of
liberty  and  property  are  involved,  although  the  evi-
dence  clearly  establishes  that  the  findings are  wrong
and  constitutional  rights  have  been  invaded,  is  to
place  those  rights  at  the  mercy  of  administrative
Officials  and  seriously  to  impair  the  sécurity  inher-
ent  in our  judicial  safeguards.

“That  prospect,  with  our  multiplication  of ad-
ministrative  agencies,  is  not  one  to  be  lightly  re-
garded.  It is said that we  can  retain  judicial  author-
ity  to  examine  the  weight  cf  evidence  when  the
question  concerns  the  right  of personal  liberty.  But,
if this  be  so,  it  is not  because we are  privileged  to
perform  our  judicial  duty  in  that  case  and  for  rea-
sons  of  convenience  to  disregard  it in  others.  The
principle  applies  when  rights  either  of person  or  of
property  are  protected  by  constitutional  restric-
tions.  Under  our system  there  is no warrant  for
the view  that  the judicial  power  of  a  competent
court  can  be circumscribed
by any legislative  ar
rangement  designed  to give  effect  to administrative

40

Argument.

action  going  beyond  the  limits  of  constitutional
authority.”  (Italics  supplied  )  (pp.  51-62)
The effect  of this principle  is that  this Court  may
inquire  not  only  into  the  reasonableness  of the  findings
of Congress,  as expressed  in the National  Labor Rela-
tions  Act,  but  also  into  the  propriety of the  petitioner’s
decision.  .  However  much  power  Congress  may  have
designed  to  confer  upon  the  National  Labor  Relations
Board  as  a fact-finding  body,  it is fundamental  that  the
Board  may  not  lift  itself  by its  own  bootstraps  and  find
that  it  has  jurisdiction  ‘where,  as  a  rhatter  of law,  no
such  jurisdiction  exists.  If this  were  not  the  rule,  fun-
damental  rights  would  be at  the  mercy  of a  subservient
administrative  agency,  and  a body such  as  the  petitioner
could  find  its  own  way  into  jurisdiction,  even  where  the
ultimate  facts  are  legally  insufficient  to  support  its  au-
thority.

The  grave  consequences  which  this  Court  pointed
out  in the  decisions  referred  to above  are  perhaps  illus-
In  one  sense,
trated  by the  facts  éf the  present  case.
the  National  Labor  Relations  Act  is  itself  responsible
for  the  atmosphere  of hostility  which  pervaded  the  pro-
before  the Board,  because  of the  unrestrained
powers  which  it places in the petitioner.  The  procedural
provisions  of the  Act  have  constituted  the petitioner  an
investigator,  a  prosecutor  and  a  judge,  at  one  and  the
same  time.  Under  Section  11,  investigatory  powers  of
the widest  character  are conferred  upon the Board.  It
is given  “access to, for the  purpose  of examination,  and
being in-
the right to copy any evidence  of  any person
vestigated  or proceeded against that relates  to any mat-
or in question.”  Proceedings
ter under investigation
against  employers  may be initiated  by the petitioner  and
it is permitted  to sit in judgment  on  its own complaints.
Its agents are sent out to make investigations  and its

ab

own  attorneys  prosecute  employers  on behalf  of the peti-

The widest  latitude  in conducting  hearings  is permitted.
The  rules  of evidence  pervailing  in Courts  of law or
equity  are not  controlling,  and yet,  the findings  of the
Board  on questions  of fact  are made  conclusive.  It  is
true  that  other  agencies,  such  as  the  Federal  Trade
Commission,  have  similarly  wide  powers,  but  they,  for
the  most  part,  deal  with  broad  questions  of public  in-
terest,  and  not  individual  cases,  where  the  factor.  of
personal  relationship  is  present  and  where,  from  the
very  nature  of  the  case,  the  hostility  and  bitterness  of
.a  disgruntled  employee  are  apt  to  intrude.

The  determination  of  the  petitioner  to  reach  the

Argument.

POINT I
The  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  Under  Any  COon-
struction  Consistent  With  the  Provisions  of  the
Constitation,  Can  Have  No  Application to Trans-
actions  Between  the  Respondent  and  Its Production
Employees.

It  is  the  respondent’s  position  that  the  National
Labor  Relations  Act  is,  in  reality,  a  labor  statute,-and
not  a true  regulation  of interstate  commerce,  from  which
it should  follow  that  the  Act  is invalid  in toto.  The  peti-
tioner  contends,  however,  that  the  statute  may  be saved
from  the  taint  of  invalidity  by attributing  to  Congress
the  intent  to  confine  its  application  to  situations  which.
Congress  may  lawfully  regulate  under  the  interstate
commerce  clause  of  the  Federal  Constitution.  The  re-
spondent’s  argument  will  therefore  be  directed  to  both
issues,  maintaining  with  respect  to  the  first  that  the
Act  is unconstitutional  in  toto,  and,  with  respect  to  the
second,  that  it cannot  be lawfully  applied  to the  respond-
ent.  However,  since  both  issues  depend,  to  a  large  ex-
tent,  upon  the  same  considerations,  it  is  impossible  to
observe  a  definite  line  of  separation  throughout  the
argument,  and we  therefore  ask that our  arguments with
respect  to the question  of constitutionality  be considered
as  bearing,  with  the  same  force,  upon  the question  of the
lawful  scope  of  the  particular  provisions  of  the  Act
which  are  in  question  in  this  case.

A.

The  National  Labor  Relations  Act  Is  a  Labor  Act  and
Not  a True  Regulation  of Interstate  Commerce.

Under this aspect of the case it is necessary  to make
an  inquiry  into  the  objects  of  the  National  Labor  Re-
lations  Act,  to  determine  whether  it represents  an  hon-

as

Argument.

est endeavor  to regulate  commerce  among  the  several  |
states,  or  an  usurpation  of  the  reserved  powers  of  the
separate  states  to  control  questions  of  internal  poligy.
As  a  consequence,  the question  resolves  itself  into  two
subsidiary  questions,  the  first  dealing  with  the  scope  of
Congressional  power  over  interstate  commerce,  and  the
second  with  the  nature  and  objects  of  the  statute  in-
volved.

L
THE  POWER  OF THE  CONGRESS  OF  THE  UNITED  STATES
UNDER  THE  INTERSTATE  COMMERCE  CLAUSE  OF  THE
CONSTITUTION  IS  LIMITED  TO  THE  BONA  FIDE
REGULATION  OF  INTERSTATE  COMMERCE.

The  problem  of measuring  the  constitutional  valid-
-
ity of the  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  in  its  applica-
tion  to  the  industrial  relations  of  the  respondent,  is
greatly  simplified  if the inquiry  begins  with the Consti-
tution  itself  and  pays  true  heed  to  the  literal  require-
ments  of  the  document.  Much  of  the  dust  of  contro-
versy  which  obscures  the  question  of  determining
the validity  of  the  labor  act  and  similar  legislation  has
been  stirred  up  by  arguments  and  discussions  which
have  lost  the  true  meaning  of the  constitutional  phrase
in an effort  to remodel  our political  system  on  a  basis
of expediency,  by attributing  to the central  government
powers  which  might  be  most  conveniently  exercised
the  plenary
from  Washington.  And  as a  consequence,
powers  of the States,  like the Charter  on which  they rest,
are  cast  aside  in  an  endeavor  to  reach  a  theoretically
convenient  goal.  The  mass  of  hypothetical  evidence
which  was. received  by the Board  in its joint  hearing  in
Washington  -  (Statement  of  Facts,  pages  19  to  21),
presents  a  graphic  pictute  of  this  improper  approach
|
to the problem.

Arguments  of this character,  which  advocates of a

the re-
ch lies between
somewhat  blurred  the line

to the  political  issue  of national  versus  local

-  So with  the  petitioner’s  brief.  Deftly it paints  the evils
of strikes  and lockouts  and plausibly  it argues  that  Con-

ment  relations.

The  Constitution  provides  in  Section  8 of  Article  I
that  “The  Congress  shall  have  power  *
to regu-
late  Commerce  with  Foreign  nations  and  among  the
several  States,  and  with  the  Indian  Tribes  *

°

*

The  draftsmen  of  the  Constitution  foresaw  that  if
economic  and  political  intercourse  between  the  States
was  to be fostered,  and the welfare  of  the nation  pro-
tected,  it  would be necessary  to curb the selfish  interests
of the  individual  States  where  they might  easily  conflict
with  the  interests  of the  federation  as  a  whole.  There-
fore,  juriediction  over  interstate  commerce—a  fertile
field  for  controversy  among.  individual  States—was
granted  to the Federal Government.  Its jurisdiction in-
volves  the power  to regulate,  restrict
and protect  inter-
state  commerce,  but it does  not include,  and was not de-
to include,  the right  to use such  jurisdiction as
signed
a  pretext  to interfere  with  the local  sovereignty  of the
States.  If this one premise  is carefully  borne  in mind,
the apparent  confusion  which  sometimes  arises  as to
the limitations  on the powers.of  each of the concurrent

th

Argument.

governments  and  which  pervades  the  soeccuand  argu-
ment is to a large  extent removed.

Breadth  and  meaning  were  given  to  the  commerce  |
in Gibbons  v. Ogden,  9 Wheat.  1  (1824).  It is ap-
clause
parent  from  the  language  of  the  case,  considered  as  a
whole,  that  Chief  Justice  Marshall,  although  a  firm  be-
liever  in  a  strong  centralized  government,  had  no
thought  but that the commerce  clause  meant  just what  it
says,  that  is,  that  Congress  may  regulate  “commerce
among  the  States.”  Even  under  the  most  careful  scru-
tiny,  the  opinion  proceeds  solely  on  the  principle  that
Congress  has  been  accorded  power  over  commerce,  that
is, the movement  of persons  and  commodities  among  the
several  States,  and  not  over  farming  or  mining  or  manu-
facturing,—things  which  may  precede  or  follow,  but  are
not  commerce.

The  true  meaning  of.  the  commerce  clause,  as
exemplified  by  this  decision,  has  sometimes  ‘been  lost
sight  of.in  recent  years.  The argument  is often  made,
as  in  the  petitioner’s  brief,  that  the  nation  has  come
to a new  conception  of commerce,  that  business  has out-
grown  the  State  and  has  become  a  matter  of  national
concern,  and  that  our  purely  internal  political,  economi-
cal and social  life  is so intimately  connected  with  our
national  commerce.  that  it has  been  swallowed  up  in the
latter’s  importance.  On  this  hypothesis,  it  is  not  diffi-
cult  to argue  that  the  wages  of a newsboy,  the  prices  of
a  grocer  and  the  advertising  policies  of  a  local  dentist
in  some  degree  affect  incomes  and  business.
The  im-
pact  may  be  slight,  but  in the  end,  it may  be  contended,
it  either  encourages  or  discourages  commerce  among
the  States.

Despite  this  confusion  of issues,  the  Constitution,  in
the last  analysis,  still  requires  the separation  of  State
and  Federal  power.  The decision  of  this  Court,  in A. L.

Argument.

|

47

A:  Schechter  Poultry Corporation,
et al., v. United  States,
295  U.  S.  495  (1935),  is a  mandate  that  the literal  re-
quirements  of the  Constitution  must  not  be ignored.  It
may be true  that purely  internal  affairs  do indirectly  af-
fect  interstate  commerce,  just  as  interstate  commerce
‘bears  indirectly  upon  internal  affairs,  but  while  this
\might  be  urged  as  a  reason  for  altering  our Constitu-
tional  form  of  government,  it  is  not  an  excuse  for  at-
tempting  to obliterate  the  Constitutional  distinction  be-
tween  commerce  and  that  which  is not.

In  other  recent  decisions,  the  sam  principle  has
been  affirmed  and  the  power  of Congress
¢gonfined  to true

tions  of  internal  affairs  under  the  guise  of  interstate
commerce  legislation.

Case,  [Railroad  Retirement  Board  v.  Alton  Railroad

‘fell, because  it  was not within  the scope  of the regula-
to be blinded  by the
tory  power  of Congress.  In refusing

Argument.

pretense that the legislation  was  justified  under  the tax-

elared :

“The  act  invades  the  reserved  rights  of  the
plan to regulate and con-
States.  It is a statutory
trol  agricultural  production,  a  ma‘ ‘er beyond  the
to the federal  government.  The
powers  delegated
‘tax, the appropriation  of the funds raised,  and the
direction  for  their  disbursement,  are  but  parts  of
the plan.  They  are but means  to an unconstitu-
tional  end.

From  the  accepted  doctrine  that  the  United
States  is a  government  of delegated  powers,  it fol-
lows  that  those  not  expressly  granted,  or  reason-
ably to be implied  from such as are conferred,  are
reserved  to the  states or  to the people.  To forestall
any  suggestion  to  the  contrary,  the  Tenth  Amend-
ment was  adopted.  The same  proposition,  otherwise
stated,  is  that  powers  not  granted  are  prohibited.
None  to regulate  agricultural  production  is  given,
and  therefore  legislation  by Congress  for that  pur-
pose  is forbidden.

It  is  an  established  principle  that  the  attain-
ment  of a  prohibited  end  may  not  be  accomplished
under  the  pretext  of the  exertion  of  powers  which
are  granted.”  (p. 68)
These  decisions  provide  a  sound  basis  for  discuss-
ing  the  constitutionality  of  the  National  Labor  Rela-
tions  Act,  because  they  enable  us to perceive  the real
fssue.  The line between  the divergent  jurisdictions is
defined  by our  political  charter,  it must  be  obeyed  and
neither  the state  nor the federal  government  may crowd
the other from  its allotted  territory  by an appeal  to a
broader  construction  than the instrument  will bear.  The

2.
THE  NATIONAL  LABOR  RELATIONS  ACT  IS,  ON  FYTS  FACS,
A  LABOR  LAW  AND  NOT  A  BONA  FIDE
REGULATION
OF  COMMERCE.

If  the  principle  which  we  have  stressed  under.  the
preceding  heading  of  this  brief  be  accepted,  then  some
pains  must  be taken  to ascertain  the true intent-and
purpose  of the  National  Labor  Relations  Act.  We  sub-
mit that the legislative  history  of the statute  and its
own  literal  provisions  are  sufficient  to. demonstrate  con-
clusively  that  the  Act,  although  disguised  as  a  regula-
tion  of interstate  commerce,  is in actuality  a regulation
of labor relationships,  a conclusion  which  the petitioner’s
brief  unwittingly  confirms.

Congress  has traditionally  exercised  plenary  juris-
diction  over  the  instrumentalities  of  commerce,  that  is,
the  carriers  of  persons  and  commodities  in  interstate
commerce,  Almost  every  session  witnesses  a  new de-
velopment  in the field of transportation  regulation.  Rail-
way labor legislation  has been  included  in the scheme  of
regulation,  since  1888, when  Congress  passed  the Rail-
road Labor Disputes Act. Since then, it  has passed other
railway  labor  legislation,  culminating  in  the  Railway
Labor  Act of 1926,  as  amended  by the  Act  of  June  21,

Despite  this development  in  railway  labor  legisla-
tion, which  may be properly  a  function  of the federal
government,  there  has been no effort  by Congress  to
meddle  in the field of ordinary  labor problems,  with the
exception  of a few brief exclusionary  references  in the

50

Argument.

Clayton  Act, until 1982,  when  Congress  passed  the Anti-
Injunction Act  (Public  Resolution  No. 65, 72nd Congress,
March  23, 1982).  This  Act,  while jt contained a
approved
declaration  of the right  to organize  and bargain  collec-
tively,  did not purport  to legislate  generally  in the field
of labor relations.  Its primary  object  was to restrict
the jurisdiction  of the United  States  courts  in awarding
injunctions  in labor disputes  and to this extent,  it  pur-
ported  to cover labor  disputes  of every  kind and charac-
ter.  Shortly  afterwards,  in Section  77 of the Bank-
ruptcy  Act,  which  was amended  March  3, 1933  to pro-
vide for the reorganization
of railroads,  Congress  took
a  longer  step by including  a  prohibition  against  the use
of reorganization
funds  to foster  so-called  plant  unions
and  discrimination  against  union  members.
Substan-
tially  the same provisions  were incorporated  in Section
TTB  of the Bankruptcy  Act, as amended  June,  1934, to
govern  the  reorganization  of  insolvent  corporations.
As bankruptcy  legislation,  both  amendments  seem  ques-
tionable;  they  are  in  reality,  labor  regulations,  having
no  real  connection  with  the  processes  of  bankruptcy,
except  for  the  disguise  which  Congress  has  employed
to justify  their enactment

:

After  the  enactment  of  Section  77  of  the  Bank-
ruptcy  Act,  Congress  made  its  first  determined  effort  to
establish  widespread  rules  of  collective  bargaining  in
Section  7  (a)  of  the  National  Recovery  Act,  approved
June  16,  1933.  Although  the  Recovery  Act  was  limited.
in  enforcement  to  transactions  occurring  in  interstate
commerce,  the  codes  which  it promulgated  purported.  to
cover  all industry  and,  by administrative  interpretation,
the labor  provisions
of Sectign  7 (a) were forced  upon
the country
at large.  To supplement  Section  7 (a)  Con-
grees  enacted  Public  Resolution  No.  44  (approved  June
19, 1934),  creating  a  National  Labor  Relations  Board  to
investigate  controversies  arising  under  Section  7 (a)

Argument.
Sl
and to inquire  into wiolations.  This resolution,  of course,
fell with the so-called  National  Recovery  Act in the
Schechter  case.

From the foregoing  summary of congressional  sc-
tion in the field of labor relations,  it will be observed that
until the year 1932,  Congress  made  no  determined  effort
to  inject  itself  into  domestic  problems  involving  labor
relations,  except  in  its  own  peculiar  domain  of  inter-
state  transportation  and-that  traditionally  Congress  has
itself  recognized  the  limitations  of the  constitution.  But
then,  beginning  with  the  Anti-injunction  Act and  con-
tinuing  through  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  it
has definitely  committed  itself to a  regulation  of labor
on  a national  scale.  Two  of its efforts,  Section  7 (a)  of
_  the National  Recovery  Act  and  Public  Resolution  No.  44
have  proved  abortive.  Its  latest  effort  in this  direction
is the  National  Labor  Relations  Act.

The  petitioner’s  brief  leans  heavily  upon  the  his-
tory  of Federal  regulation  of  railway  labor  as  an  argu-
ment  for  its  extension  into  other  fields,  but  the  point
proves  too  much.  For  one  thing,  it  draws  into  sharp
contrast  the inactivity  of Congress,  until  recent  times,  in
other  fields  of employment  relations.  What  is perhaps
more  important,  it betrays  the  petitioner’s  argument  as
being,  in truth,  an  attack  on  our  Constitutional  system
rather  than  a  justification  of  the  application  of  the
Labor  Act  to  the  present  case.
If  the  power  of  Con-
gress  over  railroads  means  that  it may  sweep  all  labor
relations  into  its grasp,  there  is no  reason  why it should
not  be  able  to  subject  every  business  activity  to  the
same  exhaustive  scheme  of regulation  which  it  has im-
posed  upon  interstate  carriers.
Statutory  limitations
on wages  and hours,  compensation  laws, safety  and fire
regulations,  and  legislative  supervision  of  factory  con-
ditions—all  would  be within the scope of Federal  control

Argument.
A
and there would be an  end to the sovereignty  of the

A brief  summary  of the provisions  of the National
:  Labor  Relations  Act will be sufficient  to show that it is
an  extension  of  this  recent  Congressional  departure
from traditional  policies  and that  it  is, at  least in its
attempted  application  to the responderit,  a  regulation  of
labor and not a regulation
of Commerce.

The title of the Act, as set forth in Section 16  is
“The National  Labor Relations  Act.”  Section  1,  which
will be considered  in detail  at  another  point,  is a  so
called declaration  of policy,  which is, in the last analysis,
a  somewhat  extended  syllogism  designed  by Congress
to support  the constitutionality  of the Act.  Section  2
contains  a  number  of  definitions.  Among  the  terms
defined  are  “employer,”  “employee,”  “labor  organiza-
tion,”  “unfair  labor  practice,”  ahd  “labor  disputes.”
Sections  8, 4,  5 and 6 are administrative  in character,
creating  the  National  Labor  Relations  Board  and  pro-
viding  for  its  personnel,  compensation  and  adminis-
trative  powers  and  duties.

Argument.

Section  11,  while  it also enlarges  the  procedure  to
be followed  by the Board in its hearings,  is primarily
to  confer  drastic  investigatory  powers  on  the
National  Labor  Relations  Board.  There  is no  indication
that its powers  in this respect  are confined  to transac-
tions  in  interstate  commerce.  On  the  other  hand  the
statute  provides

:

“The  Board  or its  duly  authorized  agents  or
agencies,  shall  at  all  reasonable  times  have  access
to,  for  the  purpose  of examination,  and  the  right  to
copy,  any  evidence  of any  person  heing investigated
or  proceeded  against,  that  relates  to any  matter  un-
der  investigation  or  in  question.  Any  member  of
the  Board  shall  have  power  to  issue  subpoenas  re-  ©
quiring  the  attendance  and  testimony  of  witnesses,
and the  production  of  any  evidence  that  relates  to
any  matter  under  investigation  or  in  question

To aid this  investigatory  function,  provisions  for  the  en-
forcement  of  subpoenas  are  inserted  in  Sub-section  11
(2)  and  under  Sections  11  (3)  and  11  (6)  evidence  nor-
mally  unavailable  is  placed  in  the  hands  of  the  Board.
Section  12  is  penal  in  character,  providing  for  a
fine  or  imprisonment  for  any  resistance  or  interference
with  any  member  of  the  Board  or  any  of  its  agents  or
agencies.  Section  14  provides,  in  effect,  that  the  Act  is
designed  to  supersede,  in case  of conflict,  the  labor  pro-
visions  of the  National  Recovery  Act,  Public  Resolution
No.  44 and  Section  77B  of the  Bankruptcy  Act.

Its  obvious  aim  is  to

this generality,  the Act  is administered  exclusively

The  Employers  Liability  Cases  (Howard  v.  Minois
Central  Railroad  Co.;  Brooks  v.  Southern  Pacific  Com-
pany,  207 U.  S. 463  (1908),  were  decided  at a  time  when
Congress  had  undertaken  a  policy  of  comprehensive
regulation  of  interstate  carriers.  Nevertheless,  the  de-
cisions  came  asa  warning  in  unmistakable  terms  that
the  power  to  supervise  interstate  commerce  did  not
imply the power  to speak  on  every  related  subject,  how-
ever  tenuous  the  connection  might  be.  The  Court  had
before  it the  Federal  Employers  Liability  Act  of  1906.
An  analysis  of the  statute  determined  that  its  applica-
tion was in terms  extended  to injuries  sustained  by
employees  of interstate  carriers  while  engaged in activi-
ties not involving  the movement  of interstate  commerce.
As  a  result,  the  Court  held  that  although  the  Act  em-
braced  subjects  within  the  authority  of Congress,  it also
applied  to matters  not  within  its  constitutional  powers,
and  that  the two  applications  of the statute  were  s0
intermingled  that  they  were  not  capable  of  separation.
The statute,  as  a whole.  was,  therefore,  declared  invalid.

Argument.

68

ously that by engaging  in interstate  commerce,  the car
riers  had subjected  themselves  to all manner  of con-
gressional  regulation.

gated  to it by the  Constitution;  in other  words,  with  .
the  right to legislate  concerning  matters of purely

nS  oy Gaaprene coer eumretees
repeating.  The  power  of Congress  over  interstate
‘commerce  is designed  to allow  Congress  to  protect com-

Argument.

65.

tion  of the  States  over  local  or  domestic  affairs  to  be’
whittled  away  by an  extension  of federal  action.

The  earlier  decisions  of  the  federal  courts  under
the  commerce  clause  do not show  many  instances  where
congressional  legislation  has  been  invalidated;  until
recent  years,  Congress  made  no  effort  to  enact  the  far-
flung  legislation  which  has  characterized  its  later  atti-
tude  toward  matters  of  domestic  policy.  As  a  result,  |
most  of  the  decisions  are  concerned  with  the  power  of
the  States  to tax  or  regulate  local  subjects  in situations
where  such  taxes  or  regulations  have  at least  an  indirect
effect  on  interstate  commerce.  Nevertheless  these  de-
cisions,  if  analyzed,  establish  two  parallel  lines  of
authority,  clearly  demonstrating  that  local  activities
are  not  drawn  into  the  current  of  interstate  commerce
merely  because  they  are  preceded  and  succeeded  by  an
interstate  movement.

U.  S. 665  (1913) ;

(1932) ;

(1933) ;

17  (1934) ;

Champlin  Refining  Co. v.  Corporation  Commis-

sion,

286 U. S. 210 (1982).

Inasmuch  as these  cas®s  have  reached  substantially  the
game  conclusions,  based  upon  substantially  #ientical
arguments,  it  is unnecessary  to subject  them  to close
analysis.  However,  for  the  sake  of emphasis,  it may  be
desirable  to single  out a few unusual  decisions.  de-
fining  the beginning  of the interstate  movement  and its
termination.

Arkadelphia  Milling  Co.  v.  St.  Louis  Southwestern
Railways  Co.,  249  U.  S.  134  (1918)  arose  out  of  actions
by certain  shippers  and  railway  companies  to enjoin  the
enforcement  of  intrastate  railways  rates  fixed  by  a
state  railway  commission.  The  facts  indicated,  in  one
of the  cases,  that  the  shipper  was  engaged  in  transport-
ing  logs  from  its  lumber  operation  in  the  state  to  its
mills,  where  the  logs  were  processed  and  manufactured
into  staves  and  other  products  and  held  in  storage,  to
be  sold  and  shipped  in  accordance  with  the  demands  of
the  market.  Almost  95%  of  the  finished  articles  were
eventually  delivered  to  points  outside  of the  state.  The
court  found  that  the  interstate  movement  did  not  begin
until  the  sale  and  delivery  of  the  finished  products,
declaring :

“Tt  is not merely that  there  was  no  continuous
movement  from  the  forest  to  the  points  without
the  State,  but  that  when  the  rough  material  left
the woods it  was not intended  that it  should be

out  of  the  State,  or  elsewhere  beyond
the mill, until it had been  subjected  to a  manufac
turing  process  that  materially  changed  its  char-
acter,  utility,  and  value.”  (p. 151)

Similarly

in, Oliver  Iron Co. v.  Lord,  262 U. 8.  172
die  cae deletes tok whee “like  manufac-
turing,  is a  local business,”  the Court added:

Argument.

-

oT

“Its character  in this regard is intrinsic,  is not
affected  by  the  intended  use  or  disposal  of  the  |
product,  is not controlled  by contractual  engage-
ments,  and  persists  even though  the business  be
conducted  in  close  connection  with  interstate
commerce.”  (p, 178)
See also Utah Light &  Power Co.  v.  Pfost, 286 U.  8.
165  (1932)  ; Chassanioj  v.  City  of Greenwood,  291  U.  S.
584  (1934).

The  reverse  situation  may  be  seen  in  Industrial
Association  v.  United  States,  268  U.  S.  64  (1925),  in
which  the  Court  held  that  a  conspiracy  in  restraint  of
trade  was  not  designed  to interfere  with  interstate  com-
merce,  even  though  it had  an  indirect  effect  on  the move-
ment  of articles  into  the  state  because  the  conspirators
applied  their  combination  to  goods  which  had  come  to
rest within
the dividing  line
the state.  In reaffirming
between  local: activities  and  interstate  movement,  the
court  declared:

“It  is  true,  however,  that  plaster,  in  large
measure  produced  in  other  states  and  shipped  into
California,  was  on  the  list;  but  the  evidence  is that
the  permit  requirement  was  confined  to such  plaster
as  previously  had  been  brought  into  the  state  and
commingled  with  the  common  mass  of  local  prop-
erty,  and  in  respect  of which,  therefore,  the  inter-
state  movement  and  the  interstate  commercial
status  had  ended.”  (p. 78)

These  two  parallel  groups  of decisions  naturally
converge  into  a third  line  of authority,  establishing  the
principle  that  local  activities  such  as  mining, manufac-
turing,  warehousing,  processing  and the like  are  not
withdrawn  from  the  jurisdiction  of  the  states.  because
of  subsequent  or  precedent  movements in  interstate
commerce.  The first  of these  decisions  is Kidd  v.  Pear-

68

Argument.

gon,  128  U.  S.  1  (1888).  The argument  was  advanced
that the state  had  transgressed  beyond  its proper  juris-
diction  in  prohibiting  the  manufacture  of  intoxicants,
because  the  statute  restricted  the  movement  of  inter-
state  commerce.  In  pointing  out  the  fundamental  dis-
tinction  between  local  activities  and  commerce,  the  court
declared  :

;

“No  distinction  is more  popular  to  the  common
mind,  or  more  clearly  expressed  in  economic  and
political  literature,  than  that  between  manufac-
tures  and  commerce.  Manufacture  is  transforma-
tion—the  fashioning  of raw  materials  into  a  change
of  form  for  use.  The  functions  of  commerce  are
different.”  (p. 20)
It  is unnecessary  to  mention  later  decisions  which
have  perpetuated  the  doctrine  of  Kidd  v.  Pearson;  the
rule  of  these  cases  offers  powerful  resistance  to  any
extension  of  the  interstate  commerce  jurisdiction  of
Congress  under  the  guise  of  the  “modern  conception”
of commerce  to which  we  have  adverted.  A few  of them
are:

United  Mine  Workers  v.  Coronado  Coal  Com-

pany et al., 259 U.  S.  344  (1922) ;

United  Leather  Workers  v.  Herkert  &  Meisel

Trunk  Co., 265  U.  S. 457  (1924)  ;

Delaware,  Lackawanna  & Western  Railroad  Co.

v.  Yurkonis,  238 U. S. 438  (1915)  ;

Champlin  Refining  Co.  v.  Corporation  Commis-

sion, etc.,  286 U.S. 210 (1932).
Commerce  has greatly  expanded  but there has been no
real shift in emphasis  from the local activities  to the
importation  or  exportation  which  precedes  or  follows
them.  All are essential  parts of trade and business  and
it is both futile and absurd  to speak of one as domi-
that we can
the other.  The only fair conclusion
nating

reach  is that  the allocation  of  power  required  by the
constitution  must  be  pursued  while  the  constitution
requires  it.  Where  either  state  or  federal  jurisdiction
attempts  to use its power  as a  pretext  to interfere  with
matters  which  are subject  to the other,  then  the effort
must  be.stricken  down.  For  this  reason,  a  state  cannot
use  its  taxing  powers  or  its  police  powers  to  discrimi-
nate  against  interstate  commerce,  and  by  the  same
token,  Congress  cannot  use  its ‘power  over  commerce  to
intrude  into  local  policies,

The  decisions  which  have  been  mentioned  are,  for
the  most  part,  concerned  with  state  police  and  taxing
measures,  but  that  may  be  attributed  to  the  fact  that
Congress  has  not  until  recently  attempted  to  interfere
in  matters  which  do  nct  concern  it.  Even  so,  these
decisions  are  contgpplling because  they  in terms  acknowl-
edge  the  exclusive  sovereignty  of the  states.  For  ex-
ample,  in  Kidd  v.  Pearson,  supra,  the  court  rested  its
opinion  upon  the  belief  that,  if  the  state  law  were
stricken  down,  the  jurisdiction  of  Congress  would  be
greatly  enlarged  at  the  expense  of  the  state.

This  same  argument  was  repeated  in  the  opinion
in  Heisler  v.  Thomas  Colliery  Co.,  et  al.,  260  U.  S.  245
(1922),  upholding  a  state  taxing  measure,  where  it  is
declared:

repel  its  acceptance.  If the  possibility,  or,  indeed,

Even  assuming  that  participation  by an  sidtiiaa
in interstate  commerce  might  subject  certain  of his
activities  to  congressional  regulation,  nevertheless  the
relationship  between  the  employer  and  his  employees
is purely  a  local  matter.  Although  an employee  may be
employed  in indirectly  assisting  the  movement  of inter-
state  commerce,  his  relationship  to  his  employer  is not
a  part  of commerce.  It is a  status  existing  wholly  within
_the  State,  whose  incidents,  such  as  wages,  hours  of
labor  and  the  like are  purely  domestic  in character.  (See
Carter  v.  Carter  Coal  Company,  298  U.  S. 238).  It may
be  true  that  congressional  regulation  of the  local  inci-
dents  of  employment  in  cases  involving  interstate  car-
riers  has been sustained,  where  the Court  has found  the
true  object  of Congress  to be the protection  of interstate
commerce.  But  there  is  no  precedent  for  interference
where  the real  purpose  of  the regulation  is deliberately
to interfere  in matters  which  are  not  the  concern  of the
Federal  Government.  If  the  proposition  should  be  ac-
cepted  that  the  most  insignificant  participation  in inter-
all of the activities  and all of
state  commerce  subjects
the  relationships  of  an  employer  to  congressional
measures,  then  there  would  be  nothing  to  prevent  con-
gressional  entry  into  the field of  fire  and  building  regu-
lations,  safety  requirements,  and  a  thousand  similar
measures,  local  in  character.  Yet  no  one  would  argue
that  regulations  governing  the  erection  and operation
of factories,  the hours  and wages  of employees,  and the
conditions  of  safe  employment  should  be  functions  of
the  central  government.

The  majority  opinion  in Carter  v.  Coal  Company,  et

that  labor  relations  are not im-or  a  part  of interstate

by Congress.  In this connection,  it  was safd:

“One  who  produces  or  manufactures  a commod-
ity,  subsequently  sold  and  shipped  by  him  in

ment  were  originally  intended  or  not,  has  engaged
in two distinct  and separate  activities.  So far as
he  produces  or  manufactures  a  commodity,  his
business
is purely  local.  So far as he sells  and ships,
or  contracts  to  sell  and  ship,  the  commodity  to
_  customers  in another  state,  he engages  in interstate
commerce.  In  respect  of  the  former,  he  is  subject
only  to  regulation  by  the  state;  in  respect  of  the
latter,  to regulation  only by the federal  government.
Utah  Power  &  L.  Co.  v.  Pfost,  286  U.  S.  165,  182.
Production  is not  commerce;  but  a  step  in-prepsra-
tion  for  commerce.  Chassaniol.v.  Greenwood,  291
U.  S. 584,  587.

We have  seen  that  the word  ‘commerce’  is  the

leading  up
of trade.’  Plainly,  the incidents

men, thé fixing  of their wages,  hours  of labor  and

and all constitute  intercourse  for

not of trade.  The latter
is a  thing  apart  from  the relation  of employer  and
