The case  of Texas  and New  Orleans  Raliroad  Co. o.
Brotherhood  of Railway  and  Steamship  Clerks,  et  al.,
281 U.  8. 548  (1930),  furnishes
no precedent  for the
National  Labor  Relations  Act.  This case arose  under
the Railroad  Labor  Act of 1926,  which  set up machinery
for  the  voluntary  adjustment  and  arbitration  of  labor
disputes  before  boards  of adjustment  and boards  of
arbitration.  The plaintiff  union  had a  controversy with
the  Railroad  before  a  board  of  adjustment;  pending
the  decision,  the defendant  railroad  created  a  rival
union,  by  indulging  in  alleged  coercive  and  oppressive
tactics.  The  union  brought  injunction  proceedings  under
the  section  of the  Act  which  declared  that  “representa-
tives,  for  the  purposes  this  Act,  shall  be designated
by the respective  parties  *  * *  without  interference,
influence  or  coercion  *
*.”  The  District  Court  ex-
pressly  held  that  the  statute  wes  constitutional  and
granted
an injunction.  The Circuit  Court  of Appeals
effirmed  the  District  Court,  stating:

*

“We  think  that  under  the  power  to  regulate
interstate  commerce  it  is  competent  for  Congress
to make  provision  for  the settlement  of  disputes
between  interstate  carriers  and  their  employees
with  reference  to rates of wages,  rules,  and working
conditions,  to  the  end  of  preventing  such  disputes
resulting  in  the  interruption  or  stopping  of  the
movement  in  interstate  commerce  of  freight  or
passengers.”  (33 F. (2d)  13, 16).

This  Court  accepted  the  argument  of  the  Circuit
Court  and sustained  the Act as a  regulation  of commerce
with  the  statement,  “Congress  may  facilitate  the ami-
the service
cable  settlement  of disputes  which  threaten
of the necessary  agents  of  interstate  transportation.”

We  submit  that  the  distinction  between  the  present
case  ancl  that  involved  in the Texas  & New  Orieans  Rail-

76

Argument.

road  Company  case is implicit  in the opinion  of this
Court.  The  transportation  of  persons  and  commodities
between  states  is,  of  course,  the  very  essence  of  inter-
state  commerce.  As  a  consequence,  congressional  regu-
lation  of rates,  transportation  facilities,  equipment,  per-
sonnel  and related  matters  has  been  uniformly  sustained.
It would  be indeed  difficult  to  find  a more  comprehensive
and  thorough  program  of  legislation  than  that  devel-
oped  in  the  Halls  of  Congress  to  govern  the  operations
of  carriers  and  communication  systems.  The  breadth
of  this  legislation  is  attributable  to  another  cause  as
well.  Railroads  are  classed  as  public  service  corpora-
tions  which,  by  virtue  of  their  public  obligations,  are
required  to  continue  operations  if  the  government  80
directs.  This  power  to  compel  continuity  of  operations
necessarily  implies  a  wide  latitude  to enact  all  manner
of  legislation  to  prevent  interruptions  and  to  increase
The  Railway
the  probabilities  of  continuous  service.
Labor  Act  of  1926  was  merely  a  part  of  a  general  pro-
gram.  It was  not  a  comprehensive  labor  statute,  but  a
device  to  promote  the  amicable  adjustment  of  disputes

threatening  the  continuity  of  carrier  operations.
cordingly,  the  admitted  power  of  Congress  to  compel
the  carriers  to operate  would  necessarily  imply  the  right
to enact  such  legislation.

:

DECISIONS  INVOLVING  CONGRESSIONAL  REGULATION  OF  THE

SO-CALLED  “STREAM  OF  COMMERCE”  ARE  INAPFIF  .

CABLE  IN  THE  PRESENT  CASE.

The  decision,  of the  National  Labor  Relations  Board

endeavored  to find  that  the  respondent’s  Aliquippa  Plant  |
is merely  a way  station  in the  transit  of commerce,  with
the  thought  of  justifying  the  application  of  the  Act  to
local  transactions,  because  they  are  merely  eddies  in
the stream  of commerce.  The  facts  which  we  have  stated
have  indicated  that  the  respondent’s  operations  are  not
merely  trifling  interruptions  of  the  movement  of  com-
merce.
Its  plant  at  Aliquippa  is  extensive  in  size  and
represents  a  very  substantial  investment  in  buildings,
machinery  and  equipment.
The  raw  materials  which
come  to  the  respondent  from  the  State  of  Pennsylvania
and  elsewhere  are  delayed  for  a long  period  of time  and,
after  being. subjected  to  the  manufacturing  processes
of the  respondent,  are  changed  substantially  as  to  char-
acter,  utility  and  value.
The  finished  products  which
emerge  are  to  a  large  extent  manufactured  without  ref-
erence  to  pre-existing  orders  and  contracts  and  are  en-
tirely  different  from  the  raw  materials  which  enter  at
the  other  end.  If importation  and  exportation  in  inter-
state  commerce  do  not  singly  transfer  purely  local  ac-
tivities  into  the  field  of  Congressional  regulation,  it
should  follow  that  their  combination  would  not  alter
the local  situation.

78

Argument.

in restraint  of trade. It appeared that 8

The origin  of the conception  of.a  stream  of com-
merce  may be traced to the case of Swift & Co. v.  United
States, 106 U. 8.  375  (1905),  which  involved  an  action
by the United States under the Sherman  Act to restrain
a  conspiracy
conspiracy  existed  among  six large meat  packing  houses
and  was  accomplished  through  a  combination  among
dealers  and commission  men in  the stockyards  to refrain
from bidding against each other.  The defendants  argued
strenuously  that all of their  activities  took place  within
the  confines  of  a  single  state.  The  Court  nevertheless
 tyok’a  broader  view  of the  facts  and  concluded  that  the
object  of the  conspiracy  was  to  monopolize  and  impede
the  movement  of  interstate  commerce  from  the  stock
farms  in  the  West  to the ultimate  consumer  in various
states.  There  was no need to refer to any other  factor
than  that  defendants  had  deliberately  designed  to  ob-
struct  the  movement  of commerce,  but  the  Court  never-
theless  made  the  statement  that:

‘When  cattle  are  sent  for  sale  from  a place  in
one  gtate,  with  the  expectation  that  they  will  end
their  transit,  after  purchase,  in another,  and  when
in effect they do so, with only the interruption  necea-
gary to find a purchaser at the stockyards,  and  when
this is a  typical,  constantly  recurring  course,  the
current  thus  existing  is a  current  of  commerce
among  the states,  and the purchase  of the cattle  is
a  pert  and incident  of  such  commerce.”  (pp.  398-
390)
Subsequently  in two  decisions,  Stafford  v.  Wallace,
U.  8.  495  (1922),  and  Chicago  Board  of Trade  v.
Olsen,  262 U.  8. 1 (1923),  the same conception  was ap-
sustain  Congressional  regulation  of certain  local
plied
activities.  The  Stafford case involved  the Stockyards
after a series  of elaborate
and Packers Act.  Congress,
investigations,  had found that the large packing  houses

Acgement:

oe

had taken  advantage  of their control  over the stoekyards.
and connected  facilities  to control  the prices  of cattle.
The stockyards  were,  of course,  centers  located  in the
larger  cities  where cattle  in transit  from western  states
were  temporarily  detained,  watered  and fed and some-
times  slaughtered.  Movs<  of  the sales from  producers to
dealers  and packers  took place while the cattle  were in
the yards,  being handled through  commission  agents.
The statute  enacted  by Congress  provided  comprehen-
sive regulation  of the yards,  connected  facilities,  the
fees  and  activities  of  commission  men  and  dealers  and
similar  subjects.  Although  the act applied  in terms  to
local  activities,  the  Court  found  that  these  activities
were  so  closely  connected  with  the  general  current of
commerce  that  they were  subject  to Congressional  regu-
lation.

|

The  Olsen  case  involved  the  Grain  Futures  Act,
which  provided  for  direct regulation  of the practices  of
grain  exchanges  and  their  members,  the  primary  object
being to control  dealings  in grain futures.  Similar  Con-
gressional  regulation  in the Future  Trading Act, through
the  device  of a  prohibitory  tax,  had  been  invalidated  in
plan of
Hill v. Wallace,  259 U.S. 44 (1922).  The second
Congress  to regulate  future trading  followed  after a
series of investigations  into abuses in future  trading
had led to the conclusion  that  such trading,  unless  sub-
jected  to regulation,  could have  disastrous  effects  upon
in grains.  In the Olsen case,  the Grain Fu-
commerce
tures Act was sustained,  although  the activities  of grain
exchanges  and their members  were purely  local in char-
acter,  because  these  activities,  unless  regulated,  consti-
tuted a  very  serious  threat  to the current  of  interstate
commerce  in grains  and because  the exchangea  were  in
the nature  of public utilities.  Cf.  Tagg  Bros.  & Moore-
head v. United  States,  280 U.  S. 420  (1930);  Tyson  &
Bro. v. Banton,  273 U.  8. 418  (1927).

80

Argument.

Advocates  of the legality  of the National  Labor Re-
__
lations  Act attribute  more  force  to this conception  of a
stream  of  commerce  than  the  decisions  establishing  it
warrant.  It  was useful  in conspiracy  cases because  it
(obviates  the necessity  of proving a direct restraint  upon
\an actual train of interstate  commerce.  In the  Olsen
and  Stafford  cases,  it served  a  somewhat  different  pur-
pose.  Both  cases  dealt  with  peculiar  situations;  they
involved  focal  points,  through  which  the  stream  of com-
merce  swept  on  its  way  from  producer  to  the  ultimate
consumer.  A stockyard  is  an  institution  which  assem-
bles  live  stock  from  various  portions  of the  country,  ar-
ranges  for  its  temporary  storage  and  then  sends  it  on
its way,  after  the shift  of ownership  has  been  made  from
The  grain  ex-
the  producer  to  the  packer  or  dealer.
change  which  was  involved  in  the  Olsen  case,  performs
a similar  function  for  grains.  Grains  are,  for  the  most
part,  warehoused  in  transit,  where  the  grain  exchange
is located,  and  the exchange  serves  as  a market  place  for
shifting  title  and  arranging  the  final  destination  of  the
produce.  In other  words,  both  stockyards  and  exchanges
are  instrumentalities  of  commerce,  in  much  the  same
way  as  the  actual  carriers  of commodities.  This  factor
was  emphasized  in both  the  Olsen  case  and  the  Stafford
case.  Thus,  in the  Stafford  case,  we  find  the  statement:
“The  stockyards  are but  a  throat  through  which
flows,  and the transactions
which  occur
the current
therein  are only incident  to this current  from the
West  to the East,  and from  one state  to another.
Such  transactions  cannot  be  separated  from  the
movement  to which  they  contribute  and  necessarily
take  on  its character.”  (p. 516)
Similar  language  appears  in the Olsen  case,  in the

declaration  that:

“The  sales on the  Chicago  Board  of Trade  are

Argument.

$1

to the continuity  of the flow
just  as indispensable
of wheat  from  the West  to the mills and distributing
points  éf the East and Europe,  as are the Chicago
sales  of cattle  to the flow  of  stock  toward  the feed-
ing places  and  slaughter  and  packing  houses of the
East.”  (p. 36)

It  would  be  impossible  to  extend  the  language  of
the  decisions  beyond  their  immediate  facts,  without  en-
tirely  obliterating  the  distinction  between  that  which
is  interstate  commerce  and  that  which  is  not.  From  a
speculative  viewpoint,  almost  every  business  partici-
pates,  to a limited  extent,  at least,  in the  general  current
of commerce.  This  participation  is merely  magnified  in
those  idustries  which  import  and  export  materials  be-
fore  and  after  their  manufacturing  or  other  operations.
But  such  industries  are  not  instrumentalities  of  com-
merce  in  the  same  sense  as  stockyards  or  grain  ex-
changes  and  to  hold  that  they  are  would  be  to  over-
emphasize  the  current  of  commerce  at  the  expense  of
local  authority.
It  is,  therefore,  reasonable  to  believe,
from  a-careful  consideration  of  both  the  Olsen  and
Stafford  decisions  and  from a realization  of the  lengths
to  which  the  conception  would  otherwise  be driven,  that
the  doctrine  is to be  confined  to  instrumentalities  of the
broad  movement  of  interstate  commerce  and  not  to  be
tortured  into  application  to  local  activities  which  have
merely  a  remote  bearing  on  interstate  trade.
Conse-
quently,  we  find  that  the  decision  in Arkadelphia  Milling
Co.  v.  8t.  Louis  Southwestern  Railways  Co.,  249  U.  8.
134  (1919),  which  came  long  after  the  Swift  case,  ex-
pressly  declared  that  the  conception  of  a  “stream  of
commerce”  did  not  change  the  character  of  local  man-
ufacturing  operations.

There  is  another  distinguishing  factor  involved  in
the cases which we have just seen.  Not only are the

Argument.
Bol
etockyarda  and  grain  exchanges,  in practical  effect,  in-
gtrumentalities  of interstate  commerce,  but  in bath  in-
stances,  Congress  had  enacted  comprehensive  regula-
tions to prevent the continuance  of constantly  recurring
which  had and were  designed  to have direct
and immediate  effects  on  interstate  commerce.  The  dis-
tinction which  we have  emphasized  throughout  comes  to
the fore  again,  to  set  these  exceptional  decisions  apart
in their  true  light,  The  Court  perceived  the  intimate
connection  between  the local activities  involved  and the
hroad  movement  of interstate  commerce  and  it recog.
niggd that the primary  object of Congress  was to pro-
tect the interstate  movement  of grains and cattle against
direct  restraints  and interferences.  But  that  is no  pre-
cedent  for  arguing  that  this  Court  should  sustain  Con-  —
gressional regulation of remote  local  activities where  the
true  abject  of the  statute  is to  regulate  such  activities
and  not  to  assist  the  movement  of commerce.

and Loewe  v.  Lawlor, 208 U. S. 274  (1908).  From  these
decisions the petitioner  induces  a double-edged  principle,
viz., that Congress  may deal with labor disputes,  either  .
or prospectively,  (1)  where there is a direct

This  is very  clear  from  the  Coronado  case,  for, on the

Coal  Co., et al.,  259 U.  8. 344
Mine  Workers  v.  Coronado
(1922) ) the record showed merely a persistent and un-
lawful interference  with the production  of the plaintiff's
coal mines  by members  of the defendant  unions.  Sincé
the record  was barren  of any evidence  of an intention  to
restrain  the movement  of commerce,  the Court  said  that
interference  with production,  regardless  of its ultimate
effect  on interstate  commerce,  would  not alone  suffices  to
constitute  a  violation  of the Sherman  Act.  As a result,
the case  was  remanded  with  the statement  that

“Coal  mining  is not interstate  commerce,  and:
the power  of Congress  does  not extend  to its regu-
lation
247 U. 8.
as such.  In Hammer  v.  Dagenhart,
251,  272,  we said:  ‘The  making  of goods  and the
mining  of  coal  are not  commerce,  nor does the fact
that  these  things  are to be afterwards  shipped  or
used in interstate  commerce,  make  their  production
a  part  thereof.  Delaware,  Lackawanna  &  Western
R.  R.  Co.  v.  Yurkonis,  238 U.  S. 439.’  Obstruction to
coal  mining  is not a direct  obstruction  to interstate
commerce  in coal, although  it, of course,  may affect
it  by reducing  the amount  of  coal to be carried  in
that  commerce.”  ( pp.  407-408)

At  the second  trial,  there  was both direct  and in-
ferential  evidence  of  a  plan  to check  the  movement  of
coal  into  other  states.
The  direct  evidence  indicated
that  the striking  unions  had planned  to prevent  the coal
from  competing  with the product  of  union mines in other
states,  while  the inferential  proof  consisted  of evidence
that  the strikers’  activities  had in fact  seriously  re-
strained  the  interstate  movement  of coal  [See  Coronado
Coal  Company  v.  United  Mine  Workers,  268  U.  S.  295,

808,  310, (1925)].  On the basis of  the new evidence,
this Court found that the transactions  were within the
prohibition  of the Sherman  Act as 4  conspiracy  with -
intent  to  refrain  interstate  commerce.  The  petitioner
is clearly  in error in arguing  that the Coronado  cases
stand  for  the propouition  thet the likelihood  of imped-
ing the movement  of  commerce will of itself satisfy the
statutory  requirement  of  intent.  Of  course,  the  intent
of the conspirators  to restrain  commerce  may be in-
ferred from  its actual stoppage on the theory  thet the

quences of their acts, but this does not  mean that there
is no  distinction  between  a  deliberate  interference  with
comnierce  (which  is obviously  direct)  and  an  act  which
might lead to a  disturbance  of the stream,  (whith  is,
of its very nature,  indirect).

A comperison  of the  second  Coronado  case,  supra,
with  the decigion  in United  Leat\er  Workers  v.  Herkert

shown  that  the  strike  seriously  impaired  the  complain-
ant’s  operations  and  thereby  diminished  its  purchases
of raw  materials  from  other  states,  as well  as  its  ship-
nient  of finished  articles  across  state  lines.  However,

products,  it was  held that  the  effect  on  interstate  com-
merce  was  too  remote  to warrant  punishment  under  the
Sherman  Act.  See also Industrial  Association  v.  United
States,  268 U. S. 64  (1925).

charges  might have led to dissatisfaction,  which  might

only are there several steps in the relation  between  the

with  boycotts,  but  with  the  relationship  between  this

respondent’s  payroll,  with back  pay,  and not to the  relief
of the  stricken  channels  of commerce.

The  petitioner  is  evidently  disturbed  by  the  tenu-
ous  character  of  the  succession  of  possibilities  upon
which  it has  relied  to  support  its  findings  of  fact,  be-
_  cause  it  argues  that  the  connection  between  the  dis-
charge  of employees  and the stoppage  of interstate  com-
merce  has  been  established  iur  it by the  declaration  of
Congress.  We  submit  that  the  involved  trail  of reason-
ing which  Congress  itself  has  followed  in  ordek  to  link
labor disputes  with  the movement  of trade,  as  it  appears
in the  Declaration  of  Policy  which  prefaces  the  Act,  is
a convincing  demonstration  of the remoteness  of the con-
nection.  The  extended  syllogism  indicates  that  Con-
gress  has  indeed  recognized  its  remoteness  and  has
hoped  to  draw  it closer  by  an  elaborate  outline  of  its
premises.  A similar  declaration  of policy  was made  in

of Trade  v. Olsen,

the Grain Futures  Act,  which  was upheld  in Ohéoage
22  U. 8.1  (1923),  but the
Board
had under-
there  was much closer.  Congress
connection
taken  elaborate  investigations  and  had  found  that  the
movement  of  grain was so sensitive  to dealings  in grain
futures  that  it  was a  comparatively
simple  matter,
through  such  dealings,  to  control  supply  and  demand
and therefore  prices.  There  was  only one step in that
argument.  Future  trading  had been  used  to produce
shortages  and  abundances  of grain,  the  object  being to
profit by manipulated  prices.  Because  of the long study
which  Congress  had  devoted  to  the  problem,  its  finding
was accepted.  But even there,
of  an intimate  connection
as a  careful  study  of  the opinion  will reveal,  this Court
reviewed  in  great  detail  the  evidence  which  was  placed
before  Congress  and  concurred  in the findings  that  Con-
gress  had  made.  The  decision  is  not  an  authority  for
the  proposition  that  a  declaration  by  Congress  of  the
need  of regulation  will  automatically  justify  its action.

This  is  well  illustrated  by  Carter  v.  Carter  Coal
Company,  supra.  The  issues  in  that  case  were  almost
identical  with  those  of  the  present  case.  The  Bitumi-
nous  Coal  Conservation  Act,  like  the  Labor  Act,  was
prefaced  with  a declaration  of congressional  policy  that
the  labor  provisions  of  the  Act  were  essential  to  pre-
vent  labor  disputes  at  the  mines  and  therefore  to
forestall  interruptions  of  the  movement  of  Interstate
of the congressional

interstate  commerce  should  be  regular,  continuous  and
free  of interruptions;  that  the predominant
portion  of

of the Carter case is

The  petitioner’s  argument  seeks  to  convince  this
Court  that  Carter  y Carter  Cool  Company,  aupra,  is in-
apposite  because  it  was  primariJy  concerned  with  the
wage- and hour-fixing  provisions of the Rituminons  Coal
Conservation  Act.  Our analysis  of the labor provisions
of  that  statute  has  indicated  that  they  were  in  many

o

Argument.

respects  identical  with  the  provisions  of  the  National
Labor  Relations  Act.  Prohibitions  against  support  of
a labor organization by an employer  and  against  requir-
ing an employee  to join a  so-called company  union  are
common  to both  Acts.  Although  the terminology  is dif-
ferent,  both  statutes  confirmed  the  right  of  collective
bargaining  and  authorized  an  administrative  agency
to  select  or  certify  the  exclusive  representatives  of  the
employees.  As  a  matter  of  fact,  the  Coal  Act  did  not
purport  to  2x  either  wages  or.  hours  of  labor  except
through  the  medium  of collective  bargaining,  for  it pro-
vided  that  wages  and  hours  should  be  determined  by
collective  agreements  between  the  representatives  of the
producers  and  the  representatives  of  a  majority  of  the
workers.  Although  this  feature  of  the  Coal  Act  was
condemned  as an arbitrary  delegation  of power,  the prin-
cipal  reason  for  invalidating  the  statute  was  that  labor
relations  and matters  of collective  bargaining  are  within
the jurisdiction of the  States  and not of Congress.  This
is clear  from  the  language  which  we  have  quoted  from
the  majority  opinion  in the  Carter  case.  The  argument
was made  in support  of the Coal Act that it  was designed
to eliminate  “the evils which  come  from  the struggle  be-
tween  employers  and  employees  over  the  matter  of
wages,  working  conditions,  the  right  of collective  bar-
gaining,  etc.,  and  the  resulting  strikes,  curtailment  and
°.”
irregularity
The conclusive  answer  was that  these evils were  “local
evils,  over  which  the Federal  Government  has no  legis-
lative  control  The relation  of  employer  and  employees
ie a local relation.”

of production  and effect  on prices  *

*

This Court  has said, again  and again,  that if the
bars  be let down  to labor  legislation  under  the guise  of
a  regulation  of commerce,  there  is an end to the sov-
ereignty  of the states  as defined  by the constitution.

Lr emma

oF.

Tue  CENTRAL  GOVERNMENT  IS ONE OF  SPECIFIED  POWEES,
WHICH  CAN  BE  ENLARGED  ONLY  BY  THE  WILL  OF  THE
PEOPLE  AND  NOT BY THE USURPATION  OF CONGRESS  OR THE
AGENCY.
SELF-SERVING  FINDINGS  OF  AN ADMINISTRATIVE

Of course  a  finding  of Congress  is entitled  to some
weight  but  it is difficult  to believe  that the Court will
accept  it  as controlling,  if it  runs  counter  to the many
decisions  that  local  activities  are  not  commerce,  al
though  succeeded  or  preceded  by a  movement  in com-
merce,  and  that  industrial  relations  have  no  such  con-
nection  with  interstate  commerce  as  would  bestow  upon
Congress  the power  to govern  them.  (Cf.  St.  Joseph
Stockyards  v.  United  States,  298 U. 3. 38  (1936),  supra).
The  same  distorted  path of argument  was  employed
by Congress  to justify  the Agricultural  Adjustment  Act.  -
This  Court  was urged  to find that the decrease  in farm
prices,  incident  to the  depression,  had  seriously  affected
the  national  income  and  therefore  the  general  welfare.
In  other  words,  it was  contended  that  farming  had  be-
come  a  national  problem,  just as it  is contended  in the
instant  case,  that  the  respondent’s  relations  with  its
employees  have  been  converted  into  a matter  of national
concern  because  of  the size  of the respondent’s  opera-
tions  and  the  magnitude  of the steel  industry  in gen-
eral.  The tremendous  maas  of data  and opinion  received
in evidence  by the Board  to show  the past history  of the
steel industry,  and  its industrial  relations  had this  ob-
ject in view;  to  convince  this  Court  that the need  of
organization  in ‘ix, steel industry  is a  matter  of national
importance.  The  majority  opinion  in United  States  v.
et al., 207 U. S. 1 (1986),
Butler,
makes  short  shrift of
this  argument  in the simple  statement  that:

“It  does  not help  to declare  that  local  condé-
tions throughout  the nation  have created  a  situation
of national  concern ; for this is but to say that  when-

88

Argument.

.

ever  there  is  a  widespread  similarity  of  local  con-
ditions,  Congress  may  ignore  constitutional  limita-
tions upon its own powers  and usurp those reserved
to  the states.”  (pp. 74-75)
We  believe  we  may  justifiably  conclude  that  the
Court  will  recognize  that  Congress  cannot  use  the  con-
ception that  transactions  “affecting  commerce”  are  sub-
ject  to  its  authority  as  a  pretext  to  interfere  in  matters
which  are  not  a  part  of  interstate  commerce;  that  it
will  confine  the  doctrine  of  the  grain  exchange  and
stockyards  cases  to  transactions  which  are  intimately
connected  with  the  movement  of  commerce,  or  directly
obstruct  and  impede  it;  and  that  it will  not  justify,  as  a
regulation  of  commerce,  a  statute  which  piles  specula-
tion  upon  speculation  to  attain  its  ends.  There  is  no
better  way  to  demonstrate  our  conclusions  than  by  a
reference  to  the  opinion  of Chief  Justice  Hughes  in  the
Schechter  case:

“The  argument  of  the  government  proves  too
much.  If  the  federal  government  may  determine
the  wages  and  hours  of  employees  in  the  internal
commerce  of  a  state,  because  of  their  relation  to
cost  and  prices  and  their  indirect  effect  upon  inter-
state  commerce,  it would  seem  that  a  similar  con-
trol  might  be  exerted  over  other  elements  of  cost,
also  affecting  prices,  such  as  the  number  of  em-
ployees,  rents,  advertising,  methods  of  doing  busi-
ness,  etc.  All  the  processes  of production  and  dis-
tribution  that  enter  into  cost  could  likewise  be  con-
trolled.  If  the cost of doing  an  intrastate  business.
is in  itself  the  permitted  object  of  federal  control,
the extent  of the regulation  of cost  would  be a ques-
tion  of discretion  and  not  of  power.”  (p.  549)

te

POINT  HL
The Procedural  Provisions  of the National  Labor Rela-
tions Act Are Invalid,  in That They Violate  Section
2 of  Article  III of  the Constitution  and the Seventh
to the Constitution.
Amendment

Although  it may  be  asserted  that  the  petitioner  is
merely  a  fact-finding  administrative  agency,  an  exami-.
nation  of  its  functions  will  indicate  that  in the  present
case,  at  least,  it has  assumed  judicial  powers  and  pur-
ported  to exercise  jurisdiction  over  a controversy  which
is  normally  subject  to  the  powers  of  the  constitional
courts.

a

:

Under  Section  10  (b)  of the  Act,  the  petitioner  is
empowered  to  hear  and  determine  charges  that  an  em-
ployer  has  engaged  in  unfair  labor  practices,  including
complaints  filed  by  employees  based  upon  charges  of
discrimination.  Under  Section  10  (a)  the  power of the
Board  to prevent  unfair  labor practices  is made  exclusive
and  under  Section  10  (c),  in  controversies  between  em-
ployee  and employer  over  discriminatory  discharges  and
demotions,  the  petitioner  is authorized,  after  hearing,  to
render  affirmative  relief,  by  requiring  the  employer  to
reinstate  the  complaining  employee,  with  or  without
In  this  respect,  the
compensation  for  loss  of  wages.
petitioner  is  given  exclusive  jurisdiction  over  private
quarrels  between  employees  and  their  employer.  and
it  has, in fact, assumed  such  jurisdiction  in the instant
case.

The  procedure  ordained  by the Act,  in controversies
like  the  present,  requires  that  a hearing  shall  be  held
and  evidence  received,  following  which  the  petitioner  is
given  authority  to  make  findings  of  fact  and  to  issue
affirmative  as  well  as  negative  orders.  .The  provisions
for  review  appear  in  Sections  10  (e)  and  10  (f),  the

Argument.

490
former  permitting  the Board to apply to the Circuit
Courts  of  Appeal  for  the  enforcement  of  its  orders
(and,  in  exceptional  cases,  to  the  District  Courts),
and the latter  providing  for similar  review  by the Cir-
cuit  Courts  of Appeal  at  the instance  of parties  ag-
grieved  by the Board’s  orders.  In either  case, the juris-
diction  of the Court in which the petition is filed is made
exclusive  and the decision  of the reviewing  Court is
made  on  the  basis  of  the  record  before  the  Board,  the
findings of the Board on the facts being made conclusive,
if supported  by eviderrce.  Only  those  objections  which
have  been  urged  before  the  Board  are  available  in  the
reviewing  court.  From  this  summary,  it  is  apparent
that  the Board  is vested  with  exclusive  original  juris-
diction of complaints  against  an employer,  the appeal
to the reviewing  court being in all respects  similar  to an
appeal  from  a  constitutional  court  and  subject  to  the
same  limitations  as to the scope  of the review.

The  procedure  which  the  statute  prescribes  was
followed  with  particularity  in the  present  case.  Written
charges  were made by the thirteen  complaihing  em-
ployees,  in  which  it  was  alleged  that  they  had  been
wrongfully  discharged  or  demoted  by  the  respondent.
The  petitioner  assumed  jurisdiction  of  the  controversy  ~
and  issued  a  complaint,  calling  upon  the  respondent  to
@le a  written  answer,  meeting  the allegations  of  the
Board’s  pleading.  An answer  was filed and the Board
to  hear and  determine  the facts  with  respect
to  the  controversy  between  the  complaining  employees
and the respondent.  Its decision  found  that  ten of the
complainants  had been wrongfully  discharged  and di-
rected  the respondent  to reemploy  them,  with full com-
pensation  for lost pay.  The petitioner  then  applied  to
the Circuit  Court of Appeals  for an order enforcing  its

|

Board’s petition  upon the basis of the written  transcript

101

of the record which had been certified  to the Cireuis
Court.

that  the Labor
it  may be suggested
Act transcends  the common  law in requiring  the restora-
tion  of  employees  discharged  for  union  activities,  an
action  for  wages  after  wrongful  discharge  is clearly  the
function-of  common  law  courts.  In this  case, the peti-

ment  of  wages  to  the  complaining  employees,  thereby
entering  against  the  respondent  an  order  which  is, in all

be urged  that  this is permissible  under  the decision  in
Texas  & New  Orleans  Railroad  Company  v.  Brotherhood
of Railway  & Steamship  Clerks,  281  U.S.  548  (1930),
which is discussed in detail in another point in this brief,
but it is to be observed  that  that  decision  did not involve
statutory  procedure,  as in the present  case; but was con-
cerned  solely  with  the  power  of  a  court  to  protect  its
injunctive  processes  by requiring  the defendant  to purgé
itself  of contempt  of the  decree  through  the  restoration
of the status  quo.

The petitioner  has endeavored  to evade  the require-
ments  of the Seventh  Amendment  by comparing  the
to a suit in equity.  In its  brief in
present  controversy
the Associated  Press  case  (No.  365),  it is argued
that  the restoration  of lost pay is merely  “an incident
of the  equitable  remedy  for the prevention  of unfair
labor practices”,  and &  comparison  is drawn  between  the
present  proceedings  and other equitable  actions.  A sim-
ilar construction  is placed  upon  the statute  in the dis-
senting  opinion  of Judge  Garrecht,  in National  Labor
Relations  Board  v.  Mackay  Radio  & Telegraph  Co.  (C.

Argument.
102  |
C. A. 9th, decided January  11, 1937), in which it is said
that “this is a  proceeding  in equity and the primary re
lief  sought  is the  reinstatement  of  the  discharged  em-
ployees  and  the  award  for  wages at the  regular  rate  of
salary  for  the  time  out  by reason  of  the  wrongful  dis-
charge  is  an  incident  to  the  equitable  relief  granted.”
In other  words,  the  petitioner’s  argument  is that  a  jury
trial  is not essential  in the  present  controversy,  because
it is in the  nature  of an  equitable  action  to  restore  the
status of the complaining  employees,  the  remedy  being
a  mandatory  injunction,  with  damages  awarded  as  an
a
incident  of  complete  relief.

If the  petitioner’s  argument  be  accepted,  its  neces-
sary  implication  is that  the  procedure  prescribed  by the
Act  is in  derogation  of  Section  2 of  Article  Il  of the
Constitution  which  ordains  that  “The  Judicial  Power
shall  extend  to all cases,  in Law  and  Equity,  arising  un-
der  this  Constitution,  the  Laws  of  the  United  States
*  ©”  Can  there  be  any  doubt  that  the  Labor  Act
*
has  sought  to vest  the  powers  of a  constitutional  court,
sitting  in equity,  in a quasi-judicial  bureau  which  has  no
standing  before  the Constitution?

In this  connection  we  submit  that  the  construction
of  the  Constitutional  clause  in  Crowell  v.  Benson,  285
U.  S. 22  (1932)  is  applicable,  and  that  the  procedural
requirements  of the  Act are  fatal  to its validity,  because
they  make  no  provision  for  and  in fact deny a trial  de
‘movo before  a  constitutional  court on jurisdictional  is-
sues.  In the Benson  case,  the Court  had before  it pro-
ceedings  under  the  Longshoremen’s  and  Harbor  Work-
ers’  Compensation  Act,  involving  a claim  for  compensa-
tion by an injured  employee.  In the present  case  the
elaim is by a discharged  employee  for restoration  of his |
employment,  with  compensation  for  lost  pay.  In  each

a  annem

ing grace,  but,  on  the contrary,  as  we  have  pointed  out,

itself.  The  Circuit.  Court  is  not  empowered  to  hear

being  confined  to  the  written  transcript,  subject  to  fur-

urged  before  the  Board  are  available  and  in  that  the
findings  of the  Board  are  made  conclusive.

The petitioner  may argue that, in the instant  case,
the award of an order of restoration  of employment  with

Argument.

POINT  IV.

the United  States.  —
As  the  statement  of  facts  has  indicated  (page:  3
to  7  supra),  the gravamen  of  the  complaint  of  the
National  Labor  Relations  Board  is the  alleged  wrongful
discharge  of ten production  employees  of the respondent.
As  we  have  pointed  out,  the  decision  of the ‘Board  has  .
andeavored  to  color  these  charges  by surrounding  them
with  other  findings  that  the  respondent  has  discrimi-
nated  against  the  Amalgamated  Association  of  Iron,
Steel  arid  Tin  Workers.  These  findings,  which  might
be  termed  the  atmosphere  of  the  petitioners  decision,
are  not  justified  by  any  competent  evidence,  being  al-
most  entirely  based  upon  conditions  and  transactions
alleged  to  have  occurred  long  before  the  enactment  of
the  National  Labor  Relations  Act.  The  Board  has  also
provided  atmosphere  for  its  decision  by  investigating
the  past  history  of  industry  in  its  attitude  toward  and
relations  with  labor  organizations.  The  great  mass  of
theoretical  data  and  opinion  evidence  received  by  the
Board  on  this  point  is by no  means  competent  evidence
against  the  respondent.  The  procedure  adopted  by the
Board  in  receiving  this  evidence  in  a  joint  hearing  in-»
volving  the  respondent,  Wheeling  Steel  Corporation  and,
Crucible  Steel  Company,  is an  implied  admission  that  it
is not germarie  to the actual issues  of  this case.  The
history of past labor disputes,  involving  companies  other
than the respondent,  can have no legitimate bearing upon
the attitude  of the respondent  toward  its employees.

i

Argument.

A.

107
PY

THE  DECISIONS  OF THIS  COURT  IN ADAIR  v.  UNITED  STATES
AND  OCOPPAGE  v.  KANSAS  ARE DETERMINATIVE IN
THE  PRESENT  CASE.
The real issue  in the present  case,  from  a  factual
standpoint,  is the  alleged  commission  by the  respondent
of unfair  labor  practices  ix  the  discharge  of  union  em-
ployees.  The  portion  of the  statute  involved  is Section
8  (3),  which  prohibits  discrimination  in  regard  to  hire
or  tenure  of employment  or  of any  term  or  condition  of
employment  to  encourage  or  discourage  membership  in  —
any  labor  organization.  We  believe  that  this  provision
constitutes  an  arbitrary  interference  with  the  respond-
ent’s  freedom  of contract  and  therefore  violates  the  due
process  requirements  of  the  Fifth  Amendment  to  the
Federal  Constitution.

Adair  v.  United  States,  208  U.  S. 161  (1908)  clearly
declared  that  Congress,  in the  exercise  of its  regulatory
power  over  interstate  commerce,  must  obey  the  dictates
of  due  process.  More  recently,  in  the  Railway  Pension
case,  (Railroad  Retirement  Board  v.  The  Alton  Railroad
Co.  et al., 295  U.  S.  330  (1935)  ), and  then  in a decision
invalidating  the Frazier-Lemke  Farm  Relief  Act,  (Louis-
ville  Joint  Stock  Land  Bank  v.  Radford,  295  U.  S.  555
(1935)  ), the  Court  has  found  Congressional  legislation
vulnerable  because  of  its  unwarrantéd  infringement  of
the  properties  and  liberties  of the  people.  In the  latter
decision,  speaking  through  Mr.  Justice  Brandeis,  the
Court  declared:  “The  bankruptcy  power,  like  the  other
great  substantive  powers  of Congress,  is subject  to the
Fifth  Amendment.”  In the  former  decision,  the  Court
declared,  in  substance,  as in the  Adair  case,  that  the
Railway  Pension  Act  not  only was  an  excessive  exercise
of power  over interstate  commerce,  but also was in plain
disregard  of due  process  requirements.

.

See Butchers  Union  Slaughterhouse  Co.  v.  Crescent  City
Live-Stock  Co.,  111  U.  S.  746  (1884),  and  Algeyer  v.
Louisiana,  165  U. S. 578  (1897).

These rights  have not been enforced  in derogation
of the Government’s  power to enact police measures  far.

citizens.  Consequently,  State  laws  regulating  hours  of

employees  and to prevent  fraud  and oppression.  But
once  the Government  has gone beyond  police  measures
and deliberately  interfered  with freedom  of contract,  to
force  its  temporary  economic  conceptions  upon  its citi-
sens, the Court has scrutinised  its legislative  efforts  with
the greatest  of care.  The present  statute  clearly  falls

parties to such an extent  as to render  them subject  to

The  provisions  of  the  Labor  Act  which  are  in  cone

troversy  in  the  present  case  present  a  much  greater
menace  to  the  employer’s  freedom  of  contract  than  the
enactments  which  were  denied  validity  in  the  Coppage
In  the  Adair  case  the  Court  had  be-
and  Adair  cases.
fore  it, a  Federal  statute  prescribing,  as  a  criminal  of-
fense,  the  discharge  of  an  employee  because of  union
activities,  while  the  Kansas  statute,  which  was involved
in the  Coppage  case,  merely  prohibited  employment  con-
tracts  which  required  the  employee  to renounce  his  mem-
bership  in  a  labor  organizafton.
The  National  Labor
Relations  Act  not  only  prohibits  the  discharge  of  em-
ployees  because  of  union  activities,  but  it also  pretends
to  give  the  petitioner  authority  to  require  the  employer
to  restore  employment.  The  dangerous  implications  of
such  procedure,  which  will  be  discussed  subsequently
in their  application  to  the  facts  of the  present  case,  are
apparent.  The  employer  may  be  compelled  to  continue
a  relationship  which  is  not  of  his  own  seeking  and  his
discretion  in  hiring  and  retaining  employees  is  com-
pletely  overridden.

The  Labor  Board  contends  that  the  principle  of
these  decisions  has been  repudiated  sub silentio  in Texas
& New  Orleans  Railroad  Co.  v.  Brotherhood  of Railway
and Steamship  Clerks,  281 U.  S. 548  (1930).  A careful
examination of the facts of that decision,  beginning  with
the several  proceedings  in the District  Court,  is con-
Vincing  that it has no application  to the present  case

Argument,

111

*

The  Texas  &  New  Orleans  Railroad  case  arose  under  the
Railway  Labor  Act of 1926  which,  as we have  stated,
set  up  the  machinery  for  the  adjustment  of disputes  as
to  wages,  hours  of labor  and  conditions  of employment.
The  statute  did  not  prohibit  or  punish  the  refusal  of an
employer  to  employ  union  members,  but  provided  for
the  selection  of representatives  by the carriers  and  their
employees  for  purposes  of  adjustment  and  arbitration
under  the Act  and declared  that  “representatives,  for the
purposes  of  this  Act,  shall  be  designated  by the  respec-
tive  parties  *
*  without  interference,  influence  or
*  *.”  The  plaintiff  organization  applied  for
coercion  *
an increase  of  wages  and  after  denial,  the  matter  was
referred  to  the  United  States  Board  of Mediation.  The
bill  for  an  injunction  alleged  that  the  plaintiff  was  the
authorized  representative  of  the  employees  and  that
while  the  controversy  was  pending  before  the  Board
of Mediation,  the  defendant  undertook  the  formation  of
a  plant  union  completely  subject  to  its  control  and  had,
in  various  ways,  deliberately  attempted  to  influence,  in-
timidate  and  coerce  members  of  the  plantiff  to  with-
draw  in  order  that  negotiations  might  be  had  with  the
new  union  instead  of  the  plaintiff.  The  District  Court,
after  a  hearing,  granted  an  injunction.
Pending  final
hearing,  the  defendant  proceeded  to  disregard  the  in-
junction  and  the  plaintiff  brought  contempt  proceedings.
The  lower  Court  found  that  the defendant  had  deliberate-
ly  violated  the  restraining  order  and  had  by  various
means  coerced  and  restrained  its employees  in the  selec-
tion  of  a  representative  under  the  Act.
One  of  the
means  complained  of  was  the  discharge  of  members
of  the  plaintiff  union.  The  Court  held  that  the  statute
was  constitutional,  that  the  injunction  was  properly
issued  and  that  it  was  violated  by  the  defendant’s
conduct.  It therefore  entered  a decree  requiring  the  de-
fendant  to purge  itself  of contempt  by restoring  the

113

status  quo.  When the case came  on to final hearing,  the
District  Court,  in an opinion  published  in 25 F.  (2d)
876,  reaffirmed  its previous  position,  denying  a  motion
The
of  the  defendant  to  vacate  the  contempt  order.
opinion  of the  Court  clearly  indicates  that  im  ordering
the  restoration  of  the  union  employees,  it  was  merely
directing  the  defendant  to  purge  itself  of its  contempt
of  the Court’s  order.  As to this the District  Court de-
clared :

“As  to  the  restoration  of  Harper,  Walker,
Stotzer,  Hudson,  and  Ginsberg:  this  also  was  mere-
ly an  act  of  restoration  of  status,  commanded  in
order  to  wipe  out  the  effects  and  results  of  diso-
bedience.  Nothing  in  the  order  attempted  to,  nor
could  it,  fix  any  time  or  conditions  to  the  continu-
It  merely  declared
ing in place  of these persons.
that  the  severance  of  these  relations  and  the  dis-
turbance  of the status  quo as to them,  which  the
court  found  from  the  evidence  had  been  brought
about  through  a  deliberate  violation  of  the  injunc-
tion  order  of  the  court,  must  be  undone,  and  infer-
entially  that  it should  not  occur  again  for  the  same
or  similar  reasons.”  (p. 877)

The  same  construction  was  placed  on  the  Trial
Court’s  decision  by the  Circuit  Court  of Appeals  in 33 F.
(2)  13  (1929).

On  the  point  of  constitutionality,  the  theory  of  the
Court is clear.  It conceived
District
that  the Railroad,
as a  public  service  corporation,  was required  to continue
operations  so long az the government  should  direct.  The
Reilway  Labor  Act of 1926 was held a  proper  exercise
of the police  power of Congress,  because  it  was a  means
exleulated  to prevent  the interruption  of service.  Since
it was proper  to provide  a  Board  of Mediation  for the
edjustment  of  labor  disputes,  Congress  had  implied

Argument.

148

power  to provide  means for the selection  of representa-
tives to appear  before  the  Board.  This clearly  appears
from  the statement  of  the District  Court  in its opin-
ion  at  24  F.  (2d)  426:

“Nor  can  there  longer  be  any  doubt  that  Con-

gress  had the power  to, and that  it must,  in
interest  of public  peace and safety,  make  certain,
the first step  in negotiations  between  the railroad
employer  and  employee  (who  have  long  since  come
to be recognized,  as to this instrument  of  interstate
commerce  in  their  hatids,  not  as  private  persons
having  the  right  to  exercise  ‘liberty  through  sheer
antipathy,’  but  as  trustees  of  the  public),  that  rep-
resentatives  of  the  railroad  companies  should  not
meet  representatives  of  the  employees,  nominally
elected  by them,  but  in fact  under  the  influence  and
control  of the railroad  companies.  I therefore  easily
find  that  the  legislation  in  question  was  not  only
within  the  power  of  Congress  to  enact,  but  that  it
should  be  liberally  construed  and  applied,  so  as  to
give effect  to the  paramount  public  convenience  sub-
served  by it.”  (p. 431)

On  appeal,  in  33  F.  (2d)  13,  the  Circuit  Court  of
Appeals  affirmed  the  doctrine  of  the  trial  court,  in  the
following  language:

‘We  think  that  under  the  power  to  regulate  in-
terstate  commerce  it  is  competent  for  Congress  to
make  provision  for  the  settlement  of  disputes  be-
tween  interstate  carriers  and  their  employees  with
reference  to rates  of wages,  rules,  and  working  con-
ditions,  to  the  end  of  preventing  such  disputes  re-
sulting  in the  interruption  or  stopping  of the  move-
ment  in  interstate  commerce  of  freight  or  passen-
gers.  Wilson  v.  New, 243 U.  8. 332, 37 S. Ct. 208, 61
L. Ed. 755,  L. R. A. 1917E,  938,  Ann.  Cas. 1918A,
1024.”  (p.  16)

114

Argument.

This  Court,  as  we  have  stated  at  another  point  in  this
brief  (see  supra  page  76),  adopted  the  arguments  of
the lower  courts  in sustaining  the  statute.

It will  be seen  from  the foregoing  discussion  that  the
Texas  &  New  Orleans  case  in no  way conflicts  with  the
Coppage  case  or  the  Adair  case.
The  more  recent  de-
cision  did  not  involve  a statutory  prohibition  against  the
discharge  of  union  members.  The  Railway  Labor  Act
merely  forbade  interference  with  the  process  of  select-
ing representatives  for  mediation  purposes,  and  the  pre-
liminary  injunction  of  the  District  Court  had  sought  to
enforce  the  statutory  command.  When  its  injunction
was  violated,  through  the  consistent  campaign  of  the
railroad  to undermine  the  plaintiff  organization,  the  Dis-
trict  Court  adjudged  the  defendant  in  contempt  and  as
a  part  of  its  judgment,  required  the  defendant  to  re-
store  the  status  existing  before  its  decree,  even  though
this  required  the  restoration  of certain  union  employees.

Thia  distinction  between  the  earlier  decisions  in the
Adair  and  Coppage  cases  and  the  recent  decision  in  the
Texas  &  New  Orleans  Railroad  Company  case  is  well
stated  in  the  opinion  of  Judgé  Wilbur  of  the  Circuit
Court  of  Appeals  for  the  Ninth  Circuit,  in  National
Labor  Relations  Board  v.  Mackey  Radio  &  Telegraph
Company  (decided  January  11,  1937).
Judge  Wilbur
says:

“It  is  thus  apparent  that  the  Supréme  Court
had  uncer  consideration  in  Texas  & N.  O.  R.  Co.  v.
Brotherhood  of  Ry.  &  8.  8.  Clerks,  281  &  S.  548,
supra,  the  single  question  of  whether  or  not  Con-
gress  in setting  up  a  scheme  for  voluntary  arbitra-
tion  of  railway  labor  disputes  could  prohibit  inter-
ference  by  the  one  party,  the  Railroad  Company,
with  the  rights  of the  other  parties,  the  employees
to select  those  who  were  to represent  them  in the

the  representatives  selected  by  the  employees  for

“What  the  petitioner  seeks  in  this  case  is  the
approval  and  the  enforcement  of  an  order  which
clearly  violates  the  fundamental  right  of  the  re-
spondent  guaranteed  to  it by the  Fifth  Amendment
to  tiie  Constitution.
If we  act  upon  the  principles
of  constitutional]  law  relating  to  the  liberty  to  con-
tract as announced  by the Supreme  Court in Adair
v.  United  States,  supra,  and  Coppage  v.  Kansas,
supra,  our  inquiry  would  end  here  for  it was  there
held  that  an employer  had  a  right  to refuse  to con-
tract  with  a  present  or  prospective  member  of a
labor  organization,  if he  so desired,  and that  an act

116

Argument.

of « state,
in one case,  and  a  statute
of Congress,
in the other,  which  prohibited  that exercise  of dis-
eretion,  or choice,  on the part  of the  employer  was
unconstitutional
and void because  a violation  of the
Fifth  Amendment.”

The  petitioner  makes  a  brave  effort  to compare  the
facts  of this  case  with  the issues  of  the Texas  &  New
Orleans  Railroad  Company  case,  by  pointing out  that
its order against  the respondent  was designed  primarily
te enjoin  the respondent  from  interfering  with the  or-
ganization  activities  of its employees  and that it directed
the restoration  of the complaining  employees  as a  mere
incident  to this  negative  relief.  This  argument
is sub-
ject  to two serious  objections.  In the  first  place, the
entire  record  demonstrates  that  the  primary  purpose
of the suit  was the restoration  of the ten employees,  the
“cease  and desist”  portions  of the petitioner’s  decree  be-
ing purely  ancillary.  In the second  place,  the petitioner’s
argument,  in  stressing  that  the  respondent  has  no  con-
stitutional  right  to  interfere  with  the  organization  ac-
tivities  of its employees,  totally  disregards  the  fact  that
its  own  order  purports  to  deprive  the  respondent  of  its
constitutional  right  to  employ  in  accordance  with  its
own  selection.

Nor is this the only distinction  between  the  legis-
lation  which  was in issue  in the  Texas  &  New  Orleans
ease  and  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act,  as  it applies
to the petitioner.

As we have  stressed  before,  the power  of Congress
over interstate  carriers  is almost  plenary  in character.
Carriers,  being public  utilities,  are required  to maintain
a continuous  service  and Congress  may, if it deems  fit,
compel  the carrier  to maintain  such service,  even against
ita wishes.  The drastic wage-fixing
provisions  of the
Adamson  Act would  not have  been  upheld  in Wilson  v.

Argument.

:

wz

Now, 243 U.  3, 332  (1917), if this Court had not believed
and
that it was  “essential  to protect  the public
safeguard  the  movement  of interstate  commerce  % *  *.”.
No one  could  contend  that  the  respondent  is in the
would have
as a railroad  or  that Congress
same  category
the  right  to  compel  it to continue  operations  against  its
will.  This  distinction  is absolutely. fundamental.

Thus  in Chas.  Wolff  Packing  Co.  v.  Court  of Indus-

trial Relations,
creating  an industrial  court with power  to hear  disputes  _

terms  of employment
was  involved.  The  statute was
confined  in operation  to employers  engaged  in the busi-
iness of manufacturing  food  and  certain  other  more
or  less  essential  industries,  the  determination  of  the
public  character  of the  employer’s  business  being  vested
in the industrial court.  The  Court  said  of the statute
that:

“It  curtails  the right  of the employer  on the one
hand,  and  of the  employee  on  the  other,  to contract
about
his affairs.  This  is part  of the liberty  of  the
individual  protected  by  the guaranty  of  the due pro-
cess  Clause  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.  Meyer
v.  Nebraska,  262  U.  S,  390  (1923).  While  there  is
no. such  thing  as  absolute  freedom  of  contract,  and
it  is  subject  to  a  variety  of  restraints,  they  must
not be arbitrary
or unreasonable.  Freedom is the
general  rule,  and  restraint  the  exception.”  (p. 534)

An  argument  in support  of the statute  was  made,
that it  applied  only to businesses  affected  with  a  public
interest,  and reference  was  made  to  the case  of Wilson
v.  New,  supra.  The  Court  pointed  out that  the distinc-
tion lay in the fact  that  Congress  had power  to compel
the continued  operation  of the railroads,  and therefore
to pass  regulations  which  would  insure  such  continuity

8

Argument.

This  element,  the  Court  declared,  was
ake in ordinary  businesses,  irrespective  of their  size
and  importance,  stating:

“But  the  chief  and  conclusive  distinction  be-
tween  Wilson  v.  New  and the case  before  ys ia that
already  referred  to.  The  power  of a
ture  to
compel  continuity  in a businegs  can  only aris«,  where
the  obligation  of  continued  service  by  the  owner
and its employees  is direct  and is assumed  when  the
business
is entered  upon.  A  common  carrier,  which
accepts  a railroad  franchise,  is not free to withdraw
the use of that  which  it  has granted  to the public.
It is true  that  if operation  is impossible  without  con-
tinuous  loss  ( Brooks-Scanlon  Co.  v.  R.  R.  Commis-
sion,  251  U.  S.  396;  Bullock  v.  R.  R.  Commission,
254  U.  S.  513,  it  may  give  up  its  franchise  and  en-
terprise,  but,  short  of  this,  it  must  continue.
Not
so  the  owner,  when  by  mere  changed  conditions
his  business  becomes  clothed  with  a  public  interest,
He  may  stop  at  will,  whether  the  business  be  losing
or  profitable.

The  minutely  detailed  government  supervision,
including  that  of their  relations  to  their  employees,
to which  the  railroads  of the  country  have  been
gradually  subjected  by Congress  through  its  power
over  interstate  commerce,  furnishes  no  precedent
for regulation  of  the business  of  the  plaintiff  in
error,  whose  classification  as  public  is  at  the  best
doubtful.”  (pp.  543-544)

This  principle was reiterated,  when  the statute  came
the Court  again  (267  U.  S. 552),  in the statement

before
that:

“The  Court  recognized  that, in a  sense,  all busi-
.  ness is of some  concern  to the public,  and subject  to
some  measure  of regulation,  but  made  it plain  that
may  go
the extent  to which  regulation  reasonably

varies  greatly  with different  classes  of business,  and

was  taken  to point  out  that  operating  a  railroad,

(

“The  system  of  compulsory  arbitration  which
the  act  establishes  is intended  to compel,  and  if sus-
tained  wil!  compel,  the  owner  and  employes  to  con-
tinue  the  business  on  terms  which  are  not  of  their

terms.
True,  the  terms  have  some  qualifications,

the  duty  imposed.  Such  a  system  infringes

erty  of contract  and  rights  of  property  guaranteed
by the  due  process  of  law  clause  of the Fourteen
Amendment.”  (pp.  567,  569)

,

erty,  in  order  to  be  affected  with  a  public  interest,

Apply  this  to the instant  case  and the results  are

rela-
be sustained.  In questions  of employer-employee
tionships,  the law has always  been  hesitant  to interfere.
Courts  of equity  from  time  immemorial  have  refused  to
of an employment  contract
compel  specific performance
as against  either  the employer  or the employee.  From

that  he should  not be forced  into  a  relationship  which
of the
to him.  From the standpoint
may be distasteful
employer,  the court  have recognized  that the right to
judge  the  capabilities  of his  employees  is absolutely  es-
of a business.  A va-
sential to the proper management
riety of reasons  may lead an employer  to discharge  an
employee.  It may  be  a question  of efficiency,  of applica-
tion,  or  discipline.  On  other  occasions  the  employer  may
be required  to take  action  to protect  the morale  of  his
other  employees,  to  prevent’  dissatisfaction  of  other
workers,  or  to  put an  end  to suspected  theft,  sabotage  or
In  such  cases,  it may  be
‘other  undesirable  conditions.
preferable,  for  obvious  reasons  of  policy,  to  withhold
disclosure  of  the  cause  of  the  discharge.  In any  case
the  question  of judgment  is a delicate  one  which  the  law
has always  seen  fit to rest  in the personal  discretion  of
the  employer.  If it is proper  for  the  law  to  deprive  the
employer  of  his right  to judgment  and to  rest  the func-
tion  in a  board  of  this  character,  and then  to  compel  the
employer  to  restore  the  employee  who  has  been  dis-
charged,  there is an ¢nd to efficient  management.

The facts  of the present  case will  provide  a  striking
illustration  of  the  menace  to  the.respondent’s  freedom
which  inheres  in the  Labor  Act.  If the facts  with  re-
spect  to the alleged  cases  of distrimination  are recalled,

122

:

Argument.

it will  be observed  that  in each  case  the  complaining  em-
ployees  were  in  some  way  at  fault,  their  principal  ex-
cuses  being  that  the  respondent  had  not  been  dil#fent  in
discharging  other  employees  for  similar  faults,  or  that
other  employees  were  the  responsible  ones.  Two  of  the
witnesses  charged  the  respondent  with  discrimination
as  to  working  time,  but  when  they  estimated  their  loss
of wages,  for  purposes  of  damages,  they  admitted  that
they  had  been  receiving  fairly  steady  work.  In  several
instances  members  of  the  Board  supplied  the  necessary
answers  for the witnesses  by the  form of their  questions.
If  the  record  is  stripped  of  the  atmosphere  which  was
engendered  by the hostility  of the  complaining  witnesses,
it will  be seen  that  in each  case  the  propriety  of  the  dis-
charge  was  a  matter  of  discretion,  with  the  petitioner
assuming  that  the  discharge  must  have  been  wrongful
because  the  complaining  witness  was  a  member  of  a
union  and  because  his  faults  were  not,  in  petitioner’s
judgment,  sufficient  to  warrant  such  drastic  action.

In  short,  the  petitioner  has  sat  in  judgment  upon
the  respondent  and  determined  that  it has  not  properly
exercised  its  discretion  to  hire  and  discharge  employees.
If  this  is  not  an  interference  with  the  normal  right  of
the  respondent  to  manage  its  own  business  (to  para-
phrase  the  language  of the  Court  in  the  Texas  and  New
Orleans  case),  then  would be  difficult  to  find anything
which  would  be.
In  no  case  has  the  law  ‘gone  to  the
lengths  of  requiring  an  employer  to  rehire  an  employee
who  is  considered  unfit.  Even  in  cases  of  a  long-term
émployment  contract,  the  law,  at most,  awards  damages
for  its  breach;  it  does  not  compel  its  continuance.

The  language  of  a  New  York  Court  in  H.  B.  Rosen-
“+,aL Bttlinger  Oo.  v.  Schlossberg,  266  N.  Y.  Supp.  762
(1933),  in dealing  with  a similar  provision  in  Section  7A
of  the  National  Recovery  Act,  is  in  point:

Argument.

123

“If  that  (Section

7A)  means  that  an  employer
may  not  discharge  an  employee  for  any  reason,  or
for  no  reason,  and in  the case of  any man discharged,
must  be prepared  to show  good  cause  for  the dis-
charge,  other  than  the  union  affiiliations  of the man
discharged,  all  employees  who  are  union  members
have  been  transferred  to a status  equivalent  to that
of  the  civil  service,  a  result  which  could  not  have
been  intefded.  Whatever  the  rule  of  the  unions
may  be,  that  is not  the  law  of the  land.”  (p. 764)

This  suggests  another  dangerous  consequence  of the
Labor  Act,  which  is of peculiar  importance  in the  present
case.  The  decision  of the  Board  argues  vehemently  that
there  must  have  been  discrimination,  because  three  of
the  discharged  employees  were  officers  of  the  union.
If  union  members  are  a  preferred  class  in  “a  status
equivalent  to  that  of  the  civil  service,”  it is not  surpris-
ing  that  some  of  the  union  officers,  relying  upon  their
sure  tenure  of  employment  and  immunity  from  disci-
pline,  should  be  found  among  those  discharged  for  in-
subordination  or  insolence.
If the  Act  gives  union  men
freedom  from  discharge,  it  encourages  laziness,  viola-
tion  of  rules  and  inefficiency.  Employees  who  are  affi-
liated  with  a  union  will  consider  that  fact  sufficient  pro-
tection  against  demotion  or  discharge,  if  the  law  is  to
make  the  employer  produce  an  iron-clad  excuse  for every
disciplinary  act.  One  of  the  witnesses  in this  case,  who
was  discharged  because  a  nut  fell  off  a  crane  which  he
was  required  to  inspect  said  he  would  not  go  to  see  if
the nut  had  fallen  off  (R.  158).  Another  was  not  on  the
job  when  he  was  summoned  to  duty  and  complains  be-
cause  he  was  disciplined  (R.  174).  He  apparently  be-
lieved  it union  discrimination  because  he  was  not  given
time  off  to  go  to  a  picnic  (R.  172).  These  are  trifling
things,  but  they  indicate  the  attitude  of  insolence  and
defiance  which  the  Board  has  encouraged.

of ten em-
ployees,  the  petitioner  ordered  the  respondent  to  post

ge or in any

Such  a  notice  would  almost  force  employees  to  join
the  union.  No  other  interpretation  would  be  put  upon
it  by  a  body  of  working  men.
It  is  the  equivalent  of
telling  them  that  if  they  become  affiliated  with  this
union,  they  will  be  thenceforth  immune  from  discharge.

The  respondent  hes  10,000  employees  in  the  Ali-
quippa  plant  and  22,000  employees  in  the  vicinity  of
Pittsburgh.  The effect  of the  reinstatement  of those  ten
‘men  upon  the  discipline  and  morale  of  thousands  of

other  employees  would  cost  the  respondent  many  thous-
ands  of dollars  and  deprive  it of property  of substantial
value  far in excess  of  the amount  of  wages  involved in
this  particular  case.

The  National  Labor  Relations  Board  in  this  case
has  said  that  the  respondent  must  revoke  its  consitiered
judgment  and  must  retain  and  pay  wages  to  employees
whom  it  has  deemed  unfit  to work  in its plant.  The
judgment  of  the  employer,  though  it  be  based  on  con-
siderations  of efficiency,  morale,  discipline  or  safety,  is
arbitrarily  set aside
and  rights  of the
e  freedom
to enforce a  dubious  economic  pol-
employer
icy.  What
would  the Congress  of  the United  States
have  to compel  this  respondent  to operate  its  plant if it

Argument.

125

desired  to discontinue  operations  or to require  it to
operate  in &  way  which  economists  might  deem  more
efficient  ?  By the same token,  what right has the Fed-
eral  Government  to require  it to enter  into  a relationship
which  ft condemns  as opposed  to its best judgment?  And
what  right has the National  Labor  Relations  Board,  with
its  apparent  objective  of  organizing  the  Steel  Industry,
to  usurp  the  management  of the  respondent  and  to  en-
courage  laziness,  disobedience  and  sabotage  among  its
employees?

In closing  this  point  of  the case,  it  might be well
to  advert  briefly  to  the  underlying  philosophy  of  the
National  Labor  Relations  Act.  As  we  have  pointed  out
in  our  discussion  of  the  substantive  provisions  of  the
Act,  Section  9 establishes  the  so-called  majority  rule,
which,  in effect,  declares  that  the  Board  has the power
to  certify  the  representatives  of  a  majority  of  the  em-
ployees  as  the  exclusive  representatives  of  all  the  em-
ployes,  for  purposes  of  collective  bargaining  with  re-
spect  to  wages,  hours  of  labor  and  working  conditions.
Individual  employees  and  minority  groups  are  thereby
deprived  of  their  right  to  bargain  with  their  employer,
although  the  right  to  present  individual  grievances  is
preserved.  The other  provisions  of the Act are  designed,
in the  last  analysis,  to buttress  the  effect  of the majority
rule.  National  labor  organizations  are  sanctioned  as  the  ©
exclusive  representatives  of  employees  by  Section  7 of
the  Act.
Section  8 penalizes  the  employer  who  seeks
to  encourage  or  discourage  membership  in  a  union,  but
at  the same  time  expressly  sanctions  the closed  shop
contract,  if entered  into  between  the  employer  and  the
union  representing  the  majority  of  his  employees.
In
other  words,  while  the  employer  may  not  discharge  a
union  employee,  he  may  insist  that  all  of his  employees
shall join, support  and pay dues to  an organization  which
Subdivision  5 of  Sec-
is  the  favorite  of  the  majority.

126

Argument.

tion 8 Jends further  weight  to the majority  rule, by re-
quiring  the  emiployer  to  bargain  exclusively  with  the
representatives  of  the  majority.

The  petitioner,  in its argument  in this  case,  does
not refer  to the question  of  majority  rule  and will  no
doubt  argue  that  its  decision  and  order  have  not  ap-
plied  it in this  case.  Nevertheless,  the  testimony  which
was  received  extolled  the  virtues  of  exclusive  represen-
tation  by an  organization,  as  well  as  the  alleged  advant-
ages  of a  closed  shop,  and  it is clearly  the  theory  of  the
.  petitioner,  as  we  have  demonstrated,  that  if the  respond-
ent’s  plant  were  organized,  the  movement  of  interstate
commerce  would  be  free  from  potential  restraints.
Be-
cause  of this  fact,  we think  that  we are  entitled  to ob-
serve  that  the underlying  philosophy  of the Act  was  con-
demned  by  the  Court  as  an  unreasonable  restraint  on
freedom  to  contract.
The  Bituminous  Coal  Conserva-
tion  Act  of  1935  provided  that  wages  and  hours  of
labor  should  be  governed  in  the  coal  industry  by  col-
lective  agreements  between  representatives  of  the  pro-
ducer  and  representatives  of  “a  majority  of  the  mine
workers.”  The  Court,  in  Carter  v.  Carter  Coal  Co.,  298
U. S. 238,  supra,  adverted  briefly  to this  provision,  in the
statement:

“The  power  conferred  upon  the  majority  is,  in
effect,  the power  to regulate  the affairs  of  an un-
willing  minority.
This  is  legislative  delegation  in
its  most  obnoxious  form;  for  it is  not  even  delega-
tion  to an  official  or  an  official  body,  presumptively
disinterested,  but  to  private  persons  whose  inter-
ésts  may  be and  often  are adverse  to the interests
of others  in the  same  business.  The  record  shows
that  the  conditions  of competition  differ  among  the
In  some,  coal  dealers  compete
various  localities.
In  other  localities,  they  also
among  themselves.

Conclusion.

127

compete  with  the  mechanical  production  of elec-
trical  energy  and  of  natural  gas.  Some  coal pro-
ducers  favor  the  code;  others  oppose  it;  and  the
record  clearly  indicates  that  this  diversity  of  view
arises  from  their  conflicting  and  even  antagonistic
interests.
The  difference  between  producing  coal
and  regulating  its  production  is,  of  course,  funda-
mental.  The  former  is a private  activity;  the  latter
is necessarily  a governmental  function,  since,  in  the
very  nature  of  things,  one  person  may  not  be  en-
trusted  with  the  power  to  regulate  the  business  of
another,  and  especially  of  a  competitor.
And  a
statute  which  attempts  to confer  such  power  under-
takes  an  intolerable  and  unconstitutional  interfer-
ence  with  personal  liberty  and  private  property.
The  delegation  is so  clearly arbitrary,  and  so  clearly
a  denial  of  rights  safeguarded  by  the  due  process
clause  of  the  Fifth  Amendment,  that  it  is unneces-
sary  to do  more  than  refer  to  decisions  of  this
court,  which  foreclose  the  question.
Schechter
Corp.  v.  United  States,  295  U.S.  at  p.  587;  Eubank
v.  Richmond,  226 U.  S: 137,  143;  Seattle  Trust  Co.  #.
Roberg,  278  U.  S. 116,  121-122.”  (pp.  311-312)

CONCLUSION:

The  bulk  of  the  testimony  heard  by  the  petitioner
consisted  of expert,  economic  and  political  opinion  which
depicted  the  harmful  effects  of  labor  disputes  and
strikes  and  extolled  the  virtues  of  organization  and
union  contracts.
Considering  that  this  phase  of  the
hearing  was  entirely  ex  parte  and  that  no  witnesses
appeared  to  point  out  the  disadvantages  of  a  “closed
shop”  and  its  injustice  to  employees  who  desire  to  work
without  joining  any  union,  it  is  not  surprising  that  the

128

Conchaston.

argument  may,  at  first  glance,  seem  persuasive.
fact is, however,  that it is wholly  immaterial.

The

In complete  disregard  of the respondent's  right to
manage  and  enjoy  the  fruits  of  its  own  property  under
the due process  clause,  the petitioner  is asking  this
Court  to find  that  the  advantages  of union  organization
outweigh  the  dangers  of  infringing  upon  the  respond-,
Legal justification  for  the
ent’s  freedom  of  contract.
action  of the Board  cannot  be supplied  by the reference
to  the  economists  who  appeared  as  expert  witnesses,  to
testify  to  their  views  that  union  organization  and  the
“closed  shop”  would  be better  for  industry  and  the  coun-
try  generally.

For  their  views  they  are  answerable  to  no  one  but
themselves  and, if they  are wrong,  they  bear  none  of
the  losses,  while  the  management  of a large  corporation
is  responsible  to  its  stockholders  and  to  its  employees
for  the  conduct  of  the  business.  The  open  shop  policy,
so long  pursued  by the  vast  majority  of American  em-
ployers,  has  enabled  them  to  protect  the  interests  of
their  employees,  who  do  not  desire  unionization,  and  to
compel  unions,  in  a  measure,  to  rely  upon  their  own
merits  to  expand  their  membership,  instead  of  depend-
ing upon  compulsory  membership  by operation  of  laws.
American  industry  has  gone  far  under  such  a policy,
with  benefit  not  only  to  stockholders,  but  also  to  em-
ployees.

UP the dinseets sind ccchettinte  tun upetn batene the

-  Board  can thrust  upon  the  employer,  as the petitioner
urges,  a  new economic  policy  with  respect  to labor,  and
if the National  Labor  Relations  Board  can, by ite de-
cision,  scrap  the considerations  which  led  to the dis-
charge  of a  few of respondent’s  employees  and force
their reinstatement,
upon the basis of the economists’
theories  as to the proper  method  of handling  labor

Conclusion.

129

problems,  there  would  seem  to  be  no  reason  why  other
boards  and  bureaus  could  not  be authorized  by Congress
to dictate  or  control  other  business  policies of employers.
The same  methods  have  been  used  to  persuade  this
Court  that  the  Labor  Act  is  an  appropriate  vehicle  for
Federal  action.  The  argument  again  is one  of  expedi-
ency,  and  not  of  law.
Perhaps  the  Congress  of  the
United  States  is  better  fitted  to  deal  with  the  problem
of labor  relations  than  the legislative  bodies  of the  sep-
arate  States.  Perhaps  the  management  and  regulation
of  every  business  by commissions  and  boards,  who  are
‘  not  responsible  to  the  owners,  would  carry  the  same
appeal  to  the  economists  and  theorists  who  testified  in
this  case.  But  such  regimentation  is not  permitted  un-
der  our  fundamental  law.  A  brief  phrase  from  the
opinion  of  Judge  Hand  of  the  Second  Circuit,  in  the
Schechter  case,  is a better  reply  to the  petitioner's  argu-
ment  than  a  host  of economic  treatises:

|

“It  may  indeed  follow  that  the  nation  cannot,
as  a  unit,  meet  any  of the  great  crises  of  its  exist-
ence,  except  war,  and  that  it must  obtain  the  con-
currence  of  the  separate  states;  but  that  to  some
extent  is  implicit  in  any  federation,  and  the  result-
ing  weaknesses  have  not  hitherto  been  thought  to
outweigh  the  dangers  of  a  completely  centralized
government.  If the  American  people  have  come  to
believe  otherwise,  Congress  is  not  their  accredited
organ  to  express  their  will  to  change.”
[United
-  States  v.  A.  L.  A.  Schechter  Poultry  Corporation,

76 F (2)  617,  627  (1935) }.

For  more  than  a  century  this  Court  has  steadfastly
adhered  to  the  simple,  literal  meaning  which  the  great
Marshall  found  in  the  commerce  chause—that  Congress
has  power—in  the  words  of  the  Constitution—“to  reg-
ulate  commerce  among  the  several  states.”
Since  Gibd-

130

 Omeheeen

he preserved.  The

bone v.  Ogden,  it has given  assurance  to the States,  to
their citizens  and‘  the federal  union  that  their  respec-
fixes  them,
Constitution
tive  rights  and  powers,  as
and police
authority
shall
powers  of the several  States  have  been  protected  from
encroachment  because  this Court has always  ruled that
local  activities,  such  as mining  and industry,  are within
their  exclusive  jurisdiction.  By the  same  devotion to
the mandate  of the Constitution,  the rights  of individu-
ala to freedom  from  unjustifiable  restraint  have  been
sanctioned.

The petitioner  is asking  that the traditions  and pre-
cedents  of a  century  be cast  aside  and the Constitution
corrupted  for the sake  of a  temporary  expediency.  To
change  the established  meaning  of the Constitution  now,
by judicial  decree,  without  a  proper  mandate  from  the
people,  would  make  a  mockery  of constitutional  govern-
ment.

Respectfully  submitted,

EArt  F..  REEp,
CHARLES  ROSEN,
W.  D.  Evans,
JoHN  E. LAUGHLIN,  JR.,

National

Labor

Relations  mca

Petitioner,
vs.

to
the  United  States  Circuit
Court  of  Appeals  for  the
Fifth  Cirenit.

[April  12,  1937.]

Mr.  Chief  Justice  Hugues  delivered  the  opinion  of  the  Court.

practices

affecting  commerce.

In &  proceeding  under  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act  of 1935,
the  National  Labor  Relations  Board  found  that  the  weapons  ones
&  Laughlin  Steel  Corporation,  had  violated  the  Act  by  engaging
in  unfair  labor
The  proceed-
ing  was  instituted  by  the  Beaver  Valley  Lodge  No.  200,  affiliated
with  the  Amalgamated  Association  of  Iron,  Steel  and  Tin  Workers
of  America,  a  labor  organization.
The  unfair  labor  practices
charged  were  that  the  corporation  was  discriminating  against  mem-
bers  of  the  union  with  regard  to  hire  and  tenure  of  employment,
and  was  coercing  and  intimidating  its  employees in  order  to  inter-
fere  with  their  self-organization.
The  discriminatory  and  coercive
action  alleged  was  the  discharge  of  certain  employees.

The  National  Labor  Relations  Board,  sustaining  the  charge,
ordered  the  corporation  to  cease  and  desist  from  such  discrimination
and  coercion,  to  offer  reinstatement  to  ten  of  the  employees  named,
to  make  good  their  losses  in  pay,  and  to  post  for  thirty  days  notices
that  the  corporation  would  not  discharge  or  discriminate  against
members,  or  those  desiring  to  become  members,  of  the  labor  union.
As  the  corporation  failed  to  comply,  the  Board  petitioned  the  Cir-
cuit  Court  of  Appeals  to  enforce  the  order.  The  court  denied  the
petition,  holding  that  the  order  lay  beyond  the  range  of  federal
power.

83  F.  (2d)  998.  We  granted  certiorari.

The  scheme  of  the  National  Labor  Relations  Act—which  is  too
long  to  be  quoted  in  full—may  be  briefly  stated.
